text: **Yuyang Zazhu** **Tang Dynasty** **Duan Chengshi** **Preface** The hexagram "chariot" in the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) is associated with strange phenomena. The poem "Nan Qi" in the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry) is nearly playful. Confucian scholars, in their spare time, write about both the strange and the playful, without compromising the seriousness of Confucianism. The taste of poetry and books is like a rich broth, history is like a dissected animal, and the *Zi* (various philosophical texts) are like vinegar and soy sauce. How could one not enjoy roasted birds and turtles? Those who work diligently without shame are the authors of books that record strange and novel stories. My writing style is somewhat loose and flowing, and I have not deeply pondered. I do not have the lament of Cui Zhenlong, but I am criticized for painting tigers like Kong Zhang. In the spare time after eating, I occasionally record my memories and name it *Yuyang Zazhu*. It consists of thirty chapters in twenty volumes, and I do not record the taste of the time. **Chapter One: Loyalty and Valor** Emperor Gaozu was young and possessed great courage. At the end of the Sui dynasty, he once defeated tens of thousands of bandits with just twelve men. In another battle at Longmen, he used all the arrows in one quiver and hit eighty people. Emperor Taizong had a curly beard and once played with a bow and arrow. He liked using large arrows with four feathers, which were as long as a skin. He shot through the door and hit the latch.

The Emperor once observed fishing at the Western Palace, and saw the fish leap. He asked the reason for this, and the fisherman replied, "This is because they are about to give birth." Thus, the net was cast and the fishing stopped. The country of Bolqan presented a hundred horses to the Emperor, ten of which were especially swift. The Emperor named them "Juebo Shu," as they had spurs near their hind legs and could run through three thresholds without stumbling. He especially cherished them. The Sui dynasty's treasury had a jade monkey with interlocking arms, its two arms joined like a linked ring. He intended to attach it to the reins. Later, the Emperor rode with his attendants for a tour and disliked its ornamentation, striking it with a whip and breaking it (one account says that the ten horses were named by Emperor Taizong). During the reign of Emperor Taizong, a white magpie suddenly built a nest on a Chinese scholar tree in front of the sleeping chamber. The nest was shaped like a waist drum and resembled a happy union. The attendants bowed and danced, offering congratulations. The Emperor said, "I once laughed at Emperor Yang of Sui for his fondness of auspicious signs. True auspices lie in finding virtuous people, so what is there to celebrate here?" He then ordered the nest to be destroyed and the magpie released into the wilderness. When Emperor Gaozong was just learning to walk, he played with a pen. The attendants placed paper in front of him, and he scribbled all over it. At the corner, where he had drawn, the character “敕” appeared in cursive script. Emperor Taizong immediately ordered it to be burned and did not allow it to be spread outside. On the night of Wu Zetian's birth, all the hens crouched. There was a black hair on her right middle finger, which twisted to the left like a black mole and could be pulled out over a foot. Luo Binwang wrote a proclamation for Xu Jingye, detailing the many faults of the Tang dynasty. Empress Wu read it and smiled when she came to the lines, "The empress refuses to yield to others, and her cunning charm has deceived the ruler." But when she reached, "The soil of the empire is not yet dry, and where is the young ruler of six feet?" she was displeased and said, "How could a chancellor allow such a person to escape?"

During the Jinglong era of Emperor Zhongzong, he summoned the scholars to go hunting, acting as attendants, with a formation that was square in front and round at the back. There were two eagles, and the emperor looked up at them. Someone cried out, "I can catch them." He then hung a dead mouse on the hawk's foot, tied its eyes, and released it as a lure. The two eagles indeed struck at the hawk's plate. A cunning rabbit rose ahead, and the emperor raised a club and struck it down. The emperor praised it, and all the attendants cried out "Long live the emperor!" On the third day of the third month, he gave the attendants willow rings, saying that wearing them would protect against scorpion poison. On the Cold Food Festival, he gave the attendants colorful balls and embroidered grass platforms. On the beginning of spring, he gave the attendants colorful flower trees. On the Laba Festival, he gave the scholars at the Northern Gate lip balm and wax, placed in green-carved ivory tubes. The emperor once dreamed that birds flew, and dozens of bats chased and fell to the ground. He woke up in alarm and summoned the monk Wanhuai, saying, "Everyone is ascending to heaven now." The next day he died. Emperor Ruizong once inspected the imperial treasury and saw a whip, golden in color, four feet long, with several sections gnawed by insects, resembling a coiled dragon. On the handle was a toothed plaque inscribed with elephant ear skin. Some said it was an old item from the Sui palace treasury. When the emperor was Prince Ji, a snail trail on the wall of his study formed the character 'tian' (heaven). The emperor was frightened and quickly swept it away. After several days, it returned to its original state. When he ascended the throne, he carved jade and cast gold into the shape of snails, placing them in front of Buddhist and Taoist statues.

Emperor Xuanzong, within the inner palace, was often called "Ahman" and also referred to as "Yah." Princess Shou'an was born to Consort Cao Yena. Because she was born in the ninth month of the lunar calendar, she did not marry. She was often dressed in religious robes and tasked with managing the burning of incense. Her pet name was "Chongniang," and the emperor affectionately called her "Shiniang." When Emperor Xuanzong was the Taishang Huang (retired emperor), during the daily routine of Emperor Suzong, the emperor said, "You have a good reputation while in the East Palace." He then pointed to Princess Shou'an and said, "Chongniang is Yah's daughter; you will give her a title later." When Emperor Suzong was in Lingwu, he ordered Su Qing to marry her, and she was given the title of Princess Shou'an. At the end of the Tianbao era, the region of Jiaozhi (ancient Vietnam) presented dragon brain resin, shaped like cicadas and silkworms. According to Persians, such precious dragon brain is only found in the nodes of old dragon brain trees, and it was called "Rui Longnao" in the palace. The emperor bestowed ten pieces of this precious dragon brain upon the concubine, and its fragrance could be smelled for over ten steps. During the summer, the emperor often played chess with his princes, and he had He Huai Zhi play the pipa alone while the concubine stood in front of the chessboard to watch. When the emperor was about to lose several pieces, the concubine placed a small object from the game on the side, which caused the pieces to become disordered, greatly pleasing the emperor. At one point, the wind blew the concubine's scarf onto He Huai Zhi's head, and it stayed there for a long time before falling off when he turned his head. Upon returning home, He Huai Zhi noticed a very unusual fragrance on his body, so he removed his head covering and stored it in a brocade bag. When the two emperors returned to the palace, they continually remembered and mourned the concubine, and He Huai Zhi presented the stored head covering, reporting the incident in detail. The emperor opened the bag and wept, saying, "This is the precious dragon brain fragrance."

An Lushan received unparalleled favor and innumerable gifts. The types of items bestowed upon him included: Sangluo wine, broad-tailed sheep dishes, mare's milk, two groups of musicians, fresh wild boar meat, carp and a specialized knife, clear wine, large brocade, a carriage adorned with precious symbols, yu-gan candy, wild pheasants from the Liaozuo region, a five-herb soup, a medicinal soup made from gold and stone ingredients prepared by a skilled apothecary, steamed pears, gold-plated ivory chopsticks and spoons, a tray for serving dumplings decorated with gold and silver, a vase shaped like a lion, possibly decorated with gold, a mat or cushion, boots made from fine silk, a large, possibly ornate plate, a silver-plated wine vessel, a screen decorated with eight-pointed flowers and black designs, a silver-plated iron lock, a bed made of sandalwood, possibly with white or floral decorations, a mat or cushion with green and white patterns, embroidered goose feather mats arranged in the residence, a set of items, possibly including a horse, decorated with gold, purple, and red silk, a type of robe or garment, a document, possibly with dragon motifs, a wine jar and bottle, possibly decorated with gold and silver, and a woven basket. Additionally, the Empress Consort bestowed upon him a jade box decorated with gold and a bowl, possibly with a metal face, decorated with gold.

Emperor Suzong was approaching a post station near Lingwu at dusk when a tall woman appeared, carrying two carp and shouting at the camp gate, "Where is the Emperor?" The crowd thought she was mad and quickly reported to the Emperor, who secretly observed her behavior. After speaking, the woman stopped under a big tree. Soldiers looked closely and saw scales on her arms. Soon it got dark, and she disappeared. When the Emperor ascended the throne and returned to the capital, Governor Wang Qiguang of Guo Prefecture reported on the tomb of Nüwa, saying that in the thirteenth year of the Tianbao era, heavy rain suddenly submerged it in darkness. On the night of the first day of this month, someone by the river heard the sound of wind and thunder. In the morning, they saw the tomb emerge, with a double willow tree growing on top, more than ten feet tall, and a large stone below. Along with a drawing, he presented it. When the Emperor first reclaimed the capital, he sent a diviner to perform a ritual at the site. At this point, it was seen, and the crowd suspected that the woman was a deity.

On the day Emperor Daizong ascended the throne, auspicious clouds appeared, and yellow qi embraced the sun. Initially, Chuzhou presented twelve national treasures, and an edict was issued decreeing that the emperor should oversee the state. The edict read: "Heavenly treasures descended, presented from Chuzhou. The gods produced symbols of destiny, and the union of the jade disk pacified the qi of calamities." Initially, in Chuzhou, there was a nun named Zhenru, who was suddenly taken to heaven by someone. The Heavenly Emperor said there would be calamities below and ordered these treasures to pacify it, with the number being twelve. The governor of Chuzhou, Cui Xin, submitted a report to present them. The first treasure was called Xuanhuang, shaped like a tablet, eight inches long, with a hole. It warded off human warfare and epidemics. The second treasure was called Yujiji, with white jade feathers. The king would see it if he governed the world with filial piety. The third treasure was called Heping, made of white jade, the size of a grain of millet, without any carved marks. If the king obtained it, the five grains would be abundant. The fourth treasure was called Xiwangmu Baihuan, two pieces. Wherever they were, foreign countries would submit. The fifth treasure is unnamed. The sixth treasure was called Ruyibaozhu, as large as a chicken egg. The seventh treasure was called Hongzhu, as large as a large chestnut. The eighth treasure was called Langgan Zhu, two pieces, larger than ordinary pearls, with a diameter exceeding one and a third inches. The ninth treasure was called Yuke, shaped like a jade ring, with one quarter missing. The tenth treasure was called Yuyin, as large as half a hand, with patterns resembling deer. The eleventh treasure was called Huanghou Caishang Gou, as thin as a chopstick, with the end curved. The twelfth treasure was called Leigong Shi, shaped like an axe, with no hole. When placed in the sun, all the treasures emitted white qi that reached the sky.

**Volume One: Rites and Differences** In the Western Han Dynasty, when the emperor met the chancellor, the usher announced, "The emperor stands up for the chancellor." When the Grand Censor appeared, the emperor respectfully thanked him. In the Han Dynasty, the wooden tablet representing the spirit of an ancestor was wrapped in tangerine bark and placed in a window, with cotton covering it to block the outside. During the ritual, a clay figure was made in a cage, headless, and it sat and stood as if alive in the dark hall. All tokens were used for different purposes: the ruler used jade tokens to govern the country, and for the capital and its outskirts, he used horn tokens; for mountain states, he used tiger tokens; for inland states, he used human tokens; for states by lakes, he used dragon tokens; for gates and checkpoints, he used symbol tokens; for trade and gifts, he used seal tokens; for roads, he used flag tokens. In ancient times, jade disks were used for peace, jade bi for official duties, jade璋 for success, jade珩 for border defense, jade璩 for battle, jade环 for sieges, jade隽 for disasters, jade龙 for droughts, and jade琮 for great funerals. In the Northern Qi Dynasty, when welcoming a southern envoy, the chief doctor of the Imperial Academy supervised the reception. Two messengers rode horses carrying letters in front, and two men in sheep-drawn carts held knives behind the messengers. One supervisor and one guest-master, both wearing scholar's hats, were present. Students in red clothes riding horses, with more than ten umbrellas, were also there. One servant in a red robe led the envoy's carriage. Six servants in red robes riding horses with flat headgear followed, and the main and deputy envoys each rode in a carriage, with horses behind. More than a hundred men in iron armor were present. There were also more than a hundred ceremonial guards, with silk cut like belts, white feathers interspersed with spears, curly-haired red robes, hats in five colors, robes matching the hair color, wooden spears, blades, and halberds painted red with frog banners.

On the first day of the lunar new year in the Liang Dynasty, the envoy to the north arrived at the imperial palace, riding in a carriage to the gate and entering the main entrance. The uppermost level of the gate was inscribed with the name "Zhu Ming Guan," the middle level was named "Ying Men," and a large decorative drum was placed below the gate. The next level was named "Tai Yang Men," with a tall building on the left side hanging a large bell, and the court hall on the right side, with the gate opening and two large decorative drums on either side. The envoy entered the gate, struck the bell and chime, and stood northwest of the northern suspension bell road. The envoy led several people, including the Prince of Xuancheng, entering after the envoy, struck the chime, and stood northeast of the road. Outside the suspended bell, there were officials on both sides. South of the northern suspension bell road, there were guests from the east near the road. West of the road, there were guests from Goguryeo and Baekje, and about three thousand officials who ascended the hall. Once their positions were set, the emperor emerged from the eastern hall, stating that he had been fasting and staying overnight outside the palace, and not coming through the upper gate. The bell and drum were struck, the imperial carriage was escorted, and the attendants ascended the eastern steps to sit in the tent facing south. The tent was made of green oilcloth with a dark blue or black skirt, very high, and tied to four columns, with a black lacquered curved table. Once seated, the emperor's ministers entered from the west gate, wearing full ceremonial attire, a specific type of hat, decorations at the ends of the hat's tassels made of jade and pearls, paired swords, and black shoes. Initially, two people led the way, followed by two people walking side by side, then one person carrying a box with teeth, a box with a board, and a box with a sword, while another twenty people wore formal attire, with over a hundred attendants following. They approached the Prince of Xuancheng, and at a few steps north, a double-seated position was prepared, where they performed a double bow, then proceeded to ascend, presenting jade to the emperor, who did not rise in response.

Wei sent Li Tonggui and Lu Cao on a diplomatic mission to Liang, and they entered through the western gate of Le You Yuan and went inside the blue oil tent. The Liang ruler prepared three rows of guards, and the imperial carriage entered from the south gate. Lu Cao and others bowed twice facing east, and the Liang ruler then entered the Lin Guang Hall from the north. Shortly after, the court envoys were invited in. The Liang ruler sat in a black tent, facing south. All the guests and officials took their seats, and the scribe Yin Ling was sent to deliver the ruler's instructions and to comfort and encourage them, with a formal reply. In the central courtyard, there were bells hanging and various entertainments on the palace platform. Wine was served in the flowing cup pool. The cup for the ruler was marked 'Imperial Cup,' and the others were marked with the officials' surnames. Those who reached it drank from it. There were also images of past events, which were made to flow along with the cups, from the beginning to the end of the gathering, they flowed continuously without interruption.

The emperor of the Liang dynasty often sent a messenger to distribute three types of traditional items to his officials on New Year's Day: New Year's Day wine, medicine to ward off evil, and pills to repel ghosts. In the Northern Dynasties, weddings involved hanging a blue cloth canopy as a temporary shelter, both inside and outside the door, called the "blue canopy." The couple exchanged vows under this canopy. During the wedding procession, the groom's family led a group of people, ranging from a hundred to a dozen, depending on their wealth, to call the bride out of the house and onto the carriage. The bride's relatives and guests would playfully hit the groom with sticks as a form of entertainment, sometimes causing him to become very exhausted. According to the law, there was a case where a man named Jia was married, and his friends, Yi and Bing, played a joke on him. They placed a cabinet next to him and compared it to a prison, lifting him and placing him inside, then covering it. Jia died from the shock, and the court ruled that he should be punished with the form of labor known as "ghost薪." In modern weddings, when welcoming the bride, three measures of millet were used to fill the mortar, one mat to cover the well, three pounds of wood to block the window, and three arrows placed on the door. When the bride got into the carriage, the groom rode around the carriage three times. On the day after the wedding, the groom's family prepared millet-based food. Before getting into the carriage, the bride covered her face with a traditional garment. When the bride entered the new home, her in-laws exited through a side door and re-entered through the main door to avoid stepping on her footprints. She then paid respects to the hearth and cooking area. The couple bowed together to their ancestors and sometimes tied a knot with a mirror, symbolizing their union. The bride's family also played with her, and if the wedding took place in the twelfth month of the lunar calendar, the bride would not see her mother-in-law on the wedding day.

In wedding ceremonies, the ritual of presenting gifts includes auspicious grains called *hehuan jiahe*, donkey-hide gelatin, a plant with nine shoots known as *jiuzi pu*, red reeds, two stones, cotton or wool, longevity cords, and dried lacquer. Each of these nine items carries a symbolic meaning: donkey-hide gelatin and lacquer are chosen for their adhesive properties, symbolizing strength and unity; cotton or wool is chosen for its softness and flexibility, symbolizing gentleness and adaptability; reeds or reed-like plants are chosen because they can bend and straighten, symbolizing resilience and adaptability; auspicious grains are chosen because they bring good fortune and blessings; two stones are chosen for their symbolic stability and mutual support. Women in the Northern Dynasties often presented shoes, socks, and boots on the winter solstice; in the first month of the year, they presented brooms and long-life flowers; on the day of the beginning of spring, they presented spring scrolls, which were painted green and sometimes included images of dragons or frogs; in the fifth month, they presented pictures and flowers representing the five seasons, placed on the bed canopy. On this day, they also presented longevity cords and twisted ropes, which were tied into human-like shapes and worn as accessories; on the summer solstice, they presented fans and cosmetic pouches, each with a symbolic meaning. Since the Qin and Han dynasties, when addressing the emperor, one should use the term "Your Majesty"; when addressing the crown prince, one should use the term "Your Highness"; when addressing a general, one should use the term "Under your command"; when addressing an envoy, one should use the term "Under your authority"; when addressing officials in the imperial court, one should use the term "Under your command"; when addressing high-ranking officials, one should use the term "Your Excellency"; when addressing parents, one should use the term "Under your care"; and when addressing peers, one should use the term "Under your feet."

### Volume One: Heavenly Distance Traditionally, it is said that the moon contains a cassia tree and a toad. Therefore, a rare book states that the moon's cassia tree is 500 feet tall, with a person constantly chopping it, and the tree heals immediately. This person is named Wu Gang, from the Xihe region, who was punished for his mistake in learning immortality by being made to chop the moon tree. A Buddhist text mentions a tree named Yanfu on the southern slope of Mount Sumeru, whose shadow falls into the moon as it passes. Some say that the moon's rabbit and cassia tree are shadows of the earth and water, respectively, which is closer to reality. The monk Yi Xing was widely knowledgeable and had no areas of ignorance, especially excelling in mathematics. His deep insights were beyond the comprehension of his contemporaries. In his youth, he was poor, and an elderly neighbor named Wang helped him financially, providing assistance worth tens of thousands of coins. When Yi Xing gained favor during the Kaiyuan period, he often thought of repaying Wang's kindness. Later, Wang's son was accused of murder, and the case was still under investigation. Wang visited Yi Xing to seek help. Yi Xing said, "If you want gold and silk, I will repay you tenfold. However, a just ruler enforces the law strictly, making it difficult to request (or show) favor, what can I do?" Wang angrily retorted, "Why should I recognize this monk!" Yi Xing tried to apologize to Wang but was ignored. Yi Xing then planned to use the laborers at the temple to create a large earthenware jar. He secretly chose two temple servants, gave them cloth bags, and instructed them, "In a certain坊 and corner, there is a废弃 garden. You should hide there and wait from noon until dusk. Seven objects will enter. You must capture all of them. If you miss one, I will punish you." The servants followed his instructions and went to the location.

After the hour of the rooster, indeed a group of pigs came, and the servants all captured them and returned. Yi Xing was greatly pleased and ordered them placed in a jar, covered with a wooden lid, sealed with six-one mud, and marked with red, inscribed with Sanskrit characters several inches long. His disciples could not understand. The next morning, an imperial messenger knocked on the door and urgently summoned him. Upon arriving at the side hall, Emperor Xuanzong welcomed and asked, "The Grand Historian reported that last night the Big Dipper was not seen. What is this omen? Do you have any way to dispel it?" Yi Xing replied, "During the Later Wei period, the planet Mars was lost. Until now, the imperial chariot (Big Dipper) has not been seen. This is something unprecedented in ancient times. The heavens are issuing a great warning to Your Majesty. If even a common woman or man does not find their place, then there will be frost and drought. Great virtue can move and cause them to withdraw. The most effective way to move them is in burying the dead and releasing the imprisoned? The Buddhist door with the mind can destroy all good, but compassion can subdue all demons. If my humble opinion is correct, it would be better to grant a general amnesty." Emperor Xuanzong followed his advice. The next evening, the Grand Historian reported that one star of the Big Dipper was seen, and it remained visible for seven days and then returned. Cheng Shi found this matter quite strange, but it was widely spread by the people, and it had to be recorded.

During the Yongzhen era, a commoner named Wang from the eastern market district was well-versed in books and possessed vast wealth, earning him the hospitality of many merchants and travelers. He had a daughter who was fourteen or fifteen years old, beautiful and intelligent, but each of her nostrils had a polyp hanging down, resembling soapberry seeds with roots like hemp threads, about an inch long, causing intense pain when touched. Her father spent millions of coins to treat her, but the condition did not improve. One day, a Buddhist monk came begging for alms and asked Wang, "Do you know that your daughter has a strange illness? May I see her? I can stop it." Upon hearing this, Wang was greatly pleased and showed the monk his daughter. The monk took a white-colored medicine and blew it into her nostrils. Soon after, he removed the polyps, and a small amount of yellowish fluid flowed out, causing her no further discomfort. Wang rewarded the monk with white gold, to which the monk replied, "I am a practitioner of the Way and do not accept large donations; I only ask for this polyp." Then, he respectfully departed, walking quickly like flying, and Wang thought him a sage or saint. After the monk had left for several blocks, another young man appeared, as beautiful as a jade ornament on a crown, riding a white horse. He knocked on the door and asked, "Just now, did a foreign monk come?" Wang hurriedly invited him in and told him about the foreign monk. The young man sighed and was unhappy, saying, "The horse is not fast enough; I finally caught up with this monk." Wang was surprised and asked why, to which the young man replied, "Two celestial physicians were lost, and I recently learned they are hidden in your daughter's nose. I am a celestial being, sent by the Heavenly Emperor to retrieve them. I did not expect this monk to take them first; I shall be punished for this." Wang was just about to bow in respect when he looked up and lost sight of the young man.

During the Longqing era, there was a person who was admiring the moonlight on an August fifteenth night. The moonlight spread over the forest like a piece of cloth. The person followed the light and found a golden-backed toad, which they suspected might be from the moon. The official Zhou Feng, a Lang of the Ministry of Works, once mentioned this story, though he forgot the name of the person who told it. During the Daye era, a relative of Zheng Renben, whose name is not remembered, often traveled with a scholar named Wang through Mount Song. They climbed vines and crossed ravines, and the scenery became extremely secluded. Eventually, they lost their way back. As evening approached, they did not know where to go. They wandered aimlessly and suddenly heard snoring sounds coming from a thicket. They pushed through the bushes and saw a man in white clothes, who was sleeping soundly with a parcel under his head. They called out to him, saying, "We accidentally entered this path and got lost. Do you know the way to the official road?" The man raised his head slightly and did not respond, then fell back asleep. They called out to him again, and he finally sat up and said, "Come here." The two people approached him and asked where he came from. The man smiled (or said) and replied, "Do you know that the moon is made of seven treasures? The moon looks like a ball, and its shadow is formed by the sun shining on its convex side. It is maintained by 82,000 households, and I am one of them." He opened the parcel and revealed some tools, including a hammer and chisel, and two packets of jade flour rice. He gave the rice to the two people, saying, "Share this. Although it won't make you immortal, it will keep you free from diseases for life." He then helped them up and pointed to a path, saying, "Just follow this, and you will find the official road." With that, he disappeared.

**Chapter Two: Jade Canon** In Daoist cosmology, the heavens are divided into three realms, each containing numerous heavens, a number that corresponds to the Buddhist cosmology, though the names differ. Beyond the three realms are four additional realms, known as the "Four Realms," named after the deities: Changrong, Yulong, Fandu, and Fuyi. Beyond these four realms lie the Three Pure Ones, known as Dachi, Yuyu, and Qingwei. Above the Three Pure Ones is the Great Luo, and there are also nine heavens, each with its own name, such as the Nine Heavens of Waves. The heavens are governed by twelve main principles, and they complete a cycle of 360 rotations. A larger cycle of 3600 rotations is called "Yang Bo." The earth completes a cycle of 330 rotations, and a larger cycle of 3300 rotations is called "Yang Shi." The distance between heaven and earth is 409,000 li, and the distance between the four cardinal directions is 10,900 li. There are 360 famous mountains and 72 blessed lands, with Mount Kunlun serving as the boundary between heaven and earth. Additionally, there are nine levels of the earth, 46 lands, and eight palaces of wine-making immortals, which are places associated with the underworld. There is also Mount Lofeng, located in the Northern Kui region, which is 30,000 li in circumference and 2,600 li in height. The "dantian" (elixir field) realm contains six palaces, each 10,000 li in circumference and 2,600 li in height.

The Six Palaces of the Celestial Realms are also the Six Palaces of Ghosts and Spirits. The Six Heavens are: the First is the Palace of Zhou's Judgment in the Yin Realm, the Second is the Palace of Taisha's Affairs, the Third is the Palace of Mingchen's Endurance, the Fourth is the Palace of Hu's Sinful Aura, the Fifth is the Palace of Zongling's Seven Transformations, and the Sixth is the Palace of Gansi's Connected Garden (or the Palace of Inquiry). All who die go to these palaces, and people should constantly remember the names of the Six Heavens. The small heavens of emptiness are governed by the Three Yin. The Palace of Mingchen's Endurance is associated with life, while the Palace of Zhou's Judgment is associated with death. The continuation of fortune and misfortune is governed by the Lord of the Big Dipper, the ruler of the Fourth Heaven, who is also the examiner of the seven stars of the Big Dipper. Xiang Liang of the City wrote the "Ode to the Fengdu Palace," which says: "Zhou's Judgment marks the dawn of the emperor, Taisha's Affairs constructs the grand palace. Flaming like smoke in the heavens, vigorous like the radiant brilliance. Wuyang wields the divine edge, Hu's light engulfs the Qing River. Opening and closing by the Crimson Well, the Cloud Gate towers majestically. The Seven Transformations connect with the strange spirits, the Connected Garden also spreads magic. The Six Heavens stretch across the northern path, this is the home of ghosts and spirits." The ode contains twenty thousand words, but this is only the names of the heavens. Read them silently at night to ward off ghosts and spirits.

Fengdu rice is named Chong Si, its grains are like pomegranate seeds, slightly larger, and taste like water caltrops. Du Qiong wrote the *Chong Si Fu* (赋 on Chong Si) saying: "Faintly at the end of spring, the green Chong Si rice is already flourishing. Clouds and mist intermingle, and the good rice responds to the season." Yu the Great is the Eastern Bright Official, King Wen of Zhou is the Western Bright Official, Duke of Shao is the Southern Bright Official, and Ji Zha is the Northern Bright Official. They govern the spirits of the four directions during the four seasons. Those who are extremely loyal and filial, when they die, all become rulers of the underworld. After one hundred and forty years, they are taught the teachings of lower immortals and given the great Dao. Those with the virtue of a supreme sage, upon death, receive the books of the Three Officials and become rulers of the underworld. After one thousand years, they transform into the Five Emperors of the Three Officials, and after another one thousand four hundred years, they can travel freely in the Great Clarity and become central immortals in the Nine Palaces. There are also righteous ghosts, pure ghosts of the Three Officials, or those who had merit in previous lives and are in the stream of the Three Officials. When their descendants change and undergo transformation, they are reborn in a new life. This is the accumulated virtue of seven generations, where roots and leaves are connected. When they die, they leave behind one bone on the path to return to the Three Officials, and the rest of their bones follow them. Men go left and women go right, all receiving books to become rulers of the underworld, and after two hundred and eighty years, they can advance to the path of terrestrial immortals.

The Northern Great Lord, Yan Emperor Jia, presides over all spirits and deities. The Three Officials' Codes, the Enlightened Canon, and the Nine Hells' Chapters are all laws. The Connected Gardens, Bent Springs, Great Calamity, Nine Hells, Cloud Night, Nine Capitals, Three Spirits, Ten Thousand Plunders, Four Extremes, and Nine Departments are all places of governance. The Thirty-Six Hells, including the Red Sands and other named Hells of the Deep Waters, are associated with the Northern Peak. There are also Twenty-Four Hells, named Nine Pings, Yuan Zheng, Nu Qing, and Hebei. Those who commit five thousand sins become spirits in the Five Hells, those who commit six thousand sins are prisoners in the Twenty-Eight Hells, and those who commit ten thousand sins fall into the Hell of Xilai. There are black, green, and white books of sins, and red and vermilion bamboo slips for recording them. Punishments include the Tang Mountain Stone, the Substitute Tai Mountain Tang Night Mountain Stone, the Cold River Source, the West River Water, the East Sea Wind Knife, and the Lightning (or Thunder) Wind, as well as the Accumulated Night River. There are seventy-five ranks of officials in the underworld. The celestial hierarchy includes nine great emperors, twenty-seven heavenly lords, one thousand two hundred celestial officials, twenty-four thousand spiritual officers, thirty-two life officials, three ranks, nine ranks, and seven cities (or domains, or lands). There are nine ranks, twenty-seven positions, and seventy-two million levels in the celestial hierarchy.

Laozi traveled westward across the desert, passing through eighty-one countries. The regions of Uddiyana and Gandhara, known as the "Buddha Lands," had a profound influence over three thousand kingdoms, with extensive Buddhist scriptures such as the "Nine Ten Thousand Precepts Sutra." Among these, the "Recitation Sutra" obtained by the Han dynasty from the Great Yuechi is a notable example. Confucius is revered as a celestial being of the Yuan Gong, while the Buddha is honored as a deity of the Thirty-Three Heavens. The teachings of the Guest Official Master in India are rooted in the land of the Buddha. Ancient sages excelled in achieving a state of non-action (wu wei). The "Biographies of the Laozi and Buddha" also states that the Buddha attained enlightenment in the Western Regions. Tao Sheng noted that in certain small countries, many people venerated the Buddha, seeking immortality through the consumption of "five bamboo spirits" and the study of the "Summer Hidden," which they believed would enable them to fly. The scriptures contain the precepts for bodhisattvas. Fangzhu Mountain is located in a specific place (乙地). Among the true immortals in the Taiji realm, Zhuangzi holds the esteemed position of Wei Bian Lang. The eighty-one precepts and twelve hundred virtues lead to entering the "Hollow Heaven" (洞天), while the two hundred thirty precepts and two thousand virtues lead to ascending to the "Mountain Spirit Official" (山上灵官). The attainment of ten thousand virtues leads to ascending to the "Jade Clarity" (玉清) realm. Those with auspicious marks such as a white record in the abdomen, green veins in the eyes, a hidden bone in the abdomen, a reclining bone in the chest, or a four-cornered eye are recorded in celestial registers and are destined to become immortals. Even without formal learning, their path to immortality is already set. Others may have auspicious signs such as a "black mountain" on the nose or a "black hill" in the abdomen, indicating potential for immortality. However, impure breath or a tendency to tolerate filth can ruin these auspicious signs.

The five organs, nine palaces, twelve chambers, four limbs, five bodies, three burners, nine orifices, one hundred and eighty mechanisms, three hundred and sixty joints, and thirty-six thousand gods reside within them. The soul is rooted in essence, and the spirit enters through the eyes. The three souls can be restrained, and the seven spirits can be controlled. On the day of Gengshen, the spirits report human faults. On one's birthday, the celestial officials record one's actions. The three spirits report to the heavens three times a day: the upper spirit, Qinggu, harms the eyes; the middle spirit, Baigu, harms the five organs; the lower spirit, Xuegu, harms the stomach. The spirit is also called Xuanling. It is said to reside in three places: one in the head, causing excessive thoughts and desires, a love for carriages and horses, with a black color; one in the abdomen, causing a love for food and drink, anger, with a green color; one in the feet, causing a love for beauty and a tendency to harm others, with a red color. By guarding the day of Gengshen seven times, the three spirits are extinguished; by guarding it three times, the three spirits are subdued. Immortal elixirs: Zhongshan white glue, Langfeng stone brain, Heihe Paigu, Taiwei purple hemp, Taiji well spring, night dew day grass, green dew blue reed, Round Altar purple apple, white water spirit conch, eight heavens red garlic, Gaoqiu surplus grain, Canglang green money, thirty-six mushrooms, dragon fetus liquor, nine tripods fish, fire jujube and pear, Phoenix Forest singing wine, Central Purple Honey, collapsing mountain electric willow, Xuan Guo exquisite onion, night cow伏 bone, Shenwu yellow algae, Yan Mountain night sun, Xuan frost rouge snow, ring hard tree seeds, red tree white seeds, return water jade essence, white Lantian frost, purple sauce, (also called juice) moon liquor, rainbow red, great red.

Different names for medicinal herbs: - Dan Mountain Soul — Realgar - Qingyao Lady — Verdigris - Linghua Ji Yu — Frankincense - North Emperor's Black Pearl — Saltpeter - East Palace Child — Asarum - Five Essence Gold — Actinolite - Flowing Red White Ointment — White Lead - Tingling Lone Birth — Clove - Reversed Running Divine Bone — Salt - White Tiger Toothless — Gold Tooth Stone - Ling Yellow — Sulfur - Lu Hu Legacy — Dragon Bone - Zhangyang Feather Black — White Attach - Green Submerged Stone, Mother's Kind Stone - Red Dawn Fetal — Poria - Submerged Dragon Plum — Su Ya Tree - Seven White Spirits, Vegetables — Allium Tuber - Also known as Guardian of the House, Family Treasure A total of twenty-four names. There are diagrams and texts containing seven thousand chapters: - Female One Jade Pick - Four Rules Mirror - Five Pillars Central Scripture - Flying Turtle Scroll - Flying Yellow Child Scripture - Deer Lu Qiao Scripture - Containing Scene Diagram - Lying Down Guide Diagram - Garden Mushroom Diagram - Wood Mushroom Diagram - Da Wei New Mushroom Diagram - Cowherd Scripture - Jade Table Record - Jade Treasure Record - Waxing Record - Dan Tai Scripture (also called Record) - Sun and Moon Kitchen Food Scripture - Golden Tower Scripture - Thirty-Six Waters Scripture - Middle Yellow Man Scripture - Collaborating Dragon Child Deer Platform Scripture - Jade Fetal Scripture - Official Record Scripture - Phoenix Framework Scripture - Six Yin Jade Woman Scripture - White Tiger Seven Transformations Scripture - Nine Immortals Scripture - Ten Ascension Transformations Scripture - Teng Middle First Shetu Scripture - Three Bonds Six Orders Scripture - White Child Transformation Scripture - Hidden Head Scripture - Entering Army Scripture - Spring Pivot Scripture - Red Armor Scripture - Diamond Eight Stacks Record (also called Scripture)

Laozi's mother was known as the Mysterious Jade Maiden. A black and yellow qi descended from heaven, entering her mouth like a ball of clay, causing her to become pregnant. She meditated in a jade palace for 3,700 years. At the beginning of the "Red Bright" era, in the year of the Jiazi cycle, she gave birth in Fubao, which was part of the Western Kingdom of Tian, on the slopes of the Yuliao Mountain in the Danxuan region. Another account says that Laozi was conceived for 81 years, being born from his mother's left armpit with white hair. Another version states that at the end of the Green Emperor's era, when the primordial qi changed, he took form in the womb of the Hong family. Yet another account says that his mother, originally a celestial being, swallowed the essence of the sun and became pregnant. Three-colored qi surrounded him, and five elements of animals protected his form. After 72 years, he was born in the village of Lai in the county of Ku in the state of Chen, near the Yang side of the Wuo River, west of the nine wells. He had 36 titles and 72 names. Additionally, he had nine more names and 1,200 more names. Laozi was also known by titles such as the Ninth Supreme Emperor of the True First Lord, the Great Thousand Dharma King, the Nine Spirits of Laozi, the Supreme True Man, the Celestial Master of the Mysterious Central Teaching, the Upper Clear Supreme True Man, and the Upper Bright Lord. His height was either nine feet or twenty-nine feet. His ears had three gates, were attached in a ring, and lacked contours. His eyebrows were shaped like the Big Dipper, green in color, with purple hair in the middle, five inches long. His eyes had square pupils, green veins, and purple light. His nose had two columns, a square mouth, and 48 teeth. His chin was flat, his cheeks broad, and his face golden and dragon-like. His forehead had three lines, his abdomen three patterns, his head three folds, and his body had ten steps and five forms. His hair was green, his blood white, and he had purple qi on his head.

When a person dies, their form resembles that of the living, their feet do not turn blue or black, their eyes do not wither, and all their hair falls out. This is all a form of *shi jie* (尸解). If it occurs during the day, it is called *shang jie* (上解); if it occurs at midnight, it is called *xia jie* (下解); if it occurs just before dawn or dusk, it is referred to as the domain of the underworld. *Tai Yi* (太一) guards the corpse, the three souls guard the bones, and the seven spirits guard the flesh. The infant's spirit records its vital energy, which is what is called the refining of the form by the Great Yin. After Zhao Chengzi died, five to six years later, his flesh decayed but his bones remained intact, with blood inside and a purple color on the surface. If a person temporarily dies, their soul passes through the underworld governed by the Three Officials, the blood settles, and the pulse disperses. The internal organs seem to regenerate, the bones become as white and smooth as jade, the three lights rest, the great spirit is internally sealed, and this process may last from three to thirty years.

In the Daoist tradition, it is said that what appears to be a daytime transformation (白日尸解) is indeed a form of immortality, but it is not a true transformation. Deer Skin Swallowing the Jade Flower (鹿皮公) consumed the jade flower and his body emitted insects, while Wang Xicheng (王西城) drank the dragon fetus and died with a final decree. Chou Jizi (仇季子) swallowed the golden liquid and his odor spread for a hundred miles, and Ji Zhu (季主) consumed the frost powder and ascended secretly, but his head and feet were in different places. Heidi (黑狄) swallowed the rainbow pills and threw himself into the water, Ning Sheng (宁生) consumed the stone brain and rushed into the fire, and Bai Cheng (柏成) absorbed qi and his stomach and intestines rotted three times. On Mount Guqu, there are five types of mushrooms (五芝). Those seeking them should throw two golden rings into the crevices between the stones and not look back, and they will surely obtain them. The first mushroom is called Long Xian (龙仙), and eating it makes one a Taiji immortal. The second is called Can Cheng (参成), and eating it makes one a Taiji Taifu. The third is called Yan Tai (燕胎), and eating it makes one a Zheng Yi Langzhong. The fourth is called Ye Guang Dong Bi (夜光洞鼻), and eating it makes one a Taiqing Zuo Yushi. The fifth is called Liao Yu (料玉), and eating it makes one a San Guan Zhen Yushi. True people (真人) use a magical sword for their transformation, which is the highest form of metamorphosis (蝉化). The sword is forged on the days of July's Geng Shen and August's Xin You, measuring three feet nine inches in length, one inch four fen in width, three fen five li in thickness, and nine inches in the tip. It is named Zi Gan (子干) and its courtesy name is Liang Fei (良非). Qingwu (青乌公) entered Mount Hua and lived for 471 years, failing three out of twelve tests. Later, he consumed the golden liquid and ascended to Taiji, but because he failed three tests, he was only a true person and did not attain the rank of a true immortal. There is a story of a master entering Ranshan for seven years, where the Lord Lao gave him a wooden drill to bore through a thick stone, five feet thick, saying, "This stone hole, when pierced, will lead to enlightenment." After forty-seven years, the stone was finally pierced, and he obtained the divine elixir (神丹).

Fan Lingzi followed Sima Jizhu into the stone chamber of Changshan. In the northeast corner of the stone chamber, there was a stone chest, and Sima Jizhu warned not to open it. Fan Lingzi thought of returning home, opened it, and saw his family, including parents and children, close but not far away. He felt sad and deep, and Sima Jizhu then expelled him. After several years, he was again ordered to guard a bronze chest, violated the warning, saw the same as before, and ultimately failed to achieve the Dao. In the southwest of Weiguo County, there is a gourd cave, which always has water flowing out year-round, looks like a white silk ribbon, and occasionally has gourd leaves appearing. A legend says that during the Former Qin dynasty, there was a person named Li Ban who had a strong interest in Daoist practices. He entered the cave and walked about 300 steps, suddenly seeing grand buildings with scriptures on the beds. He saw two people sitting opposite each other, their beards and hair were white. Li Ban knelt and bowed at the foot of the bed, and they said, "You can return, do not stay too long." He bowed and left, reached the cave entrance, saw several gourds, tried to pick them, but they turned into stone. He followed the old path and managed to return. When he returned home, his family said, "Li Ban has been gone for forty years already."

Mount Changbai, traditionally known as Mount Sujian, has a bell ringing in its southern part. During the Yan Dynasty, a monk named Shihui Xie from Guanggu traveled to the southern part of the mountain to listen to the bell. After a short distance, he suddenly saw a temple, its doors and halls brightly illuminated. He then sought food there. He saw a novice monk who picked a peach and gave it to him. Shortly after, another peach was given to him, and the monk said to him, "You have stayed here long enough; it is time to leave." As the monk left, he looked back and the temple had vanished. When he returned to Guanggu, he found his disciples and they said that he had been missing for two years. The monk realized that the two peaches were a sign of the two years. In Gaotang County, there is a mountain called Ming Shi Shan, with rocks over a hundred ren high. When struck with an object, the rocks produce a very clear and resonant sound. During the Tai Kang period of the Jin Dynasty, a hermit named Tian Xuan lived in a cave on the mountain. He often played with the stones, enjoying the wind, frost, and moonlight. He frequently saw a person wearing a white single robe, wandering on the mountain, and they would leave at dawn. Later, the hermit ordered someone to strike the stone and hid on the mountain to watch. Soon, the person came, and the hermit quickly grabbed their sleeve and questioned them. The person introduced themselves as Wang Zhonglun, from the state of Wei. They studied Daoism in the Shao Shi Mountains during the reign of King Xuan of Zhou and frequently visited the Eastern Sea, passing through this place. They loved the sound of the stone and often stayed to listen. The hermit asked for advice on living a long life and was given a stone as small as a sparrow's egg. Initially, the stone could be seen floating in the air for over a hundred steps, but gradually disappeared into the mist. The hermit kept the stone and, by holding it, could go without food for a hundred days. In the Li River area of Jingzhou, there are two stones resembling gateways, known as Shao Shi. During the Yonghe period of the Jin Dynasty, flying immortals with clothing as white as snow rested on the stones for ten days before leaving. People often saw them.

West of Bei Qiu lies the Jade Maiden Mountain. It is said that during the Dashi period of the Jin Dynasty, Peng Qiu from the North Sea, courtesy name Bojian, entered the mountain to cut wood. Suddenly, he sensed a strange fragrance and followed the wind to seek it out. Upon reaching the mountain, he found a grand and lush palace with spacious towers and pavilions. Qiu entered and peeked inside, where he saw five jade trees. Stepping forward slightly, he noticed four women, exquisitely beautiful beyond compare, playing chess on the hall. Seeing Qiu, they all jumped up in surprise and asked, "Peng Jun, why have you come here?" Qiu replied, "In search of the fragrance." They then returned to play. One of the younger ones went upstairs to play the zither, and the ones who stayed playing called out to her, "Wuhui, why are you alone going upstairs?" Qiu stood under the tree and felt a bit hungry, so he licked the droplets on the leaves with his tongue. Suddenly, a woman riding a crane arrived and angrily said, "Yuhua, why do you have such a commoner here!" The Queen Mother immediately ordered Wang Fangping to inspect the various divine chambers. Fearing this, Qiu went out the door and, looking back, suddenly saw nothing. Upon returning home, it was during the Jianping period, and his old neighborhood and houses had all become ruins and graves. Xu Jingsheng of the Jin Dynasty was a disciple of Wu Meng. At that time, there were many snake disasters in the eastern part of the Yangtze River, and Wu Meng planned to eliminate them. He selected over a hundred disciples and arrived at Gao'an, where he ordered to prepare a hundred catties of charcoal. He then measured and cut it, placing it on the altar. One night, all of them transformed into jade maidens, confusing their disciples. By dawn, Wu Meng ordered all his disciples, and all had their clothes stained, except for Xu Jingsheng, who was not. Then, he went with Xu to Liaojiang. When they encountered a giant snake, Wu was old and weak, unable to control it. Xu then performed the Yu step, commanded the sword, and struck its head, beheading it.

Sun Simiao once lived in seclusion in Mount Zhongnan, where he met and interacted with Master Xuan, a monk known for his strict adherence to Buddhist precepts. They often visited each other and exchanged their spiritual principles. During a severe drought, a foreign monk asked for a rain prayer ceremony to be held at Lake Kunming, and the emperor ordered officials to prepare incense and lamps. The ceremony lasted for seven days, and the water level of the lake receded by several feet. Suddenly, an old man visited Master Xuan at night seeking help, saying, "I am the dragon of Lake Kunming. There has been no rain for a long time, and it is not my fault. The foreign monk wants to use my brain to make medicine and deceived the emperor by saying they were praying for rain. My life is in danger, so I beg you to use your spiritual power to protect me." Master Xuan replied, "I only uphold the precepts, you can seek help from Sun Simiao." The old man then went to Sun Simiao's stone chamber to seek help. Sun Simiao said, "I know that the dragon palace of Lake Kunming has three thousand magical formulas. You can give them to me, and I will save you." The old man said, "These formulas are not allowed to be spread without permission, but now that I am in such a hurry, I have no regrets." After a while, he brought the formula to Sun Simiao. Sun Simiao said, "Just return, do not worry about the foreign monk." From then on, the water level of the lake suddenly rose, overflowing the banks after several days, and the foreign monk died of shame and anger. Sun Simiao then wrote another version of the *Qian Jin Fang*, which consisted of three thousand volumes, with each volume containing one formula. People could not understand them. After his death, sometimes people saw him.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, who fled to Shu, he dreamed of Sun Simiao requesting realgar from Wuwei. The emperor then ordered a messenger to carry ten pounds of the substance, to be delivered to the summit of Mount Emei. As the messenger ascended the mountain halfway, he saw a man wearing a headband and a coarse robe, with white whiskers and beard, accompanied by two attendants dressed in green clothes with round topknots, standing on either side of a screen. The man pointed to a large stone mortar and said, "You can place the medicine here. There are blue-green inscriptions of the emperor on it." The messenger saw red inscriptions of over a hundred characters on the stone, and he copied them down. As he wrote, the characters disappeared, and after he finished, there were no more characters left on the stone. In a moment, white mist rose, and then suddenly disappeared.

Pei Hang, the military officer of Tongzhou, often recounted how his second cousin, who was traveling from Luoyang to Zhengzhou, encountered an unusual event after several days on the road. One evening, as they were making camp, Pei Hang dismounted and heard a faint moaning sound off the side of the road. Curious, he pushed aside the tall grass and weeds to investigate. Beneath a thorny bush, he found a sick crane with drooping wings, its feathers matted and missing from the wound on its wing joint. The crane’s cry was different from what he had heard before. Suddenly, an old man appeared, dressed in white and leaning on a staff. He approached them after walking several dozen steps and said, "Young master, do you understand the plight of this crane? If it is given a drop of human blood, it will be able to fly again." Pei Hang, who was knowledgeable and had a noble and free-spirited nature, immediately responded, "I shall offer my blood without hesitation." The old man replied, "Your resolve is commendable, but the blood must come from someone who has been human for three generations. You were not human in your previous life, only the man named Hulusheng in Luoyang has been human for three generations. Is your journey urgent? Perhaps you wish to visit Hulusheng in Luoyang?" Pei Hang returned to Luoyang with a smile and, after a short rest, visited Hulusheng and recounted the entire incident, bowing and pleading for his help. Hulusheng did not seem troubled and opened a small pouch, taking out a stone vessel about the size of two fingers. He used a needle to prick his arm and let a few drops of blood fill the vessel, handing it to Pei Hang and saying, "There is no need for further words." When they reached the place where the crane was, the old man was already there and greeted them with a smile, saying, "Indeed, you are a trustworthy person." He then instructed Pei Hang to apply all the blood to the crane.

A bond was formed with him, and he invited Pei, saying, "My dwelling is not far from here; you may stay for a while." Pei realized that he was no ordinary person and addressed him respectfully as "elder." He then followed him. After traveling only a few miles, they arrived at a village with a simple bamboo and grass hut, the courtyard and hall in disarray. Pei, extremely thirsty, asked for tea, and the elder pointed to a clay niche, saying, "There is a little liquid inside; you may take it." Pei looked inside the niche and saw an almond pit, a fan-shaped object like a hat, and a milky white liquid inside. He lifted it and drank it, no longer feeling hungry or thirsty. The taste of the liquid was like almond paste. Pei knew that the elder was a recluse and bowed to request to become his servant. The elder said, "You have a small position in the world; even if you stay here, you will not fulfill your aspirations. Your respected uncle truly has something to gain; I have been with him for a long time, but you do not know. I have a letter for you to deliver." He then wrapped a bundle, as large as a soup bowl, and warned Pei not to open it secretly. He then took Pei to see a crane, and the feathers on the damaged part had already grown back. He also told Pei, "When you drank the almond liquid, you should weep for the entire clan's kinship, and use alcohol and beauty as a warning." Pei returned to Luoyang, feeling anxious about the attached letter on the way. He was about to open it when red snakes emerged from the corners of the bundle, and Pei stopped. His uncle received the letter and opened it, finding something like dry barley flour, a little more than a sheng. His uncle later went to Mount Wangwu, and his ultimate fate is unknown. Pei lived to be ninety-seven years old.

A scholar named Zhao Ye, who passed the imperial examinations during the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Zhenyuan era, was appointed as the magistrate of Qinghua County in Ba Prefecture. He fell ill due to a loss of resolve, fearing daylight and refusing food and drink for over forty days. Suddenly, he heard thunder in his room, and soon after, a red cloud resembling a drum appeared, rotating and rising to the bed, settling in the center of his chest. At first, he felt his spirit wandering like in a dream, and a person dressed in red clothes with a flat cap led him eastward. They emerged at a place where a mountain was cut through, with a river flowing east and west, and many people around. He stood there for a long time observing the scene. They continued eastward, passing a bridge adorned with gold and green. After crossing the bridge and entering a city to the north, they arrived at a government office where many officials and clerks were present. Zhao Ye saw his brother-in-law Jia Yi, who was arguing with him about the slaughtering of cattle. He suspected this was the underworld and hastily fled to hide behind a wall made of black stone, several zhang high, where he heard shouts and commands. The person in red clothes then led him into a large courtyard, where an official announced, "The Lord of Life and Death is passing by."

Upon meeting Jia Yi again, they engaged in a debate. Jia Yi was stubborn and unable to justify his position. Suddenly, a large mirror with a diameter of a zhang appeared, hanging in the air. Looking up at it, one clearly saw Jia Yi waving a knife, while Sizhufu showed an expression of pity or compassion. Jia Yi then admitted his guilt. The red-clothed person led him to the office of the officials, where a person wearing coarse clothes and a purple mist cap, resembling a revered image, scolded and said, "Why did you steal and pull the headwear? The two matters took place in Huazhou City, involving hiding three sheng of acorns." The subject knelt and bowed innumerable times. The red-clothed person led him out again and asked, "Can you travel to the upper clear realm?" They then climbed a mountain together, looking down at flowing water. The water flowed down, foaming, and thousands of people entered with the flow. Unaware, his body also followed the flow. After a long time, they stopped on a large stone, seeing a greenish-white misty path. The red-clothed person transformed into two people, one leading the way and one urging forward. They then climbed up the stone cliff and stood, calmly and free of dust.

Walking several li, he saw a plant by the side of the path resembling *honglan*, with dense stems and leaves, no thorns, and its flowers swirling and scattering into the air like mist. There was another plant like *jù*, growing close to the ground, also producing flying flowers. At first, it appeared like a *màbó* mushroom, then expanded into layers, with a crimson-yellow hue. Beyond this, he saw flames like mountains stretching across the sky; he waited until the fire had died out before proceeding. Upon reaching a great city, its walls were crowned with towering towers, and fruit trees lined the streets. Fairy maidens walked among them, singing and drumming in harmony, their divine beauty surpassing the world. Passing through three gates, each adorned with vivid red and gold, the ground and walls gleamed with a clarity that could be used for reflection. Above, the sky was not visible, as if a crimson haze covered everything. The main hall was three-tiered, and within it, revered images were arranged in rows. He saw a single Daoist practitioner, who seemed like an old acquaintance; Zhao Qiu requested to become his disciple, but was refused. Among the instruments, one resembled a zither, four chi in length, with nine strings. Near the head, it was about a chi wide and broad, with two horizontal bars in the middle to alter the pitch. Another instrument resembled a *jiu lü*, with three strings, three chi long, its front wide at the top and narrow at the bottom, and its back full and rounded.

A Review of the Soul Recently, Zhao underwent a review, and he was led to a courtyard in the south of the palace. There, a figure in a red cap and a purple cloud-like cloak was seated with two officials in red robes in the hall. Zhao was instructed to first review the records of the year Wu Shen. The records were similar to earthly documents, with the person's birth date at the top, followed by their name, age, and the day of their birth. Below, the sixty-year cycle of the zodiac was laid out horizontally, with all deeds and mistakes recorded daily. If there were none, it was simply noted as "no record." Zhao glanced at his own records and found that his name, birth date, and other details were all correct. The number of people who had undergone this review was in the billions. The officials explained that every sixty years, all people in the world were reviewed to assess their good and bad deeds, and their lifespan or fortune was adjusted accordingly. The officials then led Zhao out through the northern gate to a familiar road. They took his hand and said, "This is your soul wandering here. Follow this path, do not look back, and you will reach your home." Zhao followed their instructions, walking quickly, and then stumbled and fell. It was as if he had woken from a dream, and he realized he had been dead for seven days. Zhao wrote a detailed account of his soul's journey to the upper heavens, titled *Soul's Journey to the Upper Heavens*.

Historian Lun was in Qizhou when he went out hunting. He reached the border of a county and rested in a Buddhist temple. He noticed an unusually strong peach fragrance and inquired about it from the monk. The monk, unable to conceal it, said that someone had recently given two peaches, which he had taken from under the lectern to present to Lun. The peaches were as large as a rice bowl. At the time, Lun was hungry and ate them all. The pits were as large as chicken eggs. Lun then questioned the monk about their origin. The monk smiled and said, "I was actually mistaken. These peaches are about ten miles from here, on a dangerous path. I happened to see them while traveling and found them unusual, so I picked a few." Lun said, "Let me leave my entourage and go with the monk." The monk, reluctantly, led Lun north into a desolate area. After traveling about five miles, they reached a body of water. The monk said, "I fear the official cannot cross this." Determined, Lun followed the monk's lead by taking off his clothes and wearing them to float across. After reaching the shore, they traveled northwest, crossing two small bodies of water. They climbed a mountain and crossed several streams, arriving at a place with scattered springs and strange rocks, not a human habitat. There were hundreds of peach trees with branches touching the ground, about two to three feet tall, and an overpowering fragrance. Lun and the monk each ate a peach stem, and their stomachs felt full. Lun took off his clothes and tried to wrap the peaches. The monk said, "This might be a sacred place, and too many peaches should not be taken. I once heard an elder monk say that someone who came here once took five or six peaches and got lost." Lun also suspected the monk of being unusual and took two peaches to return. The monk strictly forbade Lun from speaking about the incident. When Lun returned to the state, he sent someone to find the monk, who had already died.

● Volume Two: The History of the Pot Wu Youxu, the nephew of Empress Wu Zetian, was fourteen years old when he secretly sold divination in the market of Chang'an, staying in one place for no more than five or six days. He then moved to ascend Mount Song, where he secluded himself and consumed ginseng and poria. The deer skin coats and rattan items left by high-ranking officials and nobles were covered with dust and moss, and he discarded them without use. In his later years, his muscles began to disappear, his eyes emitted purple light, and he could see stars and the moon during the day. He could also distinguish words spoken from several miles away. When Princess Anle was betrothed, the emperor sent an imperial edict summoning him, ordering him to accept the imperial command, temporarily lowering his high standards. Upon arriving in the capital, he received visits from nobles, but outside of polite greetings, he did not speak a word. He was bestowed the title of Marquis. When he returned to the mountains, the emperor ordered the scholars to compose poetry to see him off. Emperor Xuanzong learned the art of invisibility from Lu Gongyuan, but his belt or the end of his headscarf could not be hidden. The emperor questioned him, and Lu Gongyuan strongly stated, "Your Majesty has not yet been able to renounce the world, and yet you treat the Dao as a game. If I fully use my skills, I will carry the imperial seal into a private home, and I will be trapped in fisherman's disguise." Emperor Xuanzong became angry and scolded him harshly. Lu Gongyuan then walked into the pillar of the hall, severely criticizing the emperor's mistakes. The emperor became even angrier and ordered the pillar to be replaced and broken. Lu Gongyuan then loudly spoke from within the stone basin, and the basin was replaced and examined. The basin was clear and bright, revealing Lu Gongyuan's form inside, about an inch long, and he was then shattered into dozens of pieces, each containing his form. The emperor was frightened and apologized, but Lu Gongyuan suddenly disappeared. Later, an imperial messenger saw him on the road to Sichuan, and Lu Gongyuan smiled and said, "Tell the emperor for me."

Xing Heping was particularly skilled in the Huang-Lao philosophy, adept at mental arithmetic, and wrote letters from Yǐngyang. Sometimes, there were strange knocks, and then they would suddenly disappear into emptiness. Some people said they saw grass, but it had never been seen before. Cheng Shi heard from the recluse Zheng Zhi that Cui Sima, who lived temporarily in Jingzhou, had a long-standing relationship with Xing. Cui had suffered from illness for many years and was close to death, often relying on Xing. One day, Cui felt someone snoring in the wall north of his bedroom and ordered those around to look, but they saw nothing. North of the bedroom was where family members lived. For seven days, the snoring continued, and the wall suddenly became transparent, as small as a grain of millet. When Cui asked those around, they still did not see anything. After one day, the hole grew as large as a plate. Cui peered into it and saw that outside the wall was just the wild, with several people with shovels standing in front of the hole (or to the side). Cui asked them, and they all said, "Xing the True Person arranged to open this. Cui's misfortune is severe, and it requires double the effort." After a while, attendants appeared, all wearing flat caps and red clothes, announcing, "The True Person has arrived." Cui saw Xing with a white silk ribbon and a five-star fan, holding a five-star fan, with dozens of attendants, stopping a few steps away from the hole. Xing told Cui, "Your fate is determined. I have argued on your behalf, and you can extend your life by one jī (12 years). From now on, there will be no more." After speaking, the wall returned to normal. After ten days, Cui's illness healed. Xing also lived in Zhongnan, where many who loved the Dao often built their homes near him. Cui Shu, a young man, also followed him. They cut wood and drew water, and all were famous scholars. Xing once told his disciples, "Every three or five days, there is a strange guest. You should prepare one thing for me."

After several days of preparing all sorts of water and land provisions, he laid out a feast in a pavilion, warning against random peering. Everyone closed their doors and dared not even whisper. Xing invited a guest down from the mountain, who was five feet tall and three feet wide, with his head taking up half of his body. He wore a broad, crimson robe and held an ivory tablet horizontally. His eyelashes were sparse and fluttered, his complexion as pale as a carved gourd. He laughed heartily, shaking his beard, and his lips reached his ears. He engaged in intense conversation with Xing, mostly not about mundane human affairs. Cui Shu became impatient and walked past the courtyard. The guest looked at him closely, turned to Xing, and said, "Is this not Master Teacher of Mount Tai?" Xing replied, "Yes." The guest then said, "If he were to transform again, he would be far away, a thousand miles away, a pity." By evening, the guest left. Xing called Cui Shu over and said, "That guest was a divine being playing with me."

Speaking of the Master of Mount Tai, do you remember much or nothing?" Cui wept and said, "Indeed, I am the reincarnation of the Master of Mount Tai, but I no longer remember. As a child, I often heard my ancestors say." Fang, the Grand Marshal, consulted Master Xing about his lifetime matters. Master Xing said, "If you come from the southeast and stop in the northwest, your fate will end. The place where your soul descends is neither a hall nor a temple, neither a road nor an office. Your illness arises from fish, and your rest is on a board from Kucha." Later, Fang was transferred from Yuanzhou to Hanshou, and upon his dismissal and return, he reached Langzhou and stayed at the Purple Extreme Palace. He happened to hire a worker to treat wood and was surprised by the shapes formed by the grain of the wood. He asked about it, and the Taoist said, "A few months ago, a merchant cut several pieces of Kucha board, and now they are being treated as屠苏." Fang then remembered Master Xing's words. Shortly after, the governor invited him, and Fang sighed, "Master Xing is a divine person." He reported fully to the governor and entrusted the Kucha board to him. That night, he fell ill and died.

Mr. Wang Jiao (also known as Tian) was skilled in other arts, yet never spoke of numerology. During the Tianbao period, he once sat out in the open at night with a guest, pointing to the stars and moon and saying, "The time is about to fall into chaos." His words were overheard and passed on by a neighbor. At that time, the emperor was of advanced years and quite superstitious. His remarks were reported to the emperor, who then issued a secret decree ordering his execution. The executioner drilled his head dozens of times before he died. They then broke open his skull to examine it, and found the bone of his brain to be one inch and eight-tenths of an inch thick. Wang Jiao had corresponded with Attendant-in-Waiting Daxi, and when the An and Shi rebellions were quelled, Jiao suddenly arrived at Daxi’s house with a staff and shoes, and only then did they realize he was a truly extraordinary person. Master Zhai, whose name was Ganhou, was a man from the gorges. He was six chi tall, with hands larger than a chi in size. Whenever he bowed to someone, his hands passed over his chest. He always slept with an empty pillow. In his later years, he often spoke of future events. He once entered Kuizhou City and loudly declared, "Tonight, eight people will pass by here; treat them well." People did not understand what he meant. That night, fire burned hundreds of homes, and the eight people were the characters in the word "fire" (火). Whenever he entered the mountains, a group of tigers followed him. Once, by the riverside, he played with the moon alongside dozens of disciples. One of them asked, "What is in here?" Zhai smiled and said, "You may follow my finger and look." Two of the disciples then saw the moon’s arc fill half the sky, with palaces and golden halls full of light. After a few breaths, they no longer saw it.

A Taoist priest from the region of Shu feigned madness, and was informally nicknamed "Dust Bag." He was a disciple of the Taoist master Zhai in his later years. Zhai often warned his disciples: "Do not deceive this person. I am not as good as him." The priest would often travel through heavy snow, wearing coarse cloth, and enter Qingcheng Mountain. At dusk, he would lodge at a Buddhist temple and ask a monk for lodging. The monk said, "I am a poor monk with only one robe, and it is so cold, I fear I cannot help you survive." The priest replied, "It is enough to have a bed." By midnight, the snow had deepened and the wind had picked up. The monk was concerned that the Taoist priest might have died, so he went to check on him. The priest was several feet away from the bed, radiating heat like a cooking pot, sweating profusely, and sleeping naked. The monk knew him to be an extraordinary person. Before dawn, the priest left without saying goodbye. He often stayed in villages, never staying more than two nights. Once, he suffered from mouth ulcers and did not eat for several months, appearing as if he were about to die. People always regarded him as a divine being, so they set up a Taoist ritual for him. After the ritual was over, the priest suddenly awoke and told the gathered people, "Try to look at what is in my mouth." He then opened his mouth like a winnowing basket, and all five organs were exposed. His fellow Taoists were amazed and paid respects, asking him about it. He only said, "This is truly terrible, this is truly terrible." Later, it is unknown what happened to him. This account was told by the Taoist master Guo Caitian of Shu to the author, Cheng Shi.

A Scholar with the Title of "Quan Tongxiu" fell ill and was in dire straits during his travels in Suzhou and Huzhou. He had hired a rural messenger who had served him for a year. While ill, he longed for sweet bean soup and asked the hired person to fetch licorice root. The hired person remained for a long time without leaving, only preparing fire, water, and tea. The scholar thought the hired person was lacking in attentiveness. He then saw a handful of broken branches, which he repeatedly rubbed near the fire, and they suddenly turned into licorice root. The scholar was greatly surprised by this and thought the hired person must have some method. After a long time, he took some coarse sand and processed it, and it turned into beans. When the soup was made, it was no different from sweet beans, and his illness gradually improved. The scholar said, "I am so poor and desperate, I cannot take a single step." He then took off his dirty clothes and gave them to the hired person, saying, "You can use this to buy a little wine and meat. I will go to the village elders to beg for a little money for the journey." The hired person smiled and said, "This is indeed not enough to accomplish, but I will arrange for it."

The scholar then chopped down a dry mulberry tree, split it into strips, and gathered them on a tray. When he called out to them, the strips turned into beef. He then fetched several bottles of water, which quickly transformed into fine wine. The elderly villagers became drunk and full, and received a large sum of money (three thousand bundles of silk). The scholar, in turn, thanked the employer, saying, "I was once proud and refined, but I did not recognize you, the sage, and now I return to ask to serve you." The employer replied, "I am indeed a special person, having been demoted to a lowly position due to some minor misdeeds. I am now serving the scholar. If the time limit is not enough, I will need to seek help from others. Please, scholar, do not change your usual behavior, so that we may complete this task." The scholar agreed, but every time the employer called out, the scholar looked uncomfortable and anxious. The employer then left, saying, "If you continue like this, it will indeed hinder our task." He then explained to the scholar the concept of fate, both good and bad, and said that everything can be transformed, except for certain things, such as red lacquered ropes and hair, which cannot be changed by the magic. He then departed, and his destination was unknown.

During the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, there was a man from Jingzhou named Lu Shanshan who often traded small boats, wood, and lime, traveling between the southern grass market of Bai Di. He occasionally showed slight signs of supernatural ability, which people could not understand.
