A merchant named Zhao Yuanqing, who was interested in good things, intended to associate with him and frequently bought what he sold, setting up fruits and tea, pretending to inquire about his methods of making profit. Lu Shanshan became aware of this and said to him, "Observing your intentions, it seems you are not interested in what you are buying. What is your real intention?" Zhao then replied, "I secretly know that the elder hides his form and virtue, surpassing the divination of tortoise shells and yarrow stalks. I hope you will give me one word of advice." Lu smiled and said, "Let's see now. Your host will have an unusual disaster at noon. If my words are true, then you will be spared. You can tell him. Before noon, a baker carrying a bag will arrive. The bag contains more than two thousand coins, and it is definitely not intentional. Close the door, warn your wife and children not to lightly respond. At noon, they will definitely curse. You must have your whole family avoid it by going near water."

If so, it would be a waste of three thousand four hundred coins." At that time, Zhao was staying at the home of a commoner named Zhang, and he immediately returned to tell him. Zhang also revered Lu Sheng from the beginning and then closed the door to wait. Around noon, indeed, there was a person as described by Lu, who knocked on the door and asked to buy grain. Angered that he did not respond, the person kicked the door. Zhang placed a heavy mat to block it. Soon, several hundred people gathered, and Zhang then led his wife and children away from the door. Slightly after noon, the person left, walking several hundred steps, and suddenly fell and died. His wife arrived, and everyone told her what happened. She was deeply distressed and accused her husband of having a reason for dying. The officials could not judge, and everyone described Zhang's state of closing the door and avoiding. Someone knowledgeable said to Zhang, "You are indeed innocent, but you can arrange for his death." Zhang happily agreed to the judgment, and his wife was also happy. When the coffin arrived at the cart, it was exactly three thousand four hundred coins. As a result, people flocked to it like a market. Lu could not bear it and finally secretly left. He arrived at the border of Fuzhou and moored his boat at the gate of Mr. Lu Qi's estate.

Someone told Lu, "Lu Shansheng is no ordinary person." Lu then paid him a visit. At the time, Lu was planning to seek the acquaintance of influential figures in the capital and asked for guidance. Lu Shansheng said, "You should not move this year, for be wary of sudden misfortune. Behind your hall, there is a jar of money covered with a board, which does not belong to you. The owner of the money is now three years old. Be cautious and do not use even a single coin, for using it will lead to disaster. Will you follow my advice?" Lu was startled and thanked him. As Lu Shansheng left, while the water ripples were still unsettled, Lu smiled and said to his wife, "Lu's words are so, what more could I ask for?" He then ordered his servant to dig the ground. After digging just a few feet, they indeed found a board. They removed it and discovered a large jar filled with scattered coins. Lu was delighted. His wife used a skirt to string the coins together with grass, approaching ten thousand in number. Suddenly, their children suffered from severe headaches, which they could not bear. Lu said, "Is it because of what Lu said?" He then rode a horse to catch up and apologized for breaking the rule.

Lu Sheng, angered, said, "If you use it, it will surely bring disaster to your family. The weight of family versus profit, you must judge for yourself." He rowed away without looking back. Lu Sheng rushed back, performed a ritual, and buried it, and his children improved suddenly. Upon arriving in Fuzhou, Lu Sheng often walked with several others. On one occasion, they encountered six or seven people dressed in fine clothing, all wearing belts, and the smell of alcohol wafted through the air. Suddenly, Lu Sheng shouted at them, "Your deeds are unrepentant, and your lives are numbered!" The people all knelt in the dust, saying, "We dare not, we dare not." His companions were surprised, and Lu Sheng said, "These people are all river bandits." Such was the strangeness of the situation. Zhao Yuanhe recounted that Lu Sheng's appearance varied in age, and he was rarely seen eating or drinking. Once, he told Zhao Sheng, "There are not few assassins who can become invisible in the world. Those who practice the Dao can obtain the art of invisibility; if they do not test it, they can change their form after twenty years, called 'detachment.' After another twenty years, they will be recorded as earth immortals." He also said, "When assassins die, their bodies are also not seen." His words were filled with strange and extraordinary claims, suggesting he was of the same ilk as the immortals.

During the early years of the Changqing era, the reclusive scholar Yang Yinzi was in Chenzhou, where he frequently sought out Daoists. There was a Daoist practitioner named Tang, whom the locals called a hundred-year-old man. Yang paid homage to him, and Tang invited Yang to stay overnight. By night, Tang called his daughter and said, "Can you bring me a crescent moon?" His daughter then pasted a crescent-shaped piece of paper on the wall, just like a piece of paper. Tang got up and prayed, saying, "Tonight there is a guest, grant him light." After he finished speaking, the room became as bright as if candles were lit. In the southern regions, a commoner was walking and encountered rain and wind. He sheltered under a tree with an old man, and the person sat aside and respectfully yielded to him. When the rain stopped, the old man gave him three pills of elixir, saying to take them in case of an emergency. A year later, his wife suddenly fell ill and died. After several days, he remembered the old man's elixir and crushed the teeth of the pills and administered them to her. There was a slight warmth, and her complexion seemed alive. Now she has been dead for four years, and she appears as if in a deep sleep, with her nails grown long. The man still carries her in a palanquin, and those who speak of it say they have seen it in Siming.

●Volume Three: The Collection of the Lotus Sutra Those who enter the Buddhist path generally can speak of the three realms, twenty-eight heavens, four continents, the Pure Land of the Lotus Sutra, the eight cold and eight hot hells, the three bodies, five positions, four fruits, seven limbs, eighteen elements, and thirty-seven factors of enlightenment. Now, it is not necessary to list them all, but we will record the particularly unusual ones. The gods of the Wheel-turning King, in the ten abodes of the sixteen parts, have joys that surpass those of the two lower realms. There are four kinds of joy: one is free from resentment, the second is the remembrance of the gods and the goddesses who do not think of other gods, and the body fragrance extends for a hundred yojanas. In the realm of Kāruvapada, this is due to the ten lands of the elephant tracks. The eyes do not blink, and the multitude of bees produce wonderful sounds. The fragrant winds of the six heavens all enter this realm. The four kings have ten lands with colorful earth, and the Sthāvara earth has eight groves. The Kānchara heaven has ten lands with golden streams, shadowless mountains, and shadowed forests (one called following), and bird-following (one called numerous). In their places of movement, the pools are the same color. The multitude of birds recite verses to the self-god, saying that their bodies are like the flowers of the blue lotus, without feet and soft, rising and falling with the feet. The gods of the realm of play ride on swan palaces, with branches and leaves of precious trees like palaces. The Thirty-three Heavens have ninety-nine thousand goddesses, who remember and bring forth trees and objects as they wish. There are ten pools of flowers and a thousand-pillared palace. The six-times forest has six times in one day.

The Thousand-Spoked Wheel Palace, where the Celestial Deity Sheshi (also known as a goddess) sits. Her clothing lacks warp and weft, and as the dying person, dust sticks to her body. The chariot is pulled by a thousand swans, and the diamond-thread belt is unbreakable. The forest travels wherever heaven goes. There are many golden-breasted birds. A hundred-headed elephant, each head with ten tusks, and at the end of each tusk is a hundred bathing pools. On the top is a mountain called Boundary Splendor. A river flows from the nose, like the Ganges, and the water disperses into mist over the world. On the sides are two gardens, one called Joy Grove and the other called Bliss Grove. The elephant is named Iravatana. The Forest of Light, with four corner trees that embody purpose, where Indra, the king of gods, will fight with the asuras (demons), entering this forest among the four trees, and see the signs of victory and defeat. The Forest of Armor, where armor grows from the trees and cannot be destroyed. Lotus flowers produce the beautiful drink of Mo Tu, and those who cultivate 1,200 good deeds are born in this heaven. The supreme touch, like touching the Garuda bird, this bird is seen only when the wheel-turning king appears in the world. The Forest of Opening and Closing, where opening the eyes always sees light. The heaven of Yama resides in the void, held by the wind of Yama. The Accumulated Cliff Mountain, 300 yojanas high, with seven beds and seven chests. Those who first enter heaven have five characteristics: one characteristic is light covering the body without clothing, second characteristic is seeing things and generating a sense of rarity, third characteristic is a weak appearance, fourth characteristic is doubt, fifth characteristic is fear.

The Five Trees: One near the lotus pond, where the flowers do not bloom (one version omits the "not"); two near the forest bee (one version says crimson), which has left the tree; three, upon hearing the song of the celestial maiden, emerge with <冒犬> (uncertain term) and depart; four near the tree, where the flowers wither; five, the palace does not move in the void. Then, one sees a body radiance and clothing that touch like Vajra (diamond), and when illuminated by the Bhrikuti mirror, the path is not seen. The celestial maiden displays nine signs of retreat: one, her skin is loose; two, her headflowers scatter and fall; three, the red flowers on the path turn yellow; four, the wind blows, and her threadless clothing feels like human touch; five, her flight grows weary; six, she touches water and it becomes turbid; seven, she takes high treeflowers that are unattainable; eight, she sees the king without flattery; nine, her hair is scattered and rough. Her lips move incessantly, and her garlands and flowers are heavy. The twelve kinds of pure offerings give rise to this heaven, where flocks of birds with green shadows cover ten thousand yojanas. The jewel contains a gold-lettered verse. The Four Kings Heaven has twelve defects, often fighting with Asuras. The Thirty-Three Heavens have eight defects, including inferior heavens that are not recognized by Indra. The Nirmanarati Heaven has six defects, including eating inferior things and gradually declining. The Tusita Heaven has four defects, including not enjoying the sound of the swan king's teachings. The Nirmanarati Heaven has four defects, including the end of heavenly works, with no shadow under their feet. The Akaniṣṭha Heaven has four defects, including the departure of the jewel-winged bee.

The Stone of the Desire Realm, after ten million eight hundred thousand three hundred eighty-three years, finally reaches the earth. In the world of Jambudvīpa, people are three and a half to four elbows tall, with four (or five) sets of fifteen bones, thirteen major arteries or veins, and worms in their bodies with blood that has the appearance of a lamp wick. The "禅都摩" worm circulates in the blood. The "善色" worm lives in feces and brings comfort to the host. The "起根" worm becomes happy when it is full. The "欢喜" worm can see dreams, and there are also ailments such as "癀" and "痪." The "赊婆罗" people pierce their lips. There are people with humps on their faces and two legs. A lion has wings, and a woman has a dog-like face. There is a forest called "吱多迦," inhabited by "罗刹." A journey of hundreds of thousands of yojanas takes place in the "旬目" forest, and the continent has red earth, black jade, copper, and white stone. The "郁单越" continent and the "鸡多迦" river have seventy rivers in total. The "自在无畏" and the "四天王" are like a duck-sounding forest, a "麒麟陀" tree, and a "迦吱多那" tree. There are twenty-five deer. There is a mountain with many "牛头旃檀" trees, where gods and demons fight, and the wounded are treated with sandalwood oil. The "提罗迦" tree has flowers that open when they see sunlight.

The flowers of the *Juniyiduo* tree bloom when they see the moonlight. The *Wuyou* tree's flowers only bloom when touched by a woman. The *Shilisha* tree grows when stepped on. There are also flowers of the White Dragon, the Live Goose, and the Swirling Nostril Realm. The *Qudoni* woman is the chief milk provider, with ten billion villages and twelve thousand cities in her domain. The *Duoqida* Five Great Rivers, the Moon Power equals *Fupati*. The Three Great Forest Valleys. Three (or one as a king) great cities, the largest with thirty-five million three hundred fifty-five thousand six hundred villages. The South Continent is adorned by ear hair, the North by the eyes, the West by the crown and belly, and the East by the shoulders. Those born in *Shanzhu* see white. Those born in *Yulin* see red, and see their mother like a goose. Those born in *Qudoyi* see yellow, and see their mother like a cow. Those born in *Fupati* see blue, and see their mother like a horse. The *Ashura* (demi-gods) who capture demons and ghosts with supernatural powers, and two beasts are captured in the ocean floor, eight thousand four hundred *yojana* (a unit of distance). The wine tree. There is another tree where bees flow honey, and its color is like gold. The *Borobo* tree, its fruit is like a jar.

Four-colored women, like shadows, each have twelve hundred million attendants and live for five thousand years. The place is named Moon. There are thirteen places at the top of the mountain that are not seen, with deer lost and bees swirling. Red-eyed fish walk on ice and live in the caves of the main mountain. Fish in the pool love the water, with mouths equal to yellow. The city of Iron Vairocana, during war, hands and feet are cut off but regenerate, and half-body and path immediately die. Ghosts and monsters under Jambudvipa for five hundred yojanas, there are thirty-six types of Mara commanding ghosts, this means ghost children. Mara's bird, only eats fish, leaving the goose ghost to receive this body. Beasts have thirty-four (one says six) hundred million kinds. Dragons living in Jambudvipa, fifty-seven hundred million. Dragons in Jambudvipa do not descend into turbid water, people in the Western Continent who eat turbid water die young. People in the Southern Continent dislike cold winds, dragons do not emit cold. The continent of Pūrvavideha does not make thunder sounds, does not rise thunder light, the Eastern Continent is evil. The thunder sound, the Tushita Heaven makes song and praise sounds, Jambudvipa makes the sound of the sea tide. The rain, in Tushita Heaven it rains jewels, in the city of the world protectors it rains fine food, in the sea it pours rain continuously like a wheel, in the Asura realm it rains weapons, in Jambudvipa it rains pure water. Hells are one hundred and thirty-six. Three-cornered birth and death, good and no memory. Round birth and death, all gods.

Blue beings are sent to the realm of hell, while yellow beings are sent to the realm of hungry ghosts. Red beings are sent to the realm of beasts. The realm of living hell has sixteen sub-realms, each lasting for five thousand years, equivalent to one day and night in this realm. There are diamond insects in hot pots, yellow and blue flower hearts, mud fish, and they arrange tubes. The Black Rope Hell is extremely intense and feared by eagles. In the Combined Hell, there are copper soup rivers that cause suffering. The suffering is like entering the belly of a bird. In the Cutting and Dissecting Hell, there are creatures with hot mouths and iron ants. In the Tear Fire Hell, there are creatures that cause suffering by putting ash into their eyes. In the Great Howling Hell, there are creatures that cause suffering with their hair or beard, and the colors are golden and muddy. The Red Tree Fish Belly Hell is a place of intense suffering.

The Hot Hell, with twelve fiery places, has flames burning in all directions, including those of hunger and thirst. A wind blows from the mouth of a dragon, causing fish to suffer. The cauldron is fifty yojanas in size, with boiling foam reaching half a yojana high. The wind blows down to a distance of 360 million yojanas, affecting the earth and causing insects to suffer. The sound of drums and cows is considered evil. There is a dragon with a thousand heads. The Avīchi Hell has sixteen distinct forms of suffering. Clothing rapidly deteriorates and becomes dirty. Those who will be born in the Avīchi Hell appear as if they are eight years old, with their faces turned downward. They suffer for three thousand years due to the wind in the air, a suffering that surpasses the pleasure of the Agniṣṭhā Heaven. The foul odor in the hell can destroy the six heavens of the Desire Realm, and two mountains that rise and fall obstruct the view. There are specific areas such as the "crow's mouth," "black belly," and "one-horned and two-horned" places. The Eight Cold Hells are similar to commonly known descriptions. Those born in hell can take three forms: human, animal, or formless. Light sins result in human form, medium sins in animal form, and severe sins in formless beings, such as "meat pavilions" and "meat screens." In Buddhist temples, the depiction of hell in paintings is somewhat accurate, but there is no basis for depicting human suffering. Traditionally, it is believed that the "Aparāpāsa" (a type of ghost) appears in hell due to karma. As people gradually die, their feet become the coldest, indicating they are leaving hell. When the world is about to end, no one will be born in hell. The Asura has a pool where everything is visible, showing the outcomes of battles. The Tushita Heaven has a Mirror Forest where celestial beings can see their own good and evil actions. The Dharmarāja Heaven has a Crystal Tree where one can see whether people follow the law or not. The Brahmā Heaven constantly observes. The Trāyastriṃśa Heaven and the seven lives of humans are visible on the walls of the palace.

In the realm of the Eighth Heaven, the Bhadrakalpaka Heaven, there stands a Bhadrakalpaka tree that reflects the karma of beings in the world below. When one performs good deeds, the tree shines brightly for a distance of a hundred yojanas; when one performs bad deeds, the tree withers and dies; if one performs only half of good deeds, the tree only partially flourishes. In the subtle realm of heaven, the branches and leaves of the precious tree reveal the images of heavenly beings, and the upper, middle, and lower levels of karma are also visible. In the realm of Yama, within the Saraca tree, one can see the results of actions. The palace is as pure as a mirror, and everything done by heavenly beings is clearly visible. In another tree, there is a palace supported by a thousand columns, with records of karma, including the sixteen hells, all of which are visible. In the realm of Tāvatiṃsa, in the pool of water that reflects the imperfections of the mind, one can see oneself reflected, and what one sees on their forehead is a reflection of their past actions, revealing the results of their karma. In the shadow of a tower in the realm of Yama, one can see the sins and merits of the desire realm and the three lower realms. The phenomena in the heavens are different; if a bird is carrying heavy food and flying down, an impending solar eclipse may occur, and the sky may turn red in all directions.

The Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions: 1. **昴 (one says 角)** - It leads the way, traveling thirty (or six) hours in one night, shaped like a razor, with the name な耶尼, offering milk in rituals, and associated with fire. 2. **毕** - Shaped like a hat, it also belongs to wood, offering deer meat and 颇罗堕 in rituals. 3. **觜** - Associated with the sun (or not), it is the child of the moon, with the name 毗梨亻去耶尼, shaped like a deer's head, offering fruit in rituals. 4. **参** - Associated with the sun, it is short-lived, with the name 婆斯, shaped like a woman's necklace, offering clarified butter in rituals. 5. **井** - Associated with the sun, sharing the same name as 参, shaped like footprints, offering glutinous rice and honey in rituals. 6. **鬼** - Belonging to wood, with the name 炮波罗毗, shaped like a Buddha's chest, offering the same as 井 in rituals. 7. **柳** - Belonging to the snake, sharing the same name as 参, shaped like a snake. 8. **星** - Belonging to fire, shaped like a riverbank, with the name 宾伽耶尼, offering black sesame seeds in rituals. 9. **张** - Belonging to the celestial being of good fortune, with the name 瞿云弥, shaped and offered the same as 井. 10. **翼** - Belonging to the celestial being of the forest, with the name ╂陈如, offering black beans in rituals, shaped the same as the previous. 11. **轸** - Belonging to 毗沙梨帝, shaped like a human hand, with the name 迦遮延, offering wild rice in rituals. 12. **角** - Belonging to the celestial being of joy, with the name 质多罗, shaped the same as the previous, offering flowers in rituals. 13. **亢** - With the name 迦旃延, offering green beans in rituals. 14. **氏** - With the name 多罗尼, offering flowers in rituals. 15. **房** - Belonging to the celestial being of compassion, with the name 阿蓝婆, shaped like a necklace, offering alcohol and meat in rituals.

The Heart belongs to the Tushita Heaven, named Kalayan, with a form like barley, and offerings are made using polished rice. The Tail belongs to the Lashiti Heaven, named Chayeni, with a form like a scorpion's tail, and offerings are made using fruit roots. The Winnowing Basket belongs to the Pure Heaven, named Dhyani Kaya, with a form like a cow's horn. The Dipper is named Mokala, with a form like a stone struck by a person, and offerings are made like a well. The Ox belongs to the Brahma Heaven, named Vamana, with a form like a cow's head, and offerings are made like a pine tree. The Woman belongs to the Vishnu Heaven, named Dilika Chayeni, with a form like a heart, and offerings are made with bird meat. The Void is named Tongyi, with a form like a bird, and offerings are made using black soybean juice. The Danger is named Danroni, with a form like a pine tree (or heart), and offerings are made with polished rice. The Chamber belongs to the Snake Head Heaven, the son of the Scorpion Heaven, named Yamaduta, and offerings are made with blood. The Wall is named Dharnaka. The Warrior is named Ashtaka, and offerings are made using curd. The Pouch belongs to the Gandharva Heaven, named Ahanba, with a form like a horse's head, and offerings are made using barley. The Stomach is named Dhakapi, with a form like a tripod's legs. The four stars of Kang, Xu, Shen, and Wei cannot enter the formation. People born under the Zhen constellation take seven steps without encountering snakes. People born under the Jiao constellation like to mock and play. People born under the Nu constellation find it unfavorable to undertake tasks on the days of Kang, Shen, and Wei constellations. The Xu and Jiao constellations (sometimes with the character for 'thing') are superior. One thousand six hundred kshana make one kshana, double sixty is called Henghu Lüdu, and double thirty days is one day and night. Night means smoke is wisdom.

The light of the Dragon King is called *Yōulùo*, which means "heavenly dog." Emperor Ming of Wei first built the White Horse Temple. In the temple, a banner was hung, and its shadow entered the interior. The emperor was startled and asked those around him, "What kind of deity does the Buddha have that people revere and serve?" In the country of Uddiyana, there are Buddha's footprints, whose size varies according to one's lifespan and fortune. In the east of the city of Nāgara in the country of Nālanda, there is a stupa containing the Buddha's cranial relic, with a circumference of two feet. To discern good and evil, one applies incense to the imprint of the bone, and the traces become clear, revealing all good and evil. In the northern kingdom of Gandhāra, there is a large stupa. The Buddha foretold that it would be burned seven times and rebuilt seven times. The city of the Buddha, Xuanzang said, has been ruined for three years. In the Western Regions, the Buddha's Diamond Throne has a copper boundary marker, with two statues of the freely moving one. The people of the country say that when the Bodhisattva's body disappears, the Dharma will also cease. By the end of the Sui Dynasty, it had already disappeared past the chest. On the banks of the head river in the country of Gandhara, there is a tree tied to a white elephant, whose flowers and leaves resemble jujubes and ripen in late winter. It is said that when this tree disappears, the Dharma will also cease. During the Northern Dynasties, in the north of Jiao Cheng, Xuzhou, monks and nuns wear white cloth robes, and sometimes there are blue cloth robes.

The Persian Empire had a subordinate state called At'a, and in a great forest north of its capital city, there was a monastery known as the "Sound of Buddha." Here, the monks listened to teachings and wore boots and bound shoes. The term "boots" referred to what was called "boots" in that land. Prince Ning Xian fell ill, and the emperor ordered imperial messengers to send medicine, who were seen along the way. The monk Chongyi treated Prince Xian, and he gradually recovered. The emperor was pleased and bestowed upon Chongyi a red robe and a fish-shaped purse as a reward. Emperor Jianwen of Liang wrote a memorial table titled "Thanking the Gift of a Yuni Cloth Robe." When Wei envoy Lu Cao arrived in Liang, the King of Liang sat in a small carriage and bowed twice, then sent the mid-level official Yin Jiong to convey the emperor's greetings. They reached the Heavy Cloud Palace, where they were led up to the throne. The King of Liang wore a Bodhisattva robe, facing north, and the crown prince and his attendants all wore Bodhisattva robes, arranged according to ritual. Lu Cao stood in order facing west, and all the others faced east. A monk performed the ritual, and there were three volumes of Buddhist texts. In the third volume, it was mentioned that the Wei Emperor, the Wei Prime Minister, and the people from both the north and south were present. After the Buddhist ritual, the officials all bowed twice. When Li Qian and Cui arrived at Tongtai Temple in Liang, the officials Wang Ke, He Jiyou, and three monks welcomed them at the gate. They entered the pagoda, and beside the Buddha, there was a recorder of sins. The monk told Li Qian, "This is the recorder of sins, which specializes in recording people's faults." Li Qian replied, "This is like the monk's version of Dongfu." They then entered the second hall, where there was a bronze bowl with burning lamps. Cui said, "It's like the sun and moon rising, and the torches are still burning." East of Lu County, there was a mountain called Jin榆 Mountain. In the past, the monk Lang instructed his disciples to collect elm leaves here and go to Xiaqiu to trade them, and they all turned into money.

During the Northern Wei dynasty, Empress Hu once asked the monk (also known as a teacher) Ba Zhi about the fate of the state. In the morning, Ba Zhi said, "Holding a date and calling a chicken Zhu Zhu," which refers to the Erzhu clan. There was a person named Zhao Fahu who requested an interpretation, and Ba Zhi said, "A great arrow does not need feathers. The eastern cabinet room, hurry to act." Soon after, Zhao Fahu's father died. In Licheng County, the Guangzheng Temple had a stone磬 (a stone used for making bells or chimes), shaped like a half-moon, with a smooth and glossy surface that seemed to drip. When struck, its sound could be heard for a hundred miles. During the Northern Qi dynasty, it was moved to the capital, and when struck there, its sound disappeared. When it was returned to the temple and struck again, the sound was as before. People said, "The磬 is sacred, it longs for Guangzheng." At the beginning of the Tang dynasty, the monk Xuanzang traveled to the Five Indias to obtain Buddhist scriptures, and was revered in the West. Chengshi met a Japanese monk named Jin Gang Sanmei, who said he had visited the Middle Kingdom, and that many paintings of Xuanzang, his whisk, and his chopsticks were depicted in the temple, carried by colorful clouds, which are not found in the West. Every day during the斋 period, they would prostrate themselves before them. He also said that in the Nalanda Monastery's dining hall, during the heat, there were tens of thousands of large flies. When the monks entered the hall, all the flies would fly and gather on the trees in the courtyard. A monk named Wan Hui, who was in his twenties, appeared foolish and did not speak. His brother was stationed in Liaoyang, and they had been out of contact for a long time, and someone claimed he had died. His family held a斋 ceremony. Wan Hui suddenly rolled up some food, and loudly said, "My brother is alive, I will send it to him." He left the door as if flying, faster than a horse. By evening, he returned, having received a letter from his brother, with the seal still wet. Calculating the round trip, it was a thousand miles in one day, and he was thus named.

Empress Wu Zetian appointed tyrannical officials to fabricate charges, and those who held slightly elevated positions were separated from their wives and children daily. Cui Xuanwei, the Prince of Boling, had both his rank and reputation at their peak. His mother was worried and said, "You should invite Monk Wan Hui once. This monk is of the same kind as Master Baozhi, and you can observe his actions to see good or bad fortune." When Monk Wan Hui arrived, the mother wept and performed a ritual, also giving him a pair of silver spoons and chopsticks. Suddenly, Monk Wan Hui descended the steps, threw the spoons and chopsticks onto the hall, waved his arms, and left. The whole family considered this an omen. One day, they ordered someone to climb the roof to retrieve the spoons and chopsticks. From where they fell, a book scroll was found. Upon examination, it was a book of predictions and taboos. They immediately ordered it to be burned. Several days later, officials suddenly came to their house, searched thoroughly for the books of predictions but did not find them, and the family was cleared of suspicion. At that time, the tyrannical officials often ordered thieves to bury poisons at night and leave predictions in people's homes. After a month, they secretly recorded them. If not for Monk Wan Hui, the family would have been wiped out. The Indian monk Fokong attained the door of total control and could command a hundred spirits. Emperor Xuanzong respected him. There was a drought every year, and the emperor ordered to pray for rain. Monk Fokong said, "Yes. On a certain day, pray for it, and there will definitely be a heavy rain." The emperor then ordered Monk Jinang Sanzang to set up an altar to pray for rain. For several days, the heavy rain did not stop, and some were drowned in the marketplaces. The emperor immediately summoned Monk Fokong to stop it. Monk Fokong then made five or six clay dragons in the temple courtyard, facing the water, and said some foreign words and cursed them. After a long time, he put them back and laughed loudly. After a while, the rain stopped.

Emperor Xuanzong once summoned the magician Luo Gongyuan and Fokong to pray for rain together, and they competed in their magical powers. The emperor summoned both of them and questioned them. Fokong said, "I burned white sandalwood incense last night." The emperor ordered his attendants to take water from the courtyard and smell it, and indeed there was the scent of sandalwood. Fokong and Luo Gongyuan were together in the convenience hall. Luo was scratching his back with his other hand. Fokong said, "Lend me your wish-fulfilling jewel." The flower stones on the hall were smooth and shiny, so the wish-fulfilling jewel was thrown (or struck) to his front. Luo tried to pick it up several times but could not. The emperor wanted to pick it up, but Fokong said, "Emperor, do not get up, it is just an illusion." Fokong raised his hand to show Luo the wish-fulfilling jewel. There was a large snake on Mount Mang, which the woodcutters often saw. Its head was like a hill, and at night, it often absorbed the dew. When it saw Fokong, it spoke in human language and said, "I am a disciple suffering a bad karma, how can the master save me? I often wanted to overturn the river and flood the city of Luoyang to make my habitat more comfortable." Fokong gave it a precept, spoke of the suffering and emptiness, and said, "You are suffering this pain with a resentful heart, and you are also angry, what can I do? Think about my words, this body has been here since ancient times." After a few months, the woodcutters saw the snake die in the stream, and the stench reached for dozens of miles. Fokong always prayed for rain, without any other rules. He only set up several embroidered seats, shook a small wooden god with his hand, chanted a spell and threw it, and stood on the seat himself. When the water god's mouth, nose, and teeth appeared, and the eyes widened, the rain would come.

Sage Yi Xing was proficient in numbers and had extraordinary abilities. During the Kaiyuan era, there was a drought, and Emperor Xuanzong ordered a prayer for rain. Yi Xing said, "One must find a vessel with a dragon-like pattern to summon rain." The emperor ordered a thorough inspection of the imperial treasury, but none of the items were suitable. After several days, he pointed to an ancient mirror with a dragon on its handle and exclaimed, "This has a true dragon!" He then took it into the ritual site, and it rained that night. At the beginning of the Zhenyuan era in Jingzhou, there was a mad monk who was good at singing "He Manzi." Once he encountered a drunk man named Wu Bai who insulted him and ordered him to sing. The monk immediately sang, and his words were all about Wu Bai's past misdeeds. Wu Bai was shocked and repented. In Suzhou during the Zhenyuan era, there was a righteous warrior who appeared to be mad. There was a commoner who built over ten shops, and the righteous warrior suddenly smashed the roof with an axe. The commoner could not stop him. The commoner knew of his divine nature and said respectfully, "My livelihood depends on this." The warrior replied, "Do you value it?" and then threw the axe down and left. That night, the market caught fire, but only the few rooms the warrior had destroyed remained. He often stayed in the ruins of a temple, always kept a fire going, regardless of the season, and burned banners, wooden statues, and other items. He liked to cook live carp and ate them before they were fully cooked. He never washed his face; whenever he did, it rained, and in Wu, people considered this a sign of rain. As he was dying, he drank several buckets of ash water, then sat and recited Buddhist mantras, and stopped eating and drinking. People watched him daily, and he sat for seven days and then died. It was a hot summer, but his face did not change, and his body did not collapse. At An Guo Temple, there was a monk named Shudi who often burned wooden Buddhas. He often spoke to people and had a good understanding of the core teachings, but the monks at the temple also did not understand him.

Emperor Ruizong was born in the *Huanliang Hall*. Empress Wu Zetian then built a Buddhist temple within the hall, where there was a jade statue. As he grew up, he would often observe the statue. One day, the statue suddenly spoke: "You will become an emperor in the future." **Chapter 4: Strange Phenomena** - The people of the East have large noses, with their orifices connected to their eyes, and their strength resides there. - The people of the South have large mouths, with their orifices connected to their ears. - The people of the West have large faces, with their orifices connected to their noses. - The people of the North have orifices connected to their lower bodies, and they have short necks. - The people of the Central Region have orifices connected to their mouths. - The *Wuqimin* dwell in caves and eat earth. When they die, their hearts do not decay; when buried, they transform into humans after a hundred years. - The *Lumin* have knees that do not decay; when buried, they transform into humans after one hundred and twenty years. - The *Ximin* have livers that do not decay; when buried, they transform into humans after eight years.

Fertile land's people are beautiful, while barren land's people are ugly. The daughter of an emperor named Ze was naturally jealous and had maidservants who were scattered and driven away to Sichuan, having no support. She encountered a fox in the east and gave birth to a son named Yang. In the south, she encountered a monkey and had a son named Xi. In the north, she encountered a wild animal and raised a son named Cang. The ancestor of the Turks was Shemo, a sea deity named Shemo Sheli, whose deity resided west of the Ashtek cave. Shemo possessed supernatural abilities, and the sea goddess welcomed him into the sea every evening with a white deer and sent him out until dawn. This continued for many years. Later, the tribe prepared for a great hunt. At midnight, the sea goddess told Shemo, "The next day during the hunt, in the cave where your ancestors were born, there will be a golden-horned white deer. If you shoot this deer, we will be able to meet in full form. If you miss, then the connection will be severed." At dawn, they encircled the area, and indeed, a golden-horned white deer appeared in the cave where they were born. Shemo sent his attendants to secure the perimeter. The deer was about to jump out of the perimeter and was killed. Shemo was angry and personally beheaded the leader of the Hā tribe, swearing, "After this act, human sacrifices to heaven are required." He took the descendants of the Hā tribe to be sacrificed. Today, the Turks perform human sacrifices to the flag, often using the Hā tribe. After beheading the Hā tribe, Shemo returned at dusk, and the sea goddess told him, "You personally killed people, with blood and gore, the connection is severed."

The Turks worship the Avestan god and have no temples. They carve felt into shapes and store them in leather bags. Whenever they move, they anoint them with oil. Sometimes they hang them on poles and worship them throughout the year. The Kangju tribe is not of the wolf lineage. Their ancestral cave is in the north of Quman Mountain. They claim that their ancestors had a divine encounter with a bull in this cave. Their people have yellow hair, green eyes, and red beards. Those with black beards are descendants of the Han general Li Ling and his soldiers. The Western Tujue dye their teeth black. The Lao people in , if a woman dies during childbirth in July, they bury her in a strong coffin. The Mòrén people, west of Old Láo, use deer antlers as utensils. When they die, they bend and burn them, then bury their bones. Later, small bones resembling humans, black like lacquer, and in cold weather, they dig sand for themselves, only exposing their faces. The state of Mùyǐn, one of the Zhu Ya states, has no springs; the people do not dig wells and rely on tree sap for drinking. The Mùpū, with a tail like a turtle, several inches long, lives on trees and eats people.

The Asa tribe often hunts insects and deer, slicing the meat, stacking it, and pressing it with stones to extract the juice. In countries such as Persia and Byzantium, rice and herbs are fermented in the meat juice for several days, transforming into a drinkable wine that can intoxicate. The boundaries of the Xiaoyi Kingdom span over three thousand miles. It lies in a flat plain, with wooden fences surrounding an area of more than ten miles, housing around two thousand households. The kingdom has over five hundred large enclosures. The climate is always warm, with no winter decay. It is suitable for sheep and horses, but lacks camels and cows. The people are simple, honest, and fond of companionship. They are tall and have long hair, yellow hair, green eyes, red beards, and red-faced complexions. Their only weapon is a spear. They grow grains and produce metals. They wear clothing made from hemp. The entire population worships the deity (ビ) and does not know Buddhism. There are over three hundred (some say a thousand) temples dedicated to the deity, and one hundred thousand soldiers. They do not value trade and call themselves "Xiaoyi people." Both men and women wear ornaments. They prepare food daily and consume it for a month, often eating leftover food. The kingdom is established without wells or rivers, relying on rain for cultivation. They use purple ore to prepare the soil, using rainwater. Digging wells results in salty water. After the tide recedes, the flat areas become pools, and they fish to make food. The Pamilan Kingdom is located 25,550 li from the capital. To the west of this kingdom are steep and dangerous mountains. There are many large apes on the mountains. Heavy rain occurs frequently, with tens of thousands of apes. After spring, the kingdom gathers soldiers to fight the apes. Although they kill tens of thousands annually, they cannot destroy all their nests.

The Kingdom of Balbal, located in the southwest sea, does not consume the five grains, but only eats meat. They often pierce the veins of cattle, take blood and mix it with milk to eat raw. They have no clothes, only using sheepskin to cover their lower bodies. The women of the kingdom are fair and well-proportioned, and the people themselves capture and sell them to foreign merchants, whose prices are several times higher. The land has only ivory and ambergris. Persian merchants want to enter this country, gathering thousands, fasting and binding themselves with cloth, all old and young together piercing their blood and taking an oath, then they buy their goods. It has never belonged to a foreign country. In battle, they use elephant spears, wild bull horns as spears, armor, bows, and arrows. They have twenty thousand infantry. The Arabs frequently attacked it. The Kingdom of Kunwu, trenches are piled up to form hills, like stupas, with three stories, the dry corpses are placed above, the wet corpses are placed below, considering near burial as the utmost filial piety. They gather large felt tents to live in, hang clothes and paintings inside, mourn and worship them. In the Kingdom of Kucha, on New Year's Day, they fight bulls, horses, and camels, for seven days as entertainment, observe the victory and defeat, to predict the increase or decrease of sheep and horses in the year. In the Kingdom of Pala, they wear dog heads and monkey faces, men and women dance day and night. On August 15th, they perform processions and rope tricks as entertainment. In the Kingdom of Yanqi, on New Year's Day and February 8th, they perform the Pamoza ritual, on the third day, they hold wild sacrifices. On April 15th, they go into the forest. On May 5th, they celebrate the birth of Maitreya. On July 7th, they worship their ancestors. On September 9th, they scatter on the bed. On October 10th, the king performs the <冒犬> ritual. The king leaves the leader's house, the leader rides the king's horse, for one day and night, he handles the king's affairs. On October 14th, they make music until the end of the year.

In the Kingdom of Balasagun, on the nineteenth day of the twelfth month, the king and the leaders divided into two factions. Each faction sent out one person wearing armor, while the common people held tiles and stones, wielding sticks on both sides. The factions were alternately tied or connected. The person in armor who died first would stop the contest, and this was done to predict the abundance or scarcity of the year. In the Kingdom of Sudushen, there was a city called Night Demon City. This city once had a wild beast or demon, and its den is still there. There are over 500 families living near the den. A house was built at the entrance of the den, and a gate or barrier was set up. They held a ceremony twice a year. When people approached the entrance of the den, smoke would come out, and the first person to touch it would die. They would then throw the body into the den. The depth of the den is unknown. General Ma Fubo had ten families of soldiers who did not return. They settled in Shouqia County, marrying among themselves and forming a community of 200 households. Because they were immigrants or exiles, they were called Ma Liu. Their clothing and food were the same as the locals. The mountains and rivers have changed, and the metaphorical sinking of a bronze pillar into the sea is a reference to their history. This people are known for this, and they are also called Ma Liu.

The customs of the people in the gorges remain unchanged, preserving their indigenous ways. The Wuning people like to wear something called "Mangxin Jielí," which is known as "Zhuzui." They used rice to mark time. At burial, they would place a hairpin pointing to the sky, which they called "piercing the Big Dipper." It is said that when Panhu first died, he was placed on a tree, and a hairpin was used to pierce below him, and later, an elephant appeared. In Linyi County, there is a place called "Yanchi Pool," where there are no trees around the pool. The local people, during spring and summer, often catch wild geese and take their wings to protect against the heat. To the west of Wuhao, there is a place called "Xuandu," where the mountains and streams are impassable, and they use a rope to cross, with a worn-out rope spanning two thousand li. The local people cultivate the land between stones, build houses by piling stones, and drink by reaching out, which is called "ape drinking." To the east of Shanshan, in the southwest of Longcheng, there is an area ten li wide, all of which is salt fields. Travelers passing by, their cattle and horses lie down on felt mats. In the valleys and caves of the southern regions, there are often people who can fly their heads, hence the term "flying head Laozi." One day before the head flies, there are marks around the neck, and the neck looks like red threads, so the wife and children watch over him. At night, the person appears sick, and suddenly the head grows wings and flies away. It searches for crabs and worms to eat in the mud by the shore, and returns in the morning, as if waking from a dream, and the stomach is full. The Buddhist monk Pusa Sheng also said that in the country of Jieba, there are people who can fly their heads, and these people have no pupils in their eyes, and in the village, there is one person who controls them. According to the "Yu Shi Zhi Gui" (Strange Stories of the Yu Family): The people of the south, known as Luo people, can fly their heads. The custom they worship is called "Chong Luo," and they are called Luo people because of this.

Zhu Huan of the Jin dynasty had a servant girl whose head would fly at night. According to the *Wang Ziyang Shiyi*, during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, envoys from the mythical country of Yinchi traveled to the south and encountered people who could separate their bodies. These individuals could first send their heads flying to the South Sea, their left hands to the East Sea, and their right hands to the West Marsh. By evening, their heads would return to their shoulders. Their hands, however, would be carried away by strong winds and blown out to sea. Recently, a sea traveler bound for Silla was blown to an island where the entire mountain was covered with black lacquered spoons and chopsticks. The place was filled with large trees. The traveler looked up and saw that the spoons and chopsticks were actually the flowers and branches of these trees. He picked up more than a hundred pairs and took them home. When he tried to use them, they were too heavy to handle. Later, he accidentally used them to stir tea, and they dissolved as he stirred. ### Chapter 4: Auspicious Signs Zhang Xifu, a scholar of the Imperial Academy, once said that before Li Kui, the chancellor, was appointed, a month before his promotion, as the sun was setting, a toad as big as a bed appeared in his bedroom and soon disappeared. He also mentioned that when Li Kui was initially appointed to New Prefecture and was about to be promoted to chancellor, a well in his residence suddenly overflowed, rising about a foot deep. Zheng Gong, another chancellor, lived in a house at the south gate of Zhaoguo Lane. Suddenly, objects began to throw stones and tiles at his home for several consecutive nights. He moved to a residence at the west gate of Anren to avoid this, but the stones and tiles continued to follow him. After a long time, he returned to Zhaoguo Lane. Zheng Gong had taken refuge in the Buddhist path and had a meditation room. When he returned and was about to enter the meditation room, he saw countless spiders hanging threads from the ceiling, about one or two feet above the ground, in a number he could not count. That night, the stones and tiles stopped. The next day, he was promoted to chancellor.

Cheng Shi saw the Chief Clerk Zheng Fu say that during the use of military forces in Huai West, Liu Mian was a junior officer, and the military leader somewhat looked down upon him. Each time capturing a living enemy or scouting, Liu Mian was always present, sustaining severe injuries multiple times, nearly dying on several occasions. Later, due to the dark night and strong winds, he was again ordered to capture a living enemy. Liu Mian, filled with anger and determination, ventured deep into the enemy territory, intending to die. After traveling more than ten miles, he sat down to rest and was about to fall asleep when suddenly someone noticed him, gave him two candles, and said, "You are about to become greatly honored; as long as this candle remains in your heart, there is no worry." Later, Liu Mian was appointed as a general and often saw a dim shadow on his double flags. When the candle was no longer visible, he pretended to be ill and returned to his clan. **Chapter Four: Signs of Misfortune** Yang Shengjin and his brothers, though wealthy and noble, often felt uneasy. Each morning, they would worship the Buddha image and silently pray for protection from unseen forces. One day, three piles of dust gathered on the earthen seat in front of the Buddha image, resembling small mounds. Yang Shengjin was displeased and suspected it might be a child's prank, so he ordered it to be swept away. That night, the dust reappeared, and soon after, disaster struck. Jiang Chugong often visited the Chan Ding Temple, where the officials of Jingzhao prepared a grand banquet. During the drinking, a beautiful courtesan at the table presented cups and arranged her hair, never showing her hands, which puzzled the guests. A drunken guest joked, "Doesn't she have six fingers?" He then forcibly pulled her hand to see. The courtesan fell over, revealing a dried-up corpse. Jiang Chugong eventually met disaster.

Xiao Huan, upon his initial arrival in Suizhou, constructed two flagpoles and erected them in the temple, holding a vegetarian meal to celebrate the occasion. After the meal, music was performed, but suddenly a violent thunderstorm struck, and the flagpoles broke into dozens of pieces. A year later, on the same day of the thunderclap, Xiao Huan died. ● Chapter Four: Transformations of Things Zhiyi Zhu Jingxuan, who saw Bao Rong, recounted that during the time of Chen Sishu in Yangzhou, the shadow of a pagoda in the eastern market suddenly collapsed. An old man explained that when the reflection of the sea turns over, such an event occurs. Cui Xuanliang, a court attendant in Luoyang, often walked along the sandy shore and found a stone as large as a chicken's egg, black and glossy, and very beautiful. He played with it. After walking more than a mile, the stone suddenly cracked, and a bird as large as a skylark flew away. Duan Shuo, a Jinshi, was acquainted with Nan Xiaolian, who was skilled in carving fish. The carvings were as thin as silk threads, light enough to be blown away, and when he wielded the knife, it seemed to follow a rhythm. During a gathering where guests were showcasing their skills, he first set up a fish frame, but suddenly a violent storm with thunder struck, and all the carvings turned into butterflies and flew away. Nan Xiaolian was terrified and broke his knife, vowing never to carve again. At the end of the Kai Cheng era, the ice in the yellow fish pond in He Yang formed patterns like brocade. In the southern part of He Yang city, the Wang family had a small pond near their estate with several large willow trees. At the end of the Kai Cheng era, the leaves fell into the pond and immediately turned into fish, as big as the leaves themselves, but tasteless. By winter, the family faced legal troubles.

A Buddhist monk named Qingjian from Wuzhou had a turnip in his garden that suddenly transformed into a lotus flower. **Volume 5: Strange Practices** During the Dali period, there was a beggar at the Tianjin Bridge in the eastern capital, Luoyang. This beggar had no hands and used his right foot to hold a brush and write sutras to beg for money. When he wanted to write, he first tossed the brush three times, each toss reaching a height of more than a foot, and it never fell. His calligraphy was in the official script, and it was even better than handwriting. Yu Nu, while in Xiangyang, once had a hermit named Wanggu pay him a visit. Yu was quick-witted and noticed that Wanggu knelt and bowed slowly, showing little reverence. The hermit, unable to gain further access, felt very disappointed. He then went to the magistrate's office and was well-received by Judge Zeng Shuzheng. Wanggu told Zeng, "You think the official is curious, so you came from afar, but now it turns out that you were mistaken. I have a skill that has never existed before. Now that I am leaving, and I am grateful for your generous hospitality, I will perform a demonstration for you." He then went to Zeng's residence and took out a bamboo tube and a small drum from his sleeves. The tube was only a few inches long. After a while, he removed the stopper from the tube, snapped a branch, and continuously hit the drum. Dozens of small insects, like flies or spiders, emerged from the tube, forming two lines and dividing into two teams, like an army formation. Each time he struck the drum, the insects changed their formation, sometimes three times, sometimes five times, always following the rhythm of the drum. The formations were as varied as the balance of heaven and earth, and as intricate as the fish and crane formations, with nothing missing. The insects advanced, retreated, separated, and joined in ways that surpassed human capability. They changed formations dozens of times before finally returning to the bamboo tube. Zeng was greatly shocked and spoke to the official about it, but Wanggu had already secretly left. Yu regretted his actions and ordered a search for Wanggu, but they failed to find him.

Zhang Fen, once a personal attendant of Wei Nankang's army, was renowned for his extraordinary skills. He could lift a seven-foot stele with his strength and stabilize a double-wheeled water mill. He often played cuju (an ancient Chinese sport) at Fugan Temple, where the ball could bounce up to half the height of a pagoda and had an elasticity capable of lifting five dou (a unit of volume). He would select large sun-facing bamboo shoots, weave them into bamboo cages, and gradually cultivate them as they grew, leaving about an inch of space. When the bamboo cage reached four feet in height, he would allow the bamboo to grow longer. Only in late autumn would he remove the cage and cut down the bamboo, which was one foot long with ten nodes and had a golden color. Each time he painted walls, he would form the characters "Heavenly Peace" in a square meter. In the early years of Jianzhong, there was a military general named Xia from Hebei who could draw a bow with hundreds of jin (a unit of weight). Once, he placed more than ten coins on the pitch, rode a horse, and hit the coins with a cuju stick, sending each coin flying six or seven zhang (a unit of length) with every hit. Such was his skill. He also placed dozens of thorns on a new mud wall, took rotten beans, one zhang apart, and threw each bean to pierce the thorns, never missing once in a hundred attempts. He could also write on a sheet of paper while riding a horse. At the end of Yuanhe, there was a seventy-year-old resident in Xunxiang County, Junzhou, who raised more than ten otters. He fished as a profession, releasing them every other day. Before releasing them, he would first confine them in a deep ditch with a gate, making them hungry, then release them. Without the labor of nets and hooks, he achieved similar profits. The old man clapped his hands and called them, and all the otters came, climbing onto his lap, tame as guard dogs. The Langzhong of the Ministry of Revenue, Li Fu, personally witnessed this.

### Volume Five: Strange Arts #### Strange Arts During the Dali period, there was a magician who came from the south and stayed at Zhiqi Temple. He loved wine and rarely sobered up. During a large religious gathering at the temple, with thousands of people present, the magician suddenly said, "I have a trick that can replace the joy of smashing tiles and pearls." He mixed colors in one vessel, performed a series of unclear gestures, and slowly recited several words. Then, he mixed water and repeatedly splashed it on the wall, transforming it into a scene depicting the story of Vimalakirti asking about illness. The five colors were harmoniously displayed, like a newly painted work. After half a day, the colors gradually faded, and by evening they were all gone. Only a flower on the golden silk cap and robe remained for two days. When Zhang Wei was in Sichuan, there was a Buddhist monk named Nanda who had attained the state of being like a magical illusion. He could enter water and fire, pass through metal and stone, and perform infinite transformations. When he first arrived in Sichuan, he traveled with three young nuns. Sometimes, he would be drunk and sing wildly, and the military commander wanted to stop him. When the monk arrived, he said, "I have taken refuge in the monastic life, but I have another form of enjoyment." He pointed to the three nuns and said, "They are skilled in singing and music." The military commander respected him and prepared wine and meat for him. At night, he entertained guests and drank heavily with them. The monk wore a borrowed garment and bought lead and powder (for makeup) and performed with the three nuns. When seated, he looked at them with a smile and engaged in playful banter, his demeanor surpassing the world. When the drinking was about to end, the monk said to the nuns, "You can perform a dance to a certain tune." They slowly entered into a dance, their movements like swirling snow, quickly moving to the rhythm. The performance was unparalleled. After a long time, he shouted, "Women have a wind evil?" Suddenly, he stood up, took the military commander's sword, and everyone thought he was drunk, so they fled in fear.

The monk then drew his sword and struck them, and they all fell to the ground, their blood reaching several zhang in length. The garrison commander was greatly frightened and called for his attendants to bind the monk. The monk laughed and said, "Don't rush." Slowly he raised his staff, which had three branches, and the blood turned out to be wine. He had once attended a drinking party, where he had someone cut off his head and pinned his ears to the pillar, with no blood. Sitting on the seat, when wine arrived, he poured it into the wound. His face turned red while singing, and his hands beat the rhythm. After the gathering, he himself picked up his head and placed it back, with no marks at first. He often predicted people's misfortunes and declines, all of which were riddles, only understood after the events occurred. In Chengdu, a commoner hosted him for several days, but the monk did not want to stay. They closed the gate to keep him, and the monk walked into the corner of the wall. The commoner quickly pulled him, gradually entering, leaving only the corner of his robe, which soon also disappeared. The next day, there was a painted monk on the wall, whose appearance resembled him. Day by day, the color became thinner, and after seven days, only black marks remained. On the eighth day, the marks also disappeared, and the monk was already in Pengzhou. Later, no one knew where he went. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong, the Sushu Langzhong Lu Shao once visited his maternal uncle at Dingshui Temple. Because of the temple monk, he prepared sweet treats and seasonal fruits. A monk from a neighboring temple invited him over. After a while, the monk arrived with a scholar named Li, and they sat in a circle, laughing and chatting merrily. The temple monk asked his disciples to boil new tea, which they circulated, but it did not reach Li the scholar. Lu Shao, feeling unjust, said, "The tea has not yet reached Li the scholar, why is that?" The monk laughed and said, "Such a scholar, does he also need to know the taste of tea?" And he gave him the remaining tea. The monk from the neighboring temple said, "The scholar is a sorcerer, the host cannot speak lightly."

The monk then said, "What do you fear from unruly young men?" The scholar suddenly became angry and replied, "I have never met this monk before, how could I be one of those unruly youths?" The monk retorted loudly, "Those who look at the wine flag and play in the changing fields, could there be any good among them?" The scholar then addressed the guests, "I cannot help but act rashly in front of esteemed guests." He reached into his sleeve, sat on his knees, and shouted at the monk, "You, a monk with poor conduct, how dare you be so disrespectful! Where is your staff? It can be used to strike him." Behind the monk's door was a staff, which suddenly jumped out and struck the monk repeatedly. At the time, the crowd also tried to protect the monk, and the staff seized the opportunity to strike quickly, as if held by something. The scholar then shouted, "Grab this monk and push him against the wall." The monk leaned against the wall with his hands raised, his face turned green and he was out of breath, only saying he begged for mercy. The scholar then said, "Monk, you can go down the steps." The monk hurriedly descended, falling repeatedly, bruising his nose and forehead. The crowd pleaded for him, and the scholar calmly said, "Because of the presence of gentlemen, I cannot kill him as it would be a burden." He then bowed to the guests and left. The monk could not speak for half a day, appearing as if he had been poisoned, and no one could fathom what had happened.

In the final year of the Yuanhe era, Zhang Yan, a foot messenger responsible for managing the city's messengers, was sent to deliver documents to the capital. Upon reaching Song Prefecture, he encountered a man who asked to be his companion. The man suggested that if Zhang allowed him to handle it, he could double his speed. The man then dug two small pits, each about five or six inches deep, and had Zhang stand with his back to them, with his feet hanging over the edge. He pricked both of Zhang's feet. Initially, Zhang did not feel any pain, but after rubbing his feet from the knees down to the soles several times, black blood filled the pits. Zhang suddenly felt his feet become light and agile, and he arrived in Bianzhou by noon. The man then asked Zhang to stay the night in Shaan Prefecture, but Zhang declined, saying he lacked the strength. The man then said, "You can temporarily remove your knee cap, and you will not suffer, allowing you to travel eight hundred miles in a day." Zhang was afraid and refused. The man did not insist and said, "I have something to do and must reach Shaan Prefecture by nightfall." He then left, moving like a flying arrow and disappearing in an instant.

Shu had a man named Fei Ji Shi, whose eyes were red without black pupils, and he was originally from the Pu people. Cheng Shi first met him during the early years of the Changqing era, and he was already over seventy years old. When he would relieve someone of their misfortune, he would always use a chicken to set up an offering in the courtyard, and also take a river stone the size of a chicken egg, making the sick person hold it. Then, he would take steps and make a hollow shout, causing the chicken to spin and die, and the stone to break into four pieces. Cheng Shi's old family member Yong'an initially did not believe it, and once said to him, "You are in misfortune." Because of this, he took a small talisman and forced him to swallow it. He then removed the left foot's shoes and socks, and the talisman unfolded on the sole of the foot. He also told his servant Cang Hai, "You will fall ill." He made him bare his chest and face the door, repeatedly drawing outside the door with a pen, and shouted loudly, "Go! Go!" The ink then penetrated his back. There was a monk at the Changshou Temple named Yan Sheng, who once vowed to be in Mount Heng. A villager was bitten by a poisonous snake and died shortly after, with his hair loosening and swelling up more than a foot. His son said, "If Zan were here, what would we worry about!" So they welcomed Zan. He then used ashes to surround the body, opened four doors, and first said, "If it enters from the feet, it cannot be saved." He then took steps and held firmly, but the snake did not come for a long time. Zan became very angry and took several liters of rice, crushing it into the shape of a snake and cursing it. Suddenly, it moved slowly and went out the door. After a while, the rice snake led another snake from the dead person's head, straight into the wound, and the body gradually lowered. The snake's pouch shrank and it died, and the villager then lived.

Wang Qian was stationed in Jingzhou, where a commoner named Zhang Qizheng was skilled in treating injuries and fractures. A military man suffered a leg injury and sought Zhang's help. Zhang treated him with medicinal wine, removed a piece of broken bone as large as two fingers, applied ointment, and bandaged the wound. After a few days, the injury was as good as new. Two years later, the leg suddenly began to hurt again, and the man returned to Zhang for treatment. Zhang explained that the pain was caused by the cold and advised him to search for the bone he had previously removed. The bone was indeed found under the bed. Zhang instructed him to wash the bone in hot water and store it in cotton, and the pain immediately subsided. Wang Qian's sons and younger brothers often played with Zhang and once asked him to perform a trick. Zhang took a handful of horse grass, rubbed it several times, and it turned into dozens of flying fireflies. He also drew a woman on the wall, filled a cup with wine, and drank it, leaving no drop behind. Soon after, the face of the drawn woman turned red, and after half a day, she gradually disappeared, wet and falling apart. Zhang's magical techniques were never passed down to others. Han was stationed in Guilin, where there was a sorcerer named Fei Ying who could create fog over several miles. Previously, while walking in the wild, he saw dozens of yellow butterflies and followed them until they disappeared at the base of a large tree. He dug and found a stone box containing a large, arm-sized scroll of white writing. He thus became a follower of the left path. The common people flocked to him like a market, and he declared that on a certain day he would capture Guilin. If there was purple vapor, he would surely win. On that day, purple vapor appeared like a bolt of silk, stretching from the mountains to the city. A white vapor directly counteracted it, and the purple vapor dissipated. Suddenly, a heavy fog descended, and by noon, it began to clear. Small copper statues of Buddha, each as big as a wheat grain, began to fall from the trees in the state residence, and their number was unknown. That year, Han died.

Wei Fu, the Military Governor of Haizhou, once traveled to Jiaxing, where he met a monk named Xidun on the road. The monk was proficient in the art of health preservation and also skilled in using the calendar, which could substitute for medicine. Seeing Wei Fu plucking white hairs, the monk said, "I will choose a day for you to pluck them." After five or six days, the monk asked to pluck half of them, and when they grew back, the color was like that. After plucking three times, the temples did not change color again. There were guests who asked to have their hair plucked, but the monk said the timing was slightly off. After parting, the mustache color indeed turned green. Such is his skill. Many people say Shi has extraordinary skills, and he was in Yangzhou. Cheng Shi often met with him every ten days for several years, but he rarely predicted events correctly. Family members with headaches, sneezing, and coughing, who took his medicine, never found it effective. By the early Kai Cheng period, among acquaintances and relatives in the city, people often said Shi's skills were unpredictable. It was widely rumored during the Bao Li period that Shi accompanied Chancellor Qian Hui to Huzhou, where he often stayed in the school, and the students called him "Wen Zhang." He asked the brothers Qian for rabbit soup and pancakes, but it was the hot month, and the hunter took several days to catch the rabbit. He then ate with the students and laughed, saying, "We can keep the rabbit skin to commemorate an event." He then nailed the skin to the ground, surrounded it with trenches, and painted a red talisman on it, saying alone, "Regretful, regretful." The brothers Qian questioned him, and Shi said, "I want to share this with all of you as a record of the year of the rabbit." By the ninth year of Tai He, Qian Ke Fu was killed in Fengxiang, and the year was the year of the rabbit (Yi Mao).

During the reign of Emperor Xian (184-189 CE), a scholar from the Jianghuai region named Wang Qiong was once at the home of Duan Junxiu. He instructed his guests to take a tile, draw a tortoise shell on it, and keep it. After a meal, when they took it out, it had transformed into a real tortoise. They released it into the courtyard, where it walked along the wall, and by the next morning, it had returned to being a tile. They also took a flower that was silent, sealed it in a secret vessel, and by the next night, it had bloomed. A person from Jiangxi was skilled in manipulating bamboo, able to create utensils from several joints. Another person could transform a gourd into a bear, and it was said that turning a gourd was easier than turning a ball. For repelling mice, a ritual was performed over seven days. Nine mice were placed in a cage and buried in the ground. Nine hundred catties of earth were weighed and used to cover the pit, each two feet five inches deep, and compacted to make it solid. According to the *杂五行书* (Miscellaneous Book of the Five Elements): "Using the soil from the station's ground to coat the stove, water, fire, thieves, and robbers will not pass through; coating the corners of the house, mice will not eat silkworms; coating the granary, mice will not eat grain; using to block the hole, all mice will be exterminated." Yong Yijian said: "There is a spell for the night, holding it brings merit. It can protect against fear and bad dreams when walking at night or sleeping. The spell is ‘Basha Banyan Di’." Song Jushi said: "There is a spell for throwing dice, ‘Yidi Midi Mie Lao Di’. Recite it ten thousand times, and the outcome will follow the call."

The Well of Yun'an, originating from a branch of the Yangtze River, stretches for thirty miles. Near the well, the water is clear as a mirror, and boats are safe. However, fifteen miles closer to the river, the area is fraught with dangerous rocks and rapids, making navigation difficult. Celestial Master Zhai Gan, moved by the hardships endured by merchants and travelers, established an altar on Han Cheng Mountain to perform rituals of examination and summoning, commanding the dragons to transform the river. In fourteen locations, the dragons transformed into elderly men, responding to the summons. Master Gan advised them about the perilous nature of the rapids, which harmed people and property, and instructed them to make the riverbed level. Within a single night, thunder and lightning struck, and the fourteen-mile stretch of rapids became a calm expanse. Only one section remained unchanged, and the dragon did not appear. Master Gan then issued strict orders to the divine officials to pursue the dragon. After three more days, a woman arrived. He questioned her for not responding to the summons. The woman replied, "I did not come because I wished to assist Master Gan in his efforts to benefit all beings. The wealthy merchants and traders have ample resources, while those who carry goods have little strength. Many poor people in Yun'an transport goods from the river mouth to the well near the river to support themselves. If the river becomes easy to navigate, making it safe for boats, the poor people of the county will lose their means of earning a living and their source of food. Many will suffer as a result. I would rather have the dangerous rapids to support those who carry goods than to benefit the wealthy merchants. This is why I did not come." Master Gan found her words wise and allowed the dragons to return to their original state. The wind and thunder ceased, and the long rapids returned to their former condition. During the Tianbao era, he was summoned to the capital and received abundant favor and kindness. After more than a year, he returned to his mountain, where he eventually attained the Dao and departed.

Emperor Xuanzong summoned Yi Xing and asked him, "What are your abilities?" Yi Xing replied, "I am only good at remembering and reading." Emperor Xuanzong then ordered the imperial harem to fetch the records of the concubines and show them to Yi Xing. After reviewing the records thoroughly, Yi Xing checked back to the original and memorized them with great proficiency, as if he had been accustomed to reading them. After several pages, Emperor Xuanzong, without realizing it, descended from his throne and performed a ritual of respect, calling Yi Xing a sage. Previously, Yi Xing had become a disciple of Puji, serving him at Mount Song. Yi Xing once set up a meal at the temple, holding a large assembly of monks and hermits, many of whom lived hundreds of miles away, all arriving as scheduled, gathering nearly a thousand people. At that time, there was Lu Hong, a man of high virtue and great learning, who lived in seclusion at Mount Song. Yi Xing asked Lu Hong to write a eulogy to praise the gathering. On the appointed day, Lu Hong brought the eulogy to the temple, and Yi Xing's teacher received it and placed it on the desk.

Once the bell and drum sounds were made, Hong requested Puji, saying, "I have written several thousand words, and the characters are obscure while the language is strange. Why not select from among the monks someone who is intelligent and insightful? I shall personally teach them." He then ordered the summoning of Yihang. Upon his arrival, Yihang spread out the paper, smiled, and only after a glance placed it back on the table. Hong underestimated his carelessness and secretly found it strange. Soon after, the monks gathered in the hall. Yihang unfolded his sleeves and stepped forward, raising his voice to dictate, not forgetting a single word. Hong was astonished for a long time and said to Puji, "This is not something you can teach; I should follow him in learning." Yihang then thoroughly studied the *I Ching*, and from that point on, he sought out teachers without hesitation, traveling thousands of miles. Once, he arrived at the Guoqing Temple in Tiantai, where he saw a courtyard with ancient pines several dozen steps away and flowing water at the gate. Yihang stood between the gate and the screen, hearing a monk in the courtyard arranging calculations, the sound soft and continuous. Soon after, he told his disciples, "Today, a disciple will seek my method of calculation, and he should already be at the door. Is there no one who told him?" He immediately removed one calculation and then said, "The water in front of the door should flow westward; the disciple will arrive."

Yi Xing entered upon hearing the words, bowed deeply to request the teachings, and fully accepted the methods. The water that used to flow east near the temple gate has suddenly changed to flow west. Xing Hepu once said to Yin Xi, "Is Yi Xing a sage? During the Han Dynasty, Luoxia Hong created the Da Yan Li calendar, predicting that after eight hundred years there would be a one-day error, and a sage would correct it. This year marks the end of that period. And now Yi Xing has created the Da Yan Li calendar, exactly at the point of error, confirming Luoxia Hong's prophecy." Yi Xing also once visited the Taoist priest Yin Chong to borrow Yang Xiong's "Tai Xuan Jing." After several days, he returned to give back the book. Yin Chong said, "This book has deep and profound meaning. I have studied it for many years but still cannot understand it. Why do you return it so quickly?" Yi Xing replied, "I have fully understood its meaning." He then took out his own work, the "Tai Yan Xuan Tu" and the "Yi Jue" in one volume, to show to Yin Chong. Yin Chong greatly admired and praised him, saying, "This young person is like Yan Hui."

By the end of the Kaiyuan era, Pei Kuan served as the Governor of Henan and deeply believed in Buddhism. He served as a disciple to Master Puji, visiting him every day and evening. One day, Pei Kuan went to see Master Puji, who said, "I have a small matter at hand and no time for doubt or discussion. Please wait and rest for a moment." Pei Kuan then held his breath and rested in an empty room. He saw Master Puji in his clean and tidy hall, burning incense and sitting upright. After sitting for a while, he suddenly heard a knock on the door, followed by a voice saying, "The Master Yixing has arrived." Master Yixing entered, paid his respects to Master Puji, and then whispered something into his ear. His demeanor was extremely respectful, and he said on his forehead that there was nothing impossible. After finishing speaking, he bowed, and after the bow, he spoke again. This happened three times, and Master Puji only said, "Yes, yes," with nothing being impossible.
