After Master Yixing finished speaking, he descended the steps and entered the southern room, closing the door himself. Master Puji then slowly instructed his disciples, "Send the bell, Master Yixing has attained Nirvana." The disciples rushed to see, and Master Yixing indeed had attained Nirvana as he had said. Later, Pei Kuan wore mourning clothes and buried him, walking out of the city to see him off. At the end of the Tianbao era, the sorcerer Qian Zhimei once came to Luoyang and posted a sign on the pillars of the Tianjin Bridge to sell divination. For a month, no one came to consult him. One day, a nobleman thought he must be extraordinary and ordered the amount of silk as requested for a divination. Qian Zhimei cast the divination and said, "My divination can predict a lifetime, why are you playing around?" The man replied, "The divination is very urgent, could you be mistaken?" Qian Zhimei said, "Please listen to this rhyme: 'Two heads and two feet, a central void. People tread, but they won't pay.'" The man originally intended to deceive him by selling the Tianjin Bridge. Such was his skill.

Chapter Six: Extraordinary Skills In the Southern Dynasties, there was an elderly woman skilled in making brushes, often used by Xiao Ziyun for his writings. The core of her brushes was made from fetal hair. During the Kaiyuan era, a brushmaker named Tietao was renowned for polishing the bamboo tubes to a jade-like smoothness, a technique he never passed on to others. In the side courtyard of the Baixiang Temple in Chengdu, there was a small hall containing a statue of the Bodhi tree. The statue remained as clean as if newly made, with no dust settling on it. It is said that when the statue was first created, the craftsmen first prepared the five internal organs according to the blueprint, then the limbs and joints. After over a hundred years, not a speck of dust had accumulated on it. Li Shu Zhan once befriended a man from Fanyang, who stayed at his private residence. This man was always accurate in predicting good or bad omens and was also skilled in divination and charms. After staying for only half a year, he suddenly told Li, "I have a special skill I will demonstrate before I leave, it is called water painting." He then asked for the back hall to be dug into a pool, one square meter in size and about one foot deep, and plastered with a mixture of hemp and lime. At sunset, the pool was filled with water. He waited for the water to remain undiminished, then prepared red and blue pigments, ink, and an inkstone. First, he took up the brush, bit his teeth for a long time, and then released the brush hairs onto the water. Upon closer inspection, the water looked murky. After two days, he used four pieces of fine silk to lift out the painting. In a short while, he revealed a complete image of ancient pines, strange rocks, people, houses, and trees. Li was amazed and persistently questioned him, but he only said he was skilled in preventing the colors from sinking and dispersing.

Ancient records speak of the game of 区 causing people to part, or say that there is evidence in ancient language. Gao Ying, a candidate, was skilled in the game with good intention and skill. Chengshi once played the game of 区 in Jingzhou, where in each group of more than fifty people, he hit nine out of ten times. The hooks in the same group also knew their places, and at the time, people suspected he had some other skill. Upon inquiry, it was found that Gao Ying's words, gestures, and expressions were like those of a detective examining a prisoner or a thief. Shi Sheng, a recluse, was especially skilled in the game of 区, and he was friends with Zhang Youxin and his brothers. On a leisurely night, he invited guests and tested their skills in the game of 区, and he always hit the target. Zhang placed the hooks in the folds of his headscarf and said, "Show me empty fists." After a while, the hooks were found in the left wing of Zhang's headscarf. Such is his skill. Shi later lived in Yangzhou, and Chengshi met him and asked for his skill. Shi told Chengshi, "First, draw dozens of heads, and if you can make them look different, I will teach you." Chengshi doubted he was being deceived and never got around to drawing them.

**Chapter Six: Wondrous Objects** During the Kaiyuan era, Chen Qing, a fierce and decisive military leader from the Hexi region, was feared by the Western Rong, who repeatedly invaded the border annually. In battle, Chen Qing would wave his arms and shout loudly, carrying the heads of the enemy and returning, never hit by arrows or bullets. The Western Rong feared him, and the whole army began to rely on him. Later, Tubo captured thousands of prisoners of war. The general ordered an interpreter to ask the one wearing the skin of a large beast, "Why could you not harm Chen Qing?" The prisoner replied, "I once saw a blue dragon charge into the formation. Wherever the weapons struck, it felt like hitting copper or iron. I am a spirit assisting the general." Chen Qing then realized the hook had a spirit. After Chen Qing's death, the hook was obtained by Li Guangchen, the governor of Guazhou. Sometimes after rain or wind, the hook emitted light from the room, illuminating a square area. General Geshu of the Western Region knew about it and asked to exchange it for another treasure. Li Guangchen did not give it to him and instead gave a poem: "Carve the boat to seek transformation, Play the sword to repay kindness."

When Zheng Yun Da was young, he obtained a sword with scales on the hilt and stars on the guard, which would occasionally roar. He often played with it on his knees in his tranquil dwelling on sunny days. Suddenly, a person descended from a tree in the courtyard, dressed in purple and red, with curly hair, holding a sword, and surrounded by a black aura that appeared like dense fog. Zheng, known for his courage, pretended not to see him. The person then said, "I am from the celestial realm and know that you possess an unusual sword. I wish to borrow it for a look." Zheng replied, "This is just ordinary iron, not worthy of your attention. How could the celestial realm need such a thing?" The person continued to request it. After waiting for a long time, Zheng seized the opportunity and quickly rose to strike at him, but missed. Suddenly, the black aura fell to the ground and took several days to dissipate. Wen Jie, an acquaintance of Cheng Shi, recounted: "During the Dali era, Zhang Cun, a commoner from Gao You, made a living digging up lotus roots. Once, while working in a marsh, he found a dry lotus root with a stem as thick as an arm. They worked together to dig it up, and it turned out to be so large that it could be embraced by both arms. Unable to continue digging due to its size, they cut it off. Inside, they found a sword two feet long, green in color and without a blade. Zhang did not treasure it. The townspeople who knew about it obtained it for ten bundles of firewood. The lotus root had no threads."

At the end of Emperor Yuan's reign, in the courtyard of Xia Wei Yi in Haiting, a lily flower grew that was several times larger than usual, which was considered extraordinary. Upon digging beneath it, thirteen layers of brick boxes were found, each containing a mirror. The seventh mirror did not tarnish and, when reflecting the sun, cast a halo one zhang in diameter, while the others were merely copper discs in shape. High-ranking official Gao was in Cai Prefecture, where military officer Tian Zhihui had exchanged for a debt of several million coins. Tian returned to an outer county, more than three hundred li away from the prefecture, when Gao ordered him to be imprisoned and investigated. Distressed and with no solution, Tian's peers arranged a banquet to comfort him. Among the ten or so guests was a recluse named Huangfu Xuanzhen, who wore white clothes like goose feathers and had a very elegant appearance. Everyone offered words of comfort and encouragement, but Huangfu merely smiled and said, "This is also a minor matter." After the guests dispersed, he stayed behind and told Tian, "You once traveled to the Eastern Seas and obtained two precious items; I will help you resolve this difficulty." Tian thanked him and asked him to prepare vehicles and horses. Huangfu declined, saying he would travel quickly. That evening, he arrived in the prefecture, stayed in an inn, and visited Gao the next morning. Gao, upon seeing him, unconsciously respected him. Huangfu then asked Gao, "I came especially to beg for Tian's life from the Shangshu." Gao hastily replied, "Tian owes the state money, not my private funds; what can be done?" Huangfu requested that the attendants leave and said, "I obtained a headscarf in Silla that repels dust; I wish to offer it to redeem Tian." He immediately took it out of his chest and gave it to Gao.

High Talent, feeling a hollow and cool sensation in his body, was startled and said, "This is not something a minister should possess, and it is priceless. The life of the fields, I fear, is not enough to repay it." Huangfu requested to try it. The next day, they held a banquet outside the city. At that time, there had been a long drought, and the dust was thick. Gao looked at the horses' tails and manes, as well as several attendants, and found that none of them were covered in dust. The military governor noticed and asked Gao, "Why is it that only the Shangshu is not covered in dust? Have you encountered a strange person and obtained a priceless treasure?" Gao could not hide it. The military governor was displeased and insisted on meeting the recluse. Gao then went with him. The military governor joked, "Does the Daoist know only the Shangshu? What other treasures do you have, just for a look?" Huangfu explained the intention of saving the fields and mentioned that the medicine came from the East Sea. Now there is only one needle left, which is not as powerful as a cloth, but it can make one's entire body dust-free. The military governor bowed and requested, "To obtain this is enough." Huangfu took out the needle from his headband and gave it to him. The needle was golden in color and as large as a sewing needle. The military governor then tested it on his headband and ran through the dust, but the dust only reached the horses' tails. Gao and the military governor paid daily respects, intending to seek the essence of the Dao. One night, the Daoist suddenly disappeared without a trace.

In the palace of Xianyang, there were twelve bronze statues, each three to five feet tall, arranged on a platform. Each statue held a musical instrument—qin (zither), zhù (a stringed instrument), sheng (free reed mouth organ), and yú (a type of flute)—and was adorned with decorative ribbons, sashes, and colorful patterns, making them appear lifelike. Below the platform, there were copper pipes with spouts several feet high. One of the pipes was hollow and contained a rope as thick as a finger. When a person blew into the hollow pipe and manipulated the rope, the qin, sheng, and other instruments played, producing music indistinguishable from real music. There was also a qin, six feet long, with thirteen strings and twenty-six tuning pegs, all decorated with precious stones, inscribed with the words "之乐." Additionally, there was a jade flute, two feet three inches long, with twenty-six holes. When blown, it created the illusion of carriages and horses emerging from the mountains and forests, moving in sequence, and disappearing when the sound ceased, inscribed with the words "昭华之管."

Prince Yong of Gaoyang had a concubine named Xu Yuehua, who was skilled at playing the horizontal konghou and could perform the tune of "Wang Zhaojun Going Beyond the Frontier." There was also a man named Tian Shengchao, who could play the songs "Song of the Brave Warrior" and "Xiang Yu's Lament" on the horn. When General Cui Yanbo led his army into battle, he would often have Tian Shengchao play the song of the brave warrior before charging alone into the enemy's formation. The ancient pipa used chicken gut strings. During the Kaiyuan era, Duan Shi was skilled at playing the pipa with animal gut strings. He Zhai, however, played it with a broken plectrum and could not produce a sound. General Huangfu Zhi of Shu could discern musical notes and, by striking pottery, could tell the time and month. He also enjoyed playing the pipa. During the Yuanhe era, he once composed a tune and played it by a water pool to take a cool. Originally in the key of Huangzhong, the sound unexpectedly shifted to the key of Ruibin. Even after changing the string and playing it several times, the sound still remained in the key of Ruibin. Zheng was very puzzled, unhappy, and thought it was an omen of bad luck. The next day, he played it again by the water pool, and the sound was the same as before. When he tried playing it in another place, it was in the key of Huangzhong. Zheng then tuned it to the key of Ruibin and played it again by the water pool at night. He noticed waves near the shore and something stirring the water like a fish jumping. When he lowered the string, it disappeared. Zheng then gathered people to drain the water from the pool and searched it thoroughly. After several days, the mud was over a meter deep, and they found a piece of iron, which was the iron of the Ruibin stone chime. As for Wang Yi, he had never learned to play stringed instruments. One day, he suddenly fell asleep and woke up at night, playing the pipa and composing several tunes. One was called "The Sparrow and the Snake," another "The Tune of the Hu King," and a third "The Garden of the Hu Melon." No one recognized these tunes, but when they heard them, no one could help but shed tears. His sister asked him to teach her, and he taught her a few notes, but soon afterward, she forgot everything, and the tunes were never completed.

Someone made a flute from a monkey's arm bone and played it, and the sound was clear and round, surpassing that of string and wind instruments. The zither has a spiritual energy. A person knowledgeable in one way can tell the fortune from the zither. ● Chapter Seven: Wine and Food Jia Jiang of Wei, a wealthy man with a fortune of thousands of gold, was well-learned and skilled in writing. He had a servant who was skilled in distinguishing different types of water. He often sent him on a small boat in the Yellow River to collect water from the source using gourds. He collected no more than seven or eight liters in a day. After being left overnight, the water turned red like crimson. He used it to make wine, which was called Kunlun Wine. The wine's fragrance was unparalleled in the world. He once presented thirty shi to Emperor Zhuang of Wei. In the north of Licheng, there was a forest known as the "Forest of the Magistrate." During the Zhengshi era, Zheng Gongqu led his guests to the forest during the hottest period of the year to escape the heat. They took large lotus leaves and placed them on the inkstone tray, filling them with two liters of wine. They pierced the lotus leaves with a hairpin to create a passage to the stem, bending the stem to form a spiral shape like an elephant's trunk. They passed the wine through the stem and named it "Green Lotus Cup." People in Lixia learned this method, saying that the wine's flavor was mixed with the fragrance of lotus, and its cold, fragrant taste surpassed that of water. The Qingtian nut, whose shape is unknown, is large like a six-liter gourd. When water is poured into it, it turns into wine in a short time. It is also called the Qingtian Pot or Qingtian Wine. The Later Shu emperor had two peach pits. Each pit could hold about five liters of water. After a long time, the water turned into wine, intoxicating the drinker. They alternated storing water in the pits to provide for their feasts. It is unknown where they obtained the pits.

Tian Qiang, a chieftain of the Wuxi region, sent his eldest son Lu to occupy the upper city, his second son Yu to the middle city, and his youngest son Cang to the lower city. The three fortifications were arranged in succession (one of which was a lookout tower) to resist Wang Mang. In the 24th year of Emperor Guangwu's reign, the Wuwei General Liu Shang was dispatched to campaign against them. Before Liu Shang arrived, Cang caught a white turtle and prepared it as food, raising a beacon fire to summon his two elder brothers. When they arrived, there was no immediate threat. However, when Liu Shang's army came, Cang raised another fire, but Lu and others thought it was false, and Cang subsequently fought and died. Liu Xiao Yi, a person from the Liang dynasty, was eating pickled fish and said, "The Five Hegemons and Nine Earls should all be summoned." Cui and Li Qian were present at the gathering. Cui said, "The duties of the Censor-in-Chief have not yet been divided." Li Qian replied, "If so, the Censor-in-Chief's extensive travels should reach Mulung." Liu Xiao Yi said, "Deer tail from Ye is the best among food and drink." Cui said, "Raw fish and bear paws are praised by Mencius. Chicken feet and ape lips are favored by the Lü family. Deer tail is indeed a delicacy, yet it is not recorded in books, often causing wonder." Liu Xiao Yi said, "Indeed, it is like this, or perhaps it is due to different preferences in ancient and modern times." Liang's He Ji said, "The crab roe from Qingzhou is recorded by the Zheng family, but this item is not mentioned, I do not understand why." Li Qian said, "Zheng also mentions deer from Yizhou, but it is not considered a delicacy."

He Yin was excessive in his tastes, and his meals were always laden with an abundance of dishes. Gradually, he wanted to reduce his excesses, but he still ate white fish, 旦腊, and sweet crabs. He had his disciples discuss this, and the scholar Zhong Wu commented: "The process of drying fish (旦腊) involves quick bending and stretching, while the preparation of sweet crabs causes them to become even more agitated. A benevolent person's intention is deeply compassionate. As for scallops and oysters, lacking eyebrows and eyes, they gradually become strange like chaos. Their lips and mouths are sealed, not as cautious as a golden person. They neither flourish nor wither, even less than plants, and neither fragrant nor foul-smelling, what is different from rubble? Therefore, they should always be part of the kitchen and forever a source of food." Wei Lin, a person from Jingzhao, migrated south to Xiangyang during the Tianbao period. He was a舍人, known for his broad range of knowledge and literary talent, and was good at engaging in lively conversation. He wrote a document titled《旦表》，which discussed the preparation of 旦腊 and criticized the practices of the time.

The minister said: "I, Dawn, respectfully state: I have seen the imperial decree, which appoints me as General of the Zong (or San) Oven, Officer of the Oil Steamed Hand, and Governor of Huo State, with my previous duties remaining unchanged. I humbly accept the imperial command and remain silent. Standing by the pot, I am cautious and vigilant. My beauty is ashamed of the summer eel, and my taste is inferior to the winter carp; I always bear the ridicule of old age and fear the criticism of being out of place. Therefore, I rinse at the bottom of the lake and rest on stones and mud, but unexpectedly, I receive special favor and am lifted up. I have risen above the embroidered seat and am unworthy to be included in the jade dish. Far from the precious banquet, I receive the ivory chopsticks; blessed by the purple tent, mercy is extended to the yellow belly. Now, the ginger and pepper stir, and the fragrance of scallions and garlic fills the air. When the light ladle is just moved, the main dish is like smoke; when the thick broth is paused, the fragrant dishes are arranged in rows. Wandering in the green paste and roaming in the red lips, I repay the imperial grace and am willing to die nine times. Unable to bear the sincerity of being in a state of anxiety, I respectfully list the copper spear gate and present this memorial." The imperial edict replied: "After reviewing the memorial, I know that you, like a gentleman in the pond, are among the talented and refined, and are well-known for your skills and virtues, truly deserving of this selection, no need to thank."

I, Yi Yin, served King Tang and spoke of how the emperor could possess three groups of creatures: those living in water, those eating meat, and those eating grass. He said that aquatic creatures have a fishy smell, meat-eating creatures have a stinky odor, and herbivores have a strong aroma. The five flavors, three materials, nine boils, nine transformations, three types of vegetables, and seven types of pickled vegetables include prepared sour foods, cheese from Chu, sauce made from peony, autumn yellow celery, young plants from Chu, ground grain, and bitter mountain skin. Sweet but not overly sweet, sour but not overly sour, salty but not too salty, spicy but not too spicy, bland but not too bland, fatty but not overly fatty.
Xinlun, roasted meat, possibly from a specific animal, though the exact animal is unclear due to the unusual character;

A cake is called "tuò," or "zhǎng hún." Syrup is called "yàn" (or "zhuī"). Satisfied food is called "zhě" (or "mèi"). Non-food "zhà gǔ" (where "gǔ" is originally two characters, both from the fish radical) is food that is chewed and eaten. Membrane (or "mǐn"), "xī," "yǔ," "zhàng," "fān" are all types of meat. "Měi" and "ruò" are membranes. "Jùn" and "bēn" are one type of offering. "Yuán" is "huò." "Gé," "xǔ," "fú," "yī" are types of "sǎn." "Huá" (or "huà"), "qí," "zǎi," "liáo," "yuán" are types of "ěr." "Cān," "qiān," "sī," "lè" are types of "pēi." "Lào," "yǐ," "chún" are types of "jiāng." "[A17N]," "yǎn," "yú," "zuì;" "xī" are types of "jiàng."

Method for Preparing Rice:** Take a winnowing basket and pour in one shi of rice, then add five dou of rice flour and pound it. Rice flour can make the aroma more fragrant. **Method for Cooking Lamb:** Use a piece of betel nut that is one inch wide and one and a half inches long, and wrap it in triticale flour. **Method for Preparing Carp and Crucian Carp:** Place the heads of bamboo branches in the sun in sequence, and write the character "赍" on them. **Method for Making Multicolored Cakes:** Carve wooden lotus flowers and use animal shapes as molds. Stack the colors vertically in a container, named "斗钉." All colors are made with sugar and honey. **Method for Preparing a Dish:** Use soup-making, sand chess, and sweet-making methods. **Method for Preparing Turnips:** For turnips that have been exposed to frost, dig them up with closed eyes and cut them into thin slices. **Method for Making Steamed Buns:** Use a large amount of flour (one shi) and refined pig fat (three he). **Methods for Preparing Pears, Meat, and Catfish:** Prepare pears, meat, and catfish using specific methods. **Method for Preparing the Head of a Calf:** Remove the moon bone, which is near the base of the tongue and shaped like the moon. **Method for Preparing Mushrooms:** Cut cucumbers with a bone knife and prepare bean sprouts. **Method for Making Lung Cakes and Preparing Liver:** Prepare liver like fish paste. **Method for Preparing Pickled Vegetables:** Prepare pickled vegetables and remove them using a specific method (also called "升"). **Method for Preparing Carp and Crucian Carp:** Use a carp one foot long and a crucian carp eight inches long, remove the fins and gills. The meat of the crucian carp is behind the gills and in front of the head. Use the fatty part of the fish to clean the knife, and also use fish brain. All can make the threads not stick to the knife. **Method for Preparing Fish Meat:** Make sour fish meat using crucian carp, white carp, bream, mandarin fish, and other fish. Cook donkey and horse meat to enhance the flavor. Donkey meat is pronounced like "鲈" (mandarin fish) and "贮" (store). **Method for Roasting Meat:** The best is 宾 fish, followed by white fish, and the taste of the previous day.

Among the famous dishes of the gentry, there is Xiao Family's wontons, whose broth can be strained and used to brew tea; there is also Geng Family's zongzi, which is as white and lustrous as jade; Han Yue was able to make cherry preserves, which retained their color; there were also dishes like cold hùtǔ, black fish fillet, steamed vegetables, and herb-flavored flatbread. General Qu Lianghan was skilled in roasting the hump of a camel. During the reign of Emperor Dezong, there was a general's household that served meals, and they often said that nothing is inedible, but it depends on the cooking technique and the balance of the five flavors. They would sometimes prepare a dish from a deer (or possibly a wild animal), and the flavor was exceptionally good. Daoist Chen Jingsi mentioned that the imperial messenger Qi Risheng cultivated cherries, and by mid-May, the cherries had wrinkled skins like large persimmons and did not fall off, with a flavor that was several times more intense. People could not fathom the method he used. Chapter Seven: Medicine To the east of Lu City lies the tomb of Bian Que, said to be the resting place of a physician from the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period, who was skilled in acupuncture and medicine. People would pray to him with wine and wax, and he was known as the "Lu Physician." During the state of Wei, there was a guest from Goguryeo who was skilled in using needles. He would take a strand of hair, cut it into over ten pieces, and thread them onto a needle, saying that this demonstrated the emptiness within the hair. His skill was truly remarkable.

Wang Xuanrong captured the King of Central India, Alarnasun, and presented him to the court. He also obtained the magician Narayana (with an optional "娑" character), who claimed to live for two hundred years. Emperor Taizong found this extraordinary and housed him within the Golden Wind Gate. They prepared an elixir of longevity, supervised by the Minister of War, Cui Dunli. The magician said that in the Brahman country, there was a drug called Pancha-juqu water, which emerged from a stone mortar in a great mountain. It had seven colors, some hot and some cold, and could dissolve grass, wood, metal, and iron. If a person's hand entered it, it would dissolve and rot. To obtain the water, a camel skull was sunk into the stone mortar, and the water was then transferred to a gourd. Whenever this water was present, a stone pillar resembling a human form would guard it. If anyone from the mountains revealed the secret of this water, they would die. There was also a drug called Julaelo, located in a high mountain rock. Inside the mountain, there was a stone hole, and in front of the hole, there was a tree that looked like a mulberry tree. A large poisonous snake guarded the hole. To obtain the leaves, one shot a large arrow at the branches, and when the leaves fell, black birds would fly away. Then, many arrows were shot at the birds to collect the leaves. The magician later died in Chang'an. A Taoist from Jing named Wang Yanbo, by nature skilled in medicine, could particularly distinguish the pulse and predict life, death, and longevity with absolute accuracy. Pei Zou's son suddenly fell ill, and all the doctors were at a loss. Someone suggested Wang Yanbo, and he was quickly summoned to examine the patient. After examining the pulse for a long time, Wang Yanbo said, "There is no disease." He then boiled a few herbs, and the patient recovered upon drinking them. Pei Zou asked about the condition, and Wang Yanbo replied, "He was poisoned by a barbless carp." His son had fallen ill because of this. Pei Zou initially did not believe him, so he had barbless carp filleted and served to his attendants. They all showed the same symptoms, and Pei Zou was greatly astonished.

Liu Fang served as a Langzhong when his son Deng was gravely ill. At that time, the famous doctor Zhang Fangfu had just been appointed to Sizhou and was an old acquaintance of Fang. Fang congratulated him and told him about his son's illness, expressing his reliance on Zhang's expertise. The next day, Zhang visited Fang, who hurriedly led him to see Deng. From a distance, Zhang saw Deng's skull and said, "With this skull bone, there's nothing to worry about." He then examined Deng's pulse for five breaths and said, "It's correct; he will live past eighty." Zhang left Fang with some instructions and told Deng, "Even without taking this medicine, you will be fine." Later, Deng became a commoner and lived to ninety before he died. **Chapter Eight: Tattooing** The wicked youths of the capital city were often shaved and tattooed, displaying various shapes and patterns. They wielded weapons and forcefully robbed others, sometimes gathering at wine shops with snakes or grabbing the sheep's stomach to strike people. Now, the Governor of Jingzhao, Xue Gong, reported to the emperor, ordering the village head to secretly patrol. They arrested about three thousand people and had them all beaten to death, displaying their bodies in the market. People in the market with blue tattoos were also burned to remove them. At that time, a strong man named Zhang from Danning坊 had tattoos on his left arm that read, "I fear no Governor of Jingzhao," and on his right arm, "I fear no Yama." Another man, Wang Liniu, spent five thousand coins to have a tattoo artist depict a mountain pavilion on his chest and abdomen, complete with pavilions, ponds, trees, grass, birds, and animals, all as finely detailed as a painting. The governor had them all beaten to death.

A criminal named Zhao Wujian had tattoos on his body, including 116 characters and images such as "fan yin pan que" (a type of bird), and phrases on his arms. The phrases read: "A wild duck rests on a sandbank, hunted by a hawk every day. Suddenly it flies into the water, saving its life until the present day." In Gao Ling County, a person named Song Yuanwu, who had tattoos on his body, was caught. He had 71 tattoos, with one on his left arm saying: "In the past, when our family was not poor, we spent money and goods to make friends. Now, after losing our way, we search for true friends but find none, having traveled through many mountains and passes." On his right arm, he had a tattoo of a gourd with a human head, resembling the figure of Guo Gong from puppet shows. The county official did not understand the meaning of the tattoo and asked him about it, to which he replied that it represented a gourd spirit. Li Yijian, who was in Shu during the late Yuanhe period, heard about Zhao Gao, a person from the market in Shu who enjoyed fighting. Zhao Gao frequently entered prison, with tattoos of the Vaisravana Heavenly King covering his back. Officials intended to whip him but stopped upon seeing the tattoos. Because of these tattoos, Zhao Gao became a pest in the market and alleys. The officials reported this to Li Yijian, who became very angry and captured Zhao Gao, bringing him to the hall. Li Yijian ordered a new bamboo stick, 3 inches in diameter at the top, and ordered the guards to beat the tattoos of the Vaisravana Heavenly King until they were gone, striking more than 30 times without stopping. After ten days, Zhao Gao walked around naked, calling out and asking for money to repair his good deeds.

A minor general from Shu, Wei Shaoqing, was the elder brother of Wei Biao. He did not like reading but enjoyed tattooing. His uncle once asked him to take off his clothes to see the tattoos, and he saw a tree on his chest with dozens of birds perched on it. At the base of the tree was a mirror, suspended by a string, with someone pulling it from the side. The uncle did not understand and asked about it. Wei Shaoqing smiled and said, "Uncle, have you read Zhang Yan's poem? It says, 'Cold crows gather around a mirror being pulled.'" A street performer from Jingzhou, Ge Qing, was brave and unyielding. From his neck down, his entire body was covered in tattoos of Bai Juyi's poems. Cheng Shi often invited Jingzhou guests, including Chen Zhi, to view these tattoos and asked Ge Qing to explain them. Ge Qing could even recall them from memory. When pointing to a particular tattoo, if it was the line "Not this flower, but the chrysanthemum," there would be a person holding a cup near a chrysanthemum bush. If it was the line "Cold leaves on a yellow brocade tree," he would point to a tree with a brocade hanging from it, with intricate patterns that were extremely fine. In total, there were over thirty such tattoos, covering his entire body, and Chen Zhi called it the "Bai Juyi Poem Scroll." Cheng Shi's servant, Lu Shentong, was known for his strength. Whenever he competed in strength, he could wear a stone weight of six hundred catties and bite through dozens of stone grains. On his back was a tattoo of Vajrapani, and he claimed to have divine strength, believing that the more people helping him, the stronger he became. He would often perform rituals on the first and last days of the lunar month, preparing a milky porridge, burning incense, and sitting naked, making his wife and children worship his back.

Cui Chengcheng, when young, served in the military and was skilled in a game called "Lu Qu," where he could wield a stick with great agility, like being glued to the action. Later, he became an observer in the region of Qian Nan. As a young man, he had a snake tattooed all over his body, starting from his right hand. The snake's mouth was open, its arms reaching out to encircle two fingers, wrapping around his wrist and neck, with its teeth or jaw inside his abdomen, and its tail dragging down to his buttocks. When in the presence of guests, he would often cover his hands with clothing, but when drunk, he would reveal his hands and make a threatening gesture, grabbing actors and saying, "The snake is biting you." The actors would then pretend to be in pain and cry out, using this behavior for entertainment. During the Bao Li era, a commoner in Chang Le Li gate had a tattoo on his arm. Several dozen people gathered around to watch. Suddenly, a person appeared wearing a white robe and a hat, smiling and looking around. They nodded and smiled before leaving. Within ten steps, the commoner's son experienced bleeding as if from a nosebleed, the pain intense, almost as if it were in the bones. Soon, the bleeding amounted to more than a dou of blood. The crowd suspected the person who had just left and told the father to go after the person. The person denied it, but the father bowed several times, then picked up some soil and made as if to offer a prayer, saying, "Please transmit this." As he spoke, the bleeding stopped. Cheng Shi's third cousin, Gou, during the Zhenyuan era, once passed through Huang Keng. A follower picked up several pieces of a skull, intending to use the bones as medicine. One piece had the words "escaped slave" written in faint ink, revealing that the tattoo had been deeply embedded in the skin. The follower dreamed of a person who covered their face and asked for the bones, saying, "I am very ashamed. Please bury the bones for me, and I will bless you." The follower woke up in shock and immediately buried the bones. Later, when something happened, the ghost seemed to report it in a dream. Because of this, the follower gained wealth, almost reaching ten thousand, and then died.

During the reign of Emperor Ling, a soldier in General Yin Yan's camp was late by several hours, and Yin Yan was about to scold him. The soldier, drunk, defended himself loudly, which angered Yin Yan, who then beat him dozens of times, nearly to death. The soldier's brother, who was a camp official, was kind-hearted and felt unjustly treated by Yin Yan. He then carved the words "kill Yin" into his flesh with a knife and inked them. Yin Yan secretly knew about this and killed the official on some other pretext. During the Taihe period, the southern barbarians invaded, and Yin Yan led tens of thousands of troops to defend Qiongxia Pass. Yin Yan's strength was unmatched, and he often played with his attendants by hitting their shins with a jujube stick, causing the muscles to swell without leaving any marks. Relying on his strength, he led all his troops out of the pass, chasing the barbarians for several miles. The barbarians ambushed and attacked from both sides, causing a great defeat, Yin Yan's horse fell, and he was pierced by dozens of arrows and died. On the day he first left the pass, he suddenly saw the official he had killed holding a yellow tablet as large as a wheel, leading the way, and he felt uneasy. He asked his attendants, but none of them had seen it. He ultimately died on the battlefield. Fang Rufu's wife, Cui, was jealous, and the maidservants were not allowed to wear heavy makeup or high hairstyles; each month, they were given a bean of rouge and a coin of powder. One of the newly bought maidservants had slightly better makeup, and Cui angrily said, "Do you like your makeup? I will do it for you!" She then carved her eyebrows, filled them with blue, burned the lock beam, and scorched her two eye corners, causing the skin to char and curl, and she applied red to it. When the scabs fell off, the scars looked like makeup.

Yang Yuqing served as the Prefect of Jingzhao. At that time, there were three self-proclaimed princes in the city who were strong enough to lift huge stones. Their bodies were covered in tattoos, and no part of their skin was unmarked. They had faced death multiple times but managed to avoid it by hiding in the military. One day, they committed an offense, and Yang Yuqing ordered 500 men to capture them. He then closed the door and beat them to death. The judgment stated: "Tattooed on all four limbs, they only called themselves princes. No need to interrogate; they should be punished accordingly." The people of Shu were skilled in tattooing, and their work was as clear as a painting. Some said that using black ink made the color fresher. Cheng Shi asked the servants, and they said it was just using good ink. During the reign of Emperor Dezong in Jingzhou, there was a person selling tattoos in the market. They had a stamp with needles clustered to form various objects, such as toads, scorpions, pestles, and mortars. They would stamp according to the person's desire and brush the area with soot. After the wound healed, the tattoo was finer than any other. In recent times, makeup trends favored facial markings shaped like crescent moons, called "yellow stars" (or simply "stars"). The term "靥钿" originated from the Viceroy Sun He and his wife, Lady Zheng. Sun He, who was fond of his wife, once danced while intoxicated with a jade如意 (wish-fulfilling jewel) and accidentally cut her cheek, causing blood to flow. She was in great pain. He ordered the imperial physician to prepare a remedy, and the doctor said that obtaining white otter marrow, mixed with jade and amber powder, would remove the scar. Sun He spent a hundred gold pieces to acquire the otter and prepared the ointment. However, because there was too much amber, the scar did not fade. Instead, a red dot shaped like a wish-fulfilling jewel appeared on her left cheek, which, when seen, made her even more beautiful. The maidservants who wanted to gain favor all tattooed their cheeks with red and blue and presented themselves for favor.

Facial Decorations for Women Today, women use decorative patches on their faces, a practice that originated with Empress Dowager Guo Shengtong, who created them to cover blemishes. Before the Da Li era, many high-ranking women were jealous and fierce, and maidservants who displeased them would have their faces marked, resulting in patterns known as "moon marks" and "coin marks." Among common people, there were those who wore blue marks on their faces like tattoos. An old belief held that women who died in childbirth were marked with ink on their faces to prevent harm to others. The Yue people, who lived near water, would tattoo their bodies to ward off dangers from snakes and dragons. Today, the "Black Tiger" people of southern China still practice facial tattoos, a remnant of the ancient custom of body tattooing. According to the *Zhouli* (Rites of Zhou), there were five hundred different forms of tattooing as a form of punishment. The text explains that the practice involved first carving the face and then filling the incisions with ink. Those who were tattooed were made to guard the gates. The *Shangshu Xingde kao* (Examination of Punishment and Virtue) states, "In the era of Zhuolu, people's foreheads were carved. Tattooing was like restraining a horse by its bit." The text further explains that tattooed people were referred to as "knife and ink people," a term for outcasts or criminals. The *Shangshu Dazhuan* (Great Commentary on the Book of Documents) mentions, "In the time of Emperor Shun, those who committed tattooing were made to wear black headbands." The *Baihu Tong* (White Tiger Questions) states, "Tattooing was associated with the forehead, following the Han legal principle that fire overcomes metal." The *History of the Han Dynasty* records, "When the Han abolished corporal punishment, those who should have been tattooed were instead shaved and given wooden collars to perform hard labor." Another passage in the *History of the Han Dynasty* describes a diplomatic mission to the Xiongnu: "According to law, Han envoys had to wear their official seals and tattoo their faces to gain access to the Xiongnu leader. Wuzhu and others removed their seals and tattooed their faces, and thus they were allowed to enter the leader's tent, where he favored them." The *Jin Code* stipulates that slaves who escaped once were marked with copper-green ink or black ink on their eyes; those who escaped twice were marked on their cheeks; and those who escaped three times were marked horizontally below their eyes, each mark measuring one and a half inches in length.

In the Liang dynasty's miscellaneous laws, it was stipulated that before sentencing, prisoners had their faces marked with the character "劫" (robbery). According to the interpretation of the monk Sangzhi's monastic rules, the "Nirvana Seal" refers to a ritual where monks would carve their bodies with designs using cinnabar and copper green, creating tattoos known as "印黥." The *Tianbao Chronicle* records: "The southern mountains of Rì Nán are connected, spanning thousands of miles, inhabited by naked people. They are descendants of the Bai people. They would tattoo flowers on their foreheads, apply a substance resembling purple powder under their eyes, and remove their front two teeth for aesthetic reasons." Cheng Shi believed that "a gentleman should be ashamed of not knowing even one thing," while Tao Zhenbai often said, "not knowing one thing is a deep shame." Moreover, those destined to be kings were tattooed, with red flowers applied as part of the ritual, and they were marked with ink. Could such practices be recorded in official histories? I have recorded these observations for those who share my interests, to relieve their worries. ● Chapter Eight: Thunder Péi Yè, the county magistrate of Anfeng, was the grandson of Shì Yān. He recounted that during the winter, Emperor Xuanzong summoned the shaman Bao Chao to create thunder. Bao Chao replied, "There will be thunder by noon tomorrow." The emperor then ordered Gao Lishi to oversee the ritual. That night, Bao Chao performed the ceremony, and by dawn, the sky was clear. Gao Lishi was frightened. Bao Chao said, "General, look at the south mountain; there will be a black cloud like a plate." Gao Lishi looked and saw as Bao Chao had said. Soon, the wind arose, and the black cloud spread, followed by several loud thunderclaps. Emperor Xuanzong also often had Bao Chao accompany General Geshu on western campaigns, and they always enjoyed favorable winds in battle.

In the early years of the Zhenyuan era, a brave man named Wang from Zhengzhou was working in the fields during the summer when suddenly a violent rain and thunderstorm struck. He took shelter in a silkworm room to avoid the rain. Soon, lightning and thunder entered the room, and dark clouds enveloped the area. Wang quickly shut the door and wielded a hoe, striking wildly. The sound gradually diminished, and the cloud shrank back to the size of half a bed, then to the size of a plate, before suddenly falling to the ground and transforming into an iron, a broken knife, and a small three-legged pot. Meanwhile, in the northern capital, a villager from Jie County was delivering a document to the Jinci Temple at night. In the middle of the night, someone knocked on the door and said, "The Lord of Jie is temporarily borrowing the thunderbolt cart to come to Jie County to harvest the wheat on a certain day." After a long time, someone replied, "The great lord's message says that the thunderbolt cart is busy and cannot be lent." The person repeatedly asked for the cart, and then five or six people appeared with candles, emerging from behind the temple. The messenger from Jie County also rode in through the gate. Several people carried an object like a flagpole, with rings and flags attached, and gave it to the rider, saying, "You can check and take it." The rider then counted the flags, which totaled eighteen leaves, each with light like lightning. The villager spread the news to all neighboring villages, ordering them to quickly harvest the wheat, as there would be a big storm. The villagers all disbelieved him and continued to harvest. On that day, the common people led their relatives to a high place, waiting until noon. Black clouds like smoke from a kiln appeared on Mount Jie, soon covering the sky, and the rain fell heavily like a rope. The wind howled, and thunder roared, damaging over a thousand acres of wheat. Several villages accused the common people of being sorcerers and brought the matter to court. The Censor of the Ministry of Works, Zhang Zhoufeng, personally witnessed the trial.

Chengshi, the third uncle from the Zhidefang area, was a close relative when he was young, living in the Yangxian house. One night, he encountered thunder and rain. Every time lightning struck, in the light, he saw dozens of heads, as large as a winnowing basket. Liu Gongquan, the Attendant, once heard a close relative say that at the end of the Yuanhe era, he stayed in a mountain temple in Jianzhou. At night, he heard noise outside the door, so he secretly looked out through the window. He saw several people chopping wood to make a thunder chariot, as depicted in pictures. After a while, one sneezed, and suddenly everything went dark; the people's eyes became dim. Scholar Zhou Hong said that during the Baoli era, more than ten guests from the town gathered to escape the heat and drink. Suddenly, a violent storm came, and an object fell like a ㄑ, with two eyes ㄦㄦ. Everyone was startled and hid under the bed. Suddenly, it climbed the stairs, looked at everyone, and then disappeared. When the rain stopped, they slowly got up, looked at each other, and found their ears covered in mud. The townspeople said that the previous lightning strike caused the oxen to fight and the birds to fall. The guests from the town only felt a rumbling sound.

Yuan Zhen bought a property with a dam in Jiangxia and Xiangzhou. He constructed a new hall, and the beam-laying ceremony was just completed when a strong wind and heavy rain struck. The tenant delivered six or seven jars of oil. Suddenly, a loud noise was heard, and the jars of oil were neatly arranged on the beam without a single drop spilling. That year, Yuan died. In the early years of the Zhenyuan era, a thunderstorm struck the state of Xu, and a creature fell to the ground. It had a pig's head and two fingers on each hand and foot, holding a red snake and biting it. Soon after, the sky darkened, and the creature disappeared. At the time, people drew and circulated images of it. ### Chapter 8: Dreams Yang Yuanzhen, a man from the Wei state, could interpret dreams. Guangyang Wang Yuanyuan dreamed of wearing a ceremonial robe and leaning against a locust tree. He asked Yuanzhen to interpret the dream. Yuanzhen said he would become a high-ranking official. Later, Yuanzhen told someone privately, "I will become a high-ranking official after my death. The character '槐' (locust tree) has a ghost radical." Indeed, he was killed by Erzhurong and posthumously granted the title of Minister of Works. Xu Chao dreamed of stealing a sheep and being imprisoned. Yuanzhen interpreted the dream, saying he would become the magistrate of Chengyang. Later, he was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Chengyang. Hou Junji conspired with Crown Prince Li Chengqian to rebel. He felt uneasy and suddenly dreamed of two armored men leading him to a place where he saw a man with a tall hat and a long beard, who ordered the guards to fetch his "威骨" (powerful bones). Soon, several men with butcher's knives opened his head and right arm, taking bone pieces shaped like fish tails. He woke up muttering and still felt pain in his head and arm. From then on, he was plagued by fear and exhaustion, unable to draw a bow even as light as one catty. He wanted to confess but hesitated and eventually failed.

The Taoist priestess Kang Zixia of the Eastern Mother Temple in Yangzhou recounted that in her youth, she had a dream in which she was taken to a place where a divine command was given for a general to patrol the Southern Mountain. She was then equipped with golden armor and rode with a thousand or more horsemen, treading on empty space and heading south. Soon they arrived, and the mountain god bowed to welcome them before the horse. In the dream, it seemed as if there were instructions, and she traversed every peak, ridge, and valley of the mountain. She returned in a daze and was awakened by the crowing of the rooster. From that day on, she grew dozens of whiskers. During the time when Wei Zhengguan, the Minister of Agriculture, was taking the imperial examinations, he once visited Ruzhou, where Liu Ling, the governor, appointed him as a military judge. Liu once dreamed of a person presenting a case, stating that he owed seven hundred and seventy bundles of firewood. He consulted Wei for an explanation, and Wei said, "Firewood, or firewood." He added, "You will not have this for long." After a month, Liu died of illness. He was always poor, and Wei arranged matters for him, having requested rice, wheat, money, and silk from the government for several months. However, the government still owed him seven hundred and seventy bundles of firewood. Upon reviewing the case, Wei realized the meaning of Liu's previous dream. The Taoist priestess Qin Xiaji was young and diligent in worship, never tiring in contemplation. She once dreamed of a large tree, which suddenly had a hole. A child with a green robe and tied hair emerged from the hole and told her, "You should be a respected teacher." She was startled awake. From then on, the child vaguely reported good and bad omens to her. For five years, Qin thought it was a demon. Once, she happened to consult her teacher about something, and the teacher quickly warned her not to speak of it, saying it was proof of successful cultivation. Therefore, it ceased. The old saying is that dreams should not be frequently interpreted; indeed, they should not.

A Physician from Shu, Zan Yin, said that when the internal organs' yin energy is excessive, one dreams frequently, and when yang energy is strong, one dreams less. Dreams are also not remembered. The *Zhouli* mentions a role for dream interpreters, and it states that "the sun, moon, and stars each have their own interpretations for six types of dreams," referring to the positions of the sun (甲乙), the moon (建破), and the stars (居直, 扶, or 符). It also says, "Evil dreams are sent to the four directions," meaning that evil dreams are expelled to the four corners of the earth through a ritual involving the people and the 方相氏, who chase and send the evil dreams to the four suburbs. During the Han dynasty, there was a ritual called 大傩, where a figure named 亻辰子 would expel evil spirits, and there was a character named Bo Qi who would "eat" dreams. In Daoism, dreams are said to be caused by the soul (魄) or by the three worms (三尸). Buddhism categorizes dreams into four types: 1) the seeds of good and evil actions, 2) imbalances in the four elements, 3) blessings from saints, and 4) omens of good and evil. I once heard a monk named Sushu explain these concepts, and he said they come from the Buddhist scriptures, but I have not had the time to investigate them further. He also said that dreams should not be grasped, as doing so can lead to attachment, and attachment can bring strange phenomena. It is said that blind people do not dream, indicating that dreaming is a learned behavior. My cousin Lu Youze once dreamed of watching a drum. When he woke up, his younger brother was playfully knocking on the door, mimicking the sound of a street drum. Another time, my brother-in-law's nephew, Pei Yuanyu, told me that one of his relatives had a dream about a neighbor's daughter giving him two cherries, which he ate. When he woke up, he found the pits next to his pillow. Li Xuan wrote *《李子正辩》*, which discusses the nature of very vivid dreams, where the dreamer can see their own body and others in the dream. For example, Liu Youqiu once saw his wife in a dream, indicating that dreams cannot be explained by a single cause. Foolish people have fewer dreams, not just wise ones. I asked (or heard) about stable servants (驺皂), and they often have no dreams at all.

Secretarial Officer Han Quan was skilled in interpreting dreams. Wei Zhongxing, who served as a Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat, had a former acquaintance's son who was applying for an official position. The acquaintance approached Wei to discuss the matter, and Wei readily agreed. Just before the list of successful candidates was to be posted, the acquaintance suddenly dreamed of riding a donkey and stumbling, falling into water, but landing on the shore with his boots not wet. The acquaintance, who had a connection with Han Quan, visited him. Han Quan, half-drunk and half-joking, said, "Your current selection matter will not succeed. According to the dream, Wei has betrayed you, and your feet will not be affected." When the list was posted, the acquaintance was indeed rejected. Han Quan was knowledgeable and was the nephew of Han the Grand Commandant. A junior officer of the Wei Yuan Army named Mei Bocheng was skilled in divination. Recently, a performer named Li Boliang traveled to Jingzhou to beg for money and received a hundred hu of rice. Upon returning, he sent his brother to fetch it, but the brother did not arrive by the due date. That day, Li Boliang dreamed of washing a white horse and consulted Mei Bocheng about it. Mei Bocheng thought for a moment and said, "People often use reverse speech. Washing a white horse means pouring out white rice. Are you worried about water and wind dangers?" A few days later, Li Boliang's brother arrived and reported that their boat had capsized in the Wei River, and not a single grain of rice remained. A diviner named Xu Daosheng said that there was a man named Wang in Jianghuai who was known for interpreting dreams. Merchant Zhang Zhan was about to return home and dreamed of cooking in a mortar. He asked Wang about the dream, and Wang replied, "You will not see your wife upon your return. Cooking in a mortar means there is no pot." When Zhang Zhan arrived home, he found out that his wife had passed away several months ago, and he realized that Wang's words were not false.

补阙杨子孙堇，善占梦。一人梦松生产前，一人梦枣生屋上，堇言：“松，丘垅间所植。枣字重来，重来呼魄之象。”二人俱卒。** The official Yang Zisun Jin, a *Buchue* (a low-ranking official responsible for advising the emperor and correcting errors in official documents), was skilled in interpreting dreams. One person dreamed that a pine tree produced fruit before its time, while another dreamed that a date tree grew on the roof. Jin interpreted these dreams, saying, "The pine is planted in the hills and mounds, symbolizing premature events. The character for 'date' suggests a return, indicating a call back to the spirit world." Both individuals died soon after. **●卷九 事感** 平原高菀城东有渔津，传云魏末平原潘府君字惠延，自白马登舟之部，手中算囊遂坠于水，囊中本有钟乳一两。在郡三年，济水泛溢，得一鱼，长三丈，广五尺。刳其腹，中有得一坠水之囊，金针尚在，钟乳消尽。其鱼得脂数十斛，时人异之。 In the eastern part of Gao Wan City in the Plain, there was a fishing port. According to tradition, during the end of the Wei Dynasty, Pan Jun, the governor of the Plain, whose courtesy name was Huixian, departed from Bai Ma on a boat to his new post. During the journey, a small bag containing a piece of stalactite fell into the water. Three years later, when he was in his post, the River Ji overflowed, and a fish measuring three zhang (approximately 9 meters) in length and five chi (approximately 1.5 meters) in width was caught. When the fish's belly was cut open, a small bag that had fallen into the water was found inside, still containing a gold needle but with the stalactite dissolved. The fish yielded dozens of shi (approximately 120 liters) of water, which was a remarkable discovery. 谯郡有功曹间，天统中，济南来府君出除谯郡，时功曹清河崔公恕，弱冠有令德，于时春夏积旱，送别者千余人，至此间上，众渴甚思水，升直万钱矣，来公有思水色。恕独见一青乌于间中，乍飞乍止，怪而就焉。乌起，见一石，方五六寸。以鞭拨之，清泉涌出。因盛以银瓶，瓶满水立竭，唯来公与恕供疗而已。议者以为盛德所感致焉。时人异之，故以为目。 In Qiao Commandery, there was a *Gongcao* (an official responsible for administrative affairs). During the Tian Tong period, the governor of Jinan, named Lai Jun, was appointed to Qiao Commandery. At the time, the *Gongcao* Cui Shu from Qinghe, who was young and virtuous, was present. During a prolonged drought in spring and summer, over a thousand people gathered to bid farewell to Lai Jun. They were extremely thirsty and the price of a single unit of water was worth ten thousand coins. Lai Jun showed signs of longing for water. Cui Shu alone saw an *qingwu* (a mythical bird) in the *jian* (a type of pavilion or platform). The bird flew and landed, and Cui Shu approached it. When the bird flew away, he saw a stone measuring about five to six inches in length and width. He used a whip to push the stone, and clear spring water gushed out. They collected the water in a silver bottle, which filled up immediately but then ran dry. Only Lai Jun and Cui Shu were able to benefit from the water. People believed that this event was caused by the virtue of the individuals involved. The people of the time found it extraordinary, and thus it is recorded as a remarkable event.

During the reign of Emperor Wenzong, in the ninth year of the Taihe era, an imperial decree was issued to the troops from Fuyang to cross the Yellow River to the north. In the twelfth month of winter, they arrived at Jinan County and were instructed to break the ice to pull the boats. The ice struck the boats, causing them to capsize and the decree to be lost. The official, Li Yan'zuo, was filled with fear and did not sleep or eat for six days. His temples turned suddenly white, and his appearance and skin became emaciated. His subordinates were also surprised by his demeanor. He then ordered the ferry official, "Without the decree, you must die." The official, fearing for his life, asked the official to make a wish, and he sank and floated in the river. The official relied on his sincerity and clarity, and the subordinate searched for it with his life. Li Yan'zuo then ordered wine to be prepared and made a wish, conveying a message to question the river god. The message read: "The great emperor is above, and the officials of rivers, mountains, and rituals are all in order. Within my jurisdiction, sacrifices have never been lacking. You, the river god and the chief of the aquatic creatures, should protect the emperor's decree. Why did you cause it to be drowned? If I do not recover it, I will fast and report to heaven, and heaven will punish you." The official poured wine on the ice, and after finishing his words, there was a sudden sound like an earthquake, and the river ice broke, about thirty zhang. The official knew that Li Yan'zuo's sincerity had reached heaven, so he cast a hook into the water and retrieved the decree in one cast. The seal was intact, only the seal impression was slightly damp. Wherever Li Yan'zuo went, he enforced strict and simple orders, trusted in things, and established his authority. How could there be a boat capsizing for six days, and with one libation the solid ice breaking, and with one cast of the line the lost decree recovered, if it were not the utmost sincerity?

#### Volume Nine: Thieves and Heroes **Wei Mingdi built the Lingyun Terrace, rising majestically dozens of feet, where Wei Dian turned white-haired. There was a person at the bell tower who could climb wearing wooden clogs, as if walking on flat ground. The emperor was startled and killed him, finding two fleshy wings under his armpits, several inches long.** South of Gaotang County, there is a Xianbei City. It is said that the Xianbei people stopped here on their way to pay homage to Yan. Beside the city is the tomb of Dao Zhi, which is very high and large, and bandits often secretly prayed there. In the early years of the Tianbao era of Qi, the county magistrate of Tugu, Ding Yongxing, had a group of bandits raiding his jurisdiction. Ding Yongxing then secretly had someone watch by the tomb, and indeed, there were people praying. He had them arrested and executed, and after that, the prayers ceased.

The *Huanglan* states that the tomb of Diao Zhi is located east of the Yellow River. According to the *Huanglan*, Diao Zhi died in Dongling; this place was anciently named Dongpingling, and it is suspected that this is near the location. The assassin, a flying night demon technique, was used. Prince Han of Jin was in Zhe West when the Waguan Temple held an unrestricted vegetarian feast. Among the crowd, a young man asked to perform on the stage. He threw the canopy and climbed up, wearing a single robe, with curly hair and leather shoes, moving like a monkey hanging and a bird flying, swift as gods and ghosts. He built a [B124] water at the ridge, first flowing to the eaves, leaving one foot empty, leaning his body to catch the flow, and those who saw him were all startled. Minister Ma once treasured a jade spirit bowl, which summer flies did not approach, and water stored in it for a month did not rot or diminish. If someone had eye pain, holding it in the mouth cured it immediately. He kept it in a box in his bedroom. There was a young servant, seven or eight years old, who stole and played with it, causing it to break. At the time, Minister Ma was out and had not returned, and those around were frightened. Suddenly, the young servant disappeared. Minister Ma, upon learning of this, became very angry and whipped those around several hundred times, intending to kill the young servant. They searched for three days, but could not find him. One morning, a maid was cleaning the ground and saw a purple sash hanging from under the bed. Upon looking, it was the young servant who had lifted the bed and was carrying it. Without eating for three days, his strength did not diminish. Minister Ma was greatly startled and said, "Breaking my bowl is a minor offense." He immediately ordered those around to execute him.

Wei Xinggui once said that in his youth, he traveled in the western part of the capital. At dusk, he stopped at an inn and wished to proceed further. An old man working in front of the inn warned him, saying, "Guest, do not travel at night, for there are many bandits in this area." Wei replied, "I am attentive to my bow and arrows, so there is no worry." He then set off. Traveling dozens of miles, it became dark, and someone followed him in the grass. Wei shouted but received no response, so he shot arrows continuously, hitting the pursuer but still did not retreat. When his arrows ran out, Wei was afraid and fled on horseback. After a while, wind and rain suddenly arrived. Wei dismounted and leaned against a tree, seeing lightning in the sky pursuing each other like a ball, gradually approaching the treetop. He felt things falling in a flurry in front of him. Looking at it, he saw that it was wooden shingles. In a moment, the accumulated shingles buried him up to the knees. Terrified, Wei threw away his bow and arrows and looked up to the sky, begging for mercy. He bowed dozens of times, and the lightning gradually rose and disappeared, the wind and thunder also ceased. Looking at the big tree, Wei saw that its branches and trunk were bare. His saddle and load were lost, so he returned to the previous inn. Seeing the old man making a bucket, Wei thought he was a strange person, bowed to him, and apologized for any mistakes. The old man smiled and said, "Guest, do not carry bow and arrows; you must know swordsmanship." He led Wei into the courtyard and pointed to the saddle and load, saying, "But must take to test you." Then he took out a piece of bucket board, on which all the arrows from last night had hit. Wei asked to work to draw water but was not allowed. The old man gave a hint about swordsmanship, and Wei also learned a little bit.

According to legend, Lili served as the magistrate of the capital. At the time, there was a ritual at Qujiang where a dragon statue was used to pray for rain, and thousands of spectators gathered. When Lili arrived, only an old man stood with his staff, not moving aside. Lili became angry and beat him on the back twenty times, like hitting leather, before swinging his arm and leaving. Lili suspected that the old man was not an ordinary person and ordered the old gatekeeper to find him. They went to the Lanling neighborhood, where the old man entered a small gate and shouted loudly, "I have been greatly humiliated today, prepare some soup." The gatekeeper hurried back to report to Lili, who was greatly afraid. Wearing a tattered robe and carrying official clothes, Lili went with the gatekeeper to the place. It was already dark, and the gatekeeper entered directly, introducing Lili's official title. Lili hurriedly entered and bowed, saying, "I was mistaken about your appearance, I should be punished tenfold." The old man jumped up and asked, "Who brought you here?" He immediately led Lili up the stairs. Lili knew he could argue logically and said calmly, "I am the magistrate of the capital. If my authority is slightly diminished, I lose my official position. You are hidden and mixed among the crowd, only someone with sharp eyes can recognize you."

If you blame this person, it is like using a thief to catch a thief, which is not the heart of a righteous man." The old man smiled and said, "It is my fault." He then prepared wine and set up a table on the ground, inviting the soldier to sit. As night deepened, they spoke of methods of preserving life, with concise yet persuasive reasoning. Li became respectful and fearful, and said, "I have a skill; please allow me to demonstrate it for you." He then entered. After a long time, he emerged wearing purple clothes and red hair, holding seven swords of varying lengths. He danced in the courtyard, leaping and brandishing the swords, the movements as swift as lightning. Sometimes he moved horizontally like a broken plate, sometimes spinning like a compass. There was a short sword over two feet long, which often approached Li's mat. Li knelt and trembled with fear. After a meal's worth of time, he threw the swords onto the ground in the shape of the Big Dipper, looking at Li and saying, "I was testing your courage, Li." Li bowed and said, "From today on, my life is at your mercy; I beg to serve you." The old man said, "Your bone structure lacks the aura of a warrior; it is not something that can be taught quickly; we will meet again another day." He bowed to Li and entered. Li returned home, his complexion as if he were ill. When he looked in the mirror, he realized that he had lost an inch or more of his beard. The next day, when he returned, the room was empty.

In the early years of the Jianzhong era, a scholar named Wei Sheng moved his family to Ruozhou. On the way, he encountered a monk who, due to their pleasant conversation, traveled with him. As the sun was about to set behind the mountain, the monk pointed to the road and said, "This temple is only a few miles away. Could you not visit it?" The scholar agreed and instructed his family to go ahead. The monk then ordered his servants to prepare the way. After traveling for more than ten miles, they still had not reached the temple. Wei Sheng asked the monk, who pointed to a distant location shrouded in mist and said, "That is it." They continued to advance, and by the time the sun had set, Wei Sheng began to suspect the monk. He was skilled in archery and secretly drew his bow and nocked an arrow from his boot, carrying more than ten copper balls in his sleeves. He then scolded the monk, saying, "I have a schedule to keep, and just happened to enjoy your enlightened conversation, so graciously agreed to your invitation. Now we have traveled twenty miles and still have not reached the temple. Why is that?" The monk simply said, "Keep going." By this point, the monk had walked ahead more than a hundred steps, and Wei Sheng realized that the monk was a bandit. He then shot at him.

The monk hit Wei's brain, but Wei did not feel it initially. The monk hit him five times before Wei noticed the spot. The monk then slowly said, "Young master, don't play tricks." Wei knew he could do nothing about it and stopped shooting. He saw the monk arrive at a village, where dozens of people lined up with torches to greet him. The monk invited Wei to sit in a hall and said, "Young master, don't worry." He then asked those around, "Is the place for your wife set up properly?" He reassured Wei again, "Young master, please comfort yourself; you can stay here." Wei saw his wife and daughter in another place, and their accommodations were very luxurious. They looked at each other and wept. Wei then approached the monk, and the monk took Wei's hand and said, "I am a thief. I originally had no good intentions, but I didn't know you had such skills. Even if I were not a thief, I couldn't have stood up to you. Today, there is nothing else; please don't doubt me. The shots I hit you with are all here." He then raised his hand and pulled from the back of his head, and five pellets fell to the ground.

For, the brain could hold a bullet without injury, even though Liezi spoke of "painless whipping" and Mencius mentioned "no skin being scratched," it was not an exaggeration. After a while, a banquet was set up, with a steamed calf prepared, and more than ten small knives for cutting it, surrounded by pickled cakes. Wei Sheng was invited to sit, and the monk said, "I have several adopted brothers whom I wish to make pay respects." Before he finished speaking, five or six people in red robes and large sashes lined up at the bottom of the steps. The monk called out, "Bow to the young master; if you had encountered the young master before, you would have been reduced to powder." After the meal, the monk said, "I have been in this business for a long time, and now that I am growing old, I wish to reform my past wrongdoings. Unfortunately, I have a son whose skills surpass mine, and I want to ask the young master to help me deal with him." He then called out Feifei to pay respects to the young master. Feifei was only sixteen or seventeen years old, dressed in a green robe with long sleeves, and his skin was smooth and plump. The monk shouted, "Go to the inner hall to serve the young master."

The monk then gave Wei a sword and five balls, and said, "Please, sir, use all your skill to kill him, and do not burden the old monk." He led Wei into a hall and locked the door. In the four corners of the hall, there were only bright lamps. Feifei stood in the hall, holding a short horsewhip. Wei drew his bow, intending to hit him, but the balls had already fallen. Unconsciously, Feifei jumped onto the beam, moved along the wall without touching it, as agile as a monkey, and the balls no longer hit him. Wei then wielded his sword and chased after him, but Feifei swiftly dodged and evaded, staying within a foot of Wei. Wei cut the whip, but still could not harm him. After a long time, the monk opened the door and asked Wei, "Have you managed to eliminate the harm for the old monk?" Wei explained in detail. The monk looked disappointed and said to Feifei, "You have proven yourself to be a thief, what can be done now?" Throughout the night, the monk and Wei discussed swordplay and archery. As dawn approached, the monk saw Wei off at the road, gave him a hundred bolts of silk, and they parted tearfully.

During the Yuanhe period, there was a man from the Jianghuai region named Tang, who had read widely in history and biographies. He was fond of Daoism and often traveled to famous mountains. He claimed to be skilled in alchemical techniques, particularly those related to metal transmutation, and many had followed him as a teacher. Later, in a guesthouse in Chuzhou, he met a man named Lu, and their spirits were in harmony. Lu also discussed alchemy and referred to Tang as his distant relative, calling him "uncle." Tang could not part with Lu and invited him to join him on a trip to Mount Heng. Lu said he had relatives in Yixing and planned to visit them, but for now, he was eager to join Tang on his journey to the mountains. They stopped at a temple halfway through their journey, and at midnight, they were laughing and talking. Lu asked Tang if he could briefly explain his alchemical techniques. Tang laughed and said that he had endured great hardships to learn this technique and could not explain it lightly. Lu continued to ask, but Tang explained that the knowledge was only taught at specific times and could only be passed on in the mountains.

Lu Yin changed his expression and said, "Uncle, this evening you must deliver the message; do not delay." Tang scolded him, "I have nothing to do with you, as different as wind and cattle's ears. Unexpectedly, we met in Xuyi. I truly admire a gentleman, how could I be worse than a mere servant?" Lu Yin raised his arms and glared at him for a long time, saying, "I am an assassin. If you do not deliver the message, you will die here." He then reached into his black leather bag and pulled out a short dagger, its blade shaped like a crescent moon. Holding a fire and a hot iron, he sharpened the blade. Tang was terrified and recounted the situation. Lu Yin then laughed and said to Tang, "I almost killed your uncle." This technique works about half the time, and he apologized, saying, "My master is a divine being. He ordered us ten to seek out and kill those who falsely claim to know alchemy. Even those who spread the fraud of adulterating gold with tin must die. I have long mastered the art of riding the wind." He then bowed to Tang and suddenly disappeared. Afterward, whenever Tang encountered a Taoist, he would always recount this story to warn others.

Li Kuo was in Yuzhou when he captured seven bandits who used light and fire in their crimes. They had killed people in the past and were known to eat human flesh. After the trial was concluded, Li Kuo asked about the reason for their cannibalism. The leader of the bandits explained, "I was taught by a great thief that those who eat human flesh enter homes at night, and the people must be drowsy or unconscious. Sometimes, there are those who are bewitched and do not wake up, so we cannot help but eat them." In the inns of the two capitals, there were many paintings of magpies and teacups. The bandits referred to these as the "spicy magpies," using them to remember the direction of the beak. The "spicy 宛" also indicated urgency. In the *Volume Ten: Strange Objects*, it is recorded that the Qin mirror was located in a stone cave on the ancient bank of the Wuxi River. It was a square mirror with a diameter of over one zhang and could see a person's five internal organs. Emperor Qin Shi Huang called it the "bone-viewing treasure," and it was situated in the mountains within the borders of Wulao County. The wind-sound tree was a legendary tree whose branch could indicate illness or impending death. When Dongfang Shuo returned from the West Na Kingdom, he obtained a branch of the wind-sound tree, which the emperor gave to the ministers. If a person was ill, the branch would sweat, and if they were about to die, it would break. This corresponded to the saying, "before half a year of life, the branch does not sweat." Emperor Gaozu of Han entered the Xianyang Palace, where among the treasures, the most extraordinary was the blue jade lamp. The stand was seven chi and five cun high, and below it was a coiled dragon holding the lamp with its mouth. When the lamp was lit, the scales and armor of the dragon would all move, shining brightly like a row of stars.

**Coral Tree** In the Jade Pool of Han, there stood a coral tree, towering at twelve feet high, with one trunk and three branches, adorned with four hundred and sixty-two branches. It was presented by King Zhao Tuo of Nanyue and was called the "Fenghuo Tree." At night, it emitted a glow, often appearing as if it were about to ignite. **Stone Coal** In Wulao County, the mountain produces stone coal, which cannot be burned away even after a year. **Mysterious Inscription** In the mountains of Jing, there is a stone wall with over a thousand characters inscribed on it, yellow in color, not carved, resembling ancient script, and no one can decipher it. **Tian Gong Spring** On Huayang Leiping Mountain, there is Tian Gong Spring. Drinking from it eliminates three types of parasites in the intestines. It is better than ash water for washing clothes. **Firefly Fungus** On Liangchang Mountain, there is firefly fungus. Its leaves resemble grass, its fruit is as large as a bean, with purple flowers, and at night, it emits light. Eating one piece makes one of the seven holes in the heart visible. Eating seven pieces makes all seven holes in the heart visible, allowing one to see in the dark. **Stone Statue** On Xunyang Mountain, there is a stone statue, over ten feet tall. Tigers that reach it will fall down in front of the statue. **Winter Melon** During the Jin dynasty, Gao Heng served as the governor of Weiquan and was stationed at Shitou. His grandson, Ya, in the stable, had a deity descend, who introduced himself as "Whitehead Elder," whose staff illuminated the entire room. There was also an object resembling a winter melon, with eyes all over it. **Yuzhang Ship** In the Han dynasty, there was a Yuzhang ship in the Kunming Pool, capable of carrying a thousand people. **Bronze Camel** In the first year of Jingning during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Han, a bronze camel in Changling grew hair, with flowers blooming at the tips of the hair. **Fishing Net** In the Jin dynasty, there was a person in Qiantang who made a fishing net, which yielded millions of fish annually, and was called the "Ten-Thousand-Fish Net."

Stone Turtle and Stele** In Linyi County, north of the city, there is a tomb of Hu Gong. The stele was soon lost, leaving only the base turtle. During the era of the Shi dynasty, this turtle would carry the stele into the water at night and emerge only at dawn, with duckweed and algae often covering the surface. Someone who watched it saw the turtle about to enter the water and shouted, causing the turtle to flee, which resulted in the stele breaking. **Salt from the Ground** In Kunwu, there is a deposit of salt that extends over more than ten li.
