It forms naturally without water, and is known as "heavenly salt." When the moon is full, it appears like accumulated snow and tastes sweet. When the moon wanes, it looks like thin frost and tastes bitter. When the moon is gone, the salt completely disappears. **Yingyang Stele** In Yingyang, there is a stele marking the place where Cao Pi of Wei accepted the abdication of the Han emperor. The last six characters of the inscription turned into gold. The Sima family, associated with the era of metal, indicated that they would rule for six generations in Wei. **Spring** In Yuanjie County, there is a spring where the water swirls in the spring eye like a coiled dragon. Someone tried to stir it, and soon the water took the form of a dragon. Donkeys and horses that drank from it all ran away in fright. **Stone Oil** In Gaonu County, there is a type of oil found in the stone fat water. The greasy water floats on the surface like lacquer. It is collected to grease the wheels of vehicles and to light lamps, and it burns extremely bright. **Musk Garment** During the Jin dynasty, Xu Jing found a brocade musk garment outside the Xuanyang Gate. At home, he opened it to find an insect like a cicada, colored in five hues, with each of its two feet attached to a five-zhu coin. **Jade Dragon** In the eighth year of the Daitong era of the Liang dynasty, the military commander Yang Guangxin found a jade dragon. It was one foot two inches long and five inches high, intricately carved in a way that did not seem to be the work of humans. Its belly could hold more than a dou, and its neck was hollow and curved. When placed in water and filled, turning it over caused water to pour out of its mouth, making a sound like a zither and lute. The water ran out and the sound stopped.

Wooden Inscription, in the ninth year of Yongming (487 AD), there was an ancient tree at Anming Temple in Moling. It was cut down for firewood, and the wood naturally bore the characters "Fa Da De" (Dharma Great Virtue). Wooden Slips, in the early years of Jianyuan (479-482 AD), the old well in the temple of Yanling Ji Zi suddenly produced a sound like that of metal and stone. Digging two feet deep, a spring was discovered. In the spring, a wooden slip was found, one foot long and one and a half inches wide, with raised characters reading "Lu Mountain Taoist Zhang Ling pays his respects again." The wood was hard and white, and the characters were yellow. Red Powder, refined cinnabar into gold, ground into a fine powder with a brush, written onto stone, and when scraped off, it becomes even more visible. It is called Red Powder. Mirror Stone, in the city of Jinan, there is a square mountain. It is said that Huan Sheng attained immortality here. On the southern side of the mountain, there is a mirror-like cliff, three zhang wide, where shadows of mythical creatures can be seen clearly. During the Southern Yan period, the mirror was painted over. Folklore says that the mountain god disliked the reflection, so it was painted. Stone of Burden, in the waters of Zhuyang County, there is a solitary stone that protrudes from the water, with a clear pool below. Sometimes, people see the root of this stone resembling bamboo, yellow in color. Those who see it often meet with misfortune, and it is known as the Stone of Burden. Spike, in the pond beneath the platform of the Wei Rencheng Prince in Zhongmu County, there is an iron spike from the Han Dynasty, six chi long, three chi deep in the ground, pointing southwest, and cannot be moved. Cooking Pot Stone, in Yidao County, there is a waterfall where the stones are shaped like cooking pots, large ones like pots and small ones like cups, their shapes and colors so realistic that they deceive. They are only found in the treasure.

Fish Stone, in Xiangxiang County, Hengyang, there is a Stone Fish Mountain. The stones on the mountain are black in color, and their texture resembles raw orpiment. Each time it is opened, there are fish-like shapes, with scales, fins, heads, and tails painted on them, several inches long, and when burned, they smell like fish. Copper God, in Tang'an County, Hengyang, to the east, there is a small pond called Lüetang, which has a copper god. Often, the sound of copper stirs the water, turning it green and giving off a coppery smell, causing all the fish to die. Lower Wood, at the foot of a mountain in Su County, there is a shrine. The Zhui River reaches here, boiling and surging. Rafts floating here sink, and none ever emerge. People believe it is the lower wood of the River God. Drum Staff, on the east bank of the Weng Water mouth in Hankuang County, there is a sacred drum staff, also known as the Yangshan drum staff. It lies on the riverbank, never moved by the surging waves. Birds fly and sing, but none settle there. If a boatman accidentally touches it with a pole, he will surely suffer from malaria. Well, in Shiyang County, there is a well with water that is half blue and half yellow. The yellow part is like ash water. If used to make porridge, it turns golden, and the smell is very fragrant. Burning Stone, in Jiacheng County, there is a burning stone that is yellow in color and porous. When water is poured on it, it becomes hot. A tripod can be placed on it for cooking. Stone Drum, in Jixian County, there is a Tian Gu Mountain, on which there are stones that look like drums. When the He Gu star moves, the stone drum sounds, and when it sounds, there is disaster in Qin land. Half-Hot Lake, in Wudu Pond, Jurong County, there is a half-hot lake. The lake water is half cold and half hot. The hot water can be used to cook chicken, and all the fish are there. Those who enter it die.

Salt, in the salt well of Quxi (also known as Le) County, there is a small area of salt that rises in the center like an opened umbrella, and it is called Umbrella Salt. A spring, in the Yumen Army, there is a reed spring with a circumference of two zhang and a depth of one zhang. It is so abundant that a thousand camels and horses can drink from it without it drying up. A fungus, Shen Yue, the Prince of Shian, was given a piece of fungus weighing twelve catties and eight taels, accompanied by a letter. An ancient pot, in the Stone City Ridge of Ling County, State of Guo, there is an ancient pot in which a tree grows, and it is several zhang in circumference. King's Salt, on White Salt Cliff, there is salt that resembles crystal, and it is called King's Salt. A handboard, Prince Xiu of Shanyang, often spoke in a way that offended others. There was a person named Yu Daomin who was skilled in interpreting handboards. Xiu entrusted his handboard to Yu, who said, "This board is valuable, but it often causes conflict." Xiu then exchanged his handboard with Chu Yuan, who was known for his meticulousness. Later, Chu spoke to the emperor about his own status, and the emperor was very displeased. A mouse-chasing device, Wang Su created a mouse-chasing device made of copper that rotates continuously day and night. A wooden prisoner, according to *Lunheng*, Li Zichang, in his governance, wanted to determine the guilt or innocence of prisoners. He carved a figure of a prisoner from paulownia wood, dug a pit in the ground, lined it with reeds, and placed the wooden prisoner inside. If the prisoner was guilty, the wooden prisoner remained still. If the prisoner was innocent, the wooden prisoner would move. Su Qin's Gold, during the State of Wei, the local official Gao Xian in Luoyang discovered a hundred catties of gold inscribed with "Su Qin's Gold."

Pears from the Bao De Temple in Luoyang weigh six catties. Steamer flowers: During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu, when Zheng Jingzhen resigned and returned home, a servant was cooking rice when suddenly there was a loud noise in the pot, and the rice began to rise. Zheng went to see, and the noise grew louder; dozens of rice-like flowers appeared on the steamer, gradually growing like lotuses, turning red, and emitting a golden light, but soon withered and disappeared. Ten days later, Zheng fell ill and died. Among official gold, the lou ding gold is the finest, weighing six liang per piece, shaped like a sleeping cricket burrow or a water bank, with a depression in the center called zhi fu. Additionally, there is a purple color in the depression, known as zi dan. During the Kaiyuan period, there was a type of official gold called Da Tang Jin (which might also be referred to as Da Tang Yin). Mysterious gold: During the reign of Emperor Taizong, it was reported in Fen Prefecture that a dragon and a tiger in the sky spat out objects, which had a fiery glow and fell to the ground, sinking two feet into the earth. Upon digging, a piece of mysterious gold was found, measuring over a foot wide and seven inches high. Mushroom: In the early Tianbao period, a miraculous mushroom grew on a pillar in the home of Li Jiayin in Linchuan, resembling a deity. The governor Zhang Jingyi cut down the pillar and presented it to the court. Turtles: In the fourth year of Jianzhong, in Ningjin County, Shaozhou, there was a large pear tree near the Yellow River. Villagers often prayed there, and suddenly a group of snakes appeared, forming two piles on the north bank and one on the south bank. Soon, three turtles, each about an inch in diameter, appeared and walked around the piles, causing all the snakes to die. The turtles then climbed onto the piles, revealing that the snakes' bellies had wounds as if shot by arrows. The governor Kang Rizhi drew a picture of the pear tree and the three turtles and presented it to the court.

Snow, in the second year of the Zhenyuan era, a heavy snowfall occurred in Chang'an, covering the level ground with snow over a foot deep. On top of the snow, there was a layer of black soot or ash. Rain of Wood, in the fourth year of the Zhenyuan era, pieces of wood rained down in Chenliu, about the size of a finger and about an inch long. Each piece had a hole through the center and stood upright as if planted, covering an area of more than ten miles. Tooth, in the country of Vajrayana, there was a tooth of a golden wheel king, measuring three inches in length. Stone Pillar, in another land called Jiehua, there was a stone pillar over seventy feet tall, built by King Asoka. It had a dark blue color, was shiny, and cast shadows that reflected the good or bad deeds of people. Sandalwood Drum, to the southeast of Khotan, there was a large river that irrigated the entire country. Suddenly, the river's flow was interrupted, and the king of Khotan, consulting with the monk Luo Hong, learned that this was due to the actions of a dragon. The king performed a ritual to appease the dragon, and a woman emerged from the water, stating that her husband had died and she wished to marry a high-ranking official so that the river could return to normal. A high-ranking official volunteered to marry the woman, and the entire country sent him off. The official rode a white horse into the water without drowning. After entering the river, the white horse emerged with a sandalwood drum and a sealed letter. Opening the letter, it instructed that the drum should be hung in the southeast of the city and would sound when enemies approached. Later, when enemies arrived, the drum indeed sounded.

Stone Shoes, there are stone shoes at the Sha-li Temple in the kingdom of Khotan. Stone Mound Stone, there is a stone mound stone to the east of He-mu County, and when broken open, it reveals traces of a horse, symbolizing wealth or status. Relics, in the kingdom of Kapiśa, there is a stupa containing relics, which are commonly seen, resembling beads on a banner, circling around a tree or pillar (or possibly a column). Flea-like Image, on the stone walls of Gandhāra, there is a Buddha statue. Initially, the stone wall had golden flea-like creatures, some as large as a finger, others as small as grains of rice, gradually gnawing and carving the stone wall to form the shape of a Buddha. Burnt Rice, in the kingdom of Gandhāra, the granary of King Śibi was burned, and the burnt rice remains to this day. Consuming a single grain can prevent malaria forever. Boots of a Pratyekabuddha, there are boots of a Pratyekabuddha at the Zanmo Temple in the kingdom of Khotan, neither made of leather nor dyed, and they do not decay even after many years. Stone Camel's Urine, in the northern mountains of Kauśāmbī, there is stone camel's urine, with water droplets falling from it. When contained in vessels of gold, silver, copper, iron, pottery, or wood, they all leak, but only a gourd can hold them. Consuming it can make one shed all their unpleasant body hair and attain immortality. This is mentioned in *Lunheng*. Person-like Tree, southwest of the Abbasid Caliphate, two thousand li away, there is a country where, in the valleys, person-like heads grow on tree branches, resembling flowers, but unable to speak. When people ask them, they just smile, and if they smile repeatedly, they fall off.

Horses, the country of Juiwei cultivates horse breeds, and the horses of the Great Food (Taizhi) country understand human language. Stone figures, on the sea of Lai Zi country there are stone figures, one and a half zhang long, ten chi in circumference. In ancient times, Emperor Qin Shi Huang sent these stone figures to chase Mount Laoshan but failed, so they were placed here. Bronze horse, in the middle of the riverbank of the Bird Hu River in the country of Juede, there is a fire temple. It is said that the fire god originally came from the country of Persia with supernatural powers, frequently seen miraculous phenomena, so a fire temple was built. Inside there is no image, but large and small furnaces are placed under the big house, with the eaves facing west and people worshipping facing east. There is a bronze horse, as large as a regular horse, said by the locals to have come from heaven, with its front legs bent in the air and standing facing the god, and its hind legs buried in the ground. Since ancient times, there have been several attempts to dig it up, reaching depths of tens of zhang, but never reaching its hooves. In the Western Regions, May is considered the beginning of the year, and every year on this day, a golden-colored horse appears in the Bird Hu River, echoing the sound of the bronze horse, and then disappears back into the water. Recently, a king of the Great Food country did not believe it and entered the fire temple to destroy it, but suddenly a fire burned his soldiers, so he dared not destroy it. Snake desert, in the northwest of the country of Sudusen, there is a snake desert, the snake plains stretch for more than five hundred li north and south, with snakes everywhere in the middle, and poisonous gas like smoke. Flying birds fall to the ground, and snakes eat them. Sometimes they eat each other, and sometimes they eat fresh grass. Stone crocodile, in the Jin Liao Mountain temple in the country of Siketiao, there is a stone crocodile. When the monks' food was about to run out, they bowed to the stone crocodile, and then the food was replenished.

The Temple Kitchen, in the country of Juzhen, venerates ghosts and spirits. Twenty miles north of the city, across the Zhenjiang River, there is a deity worshipped during the spring and autumn seasons. Whenever the king needed goods or silverware, they would naturally appear in the temple kitchen, and then disappear after the sacrifice was completed. Empress Wu sent someone to verify this, and it was found to be true. The Poisonous Spear, the southern barbarians have a poisonous spear with no blade, resembling rusted iron. It kills a person without drawing blood. It is said to fall from the sky and can be found in the ground after a ritual. The Armor, in the east of Liaodong, there is a lock armor. Goguryeo says it fell from the sky during the time of the Former Yan. The Earth Betel Nut, resembling betel nuts, is found in holes or crevices. Fresh ones are still soft, and it is said to be the droppings of toads. It does not appear often and is used to treat malignant sores. The Ghost Excrement, it appears in damp, dark places, with a pale yellowish-white color. Sometimes it is seen and used to treat sores. The Stone Fence, it grows at the bottom of the sea, about one foot high, with roots and stems with holes like marks made by objects. Fishermen catch it using nets. When first taken out of the water, it is a bright red color, but gradually turns blue when exposed to the wind. It is used to treat kidney stones. The Wall Shadow, in Gaoyou County, there is a temple, not named, with a lecture hall whose western wall leans against the road. Every evening, the shadows of people, horses, carriages, and carts pass through the wall. Those wearing red or purple can be vaguely distinguished in the shadow. The wall is several feet thick, making it difficult to explain the phenomenon. It does not appear during the hours of辰 (Chen) and午 (Wu). It has been like this for more than twenty years, sometimes not appearing for a year or half a year. Cheng Shi heard about it from guests and monks in Yangzhou during the early Tahe period.

A Stone in Vinegar Cheng Shi, a group of his relatives, said that when he was young, he once destroyed a bird's nest and found a black stone about the size of an egg, smooth and round, which was very lovely. Later, he accidentally placed it in a vinegar container, and suddenly felt the stone move. Looking at it carefully, he saw four legs like silk threads. When he lifted it, the legs also retracted. A Peach Pit Du Zhi, an Assistant Department Officer of the Water Department, often saw people in the Jianghuai market using a peach pit to measure rice, which could hold one catty. He said he obtained it from the Nine Peaks Mountain stream. A Human Foot Yuan Gu, a recluse, said that in the early years of the Zhenyuan era, he once traveled with Daoist companions to Mount Hua. In the valley, he saw a human thigh, with new stockings and shoes, cut off at the knee, with no signs of wounds. A Porcelain Bowl In the Jianghuai region, there was a scholar who lived in a rural area. His son, who was over twenty years old, often suffered from illness. One day, his father was drinking tea, and suddenly a bubble rose in the cup, higher than the rim, clear and shiny like jade. Inside the bubble, there was a person, one inch tall, standing on the bubble, higher than the rim. Looking at him closely, he saw that the clothes and appearance were those of his son. After a while, the bubble burst, and nothing was seen. The tea bowl was as before, but there was a slight crack. Several days later, his son began to exhibit supernatural abilities, translating divine words, predicting good or bad fortune without error. An Iron Mirror Sung Feng was skilled in the properties of herbs, enjoyed reading Daoist books, and could discuss names and principles. Fan Huang once gave him some silk and cloth. He had an iron mirror, five inches in diameter, with a large nose like a fist, which he said he obtained from a Daoist. It had no other unusual features, but when several people looked into it together, each could only see their own reflection, not the others'.

Tiger Skin, the Salt and Iron Official of Prince Yongning, had an ancient tiger skin, large like a hand, with whiskers and tail spots like a dog. Human Mummy, in Li Zhangwu, there was a human mummy, three inches long, with a well-formed head and ribcage, said to be from the Qiao Jiao people. Cow Gallstones, the yellow gallstone is found in the cow's gallbladder, and a cow with yellow gallstones may expel or play with it. The scholar and proofreader Zhang Xifu at the Imperial Library recounted that once a person obtained the expelled yellow gallstone, cut it open, and found a butterfly-like creature inside that flew away. Shangqing Pearl, when Emperor Suzong was a child, he was often favored by Emperor Xuanzong. Whenever he sat in front of him, Emperor Xuanzong would look at his face and say to Empress Wu Huifei, "This child has an extraordinary appearance; one day he will also be a fortunate emperor of our family." Therefore, he ordered the Shangqing Jade Pearl to be taken, wrapped in red silk, and tied around his neck. It was a tribute from the country of Tabgach during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, bright and white, capable of illuminating a room. When viewed, it showed images of immortals, jade maidens, clouds, and cranes moving inside. After he ascended the throne, divine light often appeared in the treasure vault. One day, the keeper of the treasure vault reported the incident to the emperor, who said, "Is it not the Shangqing Pearl?" He then ordered it to be brought out, and the red silk was still intact. He shed tears and showed the pearl to his close ministers, saying, "This was a gift from Emperor Xuanzong when I was a child." He then ordered it to be stored in a jade box and placed in his bedroom. Whenever natural disasters or military conflicts occurred in various regions, he would pray sincerely to the pearl, and it always responded to his prayers.

In the jurisdiction of Chu Prefecture, there is a small mountain with a room on it but no water. Monk Zhiyi dug a well, and after digging three zhang (approximately 3 meters), he encountered a stone. After chiseling a hole in the stone and reaching the soil, he dug another fifty feet and found a piece of jade, one foot two inches long and four feet wide. The jade was red like □□, with six turtle patterns on each side, and it was very beautiful, appearing as if it could hold water. The monk accidentally struck one corner to examine it, and it started to bleed. The bleeding stopped after half a month. In Shu County, there is a mountain pavilion that is very secluded and quiet, where a Taoist priest named Deyang lives. During the Taihe period, the Taoist priest once climbed the altar alone one night and saw a strange light suddenly appear in the courtyard, coming from the well spring. Soon, something appeared, shaped like a rabbit, with a color like pure gold. Following the light, it emerged and circled the altar. After a while, it returned to the well. From then on, he saw it every night. The Taoist priest found this strange and dared not tell anyone. Later, when he cleaned the well, he found a golden rabbit, very small, with a wondrous and brilliant light. He immediately placed it in his hairbox. At that time, the Censorate official Li Rong served in Puzhen and was friends with the Taoist priest, who then gave it to him. Later, Li Rong was promoted from the magistrate of Fengxian County to the governor of Xinzhou. His golden rabbit suddenly disappeared. After more than a month, Li Rong died.

Li Shigu was constructing a mountain pavilion when he dug up an object resembling an iron axe head. At the time, Li Zhangwu was traveling in Dongping, and Shigu showed it to him. Zhangwu was startled and said, "This is a forbidden object, capable of drinking three dou of blood." After testing it, they found his claim to be true. ● Volume Eleven: Broad Knowledge There is a superstition that one should not go up to the roof in May, as it is said that people shed their skin in May. If one sees a shadow on the roof, their soul will depart. Gold that has been buried in a tomb and later transformed into hairpins, bracelets, or other utensils is called "dishonored gold" by Tao Hongjing, and it is unsuitable for alchemical refinement. When refining copper, one should accompany a young girl. Pouring water over the copper will cause it to split into two segments. The raised part is called "male copper," and the recessed part is called "female copper." If a cooking pot does not boil, it is because an object resembling a pig is residing in it. Removing the object will stop the problem. If a stove becomes moist without reason, it is because a red frog named "Gouzhu" is residing in it. Removing the frog will stop the moisture. Those who drink alcohol will have a greenish face if their liver qi is weak, and a reddish face if their heart qi is weak. When one's pulse is strong and angry, their face turns green; when their bones are angry, their face turns white; when their blood is strong and angry, their face turns red. Mountains tend to produce more males, marshes tend to produce more females, water tends to produce more deaf people, wind tends to produce more blind people, wood tends to produce more hunchbacks, stone tends to produce more strong people, rugged terrain tends to produce more goiters, summer tends to produce more crippled people, clouds tend to produce more long-lived people, valleys tend to produce more people with Φ, hills tend to produce more people with , plains tend to produce more benevolent people, and highlands tend to produce more greedy people.

Various gods and spirits with different names, the god of the brain is called Jueyuan, the god of hair is called Xuanhua, the god of the eyes is called Xujian, the god of blood is called Chonglongwang, and the god of the tongue is called Shiliang. Those who study the Dao must sound the heavenly drum to summon the gods. Knocking the left side is the heavenly bell; if one encounters misfortune or evil, knock it. Knocking the right side is the heavenly chime; if one passes through mountains, lakes, or evil places, knock it. Knocking the center up and down is called the heavenly drum; meditate and think of the path when sounding it. Knock it thirty-six times, or thirty-two times, or twenty-seven times, or twenty-four times, or twelve times. According to the *Yinjue*, the Outer Techniques of Taqing: If human hair is hung on a fruit tree, birds will not eat its fruit. If a melon has two noses and two stems, eating it will kill you. Vegetables dripping from the eaves are poisonous. Yellow flowers and red mustard (one says mustard) can kill you. If a cow tramples the seedlings of a gourd, the fruit will be bitter. One should not lie on millet stalks after heavy drinking, as sweat will cause hair loss. If a pregnant woman eats dried ginger, it will cause the fetus to dissolve. Eating frosty vegetables in October will make one's face dull. One should not eat old pickles in March. A sedge garment can treat scabies. Grass from the well can stop a child from crying at night; place it under the mother's pillow without her knowing. Algae from the bottom of a boat can treat epidemics. Straw from a widow's mat can cure a child's cholera. If someone hangs themselves, the person who tied the rope will go mad. A filial son's collar ash can be applied to dry skin. Ash from the roosting wood of a chicken in the east neighbor's house can treat loss of voice. The dirt on a chopping board can erode the soles of shoes. An old lining board used as the bottom of a zither can harmonize yin and yang and communicate with spirits. Fish have eyelashes, and when their eyes close, their bellies contain pearls.

A person with two different-colored eyes, or a creature with continuous white scales or spots, and a red mark on its underside, can all kill people. A turtle with white eyes and a mark shaped like a "五" (or sometimes "丹") or "卜" (or sometimes "十") on its underside should not be eaten. A crab with hair on its underside can kill people. If you burn a snake with mulberry wood, its feet will become visible. Animals with forked tails, deer with spots like a leopard, and sheep with holes in their hearts all harm people. Horses with glowing eyes at night, if eaten after May, can kill people. The hanging hooves of dogs are toxic. Eating the meat under the saddle of a white horse can harm the five internal organs. Crows that die naturally have eyes that do not close. Ducks with white eyes, crows with four spurs, and eggs with an eight-shaped mark can all kill people. If any flying bird falls into a household well, there must be something, and it should be pulled out and released. The water source should not be interrupted, and boiling well water should not be drunk. Wine and water without shadows should not be drunk. Venomous snakes and frogs are the most venomous among snakes. When a snake is angry, the poison is in its head and tail. Tombs and wells that trap air can kill people in autumn and summer. First, throw chicken feathers into it; if the feathers fall straight down, there is no poison. If they dance down, they are not to be touched. Douse it with several buckets of vinegar, then it can be entered. Polished glass is formed from ice over a thousand years. Glass and jade can be softened with natural ash from the South Sea and can be carved. Jade is formed from ghost blood. The *Xuanzhong Ji* states: "Maple resin that enters the ground becomes amber." The *Shi Shuo* says: "Peach sap that enters the ground transforms into something." The *Huainanzi* states: "Rabbit silk is the seed of amber."

Supernatural writings, such as those with a fatal aura, and scripts derived from ancient artifacts, including the "Hanging Needle," "Dripping Dew," "Qin King's Tomb-Breaking," "Golden Magpie," "Tiger Claw," "Upside-Down Leek," "Leaning Wave," "Flag Signal," "Flying Silk," "Zhou," "Mistake," "Seal," "Edict," "Column," "Daily," "Monthly," "Wind," "Office," "Insect-Eaten Leaf," "Xiongnu," "Pine Needle," "Indian," "Regular," "Horizontal," "Lingzhi Grass," "Bell," "Drum," "Dragon and Tiger Seal," "Qilin Seal," "Fish Seal," "Insect Seal," "Crow Seal," "Mouse Seal," "Ox," "Rabbit," "Cursive," "Dragon Cursive," "Wolf," "Dog," "Chicken," "Thunder," "Reversed Left," "Running Pressured," "Bow," "Scene," and "Half Cursive" scripts, are numerous and diverse. For official summonses, the "Tiger Claw" script is used because it is difficult to learn, thereby preventing fraud and forgery. Imperial decrees employ the "Leaning Wave" script. Thank-you letters and imperial edicts use the "芮脚" script. Seals and tokens are inscribed with the "Crow" script. Court greetings and marriage ceremonies utilize the "Caution" or "Fill" script. Scripts from the Western Regions include "Donkey Lip," "Lotus Leaf," "Festival," "Great Qin," "Caravan," "Cow," "Leaf," "Resurrection," "Stone Spiral," "Reversed," "Heavenly," "Dragon," and "Bird Sound" scripts, among others, totaling sixty-four varieties.

Hu Cong and the Scholarly Artifact During the reign of Sun Quan, a copper box was unearthed. The box was two chi and seven cun long, with a glass lid. Inside was another artifact, a white jade "如意" (a ceremonial object). The places where it was held were carved with dragons, tigers, and cicadas, but no one could identify its origin. Someone asked Hu Cong about it, and he replied, "In ancient times, Qin Shi Huang believed that Jinling had the aura of a future emperor. He leveled the hills and mountains, burying treasures everywhere to counteract this imperial aura. Could this be the case?" To the west of Dengcheng, about a hundred li away, there is a city called Gucheng, the capital of the state of Gubai. At the city gate, there is a stone figure with an inscription on its belly: "Modou, Modou, be cautious in speaking." It is suspected that this is similar to the "seal-lips" inscription on the golden figures in the Imperial Ancestral Temple. Two li north of Licheng lies the Lotus Seed Lake, which is twenty li in circumference. The lake is filled with lotus flowers, red and green, shining in the sunlight, and at first glance, it seems like dyed brocade. Fishing boats are scattered, and fishing nets are sparsely arranged, appearing from a distance like floating spider webs. Once, Yuan Fan of the Wei dynasty held a banquet by the lake. A military officer named Zhang Boyu consulted with him, saying, "We tried to make blood soup, but it failed repeatedly." Yuan Fan said, "If you take water from the Luo River, it will definitely work." They followed his advice, and it succeeded. At the time, Prince Qinghe found this strange and asked Yuan Fan, "What is the meaning of this?" Yuan Fan said, "Think about 'lake eye.'" Prince Qinghe laughed and agreed, but he did not understand. After the banquet, Yuan Fan spoke to the clerk, Fang Shudao, saying, "I still do not understand the 'lake eye' matter." Fang Shudao replied, "Lotus roots can disperse blood. 'Lake eye' refers to the lotus seed, so the master thought about it." Prince Qinghe sighed, saying, "A person who does not read is like walking in the dark. An old man with gray hair is not as good as a young scholar."

The Chief Guest of the Liang court, Lu Mian, said to the Wei envoy Wei Jin: "When I went to Ye, I saw the twin gatehouses towering very high with beautiful decorations. The stone gatehouse here is also not inferior. I have a ruler by Xun Xu, made of copper with golden inscriptions, which is treasured by our family. The late Prince of Linchuan loved to collect ancient artifacts and took it into the palace. This gatehouse was completed and measured with the copper ruler, its height being six zhang." Wei Jin replied: "The grand gatehouses of my capital city are indeed the splendid gatehouses of the central sky. The terrain here is too low, so it cannot be high by nature." Wei Zhao Shi said: "The ruler by Xun Xu is made by accumulating grains of millet and used to tune musical scales. Ruan Xian criticized its sound as having a narrow and confined quality. Later, a jade ruler was obtained to measure it, and it was found to be too short." An old saying goes that those who cannot see the auxiliary star will die. Among my relatives and friends, who often meet in the neighborhood of Xiu Xing Li, those who could not see it died within a year. It is said that recognizing the person star prevents one from suffering from malaria. Among my relatives and friends, those who recognize it all suffer from malaria. It is also said that people do not want to look at the star of heaven's prison. If there is a shooting star, one should sit with hair loose and weep, wait for the star to reappear, and the disaster will then be averted. The *Jin Lou Zi* says: "I am troubled by looking up and predicting, enduring frost and dew, and fearing that a shooting star will enter the celestial prison." Only now do I know that these folk taboos have been long-standing.

A Monk Named Na Zhao from Zhi Ji Temple in Jingzhou was skilled in archery. He often said that deer whose light was long and flickering, rabbits whose light was close to the ground and flickering, and tigers that were low and still could be identified. He also said that when hunting tigers at night, one would definitely see three tigers coming together, and one should stab the middle one. When the tiger died, its power entered the ground, and obtaining it could ward off all evil. When the tiger first died, one should mark the place where its head rested and dig during the moonless night. When digging, a tiger would definitely come to roar and throw itself in front and behind, but this was not to be feared, as it was the ghost of the tiger. Digging two feet deep, one would obtain something like tiger amber, which was the tiger's gaze sinking into the ground. He also said that the hawk's feathers could eat the feathers of all birds and were also good at making wind feathers. The method of making wind feathers was to remove the fletching three inches and drill a small hole, making it pass through the fletching and carve a wind channel one grain deep, from the fletching to the hole, then there was no need for feathers. The Taoist De Cai Zhen said that human shadows could reach up to nine. Cheng Shi often tried it, and it reached up to six or seven. The outer ones could not be distinguished, but De said that gradually increasing the light could distinguish them. He also said that the nine shadows each had names: the shadow spirit was named Right Emperor, the second was named Ghosts or Spirits, the third was named Release the Key, the fourth was named One-foot Duck, the fifth was named Search Gate, the sixth was named Soul Slave, the seventh was named Stove (unclear character), the eighth was named Pig Spirit Embryo, and the ninth was a fish that ate everything and could not be distinguished. The old copy of the nine shadow names was written on rough paper, and the two characters below were eaten by fish and could not be remembered.

During the reign of Emperor Wenzong (825-827 CE), there was a recluse named Wang who chose the day of one's birth year to light lamps at the fifth watch and observe shadows to predict good or bad omens. He said that a person's shadow should be deep, and if it is deep, it signifies nobility and longevity. The shadow should not be reflected in water, wells, or bathing tubs, as ancient people avoided shadows for this reason. Ancient superstitions about creatures like the ancient蠼螋, short foxes, and shadow-walking insects all harm people through their shadows. Recently, there are people who are good at treating illnesses by burning shadows. In the capital, Buddhist temples often have sacred birds that do not defile themselves. The recluse Zhang Ying from Fengxiang is skilled in flying and transforming according to the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches. He says that if there are no sacred birds gathering at a temple's Vajra, it is not because the temple is not sacred, but because the location where the soil was taken and the time when the statue was made coincidentally matched the auspicious days and hours. He also says that by observing the images facing south in temples and observatories, one can know their wealth and poverty. During the Yuan Wei dynasty, the Indian monk Bodhidharma claimed to have obtained the true image. Some say that dragon blood turning into amber when it enters the ground. According to the *Nanman Ji* (Records of the Southern Barbarians), in the sand of Ningzhou, there are bent-waist bees; when the bank collapses, the bees emerge, and the locals burn and refine them to make amber. The recluse Li Hong is skilled in talismans and has extensive knowledge. He often told Cheng Shi: "Ceramic and earthenware with cracks can be discarded. Once I encountered a Taoist who said that thunder insects and ghosts often hide in such objects." Recently, in Buddhist paintings, there are images of the Bodhisattvas of Heaven's Treasury and Earth's Treasury. Upon closer inspection, the colors and patterns are dazzling, as if they are emitting light. Some say that if verdigris and wall fish are used to set the colors, they will appear luminous up close. Often, in murals depicting monks and deities, the eyes follow the viewer, and the pupils are extremely accurate.

A scholar named Gu Feixiong said, "When fishing, one should target the one that spins around. If it loses its leader, the other fish will not scatter, and they can be caught in an instant." The monk Guangsheng of Cien Temple said, "At the end of the reign of Emperor Dezong, there was a monk named Lingjian from Langzhou who was skilled in archery. His arrowheads were square, made from three pounds of soil from under the sandbank of Dongting Lake, three liang of charcoal powder, one liang of porcelain powder, half a liang of elm bark, two shao of sediment, two liang of purple mineral, three fen of fine sand, five sheets of vine paper, and half a he of dried herb juice. He mixed nine ingredients and pounded them three thousand times, then rolled them into a ball and dried them in the shade. When Zheng Zhuang was the governor, there was a household named Yin who studied and was good at drinking. Zheng Zhuang greatly valued him. Later, he became a thief and was executed. Yin often visited Lingjian to compete in archery. Once, Yin pointed to a branch, and the distance between the branches was dozens of steps. He said, 'If I hit it, the reward is five thousand.' With one shot, he hit the target, and the arrow bounced back without breaking. It was only when it reached Lingjian that the arrow was broken." When Wang Yanwei, the Grand Secretary, was in Bianzhou, in the second year, there was a summer drought. At that time, Yuan Wang Zhuang Jiyi stayed in Bianzhou. During a banquet, he spoke about the drought. Jiyi, drunk, said, "It's very easy to make it rain. You can find four snake doctors, two ten-stone jars, and fill each jar with water. Float two snake doctors in each jar, cover them with wood and seal tightly, and place them separately in quiet places. Set up seats in front of and behind the jars and burn incense. Select more than ten children under ten years old, have them hold small green bamboo sticks, and take turns striking the jars day and night, without stopping for a moment." Wang Yanwei tried it as suggested, and it rained heavily for one day and two nights. According to old sayings, dragons and snake masters are in-laws.

### Chapter Twelve: Speech Resources #### In Wei Ming Temple, Licheng County There is a stele of General Han in Wei Ming Temple, Licheng County, which was constructed during the Tahe era of the Northern Wei dynasty. It was said that the inscription on this stele is the best in terms of wording and meaning. He often kept one copy in his pillow, so his family named this pillow the "Qilin Box." General Han's name was Qilin. Yuxin composed poetry, using a story from *The Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital*. He immediately corrected it himself, saying, "This is Wu Jun's phrase, I'm afraid it's not suitable." Wei Zhao Shi said, "There are many ancient people who composed songs. However, *The Parrot Rhapsody* is included in the collections of Mi Heng and Pan Ni, and *The Chess Rhapsody* has the same words from Cao Zhi and Zuo Si. What did the ancients intend by this?" Junfang replied, "Poets naturally like to adopt each other's works. If the words are identical, it's good that later people can't distinguish them." Wei Wei Jin said, "Sometimes *The Nine Bestowals* is attributed to Wang Can, and *The Six Dynasties* also mentions Cao Zhi." Yuxin said, "There are no talented scholars from the south today. The whole world admires Wen Zisheng, who alone dominates in Ye. I often see his writing and style, which is also worthy of being called a far-famed reputation. Recently, I got several volumes of steles by Wei Shou, whose works are rich and elegant. He is particularly a high talent."

The Liang dynasty sent Huangmen Shilang Ming Shaoxia, Moling Magistrate Xie Zao, Xingwei Changshi Wang Zuanchong, Xuancheng Wang Wenxue Xiao Kai, Jian Sanqi Changshi Yuan Xia, and Jian Tongzhi Sanqi Changshi He Wenfa to host a banquet for the Wei envoys Li Qian and Cui (name unclear). After the formalities were concluded, Ming Shaoxia recited a poem to Li Qian and said: "The wind rustling (or solemnly) lifts the curtains, still imaginable." Li Qian replied: "Not as good as 'the lamp flowers do not freeze,' which is most fitting to the current situation." Ming Shaoxia responded that his poem contained these words. Cui (name unclear) asked Ming Shaoxia: "This year is unusually cold; is the area between the Yangtze and Huai rivers not frozen?" Ming Shaoxia replied: "Here, even if there is thin ice, it does not hinder travel, unlike the river ice that freezes solid and can support vehicles." Yuan Xia said: "There are fox tracks on the river ice, which is suitable for people to cross." Cui (name unclear) said: "It should be a fox; it is probably a mistake in the text." Ming Shaoxia said: "Yes. Foxes are often suspicious, and weasels are often cautious. That is why 'doubt and hesitation' is said." Cui (name unclear) said: "Magpies build nests to avoid the wind, and pheasants leave bad governance; these are virtues of birds. Foxes being suspicious and weasels being cautious can be called shortcomings of animals."

Xu Junfang, from Liang, urged Wei Shi Jin to drink wine, and he finished a cup in one gulp, smiling and saying, "Extraordinarily refreshing!" Wei replied, "You have never before drunk a whole cup of wine in one go when you were in Ye. Since arriving at Wu Zhou, you have always finished your wine without leaving a single drop." Xu replied, "I actually drink less, but it is a habit. I have slightly improved, but not due to any particular reason." Yu Xin said, "The amount of wine one drinks varies with age and time, making it difficult to measure." Wei Zhao Shi said, "Xu Junfang's drinking habits change with his mood and environment. It is unknown whether he will drink more or less in the future."

During the Liang Dynasty's banquet for Wei envoys, Wei Zhao raised a cup to encourage Chen Zhao, saying, "After this banquet, we will be separated for a while. Thinking about this makes me very melancholic." Chen replied, "I admire famous scholars, and how can I stop? We did not fully express our deep feelings on the journey, and now we are separated again. How can I not be moved to tears!" Soon, the wine arrived in a parrot-shaped cup. Xu Junfang did not finish drinking and passed the cup to Zhao. Zhao said, "The sea snail coils, its tail and wings spread out. It is not just for play, but also for punishment. You truly cannot avoid this responsibility today." Xin said, "The younger son likes to engage in divination." Zhao then ordered a refill. Junfang asked Xin, "Why are you so eager to hold it?" Zhao replied, "This is called acting straightforwardly, not like the metaphor of beans and stalks." Junfang then overturned the bowl. Xin said to Jin and Zhao, "Just now, my family sent some fine wine, sealed in mud, but I don't know what it tastes like. I will not taste it first; I will respectfully offer it to you." Zhao said, "Every time there is a valuable treasure, it often causes much trouble, but it is better to have more gradually."

Duke Ning often hunted in the boundaries of Xian County. While searching the forest, he suddenly saw a cabinet in the grass, tightly locked. The duke ordered it to be opened, and inside was a young girl. He asked her about her origin, and she replied, "I am surnamed Mo, and I live in a village with my uncles. Last night, I encountered a band of robbers, among whom were two monks, who kidnapped me here." She moved gracefully with a slight frown, her charming demeanor radiating. The duke was both surprised and pleased, and then had her ride in the rear carriage. At that time, someone who admired the duke had just captured a bear, which he placed in the cabinet, locking it as before. At that time, the emperor was seeking the most beautiful women. Duke Ning, presenting her as a woman of good family, immediately submitted a report to the emperor, detailing her story. The emperor ordered her to be a concubine. After three days, the governor of Jingzhao reported that in a restaurant in Xian County, there were two monks. They paid one thousand coins to rent the restaurant for one day and night, saying they were performing a religious ceremony, carrying only a cabinet into the restaurant. After a long time at night, there was a sound of a bear. The restaurant owner was surprised that the door was not opened at dawn, and removed the door to look inside. A bear rushed out, and the two monks were dead, their bones exposed. The emperor heard about it and laughed, writing a letter to Duke Ning: "Duke Ning is truly capable of dealing with these monks." Concubine Mo could sing Qin songs, and at that time, she was known as the "voice of Concubine Mo." Master Yi originally did not understand chess, but at a gathering at Yan Gong's house, he watched a game played by Wang Jixian. He then challenged him, laughing and saying to Yan Gong, "This is just a matter of who goes first. If you remember my four sentences on addition and subtraction, then everyone can be a national master." Master Luo Sheng, while playing chess, picked up the dead pieces of his opponent, and the empty spaces formed shapes like dragons and phoenixes. Some say that after Wang Jixian finished a chess game with Emperor Xuanzong, he took all the pieces out.

A Man with a Nickname, a Nobleman's Garden, and a General's Spear During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, there was a man named Huang Bianque, who was short and unrefined but clever. Emperor Xuanzong often relied on him to travel. Whenever he asked about matters outside, he often received gifts or rewards. He was nicknamed "fleshly stool" or "meat stool." One day, he arrived late, and the emperor was surprised. Huang explained, "Today it rained heavily, and I just now encountered a police officer who argued with me over the road. I pushed him off his horse." He then knelt at the steps. The emperor said, "There is no report from outside; you have nothing to fear." He allowed Huang to ride with him again. Soon after, the mayor of the capital submitted a memorial to discuss the matter, and the emperor immediately ordered him to be beaten and killed. In the garden of Fang's family in Licheng, there was a mountain and water area belonging to the nobleman Fang Bao. The garden was filled with dense and tall trees, majestic and deep springs and stones, and beautiful scenery. Once, someone broke a branch of a paulownia tree, and the nobleman said, "What is the harm in breaking my phoenix branch?" From then on, people no longer dared to break the branches. The nobleman told his military secretary Yin Xiaoyi, "In the past, the beauty of Shi Chong's Jin Valley was no greater than this." Yin replied, "I once visited the west of Luoyang and toured the old place. Comparing the two, indeed, as you have taught." Yin often wanted to return to Ye, and poets饯宿于此. Yin wrote a poem: "The wind lingers over the waters of Licheng, and the moon leans against the trees of Mount Hua." At the time, people compared these two lines to Xie Lingyun's "pool and pond" ten characters. When Dan Xiongxin was young, a jujube tree was planted in front of his school. When he was eighteen, he cut it down and made it into a spear, which was 17 feet long, had a circular measurement that did not fit together, and weighed 70 pounds. It was called "cold bone white." He often met the soldiers of the Prince of Qin (later Emperor Taizong of Tang). The Prince of Qin shot the blade with a large white feather, causing fire to come out. It was broken by Wei Chi Jingde.

Qin Shubao's horse, named Hulei Bao, was often given wine to drink. Every time it was tested under the bright moon, it could leap over three layers of black felt. When Hu Gong passed away, the horse neighed and refused to eat before dying. Xu Jiyi, in his teens, was fond of shooting and throwing darts. Prince Ying often said, "This child has an ominous appearance; he will bring disaster to our clan." He always shot arrows that flew beyond the target, and when riding, he was like a shadow, faster than even experienced riders. Prince Ying often went hunting, and once he ordered Xu Jiyi to enter the forest to chase the beasts, then set fire to the forest to kill him. Knowing there was no place to hide, Xu Jiyi killed the horse and hid inside its belly. After the fire passed, he stood up covered in blood, and Prince Ying was greatly surprised. Emperor Xuanzong often kept an eye on his princes, and Prince Ning often played the drum in the summer, sweating profusely, while reading the music score of Kucha. The emperor heard of this and said happily, "Brothers of the emperor should enjoy themselves to the fullest." Wei Shuyue, when he came to Lin Dai, on the seventh day of the seventh month, climbed Mount Shun, looked around, and said to his clerk Cui, "I have traveled many places, but as for fertile lands, strategic positions, and famous states, none can surpass this. But I wonder how Dongyang compares?" Cui replied, "Qing has ancient renown, Qi has its old title, and the two places have similar landscapes. I have heard of their discussions, and they cannot surpass each other." Wei Shuyue then ordered a poem to be written. At that time, during the transition between old and new, the position was vacant, and no one could find a pen. He then used a five-bar staff to write a poem on the north wall of the hall: "Responsible for duties without the wind of virtue, the return path blocked by mountains and rivers. Still thinking of the day with banners and umbrellas, leaving thanks to this mountain and valley."

At the Eastern Shrine of Shun, there is a large stone, about three zhang wide, on which the four characters "not drunk, not returning" are carved. The official said, "This is not a legacy of virtue," and ordered it to be chiseled away. During a banquet hosted by the Liang state for envoys from the Wei state, including Li Qian and Cui (with an unclear character), music was performed. One of the Liang officials, He Ji, remarked, "The music deeply moves people." Cui replied, "In the past, Shen Xi recognized his mother's song and was moved to tears, which truly demonstrates the profound and subtle nature of music." Wang Ke, another official from the Liang state, said, "Listening to music to observe customs, it is indeed refined." Cui responded, "Yanling once sent envoys to a powerful country, and there was indeed the beauty of observing customs." He Ji asked, "Are you challenging me with these words?" Li Qian replied, "I request to hold the whip and bow, to engage with you in combat." He Ji said, "I dare not retreat three encampments." Cui added, "The matter of repeated retreats, we have long since apologized." He Ji then said, "The chariots are in disarray, the flags are down, I fear there is no place to return." Cui responded, "At the battle of Pingyin, the signal was given early." Wang Ke said, "I am about to celebrate the harvest and honor military achievements." Li Qian asked, "Who will be in charge if Wang Yishi is absent?" They all laughed and stopped the conversation. As the music was about to end, several dozen horses galloped past. There were no eunuchs present, and Li Qian remarked, "The eunuch is the same as the horse driver, is this not an invasion of office?" He Ji replied, "This is merely an appearance." Cui added, "If we were to plant ourselves like Yuan Shao, we might not be able to avoid it."

Wang Bo, when composing inscriptions and eulogies, would first grind several sheng of ink, then cover his face with a blanket and lie down. Suddenly, he would rise and write in one continuous stroke, without any revisions, and people at the time called this "draft in the belly." In his youth, he had a dream where someone gave him a full sleeve of ink balls. Yan Gong often read Wang Bo's eulogy for his teacher's school. He found the first four lines, from "Dìchē" to "Tàijiǎ," completely incomprehensible. He consulted one of the officials, who explained: "The Big Dipper is in the position of Wu, and the seven planets are in the south, indicating this auspicious sign, and a saint without a position will appear." The rest of the eulogy, starting from "Huá Gài," remained entirely incomprehensible. Li Bai's fame spread throughout the land. Emperor Xuanzong summoned him to a small hall, where Li Bai appeared with a lofty and bright demeanor, as if rising like a rainbow. The emperor forgot his imperial dignity and ordered Li Bai to remove his shoes. Li Bai then extended his foot to Gao Lishi and said, "Take off my boots." Gao Lishi, feeling humiliated, quickly removed them. When Li Bai left, the emperor pointed to him and told Gao Lishi, "This person has a poor fortune." Li Bai had attempted the imperial examinations three times but was unsatisfied with the results, so he burned all his essays, keeping only the "Lament" and "Farewell Ode." During the An Lushan Rebellion, he wrote the poem "No One from Hu," predicting that the enemy could be defeated when the moon was obscured by the planet Venus. After An Lushan's death, the moon was indeed obscured. People said that Li Bai only wrote a playful line mocking Du Fu's "At the Top of Fanke Mountain," and Cheng Shi once saw a poem by Li Bai at his shrine, which he recorded as follows: "I feel the autumn spirit is carefree; who says autumn is sad? The mountains part with the setting sun, and the water reflects the clear sky." "The smoke returns to the green sea at dusk, and the geese fly across the blue sky. We have lost each other for ten thousand miles, and I am left with only empty thoughts."

Xue Ping, the Grand Tutor, often sent Zhou Hao, the Grand Steward, as a representative. Among the various types of officials and servants, there was an elderly man over eighty years old, wearing red robes. Zhou Hao asked him alone, "How long have you served in this office?" The old man replied, "I originally had a skill in setting bones. In the early years of the Tianbao era, the son of a high-ranking general was beaten, and his lower jawbone was dislocated. I set it right for him. The general rewarded me with a million coins and specially granted me red robes." Zhou Hao nodded and dismissed him. However, Xue Ping noticed that Zhou Hao's expression was not satisfactory. After the guests left, Xue Ping stayed behind and spoke calmly to Zhou Hao, "When I asked you about the old man in red robes, it seemed you were not pleased. Why is that?" Zhou Hao was surprised and said, "You are so observant!" Then he dismissed his attendants and invited Xue Ping to stay the night. He said, "This matter is long and can be explained slowly. When I was young, I often frequented the wealthy and powerful, indulging in the pleasures of the flesh, and eventually harbored fugitives and outlaws. I visited famous courtesans in the city, pursuing them like flies chasing sweet things, and none were missed. At that time, there was a courtesan in Jinggong Ward named Yela. She was youthful and graceful, with a charming smile, and her singing and dancing were unparalleled. Noble young men spent all their wealth to welcome her. Several of us, being wealthy, took turns enjoying her. One day, her mother told Zhou Hao, 'Tonight is Yela's birthday. How could it be left empty?' Zhou Hao agreed to attend, and they sought precious goods, spending tens of thousands of coins. The musicians, He Huai Zhi and Ji Hai Hai, were both the best of their time. Just as the doors were closing, they suddenly heard a knocking sound. Zhou Hao did not allow the door to be opened. After a long time, they forced their way in. There was a young man in purple fur, with dozens of horsemen following, and he vociferously insulted her mother. Her mother and Yela cried and bowed.

The guests were about to disperse, and Zhang Hao was at the time full of youthful vigor, and relied on his strength as if he could lift a tripod, looking down on those around him as if they were enemies. Then he stepped forward and rebuked them for their arrogance, raised his arms and beat them, and they fell at his feet. He then rushed out. At the time, Wei Zhen, who was in charge of the guesthouse at the capital, had a heart of righteousness and liked to take in private guests. Zhang Hao confided in him, and Wei Zhen then hid him among his wife and daughters. At that time, the officials were in hot pursuit, and Wei Zhen feared that Zhang Hao's whereabouts would be discovered. So he secretly prepared to leave at night, carrying several bars of white gold around his waist, and said to Zhang Hao, "Zhou Jianlao of Bianzhou is a man of righteousness. He is also acquainted with your family. You can rely on him now, and you should remain humble and diligent." Zhou Jianlao is a great hero. When he saw Wei Zhen's letter, he was very pleased. Zhang Hao then bowed to him as if he were his uncle, and explained the situation. Zhou Jianlao ordered him to stay in one of his boats and warned him not to leave without permission. He provided for him very generously.

After living for more than a year, Hao suddenly heard crying on the boat. He secretly peered at it and saw a young woman dressed in white, very beautiful, comforting Jian Lao. That night, Jian Lao suddenly came to Hao's place and asked, "Have you married yet? I have a cousin, who was married to Jia, but Jia has died and left no children, so she has nowhere to go. She can serve you as a gentleman." Hao bowed and thanked him, and that night his cousin came to Hao. She has two daughters and one son, who are still on the boat. Jian Lao suddenly told Hao, "The matter has settled, you are not handsome, no one will recognize you, you can travel in Jiang and Huai." He then gave him more than a hundred thousand. Hao cried and bade farewell, and Jian Lao soon died. Hao had reached a high official rank, Jian Lao's cousin was still alive, his children had married, and it had been nearly forty years, but no one knew about it. Just now, an old official mentioned it, and Hao felt ashamed without realizing it. I don't know if gentlemen can perceive subtle things. There was someone who personally saw General Xue say it.

In the late Dali period, Master Xuan Lian resided at Zhiqi Temple in Jingzhou. He was a man of high moral character and graceful demeanor, and people found it difficult to approach him. Zhang Luo often painted ancient pines on the walls of his study, and Fu Zai praised them, while Wei Xiang wrote poetry about them. These were considered a triple marvel of the time, but Xuan Lian covered them with white powder. When asked why, he said, "It's not a problem to cover my walls." Monk Na, his nephew, caused trouble for the temple by breaking tiles, digging holes, and burning mice, yet Xuan Lian never reprimanded him. He had a disciple named Yi Quan who wore simple clothes and ate simple food, but Xuan Lian did not praise him. Some people found this strange, so he wrote a poem on bamboo saying: "The vast sea allows fish to leap, and the boundless sky lets birds fly." One night, a foreign monk knocked on the door and entered, saying, "Master, hurry and perform a ritual." Xuan Lian replied, "I have never done anything for a specific purpose." The monk looked at him carefully and then closed the door, leaving it as secure as before. Xuan Lian smiled and told those around him, "I will soon return to the Pure Land!" He quickly took a bath and then leaned back, passing away.

The Chancellor (or Prime Minister) Ma, having established his meritorious achievements, was quite proud and boastful, often displaying an attitude similar to that of Tao Kan. He would refer to Tian Yue as "Money Dragon," and to this day, righteous people criticize him for it. At the time, there was someone who understood his intentions and spread a rhyme among the troops, saying, "The bell for the morning service rings, but the monk does not enter the hall." After a month, the person changed his attire and visited Ma, claiming to be skilled in divination. Ma hurriedly received him and asked his attendants to leave. The visitor said, "Your face reveals that you are not a common minister, but there is a minor flaw that can be corrected by acquiring treasure worth several millions." Initially, Ma did not believe him, but the visitor continued, "Have you not heard the rhyme? It refers to you. 'The bell for the morning service rings' means the time has come. 'Monk' refers to your name. 'Not entering the hall' means you will not take it upon yourself." Upon hearing this, Ma became confused and prepared fat jade, patterned rhinoceros horn, and full pearls. The visitor then disappeared, and Ma fell seriously ill, realizing his mistake too late.

The people of Xindu, Su's family, had two daughters who were looking for suitable husbands. Zhang Wencheng visited the Su family, and Su said, "Your son has wealth, but he will not become wealthy or powerful; he will die after reaching a fifth-rank official position." At that time, Wei Zhigu had just passed the imperial examinations. Su said, "Although his current position is low, he will eventually achieve high rank." So, he married his eldest daughter to Wei. The daughter had hair seven chi long, black and glossy like lacquer, and astrologers said she would be greatly wealthy and powerful. Later, Wei became a chancellor, and his wife was honored as a lady. Emperor Xuanzong performed the ritual of fengshan on Mount Tai, and Zhang Yue was appointed as the envoy for the fengshan ritual. Zhang Yue's son-in-law, Zheng Yi, was originally a ninth-rank official. By tradition, after the fengshan ritual, all officials from the rank of San Gong (Three Dukes) and below would be promoted one rank. Only Zheng Yi was suddenly promoted to the fifth rank by Zhang Yue, and also granted a red robe. During a feast, Emperor Xuanzong noticed Zheng Yi's rapid promotion and questioned it. Zheng Yi had no words to respond. Huang Fancho said, "This is the power of Mount Tai." Cheng Shi once held a gathering in the hall one night. At that time, the courtesan Yuhu disliked grilled fish and showed a change in expression when she saw it. He then asked the courtesans about their dislikes. There was Pengshan who disliked mice, and Jinzi who disliked lice even more. The guests then eagerly recounted stories about lice and mice, with many more than a hundred. I playfully compiled these stories into a book called *The Book of Lice*.

● Volume 13: Mysterious Phenomena After Wei Ying's death, his wife Liang Shi married Xiang Ziji. On the day of their wedding, Wei Ying returned to the courtyard and called out, "Liang, have you forgotten me?" Xiang Ziji was startled and shot an arrow at him, which immediately transformed him into a wooden and straw figure. There is a tomb of a noblewoman on the west side of Changbai Mountain. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozhao of Wei, talented individuals from across the land were sought out. Cui Luoshi of Qinghe County, who had a distinguished reputation at the age of twenty, was summoned to the state capital. One night, he passed by this place and suddenly saw a red gate and white walls, with towers and pavilions in sight. Soon, a servant in green emerged and said to Cui, "The young lady wishes to see Cui Lang." Cui was surprised and dismounted from his horse, entering through two gates. Inside, a servant announced and led him forward. Cui said, "In the midst of my travels, I have been unexpectedly honored, but we have never met before, so it is not appropriate for me to enter deeply." The servant replied, "The young lady is the wife of Lord Liu of Pingling and the daughter of the Grand Attendant Wu Zhi. Her lord has gone ahead, so she wishes to meet you." Cui then proceeded inside and sat on the bed. The young lady stood to the east of the door and conversed with Cui. Two maids held candles, and one was called to place a jade pillow in front of Cui. Cui was known for his literary talent and was skilled in poetry and singing. Although he suspected that she was not human, he found the encounter pleasing and enjoyable. The young lady said, "Recently, I saw Cui Lang resting under a tree in the courtyard and admired his recitations. That is why I wish to meet your beautiful face."

Shi Shui then asked, "The Wei emperor wrote to your esteemed father, calling him the magistrate of Yuancheng. Is that correct?" The woman replied, "My father was the magistrate of Yuancheng in the year I was born." Shi Shui then discussed major events of the Han and Wei dynasties with her, and everything they said corresponded with the *History of Wei*. There were too many details to record fully. Shi Shui said, "Your esteemed husband, Liu, may I ask his name?" The woman said, "My husband, Liu Kongcai's second son, is named Yao, courtesy name Zhong Zhang. Recently, he was arrested for a crime and has not returned." Shi Shui then got up from his seat and took his leave. The woman said, "We will meet again in ten years." Shi Shui left a tortoiseshell hairpin with her, and she gave him a jade ring from her finger. As Shi Shui mounted his horse and rode a few dozen steps, he looked back and saw a large tomb. When he arrived in Lixia, he considered it an omen of ill fortune, so he invited a monk to hold a ceremony and donated the jade ring as an offering. By the end of the Tian Tong era, Shi Shui was occupied with royal duties and was building a river embankment at the wall of the tomb. He then spoke of this matter to Xi Shubu of Jinan in his tent, and shed tears, saying, "This year marks ten years, what can be done about it?" Shi Shui was eating apricots in the garden, saying only, "I am delivering a message to the lady, I will leave immediately." He ate one apricot but did not finish it before he died. Shi Shui was twelve when he became the head of the county's administrative office, and was highly respected in the state and locality. When he died, everyone was deeply saddened and lamented.

South Giant River is well-known for judging the deceased Zhang Shuyan, who wrote *Continuation of the Divine and Strange Records*, fully documenting his miraculous efficacy. Zhang Shuyan judged ten ghosts, among whom two were women. Moreover, even turtles and foxes also judge the deceased. When Yu Xiangyang was in office, a selected person named Liu Mou entered the capital and met a scholar around twenty years old, who spoke clearly and intelligently. They walked together for several miles and were very compatible. They rested on the grass, and Liu had wine, which they drank several cups of. At dusk, the scholar pointed to a side path and said, "My humble abode is just a few miles from here; could you visit me?" Liu declined due to his schedule, and the scholar then composed a poem: "The stream flows gently, the celery sprouts, the weaver birds fly together, the guest returns home. The desolate village has no one to make Cold Food Festival offerings, the funeral house stands empty, facing the pear and loquat blossoms." By the next morning, Liu returned to Xiangzhou. He sought out the scholar, and the funeral house still stood.

Gu Kang lost a son, who was seventeen years old. The son's spirit wandered in a dream-like state, remaining near his home. Overwhelmed with grief, Gu Kang composed a poem and recited it while crying. The poem read: "An old man has lost his son, and at dusk, his tears turn to blood. At seventy years old, he does not expect to part with him for long." The son, moved by the poem, was deeply affected and swore to himself: "If I am reborn, I will return as Gu Kang's son." After a day, it seemed as though he was taken by someone resembling a county official to a place where it was decided he would be reborn into the Gu family. He then lost consciousness. Suddenly, he woke up, opened his eyes, and recognized his surroundings. His brothers and relatives were all around him, but he could not speak. After his rebirth, he did not remember his previous life. When he reached seven years old, his brother playfully slapped him, and he suddenly said, "I am your brother; why are you slapping me?" The family was astonished, and he began to recount his past life in detail, correctly naming his siblings. It was not unusual, for this was similar to the story of Yang Shuzi. The person in question was Gu Feixiong, a successful candidate in the imperial examinations. Cheng Shi often visited him, and Gu Feixiong would tell him the story while crying. The Buddhist text *Chuchai Jing* describes the process of fetal development, which differs slightly from this account.

**Chapter Thirteen: The Coffin** In recent times, the funeral rites involve placing the body in an inner coffin immediately after death, and cutting off a piece of the deceased's back garment to keep as a memento. Next, a cover is placed over the inner coffin, and meat, rice, and wine are set in front of the coffin. The cover is shaken and tapped against the coffin, and the name of the deceased is called out three times, saying, "Rise and eat," after which the ritual ceases. Nailing and lacquering the coffin are done while stopping the crying, as the lacquer would not dry if crying continued. The mourning banner is removed from the door, and the crowd tears it apart and takes it away. When sending off the deceased, leather, iron objects, and copper mirror cases should not be sent, as it is said that the deceased should not be exposed to brightness. Dong Xun remarked, "According to the *Rites*, 'The ceremonial attire is called **.' This refers to leather." (Another reading is "mowei.") Wooden figures of houses, carriages, horses, and servants are carved to counteract insects and evil spirits. Before the Zhou dynasty, painted carriages and lotus spirits were used, but after the Zhou, terracotta figures were employed. When sending off the deceased, yellow scrolls, hemp money, hare bristles, crossbows, paper charms, and items hung on trees are also used. A special carriage, called *lou*, is also made, which resembles a screen. People make musical performances for the deceased, called "music for the dead." The practice of *tou* is to protect the spirit of the deceased. It is also known as "soothing the spirit," which is a gentle and comforting act. It is alternatively called "wild obstruction" or "touching the grave." A four-eyed creature is called *fangxiang*, and a two-eyed creature is called *renqi*. According to the story of Fei Changfang and Li E, the *fangxiang* is believed to be a ghostly creature, and ancient sages created it as a symbol.

Furthermore, one must avoid dogs seeing the body, as it will bring a heavy mourning. The deceased sits on a chair and makes a soul robe, which is called the "heavenly robe." When sending off the deceased, one does not bring a mirror, a box, or a lid. [B168] is a ghost robe. The桐人 (Tong people) raised虞卿 (Yu Qian), the明衣 (bright clothing) raised左伯桃 (Zuo Bota), and the挽歌 (funeral song) arose from绋讴 (funeral chants). According to the old law, one who opens a tomb is executed in the marketplace. The tomb is heavy, meaning it is valued by filial sons. If one disturbs even a small amount of earth, they are punished, without needing any additional evidence. The character "吊" means an arrow passing through a bow. In ancient times, burials were left in the wilderness. According to the《礼》 (Rites), one would pass an arrow through a bow to assist birds and animals in harming the body. In the later Wei dynasty, there was a trend of elaborate burials. The coffins were thick and large, often made of cypress wood, with large copper handles on both sides. Regardless of public or private status, the coffins were adorned with white oil, and the funeral carriages were decorated with white silk and white spears. Drums were beaten, and the cries sounded like those in the Southern Dynasties. The funeral songs and cries had no breaking sounds, but they were slightly different from those in the capital. According to《周礼》 (Rites of Zhou), "the Fangxiangshi (a ritual official) deals with the wu (spirits)." The wu like to eat the liver of the deceased and fear tigers and cypress trees. Cypress trees are planted on tombs, and stone tigers are placed at the entrance, for this reason. In the past, during the Qin dynasty, a man from Chen Cang caught an animal resembling a pig but did not know its name. He met two children who said, "This is called Fu Shu, and it often lives in the ground, eating the brains of the dead. To kill it, one should insert a cypress branch into its head." A woman who has experienced a death wears a face mask, while a woman who has completed the mourning period wears a headdress and does not wear a face mask. A woman also cries, covering her face with a fan. Some women cry inside a tent.

The tomb of King Pingling of Han was filled with foxes. The foxes that emerged from their dens had their fur covered in ashes. Before the Three Kingdoms period, someone approached a fox den and found a gold knife and a jade spittoon. In the northeast of Beiqiu County, there was the tomb of Duke Jing of Qi. In recent times, someone opened the tomb and descended three zhang into a stone box where they found a goose. The goose used its wings to push aside stones. They then descended another zhang, and a blue vapor rose from the tomb. Looking at it, it resembled smoke from a kiln, and birds that flew over it fell dead. No one dared to enter further. During the Northern Wei dynasty, the practice of opening tombs to take bricks was common at the Tadu Temple. A person named Cui Han, courtesy name Zihong, was found in the tomb. He claimed to have been in the tomb for twelve years, behaving like a drunken person, wandering occasionally, and not being very coherent. He feared sunlight, water, fire, and weapons. He constantly moved and stopped when he was tired. In the area of Luoyang known as Fenglu, many funeral goods were sold. Cui Han said, "Make a pine coffin, not a mulberry coffin. I was sent to command ghost soldiers in the underworld. One ghost claimed it was a pine coffin, but the authorities said, 'Though it is a pine coffin, it is actually a mulberry coffin.'" In the Southern Dynasties, it was customary to give secret gifts to those who had died. Those who were supposed to receive a jade ornament were given a goose instead, and those who were supposed to receive a ribbon were given a letter. The tombs of virtuous officials had their official titles and names inscribed on them. Officials of the fifth rank and above should have lacquered coffins, while those below the sixth rank should only have lacquered edges. In Nanyang County, a woman named Sutiao died three years ago and her body returned to her home on its own. She spoke about her husband's military affairs. She said that red adzuki beans and yellow beans, if held in the hand, would prevent suffering after death. She also advised using paulownia wood for the coffin.

Duan Gong Li Miao, a high-ranking official under Liu Yan, had his estate in Gaoling. The tenants and laborers on his estate had accumulated arrears in rent or taxes for five or six years. When Li Miao was dismissed from his official post and returned to his estate, he intended to investigate and hold them accountable. However, upon seeing that the granary was full and the transfer had not yet been completed, he was puzzled and asked about the situation. They all replied, "We have been tenants or laborers here for two or three years and have been involved in theft for a long time. Recently, we uncovered an ancient tomb, which is ten miles west of the estate, very tall and large. It took two hundred steps into the pine forest to reach the tomb. Beside the tomb, there is a stele that is broken and lying in the grass, with the characters worn away and unreadable. Initially, we dug dozens of meters to the side and encountered a stone door sealed with molten iron. After several days of pouring manure, the door finally opened. When the door opened, arrows shot out like rain, killing several people. The group was afraid and wanted to leave, but I examined it and found no other cause; it must be a trap. So I ordered stones to be thrown inside. Every time a stone was thrown, arrows shot out. After throwing more than ten stones, the arrows stopped shooting. Then we entered with torches. We reached the second door, where there were dozens of wooden figures with their eyes open and brandishing swords, wounding several more people. The group beat them with sticks, and their weapons fell off. The walls were painted with images of soldiers and guards. On the south wall, there was a large lacquered coffin suspended by iron chains, with piles of gold, jade, and precious stones beneath it. The group was frightened and did not immediately loot it. Suddenly, a strong wind started to blow from the corners of the coffin, with sand flying and hitting the faces of the people. Soon, the wind became very strong, and sand poured out like a flood, reaching up to their knees. Everyone was frightened and ran away. By the time they left, the door was already blocked. One person was buried alive by the sand and died. They all poured wine on the ground to apologize and vowed never to disturb another tomb."
