The *Shuijing* (Water Classic) states that King Goujian of Yue, when he established his capital at Langya, intended to move the tomb of King Yuanchang of Yue (or Yuanchang, as some read it). However, a wind arose from within the tomb, accompanied by flying sand that struck people, preventing anyone from approaching, and thus the attempt was abandoned. According to the *Han Jiu Yi* (Old Rites of Han), when constructing a tomb site, a central stone was placed inside, surrounded by sand. The passages or chambers were arranged in a complex manner, and hidden traps such as crossbows, fire, and arrows buried in the sand were set up. It appears that these were part of the ancient techniques used in tomb construction. In another account, Hou Bai's *Jingyi Ji* (Record of Strange Events) (or *Jingyi Ji* as some read it) records: "A thief broke into the tomb of Bai Mao. Inside the coffin, a loud roar like thunder was heard, and wild pheasants flew away. Suddenly, a fire broke out inside, with flames shooting up, and the thief was burned to death." Was this not a hidden trap of fire? In the early years of the Yongtai era, there was a man named Wang Sheng who lived north of the Xianggan Temple in Yangzhou. One summer, he drank heavily and had his hand hanging over the bed. His wife, fearing that the wind might strike him, tried to lift his hand. Suddenly, a large hand appeared in front of the bed, pulling Wang's arm and causing his body to sink into the ground. His wife and servants tried to pull him out, but the ground split open. At first, only his clothing and belt remained, but soon they too disappeared. His family dug deeply and found a dried-up skeleton that appeared to be hundreds of years old. No one knew what the strange occurrence was.

During the Yuanhe period in the Jianghuai region, there were commoners plowing fields when the ground suddenly collapsed, revealing an ancient tomb. Inside the coffin, they found fifty pieces of clothing. According to the recluse Zheng Bin Yu, he once visited Hebei and heard that a village headman's wife had recently died and was not yet buried. At dusk, her children suddenly heard music approaching, and by the time it reached the courtyard, the body had already moved. When the music entered the room, it seemed to be coming from above the beams and rafters, and the corpse began to dance. The music then stopped, and the corpse fell, turned out the door, and followed the music away. The family was terrified, and as it was a dark night, they did not dare to pursue it. By the first watch, the village headman returned home and learned of the incident. He broke off a mulberry branch as long as his arm, got drunk, and shouted loudly as he went to investigate. He entered the tomb forest, about five or six miles deep, and heard the music again coming from a pine forest. When he approached a tree, he saw a flickering fire beneath it, and the corpse was dancing. The village headman raised his staff and struck the corpse, causing it to fall, and the music ceased. He then carried the corpse back home. According to the monk Xingru, there was a monk named Hongji Shangren in Fuzhou who practiced fasting and asceticism. He often found a skull on a sandbank and stored it in a clothing basket before returning to the temple. A few days later, while sleeping, he felt something biting his ear. He reached out and felt it fall, making a sound like several shengs (a unit of measurement). He suspected it was the skull. When morning came, he found it indeed fallen on the floor, so he broke it into six pieces and scattered them in the roof tiles. At midnight, a fire the size of a chicken's egg entered the tiles one by one. When he illuminated it, Hongji Shangren rebuked, saying, "You cannot seek rebirth in heaven, relying on these rotten bones!" Thus, the strange occurrence ended.

Recently, there were thieves who opened the tomb of the first ruler of Shu, Liu Bei. In the tomb, several thieves saw two people lit lamps and played chess, with more than ten attendants. The thieves were shocked and fearful and bowed in gratitude. One of the figures looked and said, "Will you drink?" Each thief drank a cup, and they also asked for several jade belts, then ordered them to leave quickly. When they reached the outside, their mouths were already sealed. The belts turned out to be giant snakes. When they looked at the tomb, it was already as it had been. In the *Nuogao Ji* (Records of Nuogao), there is an entry about the mythical Kunlun Mountains, the imperial capital of the gods, where a hundred deities reside. In the distant wilderness, there is a sacred mountain where ten witches live, named Xian, Ji, Pan, Peng, Gu, Zhen, Li, Di, Xie, and Luo. From this place, they ascend and descend. The spirits of the underworld, which oversee punishment, can reveal their true nature; the officials who manage rituals can demonstrate their connection to the divine. All life is ephemeral, and the wandering souls transform into various forms. Thus, sages established the system of the rotating celestial pole and appointed officials of divination and sorcery to examine the auspicious signs of the ten luminaries and to rectify the chaos of the nine tribes. In times of virtue, ghosts do not harm people; in times of moral governance, spirits do not lack their masters. If Laozi spoke of the horse under the stove, Zhuangzi spoke of thunder within the house, King Chu of Chu contended with the wild ox and the misfortune shifted, Duke Huan of Qi saw the long snake and his illness was cured, omens and transformations occur every day, but they only matter insofar as they do not harm people and do not leave their masters without guidance. Cheng Shi, having perused various strange books through the ages, occasionally recorded what he found, titling it *Nuogao Ji*. These are merely street gossip and trivial tales, not worthy of distinguishing the images of the nine tripods or expanding the debates of the seven chariots. Nevertheless, they serve as entertainment during leisure time.

In the Tianshan Mountains, there is a deity known as Hunshuandun. It appears like a bag with a luminous glow, its light resembling fire. It has six legs, heavy wings, and no face. It enjoys (or is associated with) music and dance, and it is actually the Emperor Jiang. Xing Tian once challenged the emperor in a contest of divinity; the emperor severed his head and buried it in Changyang Mountain, then transformed him into a figure with confusion as eyes, navel as a mouth, and wielding axes and shields in dance. The Han dynasty's bamboo palace used purple clay for its altar, and when a heavenly deity descended, it appeared like flowing fire. There were seven thousand jade-decorated ritual objects (or branches), and three hundred dancing women. Alternatively, in one account, the Han dynasty's ritual to the heavenly deity involved ten thousand two hundred cups, and a five-year-old ox weighing three thousand catties. The Lord Taiyi, whose name is La, holds the rank of ten thousand two hundred stones. The Lord of Heaven, Zhang Jian, courtesy name Cike, was from Juyang. In his youth, he was unrestrained and fearless. He often set up nets and caught a white pheasant, which he loved and raised. In a dream, he faced the wrath of Liu翁, who wished to kill him. The white pheasant would always warn him, and he devised various ways to avoid harm, but could not be harmed. The Lord of Heaven descended to observe him. Zhang Jian prepared a grand feast and then secretly rode the Lord of Heaven's chariot, mounted a white dragon, and ascended to heaven with a whip. The Lord of Heaven rode the remaining dragons to pursue him but failed to catch him. Upon arriving at the celestial palace, Zhang Jian replaced the officials, sealed the northern gate, granted the white pheasant the title of Supreme Minister, and decreed that its descendants would not be born on earth. Liu翁 lost control and wandered among the Five Sacred Mountains, causing calamities. Zhang Jian was troubled by this and appointed Liu翁 as the governor of Mount Tai, responsible for overseeing the records of life and death.

The first star in the handle of the Big Dipper is known as the God of Holding (or Reporting) Yin; the second star is called Leaf Arrival; the third star is called Gold View; the fourth star is called Resist Reason; the fifth star is called Prevent Action; the sixth star is called Open Treasure; the seventh star is called Attract (or Beginning). The Eastern God, surnamed Ni and with the courtesy name Junming, held the rank of two thousand six hundred stones when there were no people in the world. He wore a mixed ribbon, which was six zhang and six chi long, and was accompanied by nine thousand women. He died on the day of Ding Hai. The Western Queen Mother, surnamed Yang and with the surname Hui, governed the northwest corner of Mount Kunlun. She died on the day of Ding Chou. Another name for her is Wanjin. The Kitchen God, named Kuí, appears as a beautiful woman. He is also surnamed Zhang and named Dan, with the courtesy name Ziguó. His wife, with the courtesy name Qingji, has six daughters, all named Chá (or Jì) Qià. She often ascends to heaven on the last day of the month to report people's sins. For major sins, one loses a jì (a period of 300 days); for minor sins, one loses a suàn (a period of 100 days). Therefore, she serves as a supervisor of the Heavenly Emperor and a terrestrial spirit. On the day of Jǐ Chǒu, she ascends to heaven at dawn, descends to the lower realms during midday, and on this day, sacrifice brings good fortune. Her associated deities include the Heavenly Emperor's beloved grandson, the Heavenly Emperor's minister, the Heavenly Emperor's general, the Heavenly Emperor's eldest brother, the child on the grindstone, the purple palace lord, the Great Harmony Lord, and the Jade Pool Lady, etc. Another name for the Kitchen God is Rangzi.

The River God, with a human face and riding two dragons (also known as Bingyi or Fengyi), is sometimes described as having a human face and a fish body. The *Jin Gui* text mentions his name as Feng Xun (also written as Feng Shun). The *He Tu* text states his surname is Lu and his given name is Yi. The *Mu Tianzi Zhuan* text refers to him as Wu Yi, while the *Huainanzi* text calls him Feng Chi. The *Shengxian Ji* text says, "By consuming the eight stones, one becomes a water immortal." The *Baopu Zi* text states, "On the first Geng day of the eighth month, immerse oneself in the river." The deity associated with the Jiazi day is named Gong Long. If one wishes to enter the water, calling upon Gong Long, the River God will guide with nine thousand attendants, ensuring one does not drown. The deity associated with the Jia Xu day is named Zhi Ming. If one calls upon Zhi Ming, one will not be harmed by fire. The *Tai Zhen Ke Jing* text mentions various spirits, including a ghost named Sheng on the Bing Xu day, Ting Nong on the Bing Wu day, Tian Pei on the Yi Mao day, Er Shu on the Wu Wu day, Chun on the Ren Xu day, Shi on the Xin Chou day, Nie Zuo on the Yi You day, Yao Xiong on the Bing Chen day, Ji Xin on the Xin Mao day, Fa Ting Mao on the You day, and Xu Tian Zhu (also known as Sheng) on the toilet day. There are also two spirits named Yu Wang and Jing Yi, who are called upon by women during childbirth to avoid harm. These spirits are described as being three inches and three parts long, with black clothing. The spirit associated with horses is named Ci, the spirit associated with snakes is named Xie Shi Gui (also known as Li Er), the spirit associated with wells is named Qiong, and the spirit associated with clothing is named Shen Liao. Shen Tu and Yu Lei lead all spirits.

The Ancient King of Kucha, A Zuer, possessed supernatural abilities and had the power to subdue venomous dragons. At that time, merchants bought gold and silver treasures from the market, but by night, the money all turned into charcoal. Within the kingdom, hundreds of households lost their gold and treasures. The king had a son who first entered the monastic life and attained the state of Arhat. The king asked him, and the Arhat said, "This was done by the dragon. The dragon lives in the north mountain, its head is like a tiger, and it is now sleeping somewhere." The king then changed his clothes, held a sword, and silently left. Arriving at the dragon's place, he saw the dragon lying down and was about to cut it off. He then said, "I am cutting a sleeping dragon; who would know I have supernatural strength?" He shouted at the dragon, which was startled and woke up, transforming into a lion. The king immediately mounted it. The dragon became angry and made thunderous sounds, flying through the air to twenty miles north of the city. The king said to the dragon, "If you do not submit, I will cut off your head." The dragon feared the king's supernatural strength and spoke in human language, saying, "Do not kill me; I will accompany the king, and wherever he wishes to go, it will be there immediately." The king agreed. Later, he often rode the dragon to travel.

In ancient times, the kingdom of Gandhara had a king named Gada (also known as "Jase Gada"). He was renowned for his bravery and strategic prowess. He launched military campaigns against various countries, and wherever he went, they all submitted to him. One day, he reached the five regions of India and obtained two fine silk scarves. He kept one for himself and gave the other to his queen. When the queen wore the scarf, the king noticed a handprint of saffron on her breast and was shocked and frightened. He asked the queen, "Why did you suddenly wear this garment with a handprint?" The queen replied, "It is the scarf that the king gave me." The king angrily questioned the keeper of the treasury, who explained, "The scarf originally had this handprint, it is not the fault of the servant."

The king followed the merchant and questioned him. The merchant said, "The King of South India named Sathapaha has a long-standing wish. Each year, he is assigned fine cloth, which he piles up in layers. He stains his hands with saffron and lac on the cloth and draws a thousand layers of handprints that all penetrate through. Men wear it, with the handprints on the back; women wear it, with the handprints on the breasts." The king ordered his attendants to wear it, and all was as the merchant said. The king then struck his sword and said, "If I do not cut off the hands and feet of King Sathapaha with my sword, I cannot eat or sleep." He then sent an envoy to South India to request the hands and feet of King Sathapaha. When the envoy arrived in his country, King Sathapaha and his ministers deceitfully reported, "Although our country has a king named Sathapaha, there is no actual king. We only have a golden figure set up in the palace, and all leadership and instruction are handled by ministers." The king then raised an army of elephants and horses to attack his country in the south. His country hid its king in a cave underground and cast a golden figure to welcome the king. The king knew it was a deception, and relying on his own power, he cut off the hands and feet of the golden figure. As a result, the hands and feet of King Sathapaha in the cave also fell off.

On Mount Li in Qi Prefecture, there is an ancient iron chain, as thick as a human arm, winding around its peak twice. According to legend, this mountain was originally an island in the sea, and the mountain god liked to move it, so the sea god locked it with the chain. When the chain was pulled, it broke, and the mountain flew to its current location. East of Taiyuan Prefecture, there is a cliff mountain. During a drought, the locals often burn this mountain to祈雨. According to local legend, the cliff mountain god married the daughter of the river god, so when the river god sees the fire, he must rain to save it. Now there are many aquatic plants on the mountain. The Huabu Spring is the place where Duke Qi of the State of Qi fetched water. It is about 100 steps in diameter. During the Northern Qi dynasty, someone used a thousand-foot rope to sink a stone and found it endless. When the stone came out, it was red like blood, and the person did not live long after that and died due to some matter. In the eastern village of Yongfeng County, Jingzhou, there is a lying stone, nine feet six inches long. Its shape resembles a human body, with green and yellow colors rising up, like carved. If there is a drought in the area, they will raise their hands (or perform a ritual) and lift it. A small lift brings a small rain, and a large lift brings a large rain. According to legend, this stone suddenly appeared here. Originally it was nine feet long, but now it is six inches longer.

The Clear (or Yuyu) River, near the mouth of the Wuan (or Xue) River, in the twelfth year of the Yixing era, a group of children were bathing in the water. Suddenly, money began to appear by the bank like flowing sand, and they all took it. They filled their hands with it and placed it on the ground, but it would go away again. They then tied it with their sleeves, and each got something. Among the flowing money was a copper chariot, pulled by a copper ox, moving very quickly. The children ran after it and pulled off one wheel of the chariot, which was about five inches in diameter. The wheel had a pig's snout-shaped hub with six spokes, was entirely blue-green in color, and the inside of the hub was yellow and sharp, looking like it was in constant motion. At that time, Shen Jing, the governor of Nanyang, sought and obtained the money from the wheel. When it moved through the grass, it would stop and break, and no one knew where it ultimately ended up. The Tiger Den Mountain, it is said, during the Jianping era of the Yan Dynasty, the governor of Jining, Hu Zhi, found a white tiger in a cave on this mountain and named it after that. Underneath the Wushan Mountain, there was no water. At the end of the Wei Dynasty, someone dug a well five zhang deep and found a stone box. Inside the box was a turtle as large as a horse's hoof, with five pieces of charcoal piled next to the box. They dug another three zhang and encountered a flat rock, below which was a roaring water flow. They then chiseled through the stone to the water, and it flowed north very fast. Soon, a boat hit the stone and went up. A craftsman looked into the boat and found a piece of cedar wood with the inscription: "Second year of the Chuwu era, August 10th, the boat of Prince Yizhi of Wuchang."

Ten li west of Pingyuan County, there used to be a Du Lin. During the late reign of Emperor Tai Shang of Southern Yan, there was a man named Jingbo who lived in Changbai Mountain. Someone sent a letter to Jingbo, saying: "I am an envoy from Wujiang, and I am to convey a message to Jibo. I must now pass through Changbai, so please help me to deliver it." The letter instructed Jingbo to take leaves from the Du Lin and throw them into the water, and someone would come out. Jingbo followed the instructions, and indeed, someone led him out. Jingbo was afraid of water, but the person told him to close his eyes. As if entering the water, he suddenly saw magnificent palaces. He saw an old man, around eighty or ninety years old, sitting on a crystal bed. The old man opened the letter and read: "Yuxing surpasses extinction." The attendants all had round eyes and were wearing armor. Jingbo bid farewell and left, and the old man gave him a knife, saying: "Farewell, just hold this knife, and you will no longer face the danger of water." Jingbo left and returned to the Du Lin, but his clothes were not even slightly wet. Indeed, in that year, Emperor Wu of Song destroyed Yan. Jingbo lived between the two rivers for three years, and one night, a great flood suddenly occurred, submerging the entire village. Only Jingbo sat on a couch, and until dawn, he put on his shoes. Jingbo looked down and saw that the couch was actually a large turtle (or possibly a giant tortoise). Jingbo died, and the knife was also lost. It is said that there is a tomb of the River God below the Du Lin.

Dufeijin, it is said, during the Dashi era of the Western Jin Dynasty, Liu Boyu's wife, Duan Guangming, was known for her jealousy. Liu Boyu often recited the "Ode to the Luo River Goddess" in front of his wife and told her, "If I could marry a woman like this, I would have no regrets." Duan Guangming replied, "Why do you prefer the water goddess over me? If I die, what have I to fear from becoming a water goddess?" That night, she drowned herself. Seven days later, she appeared to Liu Boyu in a dream and said, "You always wished to become a deity, and now I have achieved that." Liu Boyu woke up and realized the truth, and he never crossed the river again. Any woman who crossed the river at Dufeijin had to tear her clothes and dishevel her appearance before she could cross safely; otherwise, a storm would arise. Even ugly women who crossed the river, their appearance intact, were not envied by the deity. Women who crossed the river without encountering storms believed they were ugly and did not anger the water deity. Ugly women avoided crossing the river and destroyed their appearance to prevent ridicule. Therefore, people from the state of Qi say, "To find a beautiful woman, stand at the river crossing. When women stand by the water, their beauty or ugliness becomes apparent."

虞 Dao Shi, during the middle period of the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song, was traveling through the mountains by carriage. Suddenly, a person in black clothing climbed onto the carriage and asked to be carried. The person had a luminous head, red eyes and mouth, and a hairy face. After traveling ten miles, the person left, saying to Dao Shi, "I am the General who drives away evil spirits. I am grateful for your kindness in allowing me to ride." The person then left behind a pair of silver rings as a gift. During the middle period of the reign of Emperor An of Jin, a man from Wu County, around twenty years old, self-proclaimed himself as the Holy Lord, with the surname Xie. He had been dead for a hundred years but suddenly visited the Chen family's residence, saying it was his old residence and could see him back, otherwise he would burn it down. One night, the fire broke out and everything was burned to ashes. Then there were feathers stuck in the ground around the house, several layers thick. The people then built a temple.

In the early years of the Dading era, there was a scholar who followed a mission to Silla. The wind blew him to a place where everyone had long beards, and their language was similar to that of the Tang dynasty, so they called it the Land of Long Beards. The land was prosperous, and the buildings and attire were somewhat different from those in China, and it was known as the Island of the Rising Sun. The officials there had titles such as Chief, Water Control, Eye Servant, and Island Patrol. The scholar visited several places, and the people of the land treated him with great respect. One day, several carriages and horses arrived, announcing that the king had summoned the guest. After two days of travel, they reached a large city, where armed guards stood at the gates. The envoys led the scholar into the palace to prostrate himself in reverence. The palace was grand and spacious, and the attendants were like those of a king. The king saw the scholar and bowed slightly, then appointed him as the Master of the Winds and made him his son-in-law. The king was very beautiful, with several strands of beard. The scholar gained great power and wealth, but he was unhappy whenever he returned home and saw his wife. The king often held grand gatherings on the night of the full moon. One such gathering, the scholar noticed that all the concubines had beards, and he composed a poem: "Flowers without petals are not beautiful, and women without beards are ugly. My father-in-law, try removing all of them; perhaps they will not be as good as they are now." The king laughed and said, "The prince-in-law still cannot forget the beauty of my daughter's chin and jaw." After ten years, the scholar had one son and two daughters. One day, the king and his ministers were deeply troubled. The scholar asked what was wrong, and the king wept and said, "Our country is in danger, and disaster is imminent. Only the prince-in-law can save us." The scholar was shocked and replied, "If the danger can be averted, I will not hesitate to risk my life."

The king then ordered a boat to be prepared and commanded two envoys to accompany the official. He said, "Trouble the prince to visit the Sea Dragon King and merely state that the Long Beard Country on the tenth island of the third bay of the East Sea is in trouble and seeks help. Our country is extremely small; it must be emphasized repeatedly." They parted with tears and clasped hands. The official boarded the boat and arrived at the shore instantly. The shore was entirely made of precious materials, and the people were all well-dressed and tall. The official then approached, seeking to pay respects to the Dragon King. The dragon palace looked like the heavenly palace depicted in Buddhist temples, with light flashing and dazzling, making it impossible to see clearly. The Dragon King descended the steps to greet the official, and they ascended together to the hall. The Dragon King inquired about the official's purpose, and the official explained everything. The Dragon King immediately ordered a quick investigation. After a long time, a person from outside reported, "There is no such country within our territory." The official then pleaded again, saying that the Long Beard Country was on the seventh island of the third bay of the East Sea. The Dragon King scolded the envoys, "Search carefully and report quickly." After a mealtime, the envoys returned and said, "The shrimp from this island should supply the Dragon King's food for this month, and they had already been brought the previous day." The Dragon King laughed and said, "The guest was certainly deceived by the shrimp. Although I am the king, what I eat is all according to heavenly decree; I cannot eat arbitrarily. Now, for the guest, I will reduce the food supply." He then ordered someone to lead the guest to see something. They saw dozens of large iron pots, each as big as a house, filled with shrimp. There were five or six large red shrimp, as big as arms, jumping in front of the guest, appearing to beg for help. The leader said, "These are the shrimp kings." The official unconsciously cried in sorrow. The Dragon King ordered the release of one pot of shrimp kings and commanded the two envoys to send the guest back to China. After one night, they arrived at Dengzhou. Looking back, the official realized that the envoys were great dragons.

In the early years of the Tianbao era, An Shun presented a multicolored jade belt to the emperor, and another multicolored jade cup was found in the imperial treasury. The emperor was surprised that recently there had been no multicolored jade in the Western regions and ordered the Western tribes to be investigated. The Western regions reported that regular tributes were being intercepted by the kingdom of Soubul and did not reach the imperial court. The emperor became angry and decided to launch a campaign against Soubul. Many officials advised against it, but only Li Youzuo supported the emperor's decision and recommended Wutian Wuyong as the commander, claiming that he was capable of leading the campaign. The emperor then appointed Wutian Wuyong to lead four thousand soldiers, along with allied forces, to attack Soubul. When the army reached the walls of Soubul, the king was afraid and surrendered, offering all his precious jewels and requesting annual tribute. Wutian Wuyong refused the surrender and immediately massacred the inhabitants, capturing 3,000 people and their jewels before returning. In Soubul, a fortune-teller warned, "The general lacks righteousness and is bringing bad luck; a great storm is coming." As the army traveled hundreds of miles, a sudden storm struck, with wind and snow like wings, causing the sea to freeze into ice columns that rose and fell. After half a day, the sea swelled violently, and four thousand soldiers froze to death at once. Only one foreigner and one Han Chinese soldier survived. The survivors reported the incident to the emperor, who was greatly shocked and surprised. He immediately sent an imperial messenger to verify the event. The messenger reached the site, where the ice still stood tall, and the bodies of soldiers could be seen through the ice. As the messenger returned, the ice began to melt, and the bodies disappeared.

Lord Guo Daigong once lived in seclusion in the mountains. One night, at midnight, a figure appeared with a face as round as a plate and eyes wide as those of a tiger, glowing under the lamplight. The lord showed no sign of fear, and slowly dipped his brush to inscribe on the creature’s cheek the lines: “Those long on guard duty grow old, while horses on long campaigns do not grow fat.” These were his famous lines. After finishing, he recited them aloud, and the creature vanished at once. A few days later, while walking leisurely with the woodcutters, he saw a great tree with a white ear-like object the size of several dou, and there, inscribed upon it, were the very lines he had written. During the Dali period, there was a scholar’s estate in Weinan. The scholar fell ill and died in the capital, and his wife Liu took up residence in the estate. They had a son, about eleven or twelve years old. One summer night, the boy suddenly felt a sense of dread and could not sleep. After the third watch of the night, he saw an old man dressed in white, with two teeth protruding from his lips, staring at him intently. After a long time, the figure slowly approached the bed. There was a maid sleeping soundly near the bed, and the old man seized her throat. A loud crunching sound followed, and her clothes tore apart as she was devoured. In a moment, her bones were exposed, and the old man lifted her, drank her five internal organs, and then, with a mouth as large as a winnowing basket, the boy cried out, but saw nothing—only the maid’s bones remained. A few months later, there was no further incident. One day, the scholar was holding a ritual for the deceased. At dusk, Lady Liu sat outside in the open air to cool off. A hornet circled around her head and face. She struck it down with her fan, only to find it was a walnut. She quickly picked it up and began to play with it in her palm, and it began to grow. At first, it was the size of a fist, then like a bowl. In the moment she turned in alarm, it had grown as large as a plate. Suddenly, it split into two halves, spinning through the air with a sound like a swarm of bees. Then it flew together and struck Lady Liu’s head, shattering it. Her teeth stuck to the tree. The object then flew away, and no one could ever know what strange thing it had been.

During the time of Chancellor Jia in Huazhou, a severe drought struck the region, causing all the autumn crops to be destroyed. Chancellor Jia summoned two senior officers and said to them, "This year's drought is devastating. I trouble you two to save the troops and the people." Both officers replied, "If it benefits the army and the state, death is not worth mentioning." Chancellor Jia smiled and said, "You may serve as messengers. On the second day, there will be two riders dressed in dark red, riding fine horses with long manes and tails. They will pass through the market and out of the city. Follow them and identify where they disappear. Then my plan will succeed." The two officers prepared food and wore black clothes, and set out to follow the riders. Just as Chancellor Jia had said, the riders passed through the market and into the countryside, traveling over two hundred li, and disappeared near a large tomb. They then piled stones as a marker at the site. After returning and confirming the location, Chancellor Jia was greatly pleased. He ordered several hundred soldiers to prepare shovels and spades and accompanied the two officers to the site. They then opened the tomb and found stored grain in the tens of thousands of measures. No one could understand how this had come to pass.

Hu Xiang, serving as the governor of Guo Prefecture, once encountered a hunter who had killed a deer weighing one hundred and eighty pounds. On the deer's hoof, there was a copper ring with seal script characters that learned scholars could not identify. The scholar Qiu Ru explained that fifty years ago, in a nearby county of Ruzhou Prefecture, a villager lost his daughter. After several years, she suddenly returned on her own, saying that she had been taken away by some object while asleep and was suddenly transported to a place. When it became light, she found herself inside an ancient tower. Inside, she saw a handsome man who told her, "I am a heavenly being, destined to have you as my wife. There is a fixed term for this, so do not be afraid." He also warned her not to look outside. Each day, he would return twice to get food, sometimes bringing roasted food that was still hot. After a year, the woman waited for his departure and secretly looked at him. She saw him soar into the air like a bird, with flaming hair and blue skin, and ears that made a cracking sound like a donkey. When he landed, he returned to his human form, and she was frightened and covered in sweat. The creature returned and said, "You did indeed spy on me. I am a wild demon, but we are fated to be together, and I will not harm you." The woman, who was always kind, apologized, saying, "Since I am already your wife, how could I have any ill feelings? You are so supernatural, why do you not live among humans so that I can sometimes see my parents?" The creature replied, "Our kind carries sins, and if we live among humans, epidemics will occur."

The tracks have now been revealed, and Master Ren has observed them; you will soon return. The tower is very close to human dwellings, and the woman often looked down from above. The object in the air could not change its form and mingled with people in public places. Sometimes, when ordinary people in the dust approached, the object would gather its hands and sidestep. Other times, when it saw someone putting their head down and spitting on its face, passersby would act as if they did not see it. When it returned, the woman asked, "Why did I see some people in the street who treated you with respect, and others who mocked and played with you?" The object replied with a smile, "In this world, there are those who eat beef, and I can deceive them. But if I encounter those who are loyal, upright, filial, or who follow Buddhist or Daoist precepts and laws, I may mistakenly offend them, and in that case, I shall be punished by heaven." After another year, the object suddenly wept and said to the woman, "Our fate has come to an end. I will await the wind and rain to send you back." It then gave her a green stone, about the size of a chicken's egg, and said, "When you return home, grind this and consume it; it can expel toxic substances." One night, with thunder and lightning, the object suddenly held the woman and said, "It is time to go." In the blink of an eye, as if in the span of a Buddhist gesture, they had arrived at her home and fell into the courtyard. Her mother ground the stone and gave it to her to drink, and the substance expelled was like green mud, several dou in quantity.

During the Dali period, Li Gongzuo was in Luzhou, where there was a clerk named Wang Geng who took leave and returned home. One night, while traveling outside the city walls, he suddenly encountered a procession of horses being led and shouted to clear a path. The clerk hurriedly hid behind a large tree to observe, and he was puzzled because there was no high-ranking official present. Behind the leading horses, there was a man in purple robes, surrounded by ceremonial guards, resembling an envoy or commissioner. Following him was a carriage, which was just crossing a river. The driver approached and reported, "The rope pulling the carriage has broken." The man in purple robes said, "Check the records." Then several officials checked the records and said, "We should take the spinal cord of Zhang's wife from a certain neighborhood in Luzhou." It turned out to be the aunt of the clerk. In a moment, the officials returned, holding two white objects, each several feet long, and they crossed the river and left. When he returned home, his aunt was still unharmed, but after a night, she suddenly suffered from back pain and died within half a day. In the early Yuanhe period, there was a scholar whose name is unknown, who became drunk and fell asleep in the hall. When he woke up, he saw women and others on an ancient screen, all dancing and singing in front of the bed. They sang, "The young women of Chang'an step on the spring sunshine, but nowhere does the spring sunshine not break one's heart. Without sleeves, the bowing waist is completely forgotten, and the beautiful eyebrows vainly wear the frost of nine autumns." One of the women with twin braids asked, "What is the bowing waist?" The singer laughed and said, "Do you not see me doing the bowing waist?" She then turned her head backward, her hair bun touching the ground, and her waist posture was like a circle. The scholar was frightened and scolded them, and they suddenly disappeared from the screen, leaving nothing else behind.

During the reign of Zheng Xiang in Liangzhou, there was a monk named Zhiyuan at Longxing Temple who was skilled in the practice of the Zongchi and Cheli techniques. He was effective in controlling evil and relieving pain, with many successful cases, and every day, dozens of people waited at his door. Zhiyuan was old and somewhat tired, but Zheng Gong highly respected him. He then requested to live in a vacant area east of the city, and Zheng Gong built a thatched hut and planted trees for him, along with one novice and one attendant. After living there for several years, on a leisure day, Zhiyuan trimmed his toenails in the sun. A woman in plain clothes, very beautiful, came to the steps and performed a bow. Zhiyuan quickly straightened his clothes and asked in surprise, "Why have you come here?" The woman wept and said, "I am unfortunate that my husband has died and my son is young, and my elderly mother is gravely ill. I know that the Master's divine spells can help, and I beg for your aid." Zhiyuan replied, "I originally disliked the noise of the city, and I am also troubled by the constant requests. Your mother's illness, you can have加持here." The woman repeatedly wept and begged, saying that her mother's illness was severe and she could not be moved. Zhiyuan also pitied her and agreed. He then said that there was a village about twenty miles north, near which there was a village called Lu Family Village, where you can find Wei Shisan's residence. The next morning, Zhiyuan went as he had said, about twenty miles, but after visiting everywhere, he returned without finding anything. The next day, the woman came again, and the monk scolded her, "I was mistaken yesterday in going far to meet you." The woman said, "It is only two or three miles from where the monk stays. If the monk is compassionate, he will definitely go again." The monk angrily said, "An old monk in his declining years, I swear not to go out." The woman then raised her voice and said, "Where is your compassion? Now the matter requires you to go."
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

In the early Yuanhe era, a villager named Wang Qing in Luoyang earned five silver coins through his labor. He bought a withered chestnut tree on the edge of a field, intending to cut it into firewood to make a profit. After a night, a neighbor stole and cut the tree, injuring its belly. Suddenly, a black snake raised its head like an arm and spoke in human language: "I am the original form of Wang Qing, do not cut me." The neighbor was terrified, dropped his axe, and fled. By morning, Wang Qing led his descendants to cut the tree, dug up its roots, and found two large jars filled with scattered coins. Wang Qing gained wealth from this and returned home. After ten years, he became extremely wealthy and cast the money into the shape of a dragon, calling it Wang Qing's original form. During the Yuanhe era, Su Zhan traveled to Pengque Mountain, packing food and starting a fire, exploring every step. He suddenly told his wife, "I am walking in the mountains and saw a cave with a reflective surface, it must be a sacred place. Tomorrow I will go there, and now I must say goodbye to you." His wife wept and tried to stop him, but he left the next morning. His wife and servants followed him secretly. They entered the mountain for dozens of miles and saw a white light from a rock, about a zhang in diameter. Su Zhan approached it and let out a loud cry. His wife and children rushed to save him, but he transformed into a bug-like creature. There was a black spider as large as a drill bit, running and gathering at the base of the rock. A servant cut the spider's web with a sharp knife, just as it was breaking, and Su Zhan's head sank, and he died. His wife then piled up wood and burned the cliff, filling the entire mountain with the smell. It is said that Pei Bo was traveling in the mountains and encountered a large spider hanging threads like a piece of cloth. The spider was about to reach him, but Pei Bo shot it with a bow, killing it. The spider was as large as a wheel. He cut off several feet of the thread and collected it. His subordinates with metal wounds applied a small piece, and the blood stopped immediately.

● Volume Fifteen: Records of Nuogao In the middle of the Dali era, there was a Daliang named Liu who lived in a nearby county of Huai Zhou after being dismissed from his official post. He could eat as much as several people, especially fond of eating certain types of food, often saying that the flavor had never truly satisfied him. Guests from the town caught over a hundred pounds of fish and gathered at a pavilion in the countryside to watch him begin eating. At first, he ate several layers of food, but suddenly he seemed to choke, spitting out a small, round bone bead about the size of a black bean. He placed it in a teacup and covered it with layers. Halfway through the meal, the covered cup tilted unexpectedly. Liu lifted it to look, and the bone bead had grown several inches, taking on a human form. The guests gathered to watch, and as they watched, it continued to grow. In an instant, it grew to the height of a person, then attacked Liu, causing him to vomit blood. After a long time, everyone scattered and ran away. One followed the hall to the west, and another turned to the left of the hall, both reaching the back door where they collided, merging into one person, who was Liu. His spirit was already mad. After half a day, he could speak again, inquiring about the reason, but he did not remember anything. From then on, he hated eating. There was also a man named Feng Tan who often had an illness. The doctor ordered him to soak snake wine and take it. At first, he took one jar, and his illness was reduced by half. The doctor then ordered his family members to catch one snake in the garden and throw it into the jar, which was sealed for seven days. When the jar was opened, the snake jumped out, raising its head more than a foot, then went out the door and disappeared. The trail it left caused the ground to rise several inches. Lu Shao, a Langzhong, also mentioned that he once knew a person who soaked snake wine, killing dozens of snakes in the process. One day, the person came to inspect the wine in the jar and saw something jump out and bite his nose, which was about to fall off. Upon looking, he saw it was a snake's head bone. The wound destroyed his nose, as if it had been cut off.

During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Tang, there was a monk named Chen Pu who lived on the north street of Chongxian Lane. Outside his main gate stood a large locust tree. Chen Pu often lingered and looked out at dusk, and he saw what appeared to be women, foxes, and large old owls flying into the tree. He then cut down the tree and examined it. The tree had three branches. One branch was empty, one branch had 120 single-headed chestnuts, and another branch contained a swaddled dead child, about one foot long. The monk named Wukanyan recently heard that there was a general named Bai who often washed his horse at Qujiang. One day, the horse suddenly jumped out and ran away. On its front leg, there was something white, resembling a belt, wrapped around it several times. Bai immediately ordered it to be removed, and blood flowed several liters. Bai found it strange and sealed it in a paper envelope, storing it in a chest. One day, while sending guests to Chan River, he showed it to them. The guests said, "Why not try it with water?" Bai struck the ground with his whip to create a hole, placed the creature in the hole, and poured water over it. After a while, the creature wriggled as if growing, and water gushed from the hole. Suddenly, it spread out like a mat, and black smoke like incense shot out through the eaves. The crowd was afraid and said, "It must be a dragon." They then hurriedly returned. Not far away, wind and rain arrived, and there were several loud thunderclaps.

In the street in front of Jing Gong Temple, there was once a large well, commonly known as the Octagonal Well. During the early Yuanhe period, a princess passed by in the summer, and seeing the common people drawing water, she ordered her maid to fetch water from the well using a silver-rimmed bowl. Unfortunately, the bowl accidentally fell into the well. After more than a month, the bowl emerged from the Wei River. Before the use of military force in Dongping, there was a scholar-official candidate named Meng Bu Yi who visited the region of Zhaoyi. One night, he arrived at a post station and was about to wash his feet when he encountered a man from Zizhong named Zhang, a low-ranking official, who had several dozen servants with him. Meng wanted to pay respects to Zhang, but Zhang, being drunk, initially ignored him. Meng then retreated to the western room. Zhang kept calling for fried cakes, and Meng silently watched him, growing increasingly angry at his arrogance. After a long time, the fried cakes were ready, and Meng saw a black object resembling a pig following the plate to the light and standing up. This happened five or six times, but Zhang did not notice. Meng became afraid and could not sleep, while Zhang soon slept soundly. By the third watch of the night, Meng finally closed his eyes, but suddenly saw a man in black robes fighting with Zhang. After a long time, they entered the eastern side room together, and the sounds of punches were like those of a pestle. In a short while, Zhang emerged with disheveled hair and both arms exposed, returning to sleep on the bed. By the fifth watch of the night, Zhang called his servant to light the candle and comb his hair, then approached Meng and said, "I was drunk yesterday and did not know that you were in the same hall."

The host then ordered a meal, and they laughed and joked very happily. At times, he spoke in a low voice, saying, "Last night I was very ashamed of the elder, please do not speak of it." Meng merely nodded in assent. He then said, "I have a schedule and must leave early, the scholar may go first." He then felt in his boot and found a piece of gold, handing it over and saying, "A small gift, please keep the previous matter secret." Meng dared not refuse and immediately left. After traveling for several days, he heard that the killer was being pursued. Meng inquired of the people on the road, and they all said that Zhang Pingshi of Ziqing had arrived at the inn early and left before dawn, leaving behind an empty saddle. The innkeeper returned to the inn to search, finding a corner of the western hall with a mat, which when opened, revealed only white bones, not a single fly or piece of meat. There was no blood left on the ground, only a single shoe beside it. It was said that this inn had always been ominous, and no one knew what strange occurrence it was. Candidate Zhu Yuanying often said that he personally heard Meng Buyi recounting, always warning that night meals must be offered as sacrifices. Zhu also said that Meng never believed in Buddhism, but was quite skilled in poetry, with lines such as, "The homeland is far in the daytime, the best verses are in the green mountains." Later, he often recited and traveled, never taking the imperial examinations again.

Liu Jizhong, who often lived in a village near the capital, had a seriously ill wife. One night, Liu did not sleep, and suddenly an old woman with a white head, only three feet tall, appeared from the light of the lamp and said to Liu, "Your wife is ill, only I can treat her. Why don't you ask me?" Liu, being usually stern, rebuked her. The old woman slowly pointed at him and said, "Do not regret! Do not regret!" Then she disappeared. His wife then suffered sudden heart pain, almost dying. Liu had no choice but to pray to her. After speaking, she reappeared. Liu bowed to her and invited her to sit. She then asked for a cup of tea and spoke into it as if casting a spell, then ordered to pour the tea into the wife. As soon as the tea was swallowed, the pain subsided. Later, she often appeared, and the family no longer feared her. After a year, she again told Liu, "I have a daughter who has come of age. Please find her a good husband." Liu laughed and said, "The paths of humans and ghosts are different. It is indeed difficult to fulfill your request." The old woman said, "It is not to find a human, but to carve a figure from paulownia wood. The better the carving, the better." Liu agreed and carved the figure for her. After a night, the wooden figure was gone. She also asked the master to make a male and female figure. If possible, on a certain night she would bring a carriage to welcome them. Liu, unable to refuse, also agreed. On a day past the hour of the rooster, a carriage with servants and horses arrived at the door. The old woman also arrived and said, "Master, you may go." Liu and his wife each got into their respective carriages. It was dark when they arrived at a place with red gates and high walls. Lanterns were lit to welcome them. The guests and furnishings were as splendid as those of a prince or noble. They led Liu to a hall where there were dozens of officials in red and purple robes. Some were people he knew, some were those who had passed away. They looked at each other in silence.

His wife arrived at a hall, where candles as thick as arms burned brightly, and silk and jade competed in brilliance. There were also several women present, some of whom were living and others who had passed away, and the narrator knew about half of them. They merely looked at each other. By the fifth watch of the night, Liu and his wife returned home in a daze, as if waking from a drunken stupor, unable to remember even a tenth of what had happened. After several months, the old woman came again, bowed, and thanked him, saying, "My daughter has grown up, and I now entrust her to you again." Liu became impatient and hit her with a pillow, saying, "Old demon, dare to disturb me like this!" The old woman disappeared with the pillow. His wife then fell ill suddenly, and Liu and his family poured wine on the ground and prayed for her, but she never came out again. She eventually died of heartache. Liu's sister also fell ill again with heartache, and Liu wanted to move. Everything was stuck in place; even if it were as light as a shoe, it could not be moved. They invoked Taoist priests to write charms and Buddhist monks to recite mantras, but none of it worked. During his leisure time, Liu read a medical prescription, and his maid Xiao Bi came from outside, walking slowly with her hands hanging and speaking loudly. She asked, "Liu Si, do you remember anything from the past?" Soon, she sobbed and said, "I recently returned from Mount Tai and encountered a flying demon carrying my sister's heart and liver; I also took them." She raised her sleeve, and there was a wriggling object in her sleeve. She looked to the left as if giving an order, saying, "Can be placed somewhere." She felt wind coming from her sleeve, blowing through the curtains and entering the hall. She then went upstairs and sat facing Liu, asking about the living and the dead and reciting her life story. Liu and Du Xiangong were the same year to pass the imperial examinations, and they had a connection. Her maid's mannerisms and speech were all similar. Soon, she said, "I have something to do and cannot stay long." She held Liu's hand and wept, and Liu was also overwhelmed with grief. The maid suddenly fainted, and when she woke up, she remembered nothing. His sister also recovered from her illness.

The Magistrate of Nancheng County in Linchuan, named Dai Cha, initially bought a house in the area known as Guanwa Lane. On a leisure day, he sat idly in the hall with his younger brother when they suddenly heard the laughter of several women, sometimes near and sometimes far. Dai Cha found this very strange. As the laughter grew closer, he suddenly saw dozens of women scattered in front of the hall, who then vanished in an instant. This happened for several days, and Dai Cha did not know what to do. In front of the hall steps stood a dried-up pear tree, very thick, almost as wide as a person's arms. He thought it was an omen and decided to cut it down. Beneath the roots, a stone was revealed, shaped like a block. When he dug around it, the stone was quite large and had a shape resembling a certain artifact. He then poured vinegar over it and dug deep, about five or six feet, but could not penetrate it. Suddenly, he saw women laughing and clapping their hands around the pit. After a while, they pulled Dai Cha into the pit and placed him on the stone. Just as the family was filled with fear, the women returned, laughing loudly, and Dai Cha followed them out. As soon as Dai Cha emerged, his brother was missing. The family wept in grief, but Dai Cha did not cry and said, "He must be very happy; why should I cry?" Dai Cha refused to speak about the strange events until his death. Dugu Shuya, who often ordered his family to draw water, found the bucket so heavy that it could not be turned. Several people helped him pull it out, and it turned out to be a person. The person was wearing a hat, laughing loudly while climbing the railing, and then fell into the well. The person drawing water found the hat and hung it on a tree in the courtyard. Whenever it rained, the place where the rainwater dripped grew yellow fungus.

A Scholar Named Shi, during the Yuanhe period, once traveled with Daoists to Mount Hua. It was a hot day, and they rested by a small stream. Suddenly, a leaf as large as a palm appeared, red and lustrous, very lovely, drifting downstream. Shi alone picked it up and placed it in his chest. After a while of sitting and eating, he felt his chest becoming heavier. He secretly got up to look at it, feeling the scales on the leaf rise and tremble. Shi was frightened and threw it into the forest, quickly telling the others, "This must be a dragon, we should leave quickly." In a moment, white smoke arose from the forest, spreading over the valley. Shi had not reached halfway down the mountain when a great storm with wind and thunder arrived. When Shi later served as a general, he suddenly noticed a strange light in the room where his wife was staying. He found it unusual and, along with his wife, searched the entire room but saw nothing. One day, while his wife was applying makeup and opening her makeup box, a five-colored turtle as large as a coin suddenly appeared inside, emitting five-colored vapor that filled the entire room. She kept the turtle and raised it thereafter. The Official Zhang Zhoufeng of the Ministry of Works reported that in an old village east of the city, at a place called Dog Spine Point (mentioned in *Shui Jing Zhu*), they once built a wall on the inauspicious direction of "Tai Sui." That night, the wall completely collapsed. They suspected the foundation was weak and the construction incomplete, so they led the villagers to rebuild it. Before it reached several feet in height, the cook shouted, "Something strange has happened!" They rushed to see what had occurred, and several dou of rice all leaped out and covered the ground, sticking to the wall evenly like silkworm eggs, not a single grain being heavy. They stood upright halfway up the wall like a boundary. They then went to a shaman to pour wine on the ground and apologize, and nothing else unusual happened.

The Mountain Flail, also known as the Mountain Demon, is referred to in *The Book of Divine and Strange Things* as a "犬参" (or "操"), and in *The Records of Yongjia Prefecture* as a "Mountain Spirit." It is also known as the Mountain Camel, the Mountain Dragon, the Washed Meat, the Hot Meat, the Radiance, and the Flying Dragon. It resembles a magpie, is green in color, and is also called the "治乌." Its nest is as large as a five-bucket vessel, decorated with earth and white, alternating in color, resembling a target. Those who offend it can be compelled to harm people, burn people's houses, and are commonly known as the "Mountain Demon." The Wu Family Servant, sometimes troubling people, can be appeased by offering at the Wu Family Temple. According to old accounts, it is said to have the surnames Yao, Wang, and Wang. In times of great floods, it ate the bark of the "都树" and starved to death, transforming into the "鸟都," its skin and bones into the "猪都," and women into the "人都." The "鸟都" (or "乌都") has a mirror-like mark on its left armpit, measuring two inches and one-tenth in width, lacks a big toe on its right foot, three fingers on its right hand, a missing left ear, and is blind in its right eye. Those dwelling at the base of the tree are called "猪都," those at the middle of the tree are called "人都," and those at the top of the tree are called "鸟都." Its prohibitions include the "Earth Mound Method" and the "Mountain Magpie Method." Its technique involves using the upper joint of the second finger of the right hand to target the eye of the "山都," and the left hand to target its throat. In the southern regions, people often eat its nest, which tastes like wood. The outer part of the nest can be used to make shoes, treating foot ailments. According to old accounts, the wild fox is called the "Purple Fox," and at night, when it hits its tail, fire appears. If it is to become a monster, it must wear a skull and worship the North Star; if the skull does not fall, it will transform into a human.

Liu Yuanding was in charge of Cai Prefecture, which had recently been conquered. In the grain storage area, foxes were causing trouble, so Liu ordered officials to capture them alive and let dogs chase them in the courtyard for amusement. Over the course of a year, hundreds were killed. Later, they caught a sickly fox. When five or six dogs were let loose, none dared to chase it, and the fox did not run away. Liu found this very strange and ordered to visit the general's hunting dogs and the military inspector also boasted of his large dogs. When they arrived, all the dogs lowered their ears and stood around it. After a long time, the fox jumped up, straight to the hall, through the platform and out behind the hall, to the city wall, and soon disappeared. Liu no longer ordered to catch it. In Daoist practices, there is a special method for the heavenly fox. It is said that the heavenly fox has nine tails, golden in color, serves in the palace of the sun and moon, has talismans and ritual days, and can penetrate the forces of yin and yang. In the southern regions, there is a creature called the wind-lizard, which looks like a monkey, has long eyebrows and a shy demeanor, and lowers its head when it sees people. Its urine can cure wind-related illnesses. Guards often say that the wind-lizard staff is hard to find in the magic herb. Southern people use a long rope tied to a big tree in the wild, hid in a hole near another tree, and waited for it. Three days later, knowing no one came, they searched through the grass. Suddenly, they found a stem of grass, broke it off about a foot long, saw a bird perched on the tree, pointed at it, and it fell when pointed at, then took it and ate it. People waited for it to be tired, ran quickly to take it. Seeing people, it bit them to eat, sometimes not reaching, then threw it into the grass. If it doesn't come down, one should hit it hundreds of times, only then will it be taken by humans. Those who got it, birds and animals die when pointed at. Those who want something, point at it and get what they want.

In the late Kai Cheng era, a commoner named Wang Yi in Yongxing Ward was digging a well. He dug more than one zhang deeper than a normal well but found no water. Suddenly, he heard people talking and roosters crowing below, the noise was very loud, as if it were coming from the next room. The well digger was afraid and stopped digging. The street official reported this to General Wei Churen of the Jinwu Guard. Wei considered the matter strange and unusual, did not report further, and quickly ordered the well to be sealed. According to the lost book *Zhou Qin Guoshi*, in the archives of the Han dynasty, it was recorded that Li Si led 720,000 people to build the tomb of Qin Shi Huang at Li Mountain. They carved with leather ropes, and after thirty-seven years, the underground water and springs were solidified. They reported that it was already as deep as it could go, impossible to dig further, unable to burn, and sounded hollow when tapped, like the sky below (or 'like the sky existing'). Yet knows that beneath the thick earth lies another world. In the third year of the Taihe era, a military officer named Jing Yi from the state of Shou returned from the western capital's autumn defense. His wife had been ill for a long time. When they met, she suddenly said, "Half of my body was cut off and taken to the east garden. You can quickly chase it away." Jing Yi was greatly surprised and hurried to the garden. It was dark, and he saw an object six chi long, looking like a baby, standing naked, carrying a bamboo container. In a panic, Jing Yi was about to strike it, but the object ran away, leaving behind the container. Jing Yi looked at it and saw his wife's half body. He collapsed in shock, or perhaps he did not see anything. Looking back at his wife, he saw cracks like fingers from her hairline to her eyebrows and chest, the skin around the cracks was red. She then said to Jing Yi, "You can prepare two dou of milk and pour it on the spot where you saw the object. I was a stepmother in my previous life. I withheld milk from my stepchild, causing its death. Because of this lawsuit, the underworld judge decreed that I would lose half my body. Without you, I would have died."

In the late years of the Taihe era, in the southern region of Songzi County in Jingnan, there was a scholar staying at the village of a relative or friend to pursue his studies. On his first night there, after the second watch of the night, he had just lit a lamp and sat at his desk when suddenly a tiny figure, only half an inch tall, appeared, wearing a hemp hat and holding a walking stick. The figure entered the room and addressed the scholar, saying, "You have just arrived and there is no host, so you should feel lonely." Its voice was as loud as a fly. The scholar, always courageous, initially seemed not to notice. The figure then climbed onto the bed and scolded, "Do you not observe the etiquette of host and guest?" It climbed back onto the desk, peered at the books, and continued to curse and scold, eventually overturning the inkstone onto the books. The scholar could not bear it any longer and struck the figure with a pen, causing it to fall to the ground. The figure called out several times before disappearing out the door. Soon after, several women, some old and some young, all one inch tall, appeared and called out, "The true official has sent this young gentleman to speak with you because you are studying alone. He is here to discuss profound topics, but why are you so foolish and reckless, causing damage? Now you can see the true official." They came in succession like ants, looking like servants or attendants, and swarmed over the scholar. The scholar suddenly felt as if he were in a dream, and they bit his limbs, causing him great pain. They then said, "If you do not leave, we will damage your eyes." Several heads climbed onto his face.

The Scholar was shocked and fearful, following someone out of the door. Upon reaching the east side of the hall, he saw a door from afar, extremely small, like the door of a high-ranking official. The Scholar then cried out, "What kind of strange and disrespectful creature dares to treat a person so!" He was attacked again, this time by many creatures biting him. In a dazed state, he had already entered the small door. Inside, he saw a person wearing an elaborate hat standing on the dais, with thousands of attendants below, all of whom were very small. The person on the dais scolded the Scholar, saying, "I pitied you for being alone and sent small creatures to visit, but why did you cause harm? Your crime deserves decapitation." Then, the Scholar saw dozens of people, all holding knives and pushing him. The Scholar was greatly afraid and apologized, saying, "I am foolish and ignorant, my eyes cannot recognize the true palace, I beg for mercy and my life." After a long time, the person on the dais said that the Scholar had come to understand and regret his actions, and ordered him to be dragged out. The Scholar did not realize he was outside the small door. When he returned to his study room, it was already the fifth watch of the night, with a dim light still burning. In the morning, he searched for the trail and found a small hole like a chestnut under the ancient wall on the east side. The Chinese geckos were entering and exiting the hole. The Scholar immediately led several men to dig the hole, which was several zhang deep, and found many Chinese geckos, some large and red, about a foot long, likely the king of the geckos. The soil was piled up like a tower. The Scholar gathered dry grass to burn the geckos. There were no further incidents afterward.

In the Xuanping坊 of the capital, there was an official who returned home at night and entered a narrow alley. There was a vendor selling oil who wore a wide-brimmed hat and drove a donkey, carrying buckets and not yielding. The guide grasped him, and his head fell off immediately. Then he rushed into a large mansion's gate. The official found it strange and followed inside, reaching a large locust tree where he then disappeared. He informed the family, and they dug it up. Several feet deep, the tree root was dried up, and there was a large toad as big as a stack of something, holding two metal buckets, with the sap of the tree full in them. There was also a large white mushroom like a floating nail on a palace door, with its cap already fallen off. The toad was actually the donkey, the metal buckets were actually oil buckets, and the mushroom was actually the person. There was a village where someone sold his oil. After more than a month, people found his oil good but cheap. When the secret was revealed, all who ate it became ill with vomiting and diarrhea. At the Longxing Temple in Lingzhou, there was a monk named Huige who did not strictly adhere to monastic rules and was strong enough to lift a stone mortar. He was hospitable and many travelers stayed with him. One night, he gathered more than ten monks from the temple and served them fried cakes. At the second watch, a giant hand covered with fur like a deer appeared, and a loud voice said, "Give me one fried cake." The other monks were frightened and scattered, but only Huige picked up several fried cakes and placed them in the giant hand. The spirit then clenched its fist, and the monk held it tightly with all his strength. The spirit begged for mercy, and its cries were very pitiful. Huige called his family to cut it off. When it was cut, it turned out to be a single feather from a bird. The next day, following the trail of blood out of the temple, they went southwest into a stream and reached a rock crevice where it disappeared. Huige led people to dig, and they found a pit filled with stones.

In the early years of the Kai Cheng era, a commoner from the eastern market lost his father and rode a donkey to buy funeral items. After walking a hundred steps, the donkey suddenly spoke: "I am named Bai Yuantong. I have served your family enough; do not ride me anymore. The flour seller in the southern market owes me 5,400 units of currency, and I also owe you the same amount. Now you can sell me." The man was astonished and immediately led the donkey to find the owner and sell it. The donkey was very strong, and the price offered was only 5,000 units. He went to the flour shop and collected the 5,400 units, then sold the donkey. Two nights later, the donkey died. Kan, the granary official from Yu, lived in Jingzhou. His daughter's wet nurse, Niu, had a son whom his wife loved as much as her own. They were treated equally in terms of clothing and food. One day, the wife happened to get a stem of a quince fruit and playfully gave it to her own son. Niu became furious and said, "You have grown up and forgotten me. I usually share things equally with my son. How can you be biased?" She bit her lip and raised her arm, repeatedly scolding the owner's son. The entire family was terrified and tried to take the quince stem away from her. The son's appearance and height were similar to the wet nurse's son. The wife realized something was strange and apologized to Niu. Niu waved her hand at the owner's son again, and everything returned to normal. Kan performed a ritual to ward off misfortune. He secretly ordered a servant to hit Niu with a weapon in the dark, and the blow hit her head. The impact rebounded and hit the door. Niu became furious and scolded Kan, saying, "If you do this, don't regret it." Kan realized he couldn't do anything about it and bowed and prayed to her. Her anger subsided. Niu remains in the family and is treated like a deity. There are many more strange events.

The Reclusive Scholar of Jingzhou, Hou Youxuan, often went out to the suburbs, where he sat on a desolate burial mound. When he descended, he fell and injured his elbow severely. After walking several hundred steps, he met an old man who asked what ailed him. Hou Youxuan showed him his elbow. The old man said, "I happen to have a fine medicine; you may apply it. If you do not remove it the next day, it will surely heal." Hou Youxuan followed his advice. When he later examined it, one of his arms had fallen off. Hou Youxuan’s brothers, five or six in number, also fell ill, and whenever they were sick, they bled. After a month, Hou Youxuan suddenly developed six or seven sores on both arms. The smaller ones were like榆钱 (elm seeds), and the larger ones like coins, all of which bore human faces. The sores did not heal until his death. At that time, the scholar Du Ye of Jingzhou recounted this story to the guests at a gathering.

许卑山人说，数十年前在江左地区，有一个商人左臂上长了一个疮，形状像人脸，也没有其他痛苦。商人开玩笑地往疮里滴酒，疮也变红了。用食物喂它，任何食物它都会吃，吃得多时，感觉臂内肉会鼓起来，怀疑里面有一个胃。如果它不吃东西，那么手臂就会变得无力。有一个擅长医术的人，教他逐一尝试各种药物，金石草木都试过了。直到用贝母，疮才皱眉闭口。商人高兴地说：“这药一定有效。”于是用一根细苇管破坏了它的口，灌进去，几天后结痂，终于痊愈了。 工部员外张周封说，今年春天，他从拜扫假回，到了湖城的客栈。他说去年秋天，有一个河北的军将经过这里，到了郊外几里地的地方，突然有一个旋风像一个升器，总是在马前升起，军将用鞭子打它，它变得更大，于是旋马头，鬃毛竖起像插着的柱子。军将害怕，下马观察，觉得鬃毛有几尺长，中间有一根细绳像红线。当时马站着嘶鸣，军将生气了，于是拿出佩刀拂去。风因此散去消失，马也死了。军将割开马的肚子看，肚子里没有伤，不知道是什么奇怪的东西。

●Volume Sixteen: Expanded Records of Animals and Plants, Part One (Preface) Cheng Shi holds that the myriad transformations and creations within the realm of heaven and earth, emerging suddenly and rapidly into form, are so numerous and diverse that even the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the *Yilin* (Erya) cannot fully explore them. Therefore, he compiled works from earlier scholars, including plants, trees, birds, and fish that are not listed in the classics or histories, as well as things that are already recorded but not fully understood, or those heard and seen personally but not recorded in written texts. Thus, he wrote the *Guangdongzhi* (Expanded Records of Animals and Plants), hoping to contribute to the study of nature by adding to the knowledge base. In the past, Cao Pi wrote a treatise on fire and cloth, Teng Xun raised doubts about shrimp whiskers, Cai Mo did not recognize *pengqi* (a type of crab), and Liu Tao mistakenly called *liteng* (a type of plant). These errors are still laughed at today; how can scholars afford to overlook such details? General Introduction. *Yujia* gave birth to a flying dragon, the flying dragon gave birth to a phoenix, the phoenix gave birth to a *luan* (a mythical bird similar to the phoenix), and the *luan* gave birth to common birds. The *yinglong* (a mythical dragon) gave birth to *jian niao* (a mythical bird), *jian niao* gave birth to a *qilin* (a mythical creature), and the *qilin* gave birth to common animals. Scales gave birth to *jiaolong* (a type of mythical dragon), the *jiaolong* gave birth to *kunyin* (a type of large fish), *kunyin* gave birth to *jianya* (a type of mythical creature), and *jianya* gave birth to common fish. A deep pool gave birth to *xianlong* (a type of mythical dragon), *xianlong* gave birth to *xuan kang* (a type of mythical creature), *xuan kang* gave birth to *ling gui* (a mythical turtle), and *ling gui* gave birth to common turtles. The sun gave birth to *xuanyang e* (a type of mythical creature), *xuanyang e* gave birth to *lintai* (a type of mythical creature), *lintai* gave birth to *ganmu* (a type of mythical tree), and *ganmu* gave birth to common trees. The *zhaoyao* (a type of star) gave birth to *Cheng Jun* (a mythical figure), *Cheng Jun* gave birth to *xuan yu* (a type of mythical jade), *xuan yu* gave birth to *liquan* (a type of mythical spring), *liquan* gave birth to *ying huang* (a type of mythical creature), *ying huang* gave birth to *huang hua* (a type of mythical flower), and *huang hua* gave birth to common grasses. The sea gave birth to *qu long* (a type of mythical dragon), *qu long* gave birth to *rong hua* (a type of mythical flower), *rong hua* gave birth to *li* (a type of plant), *li* gave birth to *zao* (a type of aquatic plant), and *zao* gave birth to floating grasses.

Insects with hard shells hide in shadows, and insects with wings lie low. Herbivores are strong but dull, while carnivores are brave and fierce. Those that swallow their food whole have eight orifices and lay eggs, while those that chew have nine orifices and give birth to live young. Hornless animals have fat in front, while horned animals have fat both in front and back. Leaf-eaters produce silk, and earth-eaters never stop. Silkworms eat but do not drink, cicadas drink but do not eat, and mayflies neither drink nor eat. Worms (or earthworms) move backward, snakes move in a winding manner, dragonflies fly forward and sing, cicadas sing to the side, dragonflies sing with their wings, centipedes sing with their legs, and beetles sing with their stomachs. Cicadas live for thirty days. A certain type of fish appears in the Shangjin River three months after the start of the year. Partridges fly toward the sun. Carp and crucian carp, as well as scallops and conchs, are similar in appearance. Male phoenixes call "jie jie," and female phoenixes call "zhu zhu." When they fly, they call "return to play," and when they stop, they call "pick up clothes."

The male qilin calls "depart" (or "wander") when it is a sage, and the female calls "return in harmony." In spring, it calls "assist," and in summer, it calls "nourish and comfort." Turtles, which lack ears, are said to possess a special spiritual quality. Tigers have five claws on each paw, known as "ァ." A fish that reaches 360 years of age transforms into a dragon and can fly out of water. A fish weighing 2,000 pounds becomes a dragon. Small fish from Wuyang weigh one pound per thousand. Large fish in the East Sea have eyes as big as a three-dou bowl. Peach bamboo has a section every four inches, and wood apple trees have 121 sections in a foot. Removing the bark from a magnolia tree will not kill it. The heartwood of a thorny tree is square. Snakes are categorized into four types: water, grass, wood, and earth. The last inch of a peacock's tail is called "pearl feathers." The first toe on a crane's feet is known as "soldier claws." There are no rabbits or pigeons in Shu Commandery. There are no wolves or horses in Jiangnan (or alternatively, "coming"). There are no jays or crows south of Zhuti. There are 4,500 species of birds and 2,400 species of animals.

The Dove, born of the Chu Jujube (though mules do not give milk). Cai Zhonglang compares the reversed tongue to a frog, the *Huainanzi* compares the cricket to a small insect, the *Shi Yi* compares the pest to a mole cricket, and Gao You compares the dried magpie to a cricket. Rabbits give birth. Cormorants give birth to young. Gourd seeds are called rhinoceroses, hickory people are called frogs. Frogs have no intestines. Turtles (or yuans) have intestines that belong to their heads. When tadpoles lose their tails, legs grow. Birds that are not pregnant are called fowl, birds that raise their young are called to nurse. Snakes coil towards the king, magpies nest facing away from the Tai Sui, swallows nest in the direction of Wu and Ji, tigers leap to break through, dried magpies know what is coming, orangutans know the past. Storks embrace with their shadows, frogs embrace with their sounds. Cicadas transform into queens, birds give birth to Du Yu. Coconuts are the heads of the Yue King, pots are the necks of Du Yu. One partridge calls, "Towards the south, not the north," while an escaping door calls, "Black gourd tied to the neck" (or neck). Beans are grouped in sevens, millet is piled in twelves to make an inch.

Ginseng grows everywhere, and the orchid, which lives long, is a sign of good fortune. Fruits are called fruits, and fruits on trees are also called fruits. Wheat should avoid the dog year, and barley should avoid the rat year. Certain plants should not grow with three leaves and are killed in early summer. The outside of the plant has hair, and stone plants bloom according to the season. If trees bloom again, there will be hail in summer. If plums bloom again, there will be heavy frost in autumn. If trees grow in clusters without reason, with branches all pointing downwards, and grow up to one foot to one yard and then die, it is all ominous. If there are no birds in the town all year round, there will be bandits. If there are suddenly no birds in the county, the sun and birds will disappear. If chickens fly away without reason, there is a pest in the house. If chickens do not come down from trees at noon, wives and concubines plot. If one sees snakes mating, they will die within three years. If snakes appear in the bedroom in winter, there will be urgent military matters.
