Eating its fruit and spitting it onto the ground would transform into a phoenix, allowing one to ascend to the Taiji (Supreme Ultimate). The *baifu* fungus blooms in heavy snow. The *wude* fungus resembles vehicles and horses. The *jun* fungus resembles a building. For those who study the Dao for thirty years without tiring, a golden-winged bird will bring a fungus as a sign of good fortune. Eating a type of fungus that grows on rocks in Luomen Mountain can lead to becoming an earthly immortal. *Lian* stone: When lotus flowers enter water, they always sink, but only salted brine can make them float. Geese that eat it leave droppings that do not decay for a hundred years in the mountainous areas. It is said that acorns falling into water turn into lotus flowers. Moss: On the steps behind the Tiantang Hall of the Cien Temple, moss grew at the end of the Kai Cheng era, resembling bitter lettuce. Initially, it appeared on the bricks, with a color like salted green, light and tender, and very beautiful. Monk Yilin, who discussed the relocation of the tomb of a chess master during the Taihe era, found that when the tomb was opened, the fragrance was overpowering. The body was lying on a brick platform, appearing as if alive. The moss on the bricks was more than two inches thick, golden in color, and emitted a scent like burning sandalwood.

The *Wa Song*, according to Cui Rong's preface to the *Wu Song Fu*, states: "The *Wa Song* of the Chongwen Hall grows beneath the eaves. If called a tree, it is not clearly defined by consulting mountain dwellers. If called a plant, it is rarely recorded in the annals of Shennong." The *Fu* says: "Shining brightly and uniquely, it resembles the golden fungus growing from the eaves; clearly hanging in the air, it looks like stars planted in the sky. Its branches and flowers flourish, and its roots intertwine. Growing among purple moss and dew, it stands above green tiles and mist." It also says: "I feel ashamed of the sorrowful birds in the Wei palace, and humbled by the red lotus in the Han palace." Cui Rong was a learned man, encompassing all knowledge, so how could he not know that the *Wa Song* has already been described? The *Boya* says: "On the roof, it is called *Xi Ya*; on the wall, it is called *Yuan Yi*." The *Guang Zhi* calls it *Lan Xiang*, a plant that grows on the tiles of old houses. Emperor Ming of Wei loved it and ordered the tiles from the west of Chang'an to be transported to Luoyang to cover the roofs. In the poetry of previous generations, *Xi Ya* was often used; Liang Jianwen Emperor's *Ode to the Rose* says: "Climbing the steps, it covers the green silk; leaning against the eaves, it reflects the *Xi Ya*." Some say that when there are many pine trees planted on the wooden structure, the wood's essence leaks, causing the *Wa Song* to grow. During the *Dali* period, when the *Hanyuan Hall* was being repaired, a person submitted a petition to work on the tiles, saying: "Only I can handle the tile work, and my ancestors have already worked on this hall before." The other workers did not believe him, and he said: "If I can do the tile work, there will be no *Wa Song* growth." They eventually accepted his claim. There was also Li Ahei, who could also build houses. He laid the tiles like teeth, with no gaps, and there was no *Wa Song* growth. The *Bencao* says: "The *Wa Song* is also called *Wu You*."

Melons are particularly averse to fragrances, with musk being especially detested among them. During the early years of the Taihe era, Zheng Zhu was appointed to a position in Hezhong, and his hundreds of concubines and attendants rode on horseback, their fragrance spreading for several miles, offensive to the noses of passersby. That year, along the route from the capital to Hezhong, all the melons died, not a single stem remaining. Water chestnuts, in modern usage, are often referred to simply as "water chestnuts" or "water lilies." Various books on plants do not distinguish between them, but Wang Anpin's *Wuling Ji* states that those with four or three angles are called "water chestnuts," while those with two angles are called "water lilies." In Suzhou, the "folded-waist" water chestnuts often have two angles. Cheng Shi once received a gift of a basket of water chestnuts from Yingcheng, which were triangular and had no injuries (or thorns), and could be used to treat (or prevent) certain ailments. Water chestnuts are also known as "water chestnuts" and "water lilies." In the Kunming Pool of Emperor Wu of Han, there were water chestnuts with floating roots, whose roots emerged from the water, and their leaves submerged beneath the waves, also known as "green water chestnuts." In Xiandu, there were water chestnuts with a green color, shaped like a flying chicken, named "flying chicken water chestnuts," which were often collected by the mythical figure "Xianren Fu Bozi."

Dodder, often growing near thorns and another plant, was suspected by those living in the mountains to belong to the same category. Heavenly Name Essence, also known as Deer Reviving Grass. In the past, Liu Xin of Qingzhou, during the Yuanjia period of the Song Dynasty, shot a deer, cut open its five organs, and filled it with this grass, causing it to suddenly stand up. Liu Xin was surprised and pulled out the grass, and the deer fell down again. This happened three times. Liu Xin secretly recorded this grass and planted it, using it often to treat injuries and fractures. It is commonly known as Liu Xin's Grass. Peonies are not mentioned in earlier histories, only in the *Xie Kangle Collection* where it is said that there are many peonies among bamboo and near water. Cheng Shi searched through the seventy-volume *Planting Methods* of the Sui Dynasty and did not find any mention of peonies, thus we know that peonies were not included in the Sui Dynasty's list of flowers and herbs. At the end of the Kaiyuan period, Pei Shiyuan served as a Langguan (a low-ranking official), on a mission to You and Ji provinces and returned, arriving at the Many Fragrances Temple in Fenyang, where he found a clump of white peonies and planted them in his private residence in Chang'an. During the Tianbao period, they became a rare and beautiful sight in the capital. Famous scholars of the time wrote a poem titled *Viewing Peonies at Pei the Attendant's Residence*, but it was not found at the time. One version of the poem says: "Young people in Chang'an regret the end of spring, competing to identify the purple peonies at Cien Temple. There are also cold jade plates with dew, but no one rises to look at them in the moonlight." Zhang Cheng, a high-ranking official, once saw Pei Tong, the Attendant, say this. Fang Xiang also said: "At the peony gathering,  did not participate. During the Zhude period, Ma Pu governed Taiyuan and also found red and purple ones, transplanting them to the city." At the beginning of the Yuanhe period, they were still few, but now they compete with the number of mallow flowers.

Han Yu, the Attendant Minister, had a distant nephew from Jiangsu and Anhui who was very young. Han ordered him to stay in the academy with other students, but the students all bullied him. Han found out about this and sent him to a temple on the west side of the street to study. After ten days, the head of the temple reported that the nephew was behaving wildly. Han immediately ordered him to return and scolded him, saying, "People in the marketplace who earn their living by selling goods still have one thing they are good at. What are you doing like this? What will you become?" The nephew bowed and said, "I have one skill, but I regret that Uncle doesn't know about it." He then pointed to the peonies in front of the steps and said, "Uncle, if you want these flowers to be blue, purple, yellow, or red, just tell me." Han was greatly surprised and provided the necessary materials for him to try. The nephew set up screens and measuring tools to hide the peony bushes, so no one could see what he was doing. He dug around the roots of the peonies, deep enough to accommodate a seat, and only brought purple ore, white lead, and vermilion red. He treated the roots of the peonies every morning and evening. After almost seven days, he filled in the holes and told his uncle, "I regret that it is a month late." It was early winter at the time. The peonies were originally purple, but when the flowers bloomed, they were white, red, and green, with each flower having a couplet of poetry in purple ink, clearly the poem Han wrote when he was demoted. One verse read, "Clouds stretch across the Qin Mountains, where is my home? Snow blocks the Blue Pass, my horse won't move forward," fourteen characters in total. Han was greatly surprised. The nephew then bid farewell and returned to Jiangsu and Anhui, and he never pursued an official career.

Xingtang Temple has a clump of peony; during the Yuanhe era, it bore one thousand two hundred blossoms. Its colors include true hue, reverse hue, light red, light purple, deep purple, yellow, white, and sandalwood-like shades, but there was no deep red. There are also flowers in which the center of the bloom is not covered by petals. Those with layered petals have a flower face measuring seven to eight inches in diameter. The peonies in the Sushī Hall of Xingshan Temple are of exceptional color. At the end of the Yuanhe era, one branch of the flower was known as *hēhuā* (the happy flower). The *jīndēng* (golden lamp) is also called *jiǔxíng* (nine forms), for its flowers and leaves do not meet; common people dislike planting it in their homes, and it is also known as *wúyì cǎo* (grass of no virtue). The *hélí* (union and separation) plant has roots like the bulb of taro, with twelve rings of "wandering children" around it. Though they grow in dependence on one another, they are not physically connected, linked only by spirit. It is also called *dúyáo* (the solitary swayer) and *lí mǔ* (the separated mother), as if it were something a scholar would eat. Collectively, it is known as *chìjiàn* (the red arrow).

Shu Hui can be woven into cloth. When dry, it can be burned into ash, which can be used to hold fire, and the fire does not go out for a long time. The flowers have double layers. Eggplant, originally referred to as the stem of the lotus, pronounced 'ge xia.' Now it is called 'jia,' but the origin is unknown. Cheng Shi happened to eat some 'jia zi' with several stalks and asked Zhang Zhoufeng, an official, about the story of 'jia zi.' Zhang said, 'It is also called 'luo su,' and the details are in *Shi Liao Ben Cao* (食疗本草). This is mistakenly recorded in *Shi Liao Ben Cao*; it originally comes from *Shi Yi Ben Cao* (拾遗本草).' Cheng Shi remembered the poem by Yin Hou (隐侯) in *Xing Yuan* (行园): 'Cold melons are just lying in the fields, and autumn gomphrenes are full in the ponds. Purple eggplants are in full bloom, and green taros are lush and varied.' It is also called the 'Kunlun gourd.' In the south, eggplants have perennial roots and grow into trees that are five or six feet tall. Yao Xiang once served as a southern selection envoy and personally saw them. Therefore, *Ben Cao* (本草) records that in Guangzhou there is a tree called the 'Shen Huo Shu' (慎火树), which is three or four times around in girth. Shen Huo is the same as Jing Tian (景天), commonly known as Hu Huo Cao (护火草). Eating ripe eggplants thickens the intestines and can cause gas and illness. The root can treat frostbite. To make the seeds abundant, wait until the flowering time, spread the leaves on the path, use ash to mark them, and people will step on them, making the seeds abundant. It is commonly called 'Jia Qie Zi' (稼茄子). Monks often roast it, and it is very delicious. There is a variety from Silla (新罗) that is slightly white and shaped like a chicken egg. In the Xuan (玄) courtyard of Ximing Temple (西明寺), there is a variety. According to *Shui Jing* (水经), 'There are four pairs of stones in Cai Pu (蔡浦), which is a hundred li long. There is a large reed pond at the top and a 'qie zi' (茄子) pond at the bottom.'

**Strange Fungus** In the spring of the First Year of Kai Cheng, before Cheng Shi’s private residence in Xiu Zhu Li, several withered branches of Chinese wisteria had rotted and broken, so he cut them down, leaving about a foot remaining. By the autumn of the third year, a fungus grew from the withered root, as large as a winnowing basket. Its base had five legs, its top bore two halos of yellow and white, and its green skirt hung down like a goose’s leather bag (or harness). It was more than a foot tall. By noon, it turned black and died; when burned, it gave off a fragrance like that of flax. Cheng Shi once placed an incense burner on a dais, and whenever he recited scripture, his disciples believed it was a good omen. Later, upon reading various collections of strange tales, he encountered the story of Chu Sizhuang of Wu Prefecture in the Southern Qi dynasty. Chu had long revered Buddhism and once slept beneath a ditch. There, a short column of nanmu wood stood four feet or more above the ground, with a knot. During the Da Ming period, suddenly a thing like a lingzhi mushroom appeared, growing from the knot. It was bright yellow, and gradually grew several feet in length. After several days, it took the form of a thousand Buddhas, with fully formed faces, fingers, and hands, as well as radiant features and clothing. It looked like a golden butterfly rising, and when touched, it was unusually soft. It usually appeared at the end of spring and fell at the end of autumn. When it fell, the image of the Buddha remained unchanged, though its color turned dark brown. When it fell, his family stored it in a box. After five years, Chu Sizhuang no longer lived beneath it. He experienced no other great success, yet his entire family lived long and prosperous lives. Chu Sizhuang’s father lived to ninety-seven, his brother to seventy, both as strong as young men.

Furthermore, in the Yanxiang Garden of Emperor Wu of Liang, in the tenth year of the Datong era (544 CE), a fungus sprouted in a bamboo grove. It was eight inches long, with a cap resembling a chicken head fruit, black in color. Its stem was like a lotus stem, internally hollow and dry. The skin and texture were all pure white, with a slight red at the base. Where the chicken head fruit was, it resembled a bamboo joint, and could be removed and reattached. From the joint, another layer grew, like a network of webs, all around the same (or "all sides are the same"), about 5-6 inches in circumference, circularly around the perimeter, covering the stem, not touching each other. It was like a network of many eyes, light and charming, and its stem could also be removed. According to the immortals' books, it is similar to the Wēixǐ fungus.

Dancing Grass, originating from Yāzhōu. It has a single stem with three leaves, resembling the plant known as *jueming*. One leaf is at the tip of the stem, while the other two are positioned halfway down, facing each other. When people approach and sing or clap their hands to a tune, the leaves inevitably move as if dancing. Guardian Grass, found in the north of Chángshān. Known as Guardian Grass, it is placed on doors to ward off intruders or evil spirits at night. When someone (or something) passes by, it emits a loud shout. Immortal Grass, originating from Mount Heng. It grows on rocks without roots, resembling a three-stranded green band. It is rare and not commonly found. Sleeping Lotus, found in the South China Sea. The lotus closes its flowers at night, dipping into the water. The official Wei, who was stationed in the South China Sea, personally witnessed this phenomenon. Golden Moss, presented as a tribute during the Jin Dynasty. When gathered, it resembles a chicken's egg. When placed in water, it spreads across the surface, glowing like fire. It is also known as Night-Bright Moss. Strange Grass, found in the fields of Shí. It is a type of grass that contains tiny mice in its leaves. During the mid-Tang Dynasty, I once passed through the north of Cài Prefecture. Along the roadside, there was a grass resembling *hao*, with a stem as thick as a finger. At the top, the leaves clustered together, resembling a *jiāoliáo* nest. Upon closer inspection, there were dozens of small mice in the leaves, about the size of *zaojia* seeds, their eyes not yet open, making soft chirping sounds.

Honey Grass, native to Northern India, grows by trailing or climbing. It has large leaves that do not die in autumn and winter. Due to heavy frost and dew, it becomes sweet, like the salt found in the desert. Old Crow's Claw Fence, with leaves similar to burdock but more delicate, is beautiful. Its ripe fruits are black and shaped like claws. Duck Tongue Grass, growing in water, resembles water shield (莼菜). It is commonly called Duck Tongue Grass. Trailing Grass, native to the regions of Yong and Rong, grows in clusters. Its flowers are slightly larger than those of the hawthorn but do not form full clusters. They are yellowish-white. Its leaves are slightly black. If accidentally eaten, it can cause death within a few days. Drinking the blood of white geese or ducks can counteract it. Alternatively, one can test it by throwing an object and saying, "I buy you." Eating it will result in immediate death. Copper Spoon Grass, growing in water, has leaves shaped like a copper spoon. Water Wintergreen, this grass remains green in water during winter. The author observed it in a pond at a village in the south of the city. Sky Grass, growing in the mountains of Zhongnan, has thick leaves resembling lotus leaves. Water Garlic Chives, growing near water, have leaves similar to those of garlic chives but finer. They are edible. Moneywort, with round leaves and a fine stem, grows near streams. It is also known as Snow Grass or Moneywort. Mouse Ear Grass, shaped like a mouse's ear, is also known as Heart Grass. Cooking Pot Grass, the seed of the morning glory plant, when cut after producing seeds, resembles a cooking pot or steamer. Its seeds are turtle-like, and its vines resemble yam.

The *Wan Hutaos* originate from Nanzhao. They are as large as flat snails, with two partitions, and taste like haws. Some say they are a type of vine fruit found in the region. *You Diancao* has leaves that resemble certain plants, with black spots on opposite sides of each leaf. *San Baicao* is a grass that is not white when it first grows but turns white at the tips of its leaves in summer. Farmers wait for the right time to plant it, saying, "When the three leaves turn white, the grass will fully bloom." Its leaves resemble those of yam. *Luohui* (also known as *Boluohui*) is highly toxic and grows in the mountains and valleys of the Jiang and Huai regions. Its stem and leaves resemble hemp, and its hollow stem makes a sound like *Boluohui* when blown, which is why it is named as such. *Shao Ruo* has roots as large as bowls, and in autumn, its leaves drip dew, from which new shoots grow. *Gui Zaojia* grows in the southern part of the Jiang region. It has a soap-like substance, is about one to two feet tall, promotes hair growth when used for washing, and its leaves can remove dirt from clothes. *Tongtuo Mu* resembles certain plants and grows on the side of mountains. The powder from its flowers can treat bad sores. Its core is hollow, with a light, white, and beautiful inner part that women use to decorate things. *Pishi Sha Hua* (also known as *Rizhong Jinian Hua*) originally came from a foreign country and was introduced to China during the Dading year of the Liang Dynasty. *Zuohangcao* causes people to lose their emotions. It was offered as a tribute by the governor of Fanyang. *Qingcao Huai* grows south of Biniu Mountain in Longyang County. It grows in clusters and is about one foot tall. Its flowers resemble golden lanterns and bloom in mid-summer. One record mentions it lasting for a thousand years. *Zhuru* is found in bamboo, growing on bamboo joints like small balls, and tastes like white chicken. It grows facing north. There is also a large tree chicken that looks like a cup and is called "huzun eye."

Mount Lu has a stone called *Shi'er* (Stone Ear), which is hot in nature. Wild fox silk, in the courtyard there is a creeping grass that grows, white in color, with faintly red flowers, as large as chestnuts, which the people of Qin call *Hu Si* (Fox Silk). Money flower, it is said to originate from a foreign land, and was introduced to China in the second year of the Dading era (533 AD) during the Liang Dynasty. During the Liang Dynasty, officials in Jingzhou played a game called *Shuanglu* (Double Six), gambling with money, and when the money ran out, they used the money flower as a substitute. Yu Hong believed that obtaining the flower was better than obtaining money. Lotus, during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han, there was a branching lotus in the pond, one stem with four (or two) leaves, resembling a paired umbrella. The seeds are like black pearls and can be used to adorn a pendant. During the reign of Emperor Ling, there was a night-opening lotus, one stem with four lotuses, whose leaves open at night and close during the day. Dream grass, presented by a foreign country during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, resembles reed, shrinking into the ground during the day and sprouting at night. Holding the grass allows one to know whether their dreams are good or bad. The emperor thought of Consort Li, and whenever he held the grass, he would dream of her. Black lotus, with leaves like bird wings, commonly called *Xianrenhua* (Immortal Flower). Pigeon ginger, shaped like a pigeon's head, becomes moist when placed in a dry place and dry when placed in a wet place. Birds that touch it fall, and animals that encounter it become stiff. Moon-viewing grass, from the country of Fuzhi. The grass is red in color, with leaves like lotus leaves, which open when the moon rises and close when the moon sets. Red grass, north of Shanrong, there is a grass with a stem one zhang (about 3 meters) long, leaves like a carriage wheel, and a color like the morning rainbow. During the reign of Duke Huan of Qi, Shanrong presented its seeds, which were planted in the courtyard to signify the auspicious signs of a hegemon.

Divine Herb** During the reign of Emperor Ming of Wei, there was a *hehuan* herb in the imperial gardens that resembled the herb *shī*. It had one main stem but one hundred branches. During the day, its branches spread out widely, but at night they merged back into one stem. This herb was called the divine herb. **Three Vegetables** During the Jin Dynasty, there was a fragrant vegetable garden east of the city walls (or the golden walls). There was a vegetable called *yunwei*, which came in three types: purple was the highest quality, with a spicy flavor; yellow was of medium quality, with a sweet flavor; and green was the lowest quality, with a salty flavor. These three vegetables were often used as imperial dishes and could be served as accompaniments. **Palm Seed** The palm seed came from the country of Midao. To grow it, one would take its seeds, place them in the palm of one's hand, and blow on them. With each breath, the seeds would grow, reaching a length of three feet, after which they could be planted in the ground. **Water Net Algae** In the Kunming Pool built by Emperor Wu of Han, there was a type of algae called water net algae. Its branches stretched across the water's surface, growing eight to nine feet long, resembling a net. Ducks and geese that entered this algae could not escape, hence its name. **Ground Sun Herb** In the south, there was a ground sun herb. A three-legged bird wanted to eat this herb, but Xi He, the charioteer of the sun, covered its eyes with her hand. If the bird ate the herb, it would become drowsy and immobile. Eastern Fu, in his childhood, fell into a well and descended to the underworld, where he spent several decades without a place to stay. Someone led him to this herb, but it was separated by a red spring that he could not cross. The person gave him a single sandal, and he used it to float across the red spring, eventually reaching the herb and eating it. **Sword Bean** East of Yelang, there was a place called Rongze, where sword beans grew. Their pods resembled a person holding a sword, growing diagonally.

Grass of Detoxification Five hundred li south of Wuju Mountain in Jianning Prefecture, there grows a grass called "mumu" which can detoxify poisons. When a hundred herbs are flourishing, crows and magpies that accidentally ingest the poisonous "wu hui" will fly urgently to the mumu grass, pecking it to counteract the poison. Chapter Twenty: The Department of Meat and Capture The method of capturing eagles is best during the 20th day of the seventh month, when eagles are more numerous inland than in the border regions. The first ten days of the eighth month are the second-best time, and the last ten days of the eighth month are the third-best time, when all the eagles from the border regions have arrived. The mesh of the eagle trap is 1.8 inches square, with 80 meshes vertically and 50 meshes horizontally. It is dyed with the juice of yellow bark and mulberry leaves to match the color of the ground. Grasshoppers tend to eat the net, so yellow bark is used to prevent this. There are various types of rods used in the trapping process, including net rods, "du" rods, and "wu gong" rods. There are two types of stakes: one for pheasants and one for geese. Pigeons can fly far and often spot eagles before humans do. If they stand upright and look around, follow where they look to track the direction of the eagle. Traps for woodpeckers, sparrows, and hawks have a mesh size of 2 inches square, with 30 meshes vertically and 18 meshes horizontally. All fierce birds, when their chicks are born, show kindness. After hatching, they place the nest outside the shell. Large birds fear that their chicks will fall or be exposed to the sun, which can cause harm. They take leafy branches and place them around the nest to prevent falls and provide shade. To check the size of the chicks, use the leaves as a sign. If one or two days have passed, the leaves are still slightly green. After six or seven days, the leaves turn slightly yellow. After ten days, the leaves wither, and at this point, the chicks are large enough to be taken.

All animals must conceal their forms and shadows, blending with their surroundings. Therefore, snakes change their color according to the ground, rabbits in the grass are always red, and eagles match the color of the trees. The eagle's nest is also known as the "eagle's nest." A young eagle is called an "eagle's cub." Eagles are stopped from flying on April 1st and placed in cages by the beginning of May. When plucking feathers, start from the head, and do so at dawn, stopping at the neck. Remove neck feathers down to the tail. The feathers at the base of the tail are called "neck feathers." Remove the back feathers, large wing feathers, covering feathers, and twelve tail feathers. Each wing has forty-four large feathers, and the covering feathers also total forty-four. Eagles are released from the cage by mid-August. For eagles with hooked beaks, such as the "angle eagle," they are stopped from flying on March 1st and placed in cages by the beginning of April. For "goshawks," after the return of northern eagles, they are stopped from flying and placed in cages by the beginning of April without plucking their feathers. For another type of goshawk, they are stopped from flying in early May and placed in cages by the beginning of June with their feathers plucked. For all predatory birds, they first transform into pigeons, then into "pheasants," then into "pigeons," and finally into "true pigeons." After this, any further transformations result in the true form. White pigeons, those with white beaks and claws, transform from pigeons to pheasants and then to pigeons, retaining their white color throughout. If the beak and claws are black, with yellow markings on the chest and tail, they transform into pheasants, with the feathers on the wings and tail appearing purple and white, while the rest remains white.

In the sixth year of the Wuping era during the reign of Emperor Gao Wei of the Northern Qi dynasty, a white pigeon was received from Pan Ziguang, the Governor of Youzhou. The pigeon's entire body was as white as snow. On its chest, there was a faint vertical white stripe, which was somewhat indistinct and resembled a cloud or mist. The beak had a slight blue-white color at its base, gradually darkening towards the tip. Its claws were the same color as the beak. Its legs were a mix of yellow, white, and red. This was considered the highest quality. A pigeon of a yellowish-brown color would transform into a different type of bird, with its color not changing significantly, except for the chest pattern becoming wider and shorter. After the transformation, the pigeon would undergo multiple changes, with a slight addition of blue color on its back, the chest pattern becoming shorter and finer, and gradually adding bright white to its knees. This was considered the second-best quality. A pigeon of a bluish-brown color would undergo the same color change as the yellowish-brown pigeon. This was considered the lowest quality. There were also two other types of birds, the "Luowu Qi" and the "Luoma Qi," which would transform into hawks within a day. The "Baitu Eagle," a hawk with white beak and claws, would undergo multiple transformations, with its white color remaining unchanged. A hawk with black beak and claws, slightly tinged with blue-white, and a vertical stripe and feather patterns slightly tinged with yellow on its chest, would transform into a different type of bird, with its back, wings, and tail becoming slightly gray. The vertical stripe on its chest would become horizontal, and the color change would be very subtle, almost imperceptible, with the area between the stripes remaining white. After the transformation, the gray color would become slightly brown and gradually turn white. A hawk with extremely black beak and claws, and a deep yellow-brown pattern on its body, would transform into a blue-white type of bird. After the transformation, it would undergo multiple changes, with the horizontal stripe on its chest becoming finer, and gradually turning a certain type of gray.

Emperor Gao Yang of the Northern Qi dynasty, in the third year of the Tianbao era, obtained a pair of white rabbit eagles, the origin of which was unknown. The entire body of the eagles was covered in feathers as white as snow, their eyes were purple, and their claws were white at the base, gradually turning a lighter black towards the tip. Their legs were yellow, and at the time, they were known as the "Golden-legged Eagles." During the beginning of the Wuping era under Emperor Gao of the Northern Qi dynasty, General Zhao Yecha presented a pair of white rabbit eagles. From a distance, their heads and tops appeared entirely white, but upon closer inspection, there were purple marks on the centers of their feathers. On their backs, the feathers had a white base with purple spots on the centers, surrounded by a white and red ring, with a black border outside the white and red ring. Their wing feathers had a white base with purple bands, and on their chests, the feathers had a white base with faint red streaks. Their eyes were yellow like real gold, and their beaks were white at the base, gradually turning darker towards the tip. Their legs were a light yellow, and their toes were also yellow. Their claws were the same color as their beaks. A type of eagle with white feathers, black beak and claws with a slight bluish-white tint, can transform into an eagle with purple patterns and white feathers. After the transformation, it can undergo multiple changes, with the horizontal patterns becoming more intricate and the purple on the chest gradually fading to white. If the beak and claws are extremely black, it can transform into an eagle with blue-white feathers. After the transformation, it can undergo multiple changes, with the horizontal patterns becoming finer and the chest gradually turning grayish-white. A red eagle can transform into an eagle with black patterns. After the transformation, it can undergo multiple changes, with the horizontal patterns becoming finer and the chest gradually turning slightly white. The back color remains unchanged, making it the best quality.

White fabric, after one transformation, becomes blue headgear with a slight gray tint. After further transformations, the headgear undergoes multiple changes, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, and the front gradually turning slightly white. A decorative fabric (one says "female," another says "male"), yellow in color, after one transformation becomes headgear resembling fur, which undergoes multiple changes after further transformations, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, and the front gradually turning slightly white. Yellow fabric, after one transformation and multiple changes, has a color similar to fur but slightly deeper. In general, the headgear with a deep yellow hue undergoes the same color changes. Blue-spotted fabric, after one transformation, becomes blue headgear. After further transformations, the headgear undergoes multiple changes, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, and the front gradually turning slightly white. This is the higher grade of color. White fabric, where "唐" refers to black fabric with spots, after one transformation becomes blue-white headgear with a mix of black. After further transformations, the headgear undergoes multiple changes, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, and the front gradually turning slightly white. Red-spotted fabric, where "唐" refers to black fabric with spots, after one transformation becomes headgear with a predominance of black. After further transformations, the headgear undergoes multiple changes, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, and the front gradually turning brown, though it is still called black headgear by the people. Blue-spotted fabric, where "唐" refers to black fabric with spots, after one transformation becomes headgear with a blue-black color. After further transformations, the headgear undergoes multiple changes, with the horizontal texture becoming finer, but the front remains dark. This is the lower grade of color.

The male and female eagles differ only in size, with no other distinctions in appearance. The small male eagle, though small, is still a male eagle, with mixed-colored feathers that transform from the beginning to the end, resembling the rabbit eagle, with no further description needed. A one-year-old male eagle with broad vertical stripes on its chest is called "乞斑" in the world. When it transforms into a "弁," its chest stripes change from vertical to horizontal, but they remain broad. If the chest stripes are originally fine, they will also become fine after the transformation into a "弁." The "荆窠白" eagle has a short body but is large, weighing more than five pounds, agile and fast, and is also known as "沙裹白." It is born in the desert of the north on thorn bushes and flies towards Yanmen and Mayi. The "代都赤" eagle has a purple back and black whiskers, white eyes and white feathers. Those weighing more than three and a half pounds but less than four pounds can catch rabbits. It is born in the red rocks of the Daidian region and flies towards Xuchu, Zhongshan, and Bái. The "漠北白" eagle is long and large, weighing more than five pounds, with fine spots and short legs, considered the best among eagles. It is born in the north of the desert, with an unknown distance, and flies towards Daidian and Zhongshan. It is also known as "西道白." The "房山白" eagle has a purple back and fine spots, weighing more than three pounds but less than four pounds, and can catch rabbits. It is born on white willow and  trees in the east of Daidian and flies towards Fanyang and Zhongshan. The "渔阳白" eagle has both its belly and back white, with large ones weighing five pounds able to catch rabbits. It is born in Xuwu and the east-west curves and is also known as "大曲" and "小曲." It is born on white leaf trees and flies towards Zhangwu, He Kou, and Bo Hai.

The "Eastern White" bird has both its belly and back white, with the largest individuals weighing over six jin, making it the largest among hawks. It is born in the northern regions of Lulong and Helong, though the exact distance is unknown, and it flies toward Huanxiu, Juhēi (also called Lǐ), Zhangwu, He Kou, and Guangzhou (also called Chuān). Though somewhat soft, if it is a swift one, it surpasses previous hawks. Its color is earthen yellow, and it is found in all valleys and hills. It lives on柞栎 (zǎo lì) trees, varying in size. The Black *sāolí* bird weighs up to five jin and lives on pine and cypress trees in Yúyáng Mountain, though most of them die. Occasionally, a swift one flies to Zhangwu. The White *sāolí* bird also weighs up to five jin and is found in Yúyáng, Bái Dào, Héyáng, and the northern deserts, being present in all these regions. It lives on dead pine trees, is a common bird, and flies toward Língqiū, Zhōngshān, Fànyáng, and Zhangwu. The Green-spotted bird weighs up to four jin and lives on white poplar trees in the north of Dài and in the Dài River valley. Those with fine spots are the swiftest, and they fly toward Língqiū Mountain and Fànyáng. The *Biàn* *zhān* *rěnzi* hawk: the dark green or black ones are the swiftest. If the molt is clean and the eyes are bright, it indicates that the bird has never raised young and is especially swift. If the eyes are cloudy or the molt is not clean, it means the bird has already raised young; such birds are not fit for use and often die. Also, if the tail has no markings, though distant, it tends to gather. If it emits a sharp, abrupt sound, this is a sign of a short life. If the inside of the mouth is red, the back of the hand is hot, and it can warm a person through clothing, this is a sign of a long life. If the bird folds its tail, flaps and strikes the air, stands alone with combed feathers, or sleeps with its head hidden, these are also signs of a long life. In general, when fierce birds fly, they especially avoid mistakes in flight. If a throat disease enters the "chā," none of ten survive. The "chā" is located in the skin before the laryngeal bone, within the clavicle bone, and below the crop.

The tube, made of silver, is as large as an eagle's wing tube. Once the eagle has descended, the size of the tube is adjusted to match the eagle's wing tube. Generally, the fish called "night strip" (夜条) will have a short life if it does not have more than five stripes. If the fish's stripes are like the mixture of red kidney bean juice and white, it will die. Any damage from nets, shaking, rabbit stepping, or crane claws is considered a problem. ● Volume 1 of "Zhi Nuogao" In Silla, there was a noble named Kim Go. His distant ancestor was named Bang. Bang had a younger brother who was very wealthy. Because of the division of their households, Bang asked for clothes and food, and the people gave him a plot of land. He then asked his brother for silkworm and rice seeds, which his brother cooked and gave to him, without Bang knowing. When the silkworm season came, a silkworm was born, with eyes over an inch long. After ten days, it grew as large as a cow and could not be satisfied with eating several leaves of a tree. His brother realized this and secretly killed the silkworm. The next day, silkworms from all directions within a hundred miles flew and gathered at his home. The people called it a giant silkworm, believing it to be the king of silkworms. The four neighbors worked together to reel the silk, but it was not enough. There was only one stalk of rice planted, and its ear was over a foot long. Bang often guarded it, but suddenly a bird broke off and carried it away. Bang chased it up the mountain for five or six miles, where the bird entered a stone crevice. As the sun set, the path became dark, and Bang stopped by the side of the stone. At midnight, the moon shone, and he saw a group of children in red clothes playing together. One child asked, "What do you want?" Another said, "We want wine." The children revealed a golden awl and struck the stone, and wine and cups appeared. Another said, "We want food."

He struck it again, and cakes, pastries, stews, and roasted meats were laid out on the stone. After a long time, they ate and dispersed, and he inserted a golden spike into the crevice of the stone. 旁 was greatly pleased, took the spike and returned. Whatever he desired was accomplished with each strike, and because of this, his wealth equaled that of the state. He often gave his younger brother pearls and jade to support him. Only then did the younger brother regret his previous deception regarding the silkworms and seeds, and he said to 旁, “Try to deceive me with silkworms and seeds, and perhaps I too will obtain a golden spike like my brother.” 旁 knew he was foolish and could not convince him, so he did as he had said. The younger brother raised silkworms, but obtained only one silkworm like an ordinary one; he planted the seed, and it grew into a single stalk. When it was about to mature, it was also carried away by a bird. His younger brother was greatly pleased and followed him into the mountains. When they reached the place where the bird had gone, they met a group of ghosts, who angrily said, “This is the one who stole our golden spike.” They seized him and said to his mouth, “Do you wish to build three layers of糠 (or 塘) for us? Or do you wish your nose to grow a full zhang long?” His younger brother requested to build three layers of糠. After three days of hunger and hardship, he could not complete it. He begged the ghosts, and they pulled out his nose, which became as long as an elephant’s, and he returned. The people of the country were amazed and gathered to watch him; he was ashamed and furious, and died. Later, his descendants would playfully strike the spike in search of wolf dung, but due to a thunderclap, the spike was lost.

Near the stream (or turbulent water) to the northwest, there is a temple. The monk Zhi Tong of the temple often recites the *Lotus Sutra* in meditation. Whenever he sits in quiet contemplation, he seeks a cold, quiet, and secluded place, hardly a place that people can reach. After several years, one night, someone circled the courtyard and called out to Master Zhi Tong, and the sound only ceased until dawn. Over three nights, the sound reached the door, and Master Zhi Tong, unable to bear it, responded, "What do you want me for? Come and speak." There was a creature, six feet tall, in black clothing with a green face, wide-open eyes, and a large mouth. When he saw the monk, he initially made a gesture of respect. Master Zhi Tong stared at it for a long time and said, "Are you cold? Come and warm yourself by the fire." The creature also sat down, and Master Zhi Tong continued to recite the sutra. By the fifth watch, the creature was intoxicated by the fire, closed its eyes, opened its mouth, and snored by the stove. Master Zhi Tong saw this and used a ladle to scoop up the ashes and place them in its mouth. The creature shouted loudly and ran away, making a stumbling sound at the threshold. The temple is backed by a mountain, and when Master Zhi Tong saw the place where the creature stumbled, he found a piece of bark. He climbed the mountain and searched for it, walking several miles, and saw a large green Chinese parasol tree. The tree's branches were already bare, and its root was sunken and looked newly damaged. The monk attached the bark to the tree, and there was no gap when it fit. Half of it was carved into a step by someone who had cut wood, about six inches deep, which was the demon's mouth, filled with ashes and embers, still glowing. Master Zhi Tong burned it, and the demon was extinguished.

A tale passed down by the people of the south tells that before the Qin and Han dynasties, there was a cave lord named Wu, whom the locals called Wu Cave. He had two wives, one of whom died. They had a daughter named Yeh Lien, who was young and intelligent, skilled in smelting gold, and loved by her father. In her later years, her father died, and she was mistreated by her stepmother, who often sent her to dangerous places to gather firewood and draw water from deep wells. Once, she caught a fish scale, which was more than two inches long and had golden eyes. She secretly raised it in a basin of water. It grew day by day, requiring larger containers, until it became too large to hold, so she released it into a pond. Whenever she had leftover food, she would throw it into the pond to feed the fish. When she came to the pond, the fish would always show its head resting on the shore, but when others came, it would not appear. The stepmother knew this and often watched for it, but the fish never appeared. She then deceived her daughter by saying, "You don't have to work hard; I will make you a new shirt." She replaced her worn-out clothes and later sent her to fetch water from another spring, which was several miles away. The stepmother secretly put on her daughter's clothes and carried a sharp knife to the pond. When she called the fish, it appeared; she then killed it with an axe. The fish had grown to over ten feet long. The meat was much more delicious than ordinary fish. She buried the bones under a pile of firewood. The next day, when the daughter came to the pond, the fish was no longer there, so she cried in the field. Suddenly, a man with long hair and rough clothing appeared from the sky and comforted her, saying, "Do not cry; your mother has killed your fish; the bones are buried in the ashes. Go home and take the fish bones and keep them in your room; whatever you need, just ask, and it will come to you." The daughter followed his advice, and gold, clothes, and food appeared as she wished. When the festival in the cave came, the mother went there and ordered the daughter to guard the fruit in the courtyard. The daughter waited until her mother was far away and then went to the festival, wearing a green silk dress and gold shoes.

The daughter born by the mother recognized her and told her mother, saying, "This greatly resembles my sister." The mother also doubted it. The girl realized and quickly returned, thus leaving behind one shoe, which was obtained by the people of the cave. The mother returned and only saw the girl hugging a tree in the courtyard asleep, and did not worry about it. The cave was adjacent to an island, and on the island there was a country named Tuo Han, which had strong military forces and ruled over dozens of islands, with its territory spanning thousands of miles of sea. The people of the cave then sold the shoe to the country of Tuo Han. The king of the country obtained it and ordered his attendants to try it on. Those with smaller feet found it reduced by one inch. Then he ordered all the women in the country to try it on, but none could fit. It was as light as a feather and made no sound when walking on stones. King Tuo Han suspected that the people of the cave obtained it through improper means, thus imprisoned and tortured them, but still did not know where it came from. Then he discarded the shoe on the roadside and immediately searched every household to catch her. If anyone found a woman wearing the shoe, they would capture her and report it.

King of Tuo Han found it strange and searched his room, where he found Ye Xian and made her wear the fish bone to confirm its authenticity. Ye Xian, wearing a green silk robe and stepping into the room, had an appearance as if she were a celestial being. She then reported the whole matter to the king, who took the fish bone and Ye Xian back to the country together. Ye Xian's mother and sister were immediately struck dead by flying stones. The people of the cave pitied them, buried them in a stone pit, and named it the "Annoyed Girl's Tomb." The people of the cave believed it was a shrine, and if they sought a woman, it would respond. King of Tuo Han arrived at the country and made Ye Xian his top wife. In one year, the king, greedy for more, prayed to the fish bone, and the treasures were endless. After a year, it no longer responded. The king then buried the fish bone on the coast, using a hundred measures of pearls to hide it, and gold for the boundary. When the conscripts rebelled, they were going to open it to supply the army. One night, it was swallowed by the tide. Cheng Shi's former servant Li Shiyuan heard about it. Li Shiyuan was originally from a cave in Yongzhou and remembered many strange events in the south.

In the fifth year of the Taihe era, a physician from Fuzhou named Wang Chao was skilled in acupuncture and could cure all illnesses. One day, he suddenly died without any apparent cause, but after a night, he revived. He recounted that he had dreamed of arriving at a place where the walls, towers, and halls were like those of a royal residence. He saw a person lying down, and upon summoning them forward and examining them, he noticed a swelling on the left side of their body, as large as a cup. Wang Chao was instructed to treat it, and immediately, he extracted several liters of pus with his needle. The yellow-clad official said, "You can take him to the end." Wang Chao followed the official through a gate, which was inscribed with the name "Bi Yuan" (毕院). In the courtyard, there were thousands of eyes gathered into a mountain, blinking and flickering. The yellow-clad official said, "This is the end." Soon, two figures, extremely tall and majestic, appeared, standing on either side. They made a loud sound, and the eyes gathered and fanned out, some flying, some walking, and some transforming into human form, all disappearing in an instant. Wang Chao asked about the reason, and the yellow-clad official said, "All living beings come to an end before they die." As he spoke, Wang Chao suddenly came back to life.

A former candidate for the imperial examinations, Li Hu, visited Yingchuan. One night, he arrived at an inn and was just about to lie down when he saw a creature resembling a pig suddenly appear on the hall steps. Startled, Li Hu ran, passing through the back door, and hid in the stable, concealing himself among the hay. He held his breath and waited. The strange creature also followed, its sound circling around the hay several times. It stared intently at the place where Li Hu was hiding. Suddenly, it transformed into a giant star, rising several times to illuminate the sky. The attendants lit candles to search for Li Hu among the hay, but he was already dead. After half a day, he regained consciousness and recounted what he had seen. Within ten days, he died without any illness. During the Yuanhe period, a student named Zhou Yi at the Guozijian often studied at night. One night, he suddenly saw a small ghost with a head longer than two feet, covered with tiny lights like stars, blinking (or flickering) and annoying. The ghost played with the lamp and inkstone, struggling incessantly. Zhou Yi, who was usually brave, shouted at it, and it retreated slightly, then returned to the side of the desk. He then watched its actions, gradually approaching, and eventually captured it. The ghost sat down and begged for mercy, its words being very sad and urgent. As dawn approached, he heard a sound like something breaking, and when he looked, it was an old wooden ladle with over a hundred grains of millet stuck to it.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang (Zhenyuan period, or alternatively, the Shangyuan period of Emperor Xuanzong), there was a monk named Zhi Gong (or alternatively, Zhi Xu) who lived in Baoxiang Temple and recited scriptures. One night, suddenly, five or six flying insects, as large as flies and golden in color, flew in a pattern that resembled the flickering of a flame. Some of them perched on the flower-like wick of the lamp and flapped their wings, appearing the same color as the flame, and eventually disappeared into the flame. This phenomenon occurred over several nights. One night, a young boy hit one of the insects, and it turned out to be a particle of xunlu incense, which had no distinct shape. After this, the phenomenon did not occur again. In the early part of the Yuanhe period, in the eastern market of the capital, there was a ruffian named Rongzi, whose father was cross-eyed. Rongzi was cruel by nature and often stole and ate dogs and cats, causing problems in the market area. One day, he stood in the street with a hawk on his arm and saw two men in purple clothing. They called out to him, saying, "Are you not Rongzi, the son of the cross-eyed man?" Rongzi immediately bowed in greeting. They then said, "We have a reason to speak privately." They led him away from the crowd and said, "The underworld is summoning you. You should go now." Rongzi initially refused to believe them, saying, "You are just humans. Why would you lie?" They replied, "We are spirits." They then pulled a document from their sleeves, which still had wet ink. The document clearly listed Rongzi's name and specified that he was being summoned for a lawsuit involving 460 cats and dogs. Rongzi became frightened and pleaded with the spirits, asking them to stay and provide some wine.

The ghost firmly declined, but was unable to avoid the situation. Initially, he was about to enter the *bìluó* store, but the ghost covered his nose and refused to go forward. Instead, he extended an invitation to the tavern "Du's Pavilion." Bowing and speaking alone, people thought he was mad. He then ordered nine bowls of wine, drank three himself, and left six empty on the west side, asking for their assistance to avoid the situation. The two ghosts looked at each other: "We have received the kindness of this one drink, so we must make a plan." One of them stood up and said, "Wait for me for a few moments, and I will return." Soon after, he returned and said, "You must prepare forty thousand coins for me, and I will grant you three years of life." He agreed, setting the deadline for the next day at noon. He then paid for the wine and returned it, finding it tasteless and cold as it froze his teeth. He quickly returned home, prepared the ritual items, and performed the ritual as scheduled, seeing the two ghosts take the money and leave. Three days later, he died. The ghost had said three years, which, in human terms, was actually three days.

At the end of the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, Ran Congchang, a military officer from Kai Prefecture, was known for his generosity and fondness for good deeds, and many scholars and Daoists in the prefecture often relied on him. A painter named Ning Caitu created a work titled "The Bamboo Grove Gathering," which was very skillful. Among the guests were two young scholars, Guo Xuan and Liu Cheng, who often competed in spirit. Liu suddenly glanced at the painting and said to the host, "This painting is skillful in form and posture but lacks in spirit and interest. I want to show you a small skill, without using colors, to make it more vivid and outstanding. How about that?" Ran was surprised and said, "I didn't know you had such talent! But how can you do this without using colors? What is the reasoning behind it?" Liu smiled and said, "I will enter the painting and fix it." Guo clapped his hands and said, "Are you trying to fool a three-foot-tall child?" Liu then invited him to a bet, and Guo offered to bet five thousand coins, with Ran acting as a guarantor. Liu then leaped into the painting and disappeared, greatly startling the guests. The painting was displayed on the wall, and everyone searched but could not find him. After a long time, Liu suddenly spoke, "Is Guo Zixin coming?" The voice sounded as if it came from inside the painting. After a meal, he suddenly fell from the painting and pointed to the image of Ruan Ji, saying, "The effort was only up to this point." The guests looked and saw that the image of Ruan Ji was uniquely different, with his mouth seeming to be smiling. Ning Caitu saw it and no longer recognized it. Ran thought that Liu had attained some form of enlightenment and thanked both Guo and Liu. After several days, Liu finally left. When Song Cunshou, a recluse, was in the Buddhist community, he witnessed this event.

In the village of Guosheng in Fentai County, there lived a man surnamed Liu who suffered from madness. During his episodes, he would run around wildly, disregarding wells and ditches. His family summoned an exorcist named Gong Min to treat him. When Gong Min arrived, Liu suddenly stood up and said, "I am going out for a moment; I don't need you to treat me." He then took a stick and a bundle of firewood and went to the fields, where he undressed and carried the bundle, appearing as if he were hitting something. After a long time, he returned and smiled, saying, "My illness is cured. Just now I hit a demon's head and it fell off; I buried it in the field." His brothers and the exorcist still thought he was crazy and did not believe him, so they all went together to check it out. Liu dug up a skull with red hair over ten strands, and his illness was finally cured. This was the year of Gaitian in the fifth year of the Kai Cheng era.

In the year when Liu Yancang was in charge of the imperial examinations, there was a scholar from the Guozijian who specialized in the *mingjing* (明经) examination but had lost his name. One day, while he was napping during the day, he dreamed of lingering at the gate of the Guozijian. In the dream, a man carrying a bag of clothes, wearing yellow, approached him and asked for the scholar's name. The scholar told him, and the man smiled and said, "You will pass the exam next spring." The scholar then asked several people from neighboring rooms and his hometown if they would also pass, and some said they would. The scholar then invited them to the Biro Store in Changxing Lane, a place he often visited. Outside the store, dogs were barking, and he woke up, exclaiming, "That's strange!" He immediately called out to the people from neighboring rooms and told them about his dream. Suddenly, the store owner entered and said, "Young master, you and your guests ate two pounds of Biro, why didn't you pay before leaving?" The scholar was greatly startled and undressed to examine himself. As he checked the furniture and objects in the room, everything matched his dream. He then told the store owner, "Both I and my guests arrived here in our dreams; did the guests eat anything?" The store owner was surprised and said, "At first, I was puzzled that the Biro was still intact, thinking the guest might have disliked the garlic." The following spring, the scholar and the three people he had visited in his dream all passed the exam.

Guo Yi, a military officer from Lü Prefecture, was previously the governor of Handan County. After his brother's death, he moved to Yü Prefecture to perform rituals for his ancestors and buried them in a common tomb on the western hill of Fuyang County, Cizhou. The county borders a mountain, and the soil contains many stones. Those with the means to bury their dead often excavate stone caves. Guo Yi chose a site that required excavation as well. After a long period of increased labor, they unexpectedly discovered a hidden chamber. Inside the chamber, they found a stone about four feet long, shaped like a lizard (守宫), with all limbs and head intact, which was accidentally broken by the workers. Guo Yi disliked the stone and wanted to choose another burial site, but this was denied by Liu Congjian, who insisted on the current location. A month later, Guo Yi fell into the latrine, nearly dying. Over twenty people, including family members and servants, died subsequently. From then on, Guo Yi became constantly fearful and restless, speaking incoherently in his sleep. He requested to be relieved of his duties, and Liu Congjian agreed to swap his responsibilities with Jiao Changchu, a military official. When the rebel Zhen (or Liu Ji) led a military uprising, Guo Yi became the leader, and his forces were defeated, resulting in his beheading. All members of Guo Yi's family, regardless of age, committed suicide by jumping into a well. Zheng Bin, an official from Yanzhou, reported that the stone shaped like a lizard is now kept in the official treasury of Cizhou.

The Magistrate of Yique County, Li Shihui, had brothers who served as officials in southern Jiangnan, and he had dealings with a Buddhist monk. On one occasion, while foraging for herbs, he encountered a violent storm and took shelter under a leaning tree (or a qing tree). After a short time, there was a major tremor, and something suddenly fell to the ground. The sky quickly cleared, and the monk went to examine it. It turned out to be a stone, shaped like a musical instrument that could be hung and struck. The top of the stone was smooth and flat as if cut, with a hollow space that could hold something. The bottom gradually widened and became circular, resembling a hanging bag. The stone was two feet long and three inches thick, with a small crack on its left side. Its surface was speckled like broken brocade, shiny and smooth, and could be used as a mirror. When struck, it made a sound. The monk believed it to be an unusual object and placed it among the firewood before returning home. He stored it in a chest and buried it beneath his meditation cushion, where it was occasionally seen by his disciples, some of whom recognized it. Li Shihui earnestly requested to see it, but the monk firmly denied having it. Suddenly, one day, the monk summoned Li Shihui. Upon his arrival, the monk took his hand and said, "I am already weak and frail, and my end is near. The object you sought earlier, I will give to you as a parting gift." He then dismissed all his attendants, led Li Shihui into his inner chamber, removed his bed, dug the ground, and presented a chest containing the stone, after which he passed away. During the time when the rebel Zhang Zhen defied the imperial command, a blind mule in the market of Luoyang was used to grind grain and died for no apparent reason. It was then sold. A butcher cut open its belly and found two stones inside, each as large as a clenched fist, purple with red spots, shiny and smooth, and very lovely. The butcher then presented the stones to Zhang Zhen, who kept them.

韦温担任宣州刺史时，头部生疮，因此将后事托付给女婿，并说：“我二十九岁时为校书郎，梦见在水中流，看见两个吏人拿着文书互相召唤。一个吏人到，说那坟墓非常大，需要万日才能完成，现在还没有完成。现在正好万日，我岂能逃脱吗？”没有几天他就去世了。 醴泉尉崔汾仲的兄长住在长安崇贤里。夏天在庭院中乘凉，稀疏的月色，中午的风吹过，感到有奇异的香气。不久，听到南墙的土动簌簌，崔汾仲以为是蛇鼠作祟。忽然看见一个道士，大声说：“这月色真好。”崔汾仲惊慌恐惧地逃跑。道士缓慢地在庭院中行走，年龄大约四十岁，风度清雅古朴。过了很久，十几个妓女排着队从大门进来，穿着轻薄的绸衣，戴着翠绿的发饰，非常艳丽。有随从铺上香软的垫子，排列坐在月光下。崔汾仲以为是狐狸媚惑，用枕头投向门扇警示她们。道士稍微看了一眼，生气地说：“我在这里还算安静，又贪恋月色。本来没有停留的意思，竟敢如此粗鲁！”又大声说：“这里有什么界限吗？”有两个小人，身高约三尺，大头大耳，只有伏在前面。道士用下巴指着崔汾仲所在的地方，说：“这个人应该有亲属进入阴间，可以带他们来。”两个小人急忙出去。

In a short while, Cui saw his parents and brother arrive, accompanied by dozens of guards, who then dragged and beat him. The Taoist priest scolded, "I am here; dare you allow your child to be disrespectful?" The parents bowed and said, "The separation between the living and the dead prevents us from teaching or disciplining him." The Taoist priest dismissed them and turned to two ghosts, saying, "Bring this foolish man here." The two ghosts jumped to the door and threw a red object like a bullet into Cui's mouth, which turned out to be a thin red rope. Cui was pulled out of the courtyard and further insulted. Cui was so shocked that he could not speak or defend himself. Cui's servant or concubine wept. The courtesans knelt and said, "He is just an ordinary person who was surprised by the unexpected arrival of the official and did not do anything wrong." The Taoist priest calmed down and left through the main gate. Cui fell ill as if poisoned and took several days to recover. He then invited a Taoist priest to perform a ritual to atone for the incident, and there were no further issues. Initially, Cui saw his deceased brother through a paper gap, with a cloth wiping his lips as if they were damaged, causing the servants to be surprised. A maid wept, explaining that when her brother died, he forgot to open his mouth, and the face mask accidentally cut his lower lip, but no one else saw it. She lamented that he still bore this pain in the afterlife for over twenty years.

Xin Mi, after passing the Five Classics examination, traveled to Changzhou for a wedding. On the way, he stopped to rest in the shade of a tree. Nearby, a beggar sat in a basket-like posture, with a scabbed face and lice-infested clothes. The beggar inquired about Xin's journey, and Xin, becoming impatient, left. The beggar followed him. Xin's horse was poor, unable to keep up, and the beggar kept talking insistently. Ahead, they encountered someone dressed in green. Xin bowed and spoke with him, and the beggar responded in agreement. After walking a short distance, the green-clad person suddenly galloped away. Xin was surprised and muttered, "Why did this person act so suddenly?" The beggar replied, "When his time comes, how can he help it?" Xin felt that the words were strange and asked, "What do you mean by 'when his time comes'?" The beggar said, "You will know soon enough." As they approached an inn, they saw dozens of people gathered around it. They asked what was happening, and learned that the green-clad person had died. Xin was greatly shocked and immediately lowered himself, stripping off his clothes and giving them to the beggar, as well as his horse. The beggar showed no sign of gratitude, but his words often had profound meaning. When they reached Bianzhou, the beggar told Xin, "I will stop here."

"What are you doing, sir?" the beggar asked. Xin told him about the marriage agreement and begged him to help. The beggar laughed and said, "You are a scholar, your career should not be stopped. This is not your wife, your marriage date is far off." A day later, the beggar carried a jar of wine and bid Xin farewell. He pointed to the pagoda of Xiangguo Temple and said, "Burn it by noon, and delay your departure." As promised, the pagoda burned at noon, damaging its top wheel. Before leaving, the beggar gave Xin a fine silk handkerchief with a knot, telling him to untie it if he had any doubts. After more than twenty years, Xin became the magistrate of Weinan and married the Pefamily. On the day of his birthday, he gathered relatives and friends. Suddenly, he remembered the beggar's words and untied the handkerchief. Inside was a sheet of paper the size of a palm, inscribed with "Xin's secret wife, Pefamily, born on a certain day," which was indeed his wife's birthday. Xin calculated the time since he parted with the beggar, and his wife had not yet been born. Could it be that the beggar was an immortal from the heavenly realms who had been exiled to the mortal world? Comparing this to黔娄's modesty and杨子's wisdom, it was quite different.

The Northern Capital's Hun Mansion, within the gate adorned with halberds, there is a small locust tree with a hole in it, as large as a coin. Every night after a clear moon, there appear earthworms as large as human arms, about two feet long, with white necks and red spots, leading hundreds of others like ropes, climbing up the tree branches. By dawn, they all retreat into their holes. Sometimes they make a collective sound, often forming a melody. Scholar Zhang Chengyan noted that during the time of Lord Hun, in the hall before the mansion, a tree suddenly emerged from the ground, and earthworms hung all over it. There is a reference to this event, but the book title is forgotten. In the Eastern Capital's Respectable Scholars Lane, at the residence of the magistrate Tian, inside the central gate, there is a purple peony tree that blooms with thousands of flowers. When the flowers are in full bloom, every night there are five or six small figures, about a foot long, playing on the tree. This continued for several years. Whenever people tried to cover the tree, the figures would disappear. In the seventh year of the Tahe era, at the Qinglong Temple in the Northern Capital, there was a monk named Qizong, whose family lived in Fanzhou (or Fan River). His brother, Fan Jing, fell ill with a fever and began to speak nonsense and laugh. Qizong, maintaining his composure, burned incense and chanted. His brother suddenly cursed him, saying, "You are a monk, just go back to the temple and mind your own business. I only came here because I love your abundant harvest. I live in the South of Dreams, and I only came to visit." Qizong suspected it was a fox spirit and struck him with a peach branch. His brother merely laughed and said, "You are not treating your brother properly. The gods will punish you. You should use more force and not stop." Knowing that he could not do anything, Qizong stopped. The sick man then stood up and grabbed his mother, who fainted; he then grabbed his wife, who died; and finally, he mimicked his younger sister-in-law, causing her to lose her sight. After several days, everything returned to normal. He then told Qizong, "If you don't leave, I will call my family members."

After speaking, hundreds of mice appeared, making a loud rumbling sound, larger than ordinary mice, touching people and unable to be driven away. By dawn, they had disappeared. Qizong was terrified beyond measure. His brother said, "Be careful with your words and breath; I am not afraid of this. Now I need my elder brother to come." Therefore, he called out, "Han Yue, Han Yue, come here." After calling three times, there was something as big as a weasel, red like fire, starting from the patient's feet, climbing up the blanket to the patient's abdomen, with eyes shining in all directions. Qizong took a knife and struck it, hitting it on one foot, and it then jumped out of the door. They traced its hole, finding it in a room, where they saw the creature hiding in a jar. Qizong lifted a large basin to cover it, sealed the gaps with mud. After three days, they opened it to see, and the creature was as hard as iron, immobile. Therefore, they fried it with oil to kill it, and the stench spread for miles. His brother then recovered. After over a month, there was a family in the village where six or seven people died suddenly, and people believed it was caused by sorcery.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong, west of Wangyuan Posthouse lived a commoner named Wang Shen. He planted elm trees along the roadside, forming a grove, and built a few thatched huts. In the summer, he often offered water to travelers, and officials were invited to rest and drink tea. He had a son who was thirteen years old, and Wang Shen often sent him to watch for guests. One day, the boy told his father, "A woman on the road is asking for water." Wang Shen then asked the boy to call her in. The woman was young, wearing a green-sleeved robe and a white headscarf. She spoke for herself, saying, "My home is more than ten miles south of here. My husband died, and I have no children. I have now finished my mourning period and am going to Ma嵬 to visit relatives and beg for clothes and food." Her speech was clear and intelligent, and her behavior was endearing. Wang Shen then invited her to stay for the night and said, "You can stay here until before dawn tomorrow and leave." The woman happily accepted. Wang Shen's wife then welcomed her into the inner room and called her sister. The woman asked her to make several clothes, and from noon to the ninth hour, everything was done. Her stitching was fine and meticulous, almost beyond human skill.

Wang Shen was greatly surprised, but his wife still loved her. She then joked and said, "Since your sister has no close relatives, can you be my family's new daughter-in-law?" The woman laughed and replied, "Since I have no place to rely on, I wish to do the rough work of cooking and cleaning." That day, they rented clothes and held a wedding ceremony to make her a new daughter-in-law. That night, it was hot, and she warned her husband, "There are many thieves nearby, do not open the door." She immediately picked up a large beam to guard and went to sleep. By midnight, Wang Shen's wife dreamed that her son, with his hair loose, complained, "The food is almost gone." She was shocked and wanted to check on her son. Wang Shen scolded her, "An old man getting a new wife, is he talking in his sleep out of joy?" The wife went back to sleep and dreamed again as before. Wang Shen and his wife held candles and called out to their son and the new daughter-in-law, but they did not respond at all. They opened the door, which was as solid as iron, and then broke it. As soon as the door opened, there was something with round eyes and sharp teeth, its body blue, which rushed at them and left. Their son was left with only his skull and hair. Zhang Ting's son, whose name was Shenggong, was the magistrate of Zhijiang County. After Ting died, he lived in Zhijiang. There was a man named Zhang Chui who passed the imperial examination but did not rank high, and he traveled to Shu (Sichuan). He had never met Shenggong before. In the eighth year of the Taihe era, Shenggong napped during the day and suddenly dreamed of a person who said his name was Zhang Chui. So he interacted with him, and they enjoyed each other's company for a whole day. As Zhang Chui was about to leave, he left behind a poem as a gift: "Sad and sad, the autumn hall with its hundred-year-old color. But I am alone and lost, in the desolate countryside during the Cold Food Festival." Shenggong woke up in shock and quickly recorded the poem. A few days later, he died.

In the Jianghuai region, there was a man named He Yaqin. He could draw a bow weighing three hundred pounds and often wrestled with a cow, and one of its horns fell off. Once, he passed through the city of Qizhou and encountered a man who was more than six feet tall, with a beard and a wide mouth. The man called out to He Yaqin, saying, "Can you carry me across the bridge?" He Yaqin knew that the man was not human, so he carried him on his back. He felt his head as cold as ice and immediately threw himself onto a crossbeam of the ox shed. He then struck the man, who transformed into a pine tree and bled several liters. During the early Zhaqing era, in the Lishufang district of Luoyang, several carts left through the Longxia Gate. One man was carrying a cloth bag and asked to leave it in the cart, warning not to open it. He then returned to the Lishufang district. Soon after, the man arrived and said, "I have a sore foot and want to rest in your cart for several miles. Is that okay?" The common people, knowing he was unusual, agreed. The man climbed into the cart and, upon seeing his bag, looked displeased. He turned to the people and said, "Why didn't you trust me?" The people apologized. He then said, "I am not a human. The underworld ordered me to record the names of five hundred people, passing through Shan, Guo, Jin, and Jiang, and up to this point, many people had worms. I only managed to find twenty-five people. Now I must go to Xu and Si." He added, "Do you understand what I mean by 'worms'? People with red sores have worms." The cart traveled two miles, and the man then said, "I have a schedule and cannot stay long. You have a long life ahead, so you won't have to worry about it anymore." He suddenly carried the bag off the cart and disappeared. That summer, many people in the world had red sores, but few died.

During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Tang, in the Guangzhi Ward, there was a commoner whose name is not recorded. His family had a sick person who was near death. They invited a monk to recite scriptures, and the wife and children surrounded the sick person. One night, the family saw a figure enter the house. They chased the figure away, and it was thrown into a jar. The family poured hot water over the jar and found a bag inside. This bag is known in the spirit world as a "搐气袋," used to cause illness. Suddenly, they heard a voice from the air asking for the bag, which sounded very sad and urgent. The voice said, "I will take another person to replace the sick one." The family then threw the bag back, and the sick person immediately recovered.

It is said that when a person is about to die, lice leave their body. Some claim that taking lice from a sick person's bed and placing them in front of the bed can be used to predict the outcome of the illness. If the lice move towards the sick person, it indicates recovery; if they turn away, it signifies death. In Xingzhou, there is a place called Lei Xue, where water often fills half the cave. Whenever thunder sounds, water fills the cave and flows out, carrying fish with it. The people wait for the thunder and set nets around trees, catching an endless number of fish. When there is no thunder, fishermen gather drums at the cave mouth, and fish still emerge, though the catch is only half as much as during thunder. Wei Xinggui, when he was the governor of Xingzhou, wrote to his relatives about this phenomenon. In Shangdu Wubenfang, during the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, a family was digging while building walls and discovered a stone box. When they opened it, they saw things like threads filling the box, which flew out. In the midst of being surprised and watching, suddenly a person rose from the box, with white hair, over ten feet long, stood up and shook his clothes, then left the door and disappeared. The family experienced no other unusual occurrences. Many records mention this event, suggesting that it is related to Taoist practices of transforming the body through the influence of the Dark Moon. As the day nears completion, people must reveal themselves. Yu Jiyou, when he was the governor of Hezhou, there was a temple in Linjiang where fishermen gathered. One fisherman cast his net and found it very heavy, breaking the net. Upon looking inside, he found a stone the size of a fist. He asked the temple monks to place it in the Buddha hall, and the stone kept growing, weighing forty pounds after a year. Zhang Zhoufeng, an official who traveled to Sichuan, personally witnessed this event.

The Scholar Wang Yun, a talented and refined man, excelled in depicting objects and phenomena, and he wrote the *Sending You to the Southern Bank of the River*, which was praised by poets. In the second year of the Kai Cheng era, his friend Lu Xiufu suddenly dreamed of being summoned to a place, where guards stopped him outside a screen. He saw about thirty people resembling laborers or convicts, among whom was Wang Yun. Lu wanted to approach him, but Wang looked ashamed. Lu forcibly pulled him to talk, and Wang tearfully said, "I recently accepted a position and hated people to hear about it." He pointed to the others and said, "All of them are in the same position." Lu awoke in a daze. At that time, Wang was going to Yangzhou, where his wife and children lived near Taiping. Lu found the dream strange and waited until morning to inquire about his family. He received a letter from Wang Yun to Luoyang. Seven days later, the news of Wang's death arrived. Calculating the day of his death, it was the same evening as Lu's dream. In the first year of the Wu Zong era, Deng Yan, a military official in Jin Prefecture, had died several years earlier. Jiang Gu, a clerk handling his case, suddenly died of a heart attack.
