Yan jokingly agreed, and the scholar entered the gourd. The gourd was not very spacious, yet the scholar sat with two geese, and Yan did not feel the weight. They reached a tree, and the scholar emerged from the gourd, saying to Yan, 'I wish to set up a simple meal.' Yan replied, 'Very good.' The scholar then spat out a copper dish, filled with a variety of delicacies from both sea and land, spread out before them. After several rounds of wine, the scholar said, 'I previously brought a woman with me, and now I wish to summon her.' Yan said, 'Very good.' The scholar then spat out a girl, about fifteen or sixteen years old, with extraordinary beauty, sitting close to Yan. Soon, the scholar fell asleep, and the girl said to Yan, 'I previously brought a man with me, and now I wish to summon him briefly. Please do not speak.'"

The scholar spat out a man, who was around twenty years old, with a clear and kind demeanor. The man exchanged pleasantries with Yan and drank together in a friendly manner. The scholar seemed to be waking up, and the woman spat out a brocade screen to cover the scholar. After a long time, as the scholar was about to wake up, the woman swallowed the man and sat alone with Yan. The scholar slowly rose and said to Yan, "A short nap turned into a long stay. It is already late, so it is time for us to part." The scholar then swallowed the woman and all the bronze plates, taking them all into his mouth. He left a large bronze plate with Yan, saying, "There is nothing to express my feelings, so I will keep you in mind." According to the Buddhist text, *The Sutra of Parables*, it is recorded that a Brahmin (or Buddhist monk) once performed a magical act, spitting out a jar. Inside the jar was a woman, whom he took to a secluded place to establish a household. The Brahmin rested briefly, and the woman then performed a magical act, spitting out another jar containing a man. They continued to sleep together. When the Brahmin woke up, he swallowed each other in turn and left with a staff. Wu Jun once read this story and was surprised by the account, considering it extremely strange.

It is said during the Tianbao era, a Taoist priest named Gu Xuanji from the Central Mountain (Mount Song) often carried gold and wandered in the market. After many years, he suddenly met a man who forced his way into the flag pavilion, carried a pot, and drank to his heart's content. They grew familiar with each other day by day, and in a year, the man paid him hundreds of gold pieces. The man suspected there was a purpose and bowed, asking for his wishes. Gu Xuanji smiled and said, "I have already refined the elixir of immortality eight times. I need one person to guard it, and to endure one night without speaking. Then, my task will be complete. I see that you have a calm spirit and bold courage. I will trouble you with one night's labor. If the elixir is successful, we will meet in the Great Clarity." The man replied, "Death is not enough to repay your kindness, why go to such lengths?" So, he followed him into the Central Mountain. The peak was extremely dangerous, and in the cave, there was an alchemical furnace and a basin, with dripping milk-like spring water. The wild pines blocked the view. Gu Xuanji took some dry rice to eat, and on that day, he submitted a petition to seal the gāng. By evening, he gave him a board and said, "You can strike this to know the time. At the fifth watch, someone will come here; be careful not to speak with them." The man said, "I will follow the agreement." At the fifth watch, suddenly, several iron-clad horsemen shouted, "Move aside!" The man did not move. After a while, a figure appeared as if a king, with a grand retinue. He asked, "Why don't you move aside?" He ordered his attendants to kill the man. The man woke up as if from a dream and was born into a wealthy merchant's family. When he grew up, he remembered Gu Xuanji's instruction to remain silent. His parents arranged a marriage for him, and he had three sons. One day, his wife wept, "You never speak, what use are these sons to me!" She proceeded to kill them one by one. The man cried out, and suddenly woke from his dream. The alchemical furnace shattered as if by an earthquake, and the elixir had already flown away.

In the *Records of the Western Regions* by the monk Xuanzang, it is recorded: "In the kingdom of Central Tianbaola (a transliteration of a foreign name) east of the Deer Field, there is a dried-up pool named 'Save Life,' also known as 'Loyal Hero.' In ancient times, a hermit built a hermitage by the side of the pool. He possessed the power to transform humans and animals into other forms, to transmute rubble into gold and silver, but he could not yet ascend to the heavens. Therefore, he erected an altar and performed rituals, seeking a loyal hero. For many years, he found none. Later, he encountered a man in the city and befriended him. They visited the pool together, where the hermit gave the man five hundred pieces of gold and silver, saying, 'Come to claim them all at once.' They did this several times, and the loyal hero repeatedly asked to sacrifice himself. The hermit said, 'I ask you to remain silent all night.' The loyal hero replied, 'I fear no death, but why should I remain silent for just one night?' The hermit then instructed the loyal hero to stand holding a knife beside the altar, while he pressed his sword and recited spells. Just as dawn approached, the loyal hero suddenly cried out, and fire descended from the sky. The hermit quickly led the man into the pool. After a long time, he emerged and scolded the loyal hero for breaking his promise. The loyal hero explained, 'After midnight, I became dazed as if in a dream and saw my former master come to comfort and advise me. I restrained myself from speaking, which angered him, and he caused my death. I was reborn in a Brahmin family in the southern heaven, enduring great hardships. I never spoke of my gratitude. When I married and had a son, I lost my parents and remained silent. At sixty-five, my wife suddenly became angry and threatened, "If you do not speak, I will kill your son." I realized that I had lived another life, and being old and frail, I had only this son. I should have immediately stopped her, but I unconsciously spoke these words.' The hermit said, 'This was done by a demon; it was my fault.' The loyal hero, filled with shame and anger, died." This account, when misinterpreted, led him to become a Daoist priest in the central mountains.

It is said that when Master Yi first visited Master Huayan, Huayan invited him to sit. After a while, Huayan asked, "Where is my mind?" Master Yi replied, "The teacher is riding a white horse past the temple gate." Huayan asked again, and Master Yi said, "How dangerous! Where is the teacher standing at the end of the stupa?" Huayan responded, "Your intelligence is indeed not in vain. Try to observe me again." After a long while, Master Yi bowed deeply with his forehead touching the ground and his face turning red, and said, "Hasn't the teacher entered the realm of Universal Virtue?" Zheng Fu, the editor at the Imperial Library, said, "Liu Zhongyong was skilled in the *I Ching* and once visited Master Pujing. The master said, 'Divine where my mind is.' Liu replied, 'The master's mind is on the seventh beam of the eaves.' When asked again, Liu said it was somewhere else. Pujing said, 'All things cannot escape their destiny. I will try to escape it, and you can test it.' After a long time, Liu said, 'It is true. Being still and motionless, I cannot know it.' The biography of Master Shen records that when Master Rizhao, a high-ranking monk, visited Shen, Shen did not greet him and directly reprimanded him, saying, 'Why did a monk enter a noisy and chaotic place?' Shen looked slightly surprised and did not respond. Shen then said, 'Standing should not exceed the height of a person's head. How could one allow one's body to be outside like a bird?' Shen replied, 'My mind is in the market, and my heart is at the end of the stupa. If the three藏 are truly wise, they will understand me.'"

Sunlight then counted on his fingers, saying, “This realm is empty and quiet, all Buddhas arise from it naturally.” I refer to the *Liezi* which states: “There was a divine sorcerer who came from the state of Qi and settled in Zheng, named Ji Xian. Liezi saw him and was deeply impressed, and told Huqiu Zi. Huqiu Zi said, ‘Try to bring him, let me show you.’ The next day, Liezi brought him to see Huqiu Zi. Huqiu Zi said, ‘I showed you using the earth's pattern, probably you saw my way of concealing my virtue.’ He tried again to bring him, and Liezi again brought him to see Huqiu Zi. Huqiu Zi said, ‘I showed you using the heavens and the earth.’ The next day, Liezi again brought him to see Huqiu Zi, and upon leaving, said, ‘Your teacher is not consistent, I cannot discern anything. I showed you using the great void without sign.’ He tried again to bring him, and the next day he brought him to see Huqiu Zi, but Ji Xian lost control and ran away. Huqiu Zi said, ‘I interacted with him in a way that was empty and swaying, thus he became square and fine, thus he became a flowing wave, and that is why he ran away.’” I believe that all these stories are interconnected and refer to the same event. For example, during the Jin dynasty, there was someone who rolled a hundred times and got a hundred ‘Lu’, and Wang Yan said, “The later roll is like the earlier roll.” This was derived from the *Liezi* text, which suggests equality or consistency in outcomes. At the time, people considered this a famous saying. People are easily deceived, and there are many such cases.

It is said that in the Jiangsu-Anhui region there is a station, commonly known as Lujin. Once a man, drunk, stopped there, and one night, he was bitten by mosquitoes, causing his blood to drip and his tendons to show, and he died. According to Jiang Dezao's *Peng Beidao Ji*, it states: "From Shao Bo Di, thirty-six miles, to Lujin. There was a patrol during the Liang dynasty. This place is full of mosquitoes, and the elders say that a deer passed through here, and one night it was eaten by mosquitoes, and by dawn only the tendons were left, hence the name." There is a tomb in the Kunming Pool, commonly known as Hunzi. It is said that in ancient times, there was a man named Hunzi who lived in the area. He often disobeyed his father's words, going east when he should have gone west, and water when he should have gone fire. When he was on the point of death, he wanted to be buried in a place called Lingtun, and falsely said, "When I die, I must be buried in the water." When he died, the people wept, saying, "I cannot disobey my father's command today." So he was buried there. According to Sheng Hongzhi's *Jingzhou Ji*, it states: "Gu Cheng is situated by Er River, and on the north bank of Er River, there is a Five Women's Mound. In the Western Han dynasty, someone was buried in Er River, and the tomb was about to be damaged by water. The person had five daughters, who together created this mound to protect the tomb." It also says: "One daughter married a man named Henzi from Yin County, who had a fortune of ten thousand gold, and from youth to old age, he did not follow his father's words. On his deathbed, he wanted to be buried on a mountain, but feared that his son would not agree, so he said that he must be buried in a shallow area in the water. Henzi said, 'I have never listened to my father's teachings, but now I will follow this one statement.' So he scattered all his wealth, built a stone tomb, and surrounded it with earth, forming a small island several steps long. In the Yuan Kang period, it began to be damaged by water. Now the remaining stones form a half-table shape, numbering several hundred, gathered in the water."

In the army, they will shoot deer, and often, the shooting range is also painted with deer. In Li Ji's "Record of Visiting Liang," it is said: "The Liang envoy He Ji pointed and shot from horseback, praising his skill. He said, 'You Yu hits the target every time, and King Gong of Chu considers it a disgrace.' He Ji could not respond. There was also a shooting range where targets were placed on boards, and many hits were recorded. He said, 'How could one not shoot deer?' He Ji replied, 'The emperor values life and does not want to harm deer.' Thus, from deer to deer, it is not different. Now, when speaking of owls and mirrors, people often call the spiders in the wall mirrors, seeing their round and flat shapes, and noting that their young are eaten by them. In the Western Han, it is recorded: "In the spring sacrifice to the Yellow Emperor, an owl and a broken mirror were used." Because owls eat their mothers, on the fifth day of the fifth month, an owl soup was made. A broken mirror eating its father is like a tiger's eye. The Yellow Emperor wanted to eliminate the owl's kind, so all things were used to represent it. Fu Xuan's poem says: "Offerings for the sacrifice included a broken mirror, and the meal used an owl."

The *Chao Ye Qian Zai* records: "At the end of the Sui dynasty, there was a man named Zan Junmo who was skilled in archery. He could shoot with his eyes closed and hit exactly where he aimed, saying that if he aimed at an eye, he would hit the eye, and if he aimed at a mouth, he would hit the mouth. There was a man named Wang Lingzhi who learned archery from Zan. He believed that he had mastered all its subtleties and wanted to shoot Zan to death so that he could alone possess such skill. Zan held a short knife and would cut any arrow that came his way. Only one arrow remained, and Zan opened his mouth to catch it, biting the arrow shaft. He laughed and said, 'After three years of learning archery, I have not yet taught you the technique of biting the arrow.'" The *Liezi* states: "Gan Ying was an ancient master archer. His disciple, named Fei Wei, surpassed his teacher in skill. Ji Chang, another student of Fei Wei, used a bow made from the horn of a ram and a bamboo rod from the north, and could shoot through the heart of a flea. After mastering all of Fei Wei's techniques, Ji Chang believed that there was only one person in the world who could match his skill, so he plotted to kill Fei Wei. They met in the wild, and the two engaged in a duel. Their arrows touched each other in mid-air and fell to the ground without disturbing the dust. Fei Wei's arrows ran out first, and Ji Chang fired one more arrow. Fei Wei caught it with the tip of a thorn without missing. Moved by this, the two wept and threw down their bows, asking to become father and son. They cut their arms to swear that they would never reveal their techniques to others." The *Mencius* says: "Feng Meng learned archery from Yi Di and mastered all his methods. He believed that only Yi Di was better than him, so he killed Yi Di."

When I was young, I once heard from relatives and acquaintances about Zhang Fen, the Zhongcheng, who was in the entourage of Wei Nan Kang Gao. At a banquet, there was a guest who hit flies with beans from a bowl using a counting stick, and he did not miss one out of ten. The entire audience was surprised and laughed. Zhang Fen said, "Don't waste my beans." Then he pointed to a fly, picked it up by its hind legs with his fingers, and not one escape occurred. He could also balance a bowl on his fist and walk ten steps without it falling. The *Chao Ye Qian Zai* records that Yuan Sizhong, the son of Ping, the Lushi Canjun of Tengzhou during the false Zhou dynasty, could hit flies with chopsticks, causing them to fly up, and pick them up by their hind legs, not missing one out of a hundred.

Scholars often refer to the protective nets on palace rafters as "罘ぜ," which is a shallow but significant error. The *Rites of Zhou* states, "The *shu ping* is a decorative element in the emperor's temple." Zheng Xuan notes, "The *ping* is called a tree and is now referred to as *fu ze*. It is adorned with designs resembling clouds, animals, and insects, similar to modern gates." Zhang Yi's *Erya* defines, "Repeated thoughts are called *ping*." Liu Xi's *Shiming* explains, "The *fu ze* is located outside the gate. *Fu* means to repeat or reinforce. Officials entering the palace to present matters use it to emphasize their thoughts." The *Western Han* records, "In the seventh year of Emperor Wen's reign, there was a disaster at the eastern gate of Weiyang Palace. The *fu ze* outside symbolized the feudal lords. Later, the Seven States rose in rebellion." Another account states, "Wang Mang, who was fond of numerology, sent envoys to destroy the *fu ze* at the tombs of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing, saying, 'To prevent people from thinking about the Han dynasty.'" Yu Huan's *Wei Lue* records, "In the third year of Huangwu, *fu ze* were constructed outside the gates of various palaces." Since I began my official career, I have encountered dozens of scholars who have made incorrect statements about "枭镜" and "fu ze."

In the *Shìhuà*, it is said that birds make nests with straw and mud, and those with many sounds but slightly smaller are called Han swallows. Tao Shengli, in his commentary on *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine), states: "Those with purple chests and are light and small are called Yue swallows. Those with black chest spots and loud sounds are called Hu swallows, and they prefer to build long nests. Yue nests are not used in medicine." Yue swallows differ from Han swallows in these aspects. I have often heard that during the reign of the Empress Dowager, a three-legged crow was presented. Some said one leg was fake. The Empress Dowager smiled and said, "But should we not just record it in the historical records? Why bother to investigate its authenticity?" The *Tangshu* (History of the Tang Dynasty) records: "In the first year of Tianzhu, a three-legged crow was presented. The Empress Dowager considered it a good omen for the Zhou dynasty. Emperor Ruizong said, 'The front leg of the crow is fake.' The Empress Dowager was displeased. Soon after, one leg fell off the ground." The *Shìhuà* also mentions that the lamentation songs originated from Tian Heng, who died, and his followers dared not cry loudly, so they sang songs to express their grief. In *Chūlǐ* (Early Rituals) by Zhì Yú, it is said: "The lamentation songs originated from Emperor Wu of Han. The laborers were tired, and their singing was sorrowful, thus they were used to send the dead, which is not an ancient custom." Yan Houben, a government official, said: "The lamentation songs have been around for a long time. According to *Zuo Zhuan* (Commentary of Zuo), during a meeting with the Duke of Wu to attack Qi, just before the battle, Gong Sun Xia ordered his men to sing *Yú Bìn* (Lamentation for the Dead), showing their determination to die." Recently, I read *Zhuangzi* (Master Zhuang), which says: "The lamentation songs for the living must be both gentle and harsh." Sīmǎ Bīao commented: "The rope used to pull the coffin is called *fú*. The songs are lamentation songs. Gentle means soft, and harsh means urgent. The person pulling the rope is exerting effort for others."

Traditional accounts say that the game of "cang gou" (藏钩) originated with the Hooked Hand Lady, as recorded in Xin Shi's *San Qin Ji*: "Emperor Wu of Han's Hooked Hand Lady had a clenched fist, and people at the time imitated her, calling the game 'cang gou.'" The *Liezi* (列子) states: "Those who hide in clay are clever, those who hide in hooks are cautious, and those who hide in gold are dazed." According to Yin Jingshun's commentary: "The characters '抠' and '抠' are the same. People are divided into groups, each hiding an object in their hand and then trying to retrieve it. If there is an extra person left, they move between the two groups, and this person is called 'hungry kite.'" The *Fengtu Ji* (风土记) says: "The game of 'cang gou' divides people into two groups to determine a winner. If the number of people is even, they face off against each other; if odd, one person is designated as a wandering addition, belonging to either the upper or lower group, and is called 'flying bird.'" Today, this game is played in the first month of the lunar calendar. According to the *Fengtu Ji*, it is played after the New Year's Eve sacrifice. In the preface to *Cang Gou Fu* (藏钩赋) by Yu Chan (庾阐), it is said: "After the New Year's Eve sacrifice, I ordered people inside and outside the palace to play 'cang gou.'"

The *Shi Shuo* says that the game of *danqi* originated in the Wei court, and it was a game played in the makeup box. In *Lunheng*, it is stated: "I have little interest in other playful activities, but only in *danqi* have I mastered its intricacies. There are the Marquis of Machexiang, Dongfang Shian, and Zhang Gongzi in the capital, and I regret not being able to play against these few." It is clear that *danqi* did not originate in the Wei court. Today, *danqi* uses twenty-four pieces, distinguished by color to signify rank, and one bean is placed after the game ends. *Zuo You Fang* states: "There are six white and six black pieces, arranged in the shape of a *liubo* board (or in the shape of a large board). They are quite like pillows. Additionally, the Wei method of playing involves first placing one piece in the center of the board, then the remaining players think about surrounding the white and black pieces, and eighteen sticks make up a city." *Nan Liang Zhiyi* says: "The eight-seat Shangshu (ministers) wrap their handboards with purple gauze and hang white silk at the top like a pen." *Tongzhi* states: "Today, the handboards of the Sheshi (Counsellors) and Shangshu (ministers) are wrapped in purple leather, called *hu*. Since the middle of the Liang Dynasty, only the eight-seat Shangshu who hold the *hu* have white pens attached to the top, wrapped in purple gauze. The other officials only hold handboards. People today say that Chen Xilie found it inconvenient to tax the *hu*, so he wrapped it in silk when riding a horse and had his attendants hold it. Li Right Seat said, 'This became a future precedent, which was very wrong.'" People today call ugliness "maoqin," which is a mistake. *Wei Zhi* states: "Liu Biao, because of Wang Can's appearance, thought he was too ordinary, and did not highly regard him." One says, "His appearance was ordinary, but he was very knowledgeable, and he was highly regarded," with a note stating, "qin means appearance is insufficient."

During the late Tahe period, on my brother's birthday, I watched various performances. There was a street performer who mispronounced "扁鹊" as "褊鹊," with the character pronounced in the rising tone. I asked my guest, Ren Dao-sheng, to correct the pronunciation. The performer explained that twenty years ago, during a gathering in the capital, a scholar greatly appreciated the pronunciation of "扁" as similar to "褊," claiming that the common people were all mistaken. I suspected this was an attempt to cover up their error and laughed at it. Recently, I read in Zhen Liyan's *Bencao Yinyi* (Phonetic Meanings of Herbal Names), citing Cao Xian, that "扁" should be pronounced as "bian," not "bu." According to historical records, Bian Que's surname was Qin, and his courtesy name was Yue Ren. The county of Bian belonged to the state of Bohai. Today, in the game of Liubo, the character "乘" is pronounced with a falling tone, and when there are no teeth, it is called "乘." According to *Bo Sai Jing* (Treatise on Board Games), "无齿为绳，三齿为杂绳" (without teeth, it is a rope; with three teeth, it is a mixed rope). The current practice of the game of *chupu* involves eleven characters. According to the *Jin Shu* (History of the Jin Dynasty), Liu Yi, along with Song Zu and Zhu Ge Changmin, gathered at the eastern government office to play games, and they all agreed to large bets, expecting to reach millions. Others returned with black calves, but Liu Yi later rolled a wild goose. Now, at the palace gate, palace attendants lead the officials through a ceremonial process. Some say this practice began with Empress Wu Zetian, while others attribute it to the Northern Wei Dynasty. According to the *Kaiyuan Lishi Shu* (Commentary on the Kaiyuan Rites), Empress Chu of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, who ruled while seated, had palace attendants lead the officials in bowing. Envoys from the northern barbarian states saw this and adopted the custom upon returning to their country. At that time, rituals and music were prevalent in the south, and the north followed their customs. The Zhou and Sui dynasties continued this tradition, and the current dynasty has not changed it.

The position of Shizhong (侍中) was of very low rank during the Western Han dynasty, akin to the modern-day imperial guard. Those who served in this capacity were all part of the inner palace. The term "Zhongyan" (中严) refers to the state of the emperor being fully dressed and ready for official duties, as it is improper to directly mention the emperor, hence the use of "Zhong." Today, the rank and status of Shizhong are vastly different from the Han dynasty, yet the term "Zhongyan Wai Ban" (中严外办) is still used, which is incorrect. According to the "Rites" (礼), weddings must be held at dusk, as it symbolizes the union of male and female energies, with yang energy going out and yin energy coming in. However, today the ceremony is held in the morning; sacrifices are performed at dawn, but now the custom is to perform ancestral sacrifices at dusk, which is a significant deviation from tradition. Rituals for spirits and burials within the palace are also held at dusk. Additionally, in the homes of today's literati and officials, the wedding ceremony involves setting up a canopy for the bride and groom, known as "Ruzhang" (入帐). The bride rides on a horse, a practice that is a remnant of the Northern Dynasties. The "Pihuo Beidao Ji" (聘北道记) states: "In Northern weddings, a blue cloth tent must be set up, called Qinglu (青庐). Here, the couple bows to each other and the bride is welcomed. The groom's family, numbering over a hundred, surrounds the carriage and shouts, 'New bride!' urging her to come out. The shouts continue until she boards the carriage. This is known as 'Cui Zhuang' (催妆) today. Hitting the groom with a bamboo stick is a playful ritual, and sometimes it can be quite forceful." Jiang Dezhao (江德藻) recorded these customs as unusual, indicating that they were not practiced in the Southern Dynasties. Furthermore, the practice of using geese instead of doves in the wedding ceremony, tying the bride's hair in a specific way, lighting candles and playing music during the ceremony, and serving wine to the parents and children are all too chaotic and unstructured, mixed and borrowed from various sources.

In the current times, scholars and officials who lose their wives often use very long walking sticks, which are called "over-the-head canes." According to the *Book of Rites*, if the father is still alive, the eldest son does not use a walking stick when his wife dies, but other sons do. According to the *Book of Rites*, they wear mourning clothes for their father, and I wear mourning clothes for my mother. The walking sticks are the same as those used for mourning. ●Continued Collection, Volume Five: Records of Temples and Pagodas In the third year of Emperor Wuzong's reign during the year of the Water Rat (癸亥), I, along with Zhang Xifu and Zheng Fumeng, who were both officials in the imperial secretariat, took a day off and visited the Daxing'an Temple. We inquired about the *New Records of the Two Capitals* and the *Record of Sightseeing*, which were incomplete and omitted many details. We decided to visit temples on both sides of the city for ten days, starting with the temple on the east side of the street. We recorded anything that was not mentioned in the two books. We also visited the Cien Temple, where we learned that the government planned to merge the temple, and the monks were in a hurry. We casually asked a few monks and recorded the paintings under the pagoda. Our visits to temples ended here. Three years later, I was stationed in Luoyang. I was appointed as the governor of Ancheng and returned to the capital in the seventh year of Emperor Dazong's reign. I was away for six decades, and many of my books were damaged. In the old records, I found my visits to temples with two deceased friends, and my tears mixed with blood. The happy moments of that time were long gone. I was in the process of editing and organizing the records, but only enough to continue the damaged records, and more than half were lost. I completed two volumes and passed them on to Buddhist monks. I am Duan Chengshi, a native of Dongmu, with the courtesy name Kegu.

Jing Shan Fang Da Xing Shang Temple, named after two characters from "Da Xing" and one from the district name. According to a new record, "The Udayana Buddha statue was burned by fire at the beginning of the Zongzhang era." It is said that during the Liang dynasty, the Udayana statue was in Jingzhou, and it was claimed that the Sui dynasty moved it from the capital city to this temple, which is incorrect. Now there is another Ya檀 statue with open eyes, and its craftsmanship is quite crude, even more erroneous. Before Faguo's stupa, there are many old pines. During a drought, officials cut branches to make dragon bones to pray for rain. It is believed that Faguo controlled dragons, and the tree must be divine. On the wall behind the main hall of Xiangxiang Temple, during the middle of the Yuanhe era, painter Liang Qie painted two pines, slightly transcending the mundane style. The sculptures in the Manjushri hall are extremely exquisite, and there is a gold-leafed frame on the exterior wall, brought by Faguo from the Western Regions. There is a Sui dynasty relic stupa, with an inscription below it that says, "Originally in the palace, the place where the emperor lived, relics showing signs of divine influence, not just once but multiple times. December 8, 601 AD." In the hall of the Ya檀 statue, there is a scripture called "The Timeless Scripture," written in red ink and placed in a lacquered box. The monks say it is an old item from the Sui dynasty. Behind the temple, there was originally a curved pond, which suddenly dried up when Faguo was on his deathbed. Until Vikan Chan Master stayed there, the spring became flowing because of the rain, and white lotus and algae grew naturally. Now it has become land again. To the south of the east corridor, there is a courtyard of the monk Suhuang, with four green paulownia trees in the courtyard, planted by Suhuang himself. During the middle of the Yuanhe era, many officials and nobles visited this courtyard. The paulownia trees exude sap in summer, staining clothes like fruit oil, which cannot be washed off.

In the eastern gate of Zhao Guo, there was a prominent official named Zheng Xiang. Once, he and several officials were trying to cool off and were bothered by sweating. They asked Sùgōng to plant pine trees for them. By evening, Sùgōng playfully cursed the tree, saying, "I have been planting you for over twenty years, and you cause people to sweat. If you make people sweat again next year, I will chop you into firewood." From then on, there was no more sweat. At the end of the Baoli era, I heard that this effect had lasted for over fifteen years. Sùgōng did not leave the monastery and recited the *Lotus Sutra* 37,000 times. At night, there were often foxes listening to the sutra, and during the fasting period, birds and crows would come to his hand for food. In the beginning of the Changqing era, a peony flower bloomed in harmony in front of the courtyard. Xuanyou wrote a poem about this monastery, and the memorable lines said, "Recited the *Lotus Sutra* 30,000 times over thirty springs, half a life without stepping on the dust of the monastery door."

There is now a Buddhist monk named Tienru Nanduo, who draws on altars with powdered pigment. His nature is impulsive and arrogant, and he is not very familiar with Chinese scriptures. He looks to the left at an image of a clam. According to old traditions, the Sui Emperor had a fondness for clams, and whatever he ate always had the flavor of clams, exceeding tens of thousands in number. Suddenly, a clam appeared, and it was struck as usual. The emperor was surprised by this. He placed it on the table, and during the night, there was light. By morning, the flesh had fallen off by itself, revealing inside one Buddha and two Bodhisattvas. The emperor grieved and regretted, vowing never to eat clams again. This was not during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Chen. There is a jade statue from Khotan, one foot seven inches high and a little more than an inch wide, depicting one Buddha, four Bodhisattvas, and one flying immortal. It is made from a single piece of jade, cut from a flawless source, with rich color as if dripping. The Heavenly King Pavilion was built during the Longqing era. Originally located inside the Chunming Gate, it was connected by a wall to the Southern Palace. Its size is the largest in the world. During the second year of the Taiping era, an imperial decree moved it to this temple. When dismantled, inside were found five hundred pieces of cloth and several dozen buckets of lacquer. Now, the images of tribal spirits and deities are ruined, but only the Heavenly King remains undamaged.

Words. Twenty-character linked verse: On a sunny day, I enter the monastery, speaking and silent, thinking of the Eastern Forest. All are friends who have forgotten their minds, who would startle birds resting in the shade (Shan Ji). There are pines to tie horses to, and when encountering a bowl, one might throw in a needle. Remembering the lines of Master Tang, deep Zen helps in clear recitation (Ke Gu). A rain washes away all the dust, and Zhi is allowed to pass through. We will smell the fragrant flowers together, without counting the leaves of the Bodhi tree (Meng Fu). Twenty-character linked verse about a sea creature. Though it changes due to a sparrow, it does not follow the waxing and waning of the moon. Even if there were a divine craftsman, divine work cannot be accomplished (Ke Gu). The appearance is entirely like the Buddha, the beginning only for the Sui dynasty. It is not the same as a stubborn oyster, but rather a contest between a bird and a shrimp (Shan Ji). Sacred pillar linked verse, with traces of an iron chain above. The will of heaven assists in good deeds, the sacred trace opens the sun (Ke Gu). One imagines the heavy wheel of thunder, a long, lightning-like rope (Shan Ji). It rises to support the heavens, unmoving, like a silver bell (Ke Gu). Hungry birds have not pecked at it, a mischievous dragon would not dare to hide (Shan Ji).

Each representation must be precise and relevant, and common books should not be quoted. A single treasure, without a hook, cannot be obtained (鼎上人). Only the lion can be tamed, not something a camel can endure (柯古). The pit has no bottom, and there is no victory in the traces (文上人). Crossing the river with a horse, carrying a monkey while walking (善继). Green in color and weak in strength, how many layers of fragrance does it have (梦复). The tail has already come out of the window, and the body can be taken as an example (约上人). Six tusks have flowers, and seven branches support the ground (柯古). Shape like jade snow, strength surpasses trivialities (善继). Garden opens on the side, river flows out of the nose (柯古). One drunkenness is hard to regulate, six pairs have been victorious (日高上人).

An Guo Temple in Changle Ward. The Red Hall, a dance pavilion used by Emperor Ruizong when he was a prince. The Eastern Zen Hall, also known as Water Tower Hall, has five walls along the northwestern corridor of the gate. Li Si Dao, a disciple of Wu Daozi, painted the eight classes of celestial beings on these walls, without using colors, yet the paintings still retain their original form. The shadow hall of Master Fa Kong, known as Ji Zhou Kong in the world, kept a donkey for a long time, which died after making a sound and running away when it was about to die. One of his disciples, Yun Song, suffered from a wind-related illness. He would often bury a pillar in an empty room and lock it, and whenever there was a problem, the illness would be cured. The Buddha hall, built in the early Kaiyuan era when Emperor Xuanzong dismantled his bedroom to donate it. The Maitreya statue facing south, moved by Master Fa Kong from Guangming Temple. Before the capital was established, the statue was in a village temple and often emitted light, and it was then called Guangming Temple. The temple was in Huaiyuan Ward and was later burned down by a fire, but only the statue remained. When Master Fa Kong first moved the statue, the rope was as large as a tiger's mouth, and dozens of oxen could not move it. Master Fa Kong held a censer, performed nine bows according to ritual, and wept while taking an oath. The statue suddenly made a loud noise and split into dozens of pieces. Within a day, it was moved to the temple. The Li Si Hall, in the Yuanhe period, was converted into a hall for the image of the sage, and the statue was moved to the eaves. The emperor suddenly dreamed of a monk with a remarkable appearance, who said, "Exposing it for several days, is this the will of the sage emperor?" Upon waking, the emperor visited and verified it, just as in the dream. He then ordered it to be moved to the hall and placed it with curtains on the side.

In the Guangming Temple, there are statues of the Buddhist deity Guizimo and Prince Wenhu, whose gestures and expressions are lifelike. The sculptor's name is Li Xi. The temple features a courtyard nestled in a mountainous setting, surrounded by ancient trees and towering hills, creating an atmosphere as secluded as a valley. At that time, earth was transported to build it. In the main hall of the Zen Master's courtyard, there is a pine tree with a grain-like appearance, whose branches spread out and cover the area, providing shade for over ten people.

Poetry. Collaborative verses in the style of "Yin Hou": Layered and fragmented, the clear sky, the remaining afterglow enhances the red. The moon's path nears the mythical bird, the bird's path meets the wind (Shanjie). Moss-covered paths, serene under the golden wheel, light clouds surround the palace of the white sun (During the reign of Emperor Yuanhe, the emperor visited this place). Wall poems passed down by the guest (The poet Chen Zhi inscribed a poem in this courtyard: "The algae are still cold, the dragon's trace remains; the red building opens to the sunlight.") The door inscription bears the name of Xiu Gong (Guangxuan, a monk who lived in this courtyard, was renowned for his poetry, known as the *Honglou Collection*. Kegu). Collaborative verses in the style of Suibai: One courtyard, difficult to invade by the heat, moss-covered paths with deep shadows. Branches compete with resting birds, the gentle breeze opens one's sleeves (Kegu). The lingering scent of rain adds color, the fading sunlight casts shadows on stones. In idle moments, poetic thoughts arise, aiding in entering a meditative state (Shanjie). Inscription for the Courtyard of Daji (One to seven characters, each person takes two subjects): Stillness, emptiness. The heat of summer, finding peace (Mengfu). The lamp is extinguished, the incense removed. Guests from the east, books from the west. Green beans hang from the eaves, white lotus flowers appear in the scriptures. Debating endlessly the origins of the Dharma, silent understanding of affairs (Kegu concludes). Will the soul enter the stream of the spring palace? The lingering sound of the neighbor's flute raises doubts about the remaining purity of the Dharma (Kegu continues). Discussing obscure matters within the Buddhist tradition (Requires a response): The characters "mí," "shā" lamp, "huá" silk, "xiàng" recommendation, (Sheng shangren) the place of imitation, the forest of emulation (Kegu continues at night, unfinished).

The Zhaoying Temple in Changle Lane was established in the third year of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty (583 AD). Originally named Hongshan Temple, it was renamed in the eighteenth year (598 AD) of Emperor Wen's reign. On the eastern wall inside the southern middle gate, Wu Daoxuan painted a white depiction of the Hell Transformation (Di Yu Bian). The brushwork is powerful and fierce, the transformations eerie and strange, and the sight of it makes one's hair stand on end. This is a particularly successful part of Wu Daoxuan's work. In the western corridor of the Three-Stage Courtyard, Fan Changshou painted the Western Transformation (Xi Fang Bian) and sixteen pairs of events. The pond is especially exquisite, so realistic that it seems the water is flowing into the floating wall when viewed closely. On the temple gate, the white-painted trees and rocks resemble the style of Yan Lide. The author carried a rubbing of Yan Lide's work from the Xingtian Shrine to compare it, finding no difference. In the southern part of the western middle gate, Wu Daoxuan painted dragons and the mustaches of Heavenly Kings, with brushstrokes as strong as iron. There is a celestial maiden holding a brazier, whose eyes seem to want to speak. In the Huayan Courtyard, there is a statue of Lusha, six feet tall, with a classical and intricate design. Under the pagoda, there are relics (舍利) stored, measured in three dou and four sheng (ancient Chinese units of volume).

During the time of moving the pagoda, the monk Shouhang built a dharma assembly and displayed relics for the common people to view. The chanting and praise were not yet finished when relics appeared all over the ground, and the men and women dared not step on them, all leaving the temple. The official Shou then made nearly ten thousand small clay and wooden pagodas to bury them, and now there are still tens of thousands remaining. The temple has over six hundred small silver statues, a golden Buddha statue several feet long, and a large silver statue over six feet tall, all ancient and exquisite. There is also a small folding screen with seven treasures inscribed with the *Heart Sutra*, stored in a precious box, adorned with multicolored beads and white beads arranged in a dazzling array. During the An Lushan Rebellion, imperial concubines hid here in the temple. The screen has fifteen sheets, thirty lines, and after the text it says: "The sponsor of the merit is Sima Hengcun, who wishes to dedicate this for the benefit of all sentient beings." The golden text of the sutra, Master Shanji suspected was a foreign object.

The poem "Wu Painting" describes a series of poetic lines or verses associated with Wu, depicting a painting or artistic work that captures the essence of life and death. The painting portrays a scene of chaos and turmoil, where the depiction of ghosts and gods appears as if they are emerging from the wall, creating a lifelike and terrifying effect. In the darkness, one cannot tell whether a thunderbolt might strike, suggesting an element of unpredictability or danger. A momentary illusion of life, perhaps in the painting or a literary work, causes a sense of awe or fear. The painting also depicts scenes of danger or chaos, where one might encounter a fierce tiger or a strange, towering rock. The painting's depiction of the underworld or hell is too terrifying to be imagined, causing a state of extreme fear or terror. The painting suggests that if one could create a Buddhist temple or sanctuary through the power of water, then one would not need to dwell in the suffering and impermanence of life (火宅). The poem then records the good sayings of Zen masters, including a statement by Lanruo: "Every door leads to the path to Chang'an." Another statement by a Zen master from Jingzhou: "One must look at the amount of effort one has put in." A statement by an unnamed Zen master: "The final great breath must be clear and distinct." The poem also includes a four-character inscription for a public hall or temple: "The flickering flame, the blue flame continues." The inscription refers to a Buddhist mantra or incantation and the four *Agamas* (collections of early Buddhist teachings). The inscription suggests that one should merely attend to the plain screen, possibly referring to a simple or unadorned object of meditation or contemplation. The inscription also refers to a master's room or a place of retreat, often used in Zen contexts to describe a place of meditation or study. The inscription suggests that one should welcome guests without locking the door, symbolizing openness and hospitality.

Linghua Temple in Datong Lane, during the early years of Emperor Da Li, had a monk named Yan preaching the scriptures. Suddenly, flowers rained from the sky, disappearing after reaching the ground within a short distance. At night, there was a light illuminating the room, and an imperial decree renamed the temple as Linghua. Venerable Yan was the teacher of Kang Zang, who originally lived in Jinggong Lane. One day, Kang Zang suddenly saw a wheel-like light, which everyone else also saw. Following the light, they arrived at the place where Venerable Yan was preaching, where the light disappeared. In the west corridor of the Buddha hall, there were sixteen statues of eminent monks, which were moved from the southern palace during the early period of Emperor Tianbao. The paintings were described as crude and vulgar. The Guanyin Hall was located in the northwest corner of the temple. At the end of the Jianzhong period, a commoner named Qu Yan suffered from a severe illness near death. In a dream, a Bodhisattva touched his wound and said, "I reside in Linghua Temple." Qu Yan woke up sweating and recovered after several days. He went to the temple to search for the statue and found it in the Holy Painting Hall, matching his vision. The entire city's population worshiped the statue, and Qu Yan established a community and built a hall to move the statue. In the Holy Painting Hall, large wooden frames were constructed as walls, adorned with colorful and intricate decorations. The paintings were originally created by Shao Wu Zong, but the reason for calling them "holy" is unknown. According to the *Record of the Western Regions*, there was a shrine east of the Bodhi Tree. In the past, Brahmin brothers wanted to depict the Buddha, but no one responded to the call for a long time. Suddenly, a person claimed to be skilled in painting the Buddha's perfect form, requiring only incense mud and a lamp to illuminate the room, and staying closed for six months. The final stage of the process was reached, with only four days remaining. Opening the door, no one was there, but only the right arm was unfinished. It seems that a devout monk embellished this story. There was also an ancient statue in the hall. The cypress tree mentioned in the *Yumuye Ji* was cut down during the Tahe period for temple construction materials.

A Collection of Verses. Together we entered the Sunset Temple, peering into the Gate of Sweet Dew (by the ascetic). The fragrant aroma attracted moss, enduring grass mixed with orchids and scented reeds (in a dream). Quickly answering with a verse in a flying box, new poetry leaning on a cane for discussion (by the ancient tree). A broken banner marking an ancient temple, holy paintings shining on the high walls (by the one who continues the good tradition). How could one envy birds in cages, it is hard to prevent apes on the windowsill (by the ancient tree). One sound has one nature, three words repeated three times (by the one who continues the good tradition). The Temple of Precious Response in Daozheng Ward. Han Xie, a person from Lantian, often delivered wine to a pawnbroker's house when he was young. Wang Wei's brothers were not yet successful, and he often wandered around while delivering wine. Han Xie often collected debts from Wang's family. Playfully drawing people and horses on the ground, Wang Wei was deeply engaged in painting and found his intention and interest strange, so he gave him two million coins annually and ordered him to study painting for over ten years. The temple's collection of Buddhist and Hindu goddesses, all portraits of the courtesans of Qi Gong. The temple has Han Xie's painting of Maitreya, wearing purple robes, with a smiling Bodhisattva and two lions on the right side, still very lifelike. Old iron and stone from Wang's family and the one-year-old son of Qi Gong who had died, painted like a lotus, each day placed in a basin and taken out to the temple. The Maitreya Hall, Qi Gong's bedroom. On the north side of the east corridor, Yang Xu's paintings of ghosts and gods. Qi Gong disliked his handwriting as not skillful, so he only allowed one wall.

Poetry. Collaborative Poem by Monks: Ancient paintings evoke thoughts of the mountain of the ancients, the upper part seems to doubt the place of the legendary site. A butterfly moves at leisure through the grass, a cicada reveals a tall pine tree in the morning (by Ko Kuo). Fragrant characters fade, the magical seal disappears, the golden sutra is opened (by Shan Ji). A well is connected to the veins of a pine tree, a book unfolds a secret message in a cave (by Meng Fu). A poem about a small, true form: as if alive, a small, true form, still not yet settled in the dust (by Meng Fu). Sleeves move away from the wall, a slanted branch wants to approach people (by Ko Kuo). In the past, known as outstanding, affection and favor occupied a specific arrangement (by Shan Ji). Not sent to play in a specific place, how could be taught to peek at a specific neighbor (by Meng Fu). A loud, piercing sound from Yuhu Tower, enjoying new songs in Hong Pavilion (by Ko Kuo). A cicada timidly performs a specific dance, a moth startled by a specific facial expression (by Shan Ji). Who has the skill to draw a figure, buying laughter and not hesitating to be poor (by Ko Kuo). Repeated hills and lost village paths, deep springs separated by the Han River (by Meng Fu). Knowing to make wings, definitely becoming scales (by Shan Ji). A waning moon on Wushan Mountain in the evening, remaining霞 on the Luo River in the morning (by Ko Kuo).

The Xuan (or Xuan) Law Temple in Anyi Ward was originally the residence of Zhang Pin. He once supported a monk who made reciting the *Lotus Sutra* his profession. After over ten years, Zhang's disciples slandered the monk for having an affair with his servant girl and killed him on some pretext. After the monk's death, the entire house often heard the sound of reciting sutras without interruption. Zhang soon realized his injustice and regretted too late. Therefore, he donated his house to become a temple, casting 100,000 gold-colored bronze statues. The stone niches were all full, with still several tens of thousands more. In the East Corridor, the Southern Guanyin Hall, the Nalanda Hall inside the north-facing wall, there are murals depicting the transformation of Vimalakirti. On the screen, it is said that Ouyang Xun wrote it. On that day, Shanji ordered the screen to be removed and climbed onto the bed to read it, and found that Ouyang Xun indeed wrote it, thus confirming it was not a mistake. In the northwest corner of the courtyard, there are calligraphies by Huaisu, a preface by Yan Zhenqing, and inscriptions by Zhang Wei, the Attendant, and Qian Qi, the Censor. In the East Corridor of the Manchu Hall, during the Dali period, the painter Chen Zi'ang painted images of elephants, horses, and figures in the court, which were considered the best of their time. And on the eaves, there are images of the Buddha and his attendants, the style being similar to Han Hunchun. On the west wall, Liu Zheng painted two pine trees, which also do not follow the usual style.

Citing stories from Buddhist scriptures about birds, the following must be precise and relevant: the vulture's head forms a mountain, the chicken's foot names a famous mountain. The peacock is a scripture, the parrot recites a verse. The mythical bird transforms, entering the theory of numbers and greed. Not yet able to escape the cage, how could it offer fruit? The mythical bird is above, the goose falls in front. The top of the nest is safe, entering the shadow is not feared. Within the characters, one suspects a crane; within the red, one recognizes a goose. Citing stories from Buddhist scriptures about animals, the following must be precise and relevant: the king of the Garuda birds, the calf with silver horns. The place is named Deer Park, the pagoda is named Partridge Departure. Pecking and hatching at the same time, the mythical bird is tamed. Citing stories about horses: adding to the suffering (升上人), ascending (善继), horse treasure (梦复), carrying scriptures (柯古), loving horse (升上人), blue horse (善继), wheat agreement to eat rice (柯古), iron horse (升上人), first Thodha (柯古), victorious step (升上人), entering the right path (柯古).

The Bodhidharma Temple in Pingkang Ward: Murals and paintings on the east and west walls and columns of the main hall are remnants of the east corridor, painted by Zheng Fashi. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, they were moved to the north wall of the inner aisle of the Great Buddha Hall due to the destruction of the building. On the eastern wall near the windward side of the dining hall, Wu Daozi painted the *Vimalakirti Sutra* with verses inscribed by himself, his handwriting strong and vigorous, as if tearing through the hair of demons. The next section of the mural depicts a bone-ritualist sage, with flowing robes and a wall that seems to move with the wind. In the rear wall of the inner aisle of the Buddha Hall, Wu Daozi painted scenes from the *Disaster Elimination Sutra*, featuring ancient and perilous landscapes. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, the emperor wanted to move the murals but was concerned about their potential damage, so he ordered skilled painters to recreate them. On the eastern wall of the inner aisle of the Buddha Hall, Wu Daozi painted the transformation of Vimalakirti, with Shariputra's eyes turning and glancing. By the end of the Yuanhe period, the popular lecture monk Wen Shu restored the mural, but the original brushwork was no longer visible. Therefore, Chancellor Zheng Gong of Xingyuan inscribed a poem on the north wall of the temple, saying, "I only worry about the colors being stained, not about the arms becoming fat." The inscriptions on the back of the temple stele are intricately carved and are believed to be modeled by Zheng Fashi. Initially, Monk Huijue built a large residence using donations, covering more than ten acres. After the construction was completed, he brewed a hundred shi of wine, arranging the bottles and jars in the side aisles, and invited Wu Daozi to view them. He then said, "May you paint for me, and I will reward you with this wine." Wu Daozi, who loved wine and was attracted by the abundance, happily agreed. I find that the quality of the murals here seems inferior to those at Jinggong Temple. The sculptures inside the eastern gate of the middle three gates are said to be the work of Wang Nai'er, a disciple of Wu Daozi, according to the account of Shan Ji.

There is a ghost on the side that has some spirit, and often when common people playfully offend it, they fall ill, with their mouths and eyes resembling the ghost. The regulations of the temple stipulate that the bell tower is on the east side, but only this temple has it on the west side because the residence of Li Youzuo Linfu is on the east side, hence the bell tower was constructed on the west side. Inside the temple, there are the tortoiseshell whip of De Ling and the seven treasures tent of Lady Wang, the wife of De Ling. The abbot of the temple, Yuan Jing, knows many stories of the Buddhist tradition and says that Li Youzuo often invites monks from this temple to his residence to perform a ritual on his birthday. There was a monk named Yi who once praised Buddha and donated a saddle, which was sold for a material value of seven thousand. Another monk named Guang had a good reputation for several years, next praised Buddha, and greatly praised the merits of Li Youzuo, hoping to gain favor. After the ritual was completed, a colorful basket was brought out from behind the curtain, containing a scented cloth and a pad for a thing, which looked like a rusted nail, several inches long. The monk returned, feeling disappointed, ashamed, and regretful for several days.

Furthermore, the emperor realized that his ministers could not deceive him, so he carried the object to the western market and showed it to the foreign merchants. When the merchants saw it, they were astonished and said, "How did you obtain this object, venerable monk? You must sell it, at no price." The monk tried to ask for 100,000 coins, but the merchant laughed and said, "Not yet." The monk then tried even harder to persuade him, increasing the offer to 500,000 coins, and the merchant said, "This is worth one million." The monk then gave it to him. The monk inquired about the object's name, and it was said, "This is a bone of a treasure." There was another monk in the temple who did not mention his name and would sit and lie on the two corridors of the temple, carrying a bundle of straw, refusing to stay in the lodging. After several years, the head monk of the temple urged him to stay in the lodging, saying, "Are you pressing on me?" That night, the monk burned his body with the bundle of straw. By morning, only ash remained, with no smell of blood or bodily fluids. The crowd then realized he was a strange person and molded the ash into a statue. Now, the statue is in the Buddha hall, and the world calls him the Bundled Straw Master.

In poetry, the verses read: All are idle, left to be hated by the common folk (Dream Revisited). Guests are different from contemporary guests, monks are not those who leave the monastery (Ko Gu). Far away, the sound of the gong can be heard through the sparse window, in the morning, the light of the lamp in the secluded niche is discernible (Shan Ji). When walking, the sound of the pearl banner is heard, when reciting, the clear water in the bowl is observed (Dream Revisited). The lines are rich in the interest of the world beyond, the pleasure of traveling is shared with friends in the community (Ko Gu). In the quiet, the tamed pigeons are already there, in the temple, even the eagles are well cared for (Shan Ji). The gold-coated pen is [unclear], the colorful paper is not silk (Sheng Shangren). The tin staff has been hammered and forged, the field clothes follow the broken dikes (Ko Gu). Taking up the bed makes one feel ashamed of one's shoulder, rolling up the curtain relies on the long arm (Shan Ji). The Buddha's light begins to shine, the layers of the demon's sky are broken (Ko Gu). In the incantation, secret plans are revealed, in the discussion, the correct order is established (Shan Ji). Brave, wearing a needle and stone (Sheng Shangren), dangerous, guarding the hill and well with vines (Sheng Shangren).

● Volume 6 of the Continuation: Records of Temples and Pagodas The Fongci Temple in Xuanyang Ward was originally the residence of Yang Guoqin, a concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, during the Kaiyuan era. Later, An Lushan, who usurped power, appointed Tian Gan Zhen as the Governor of Jingzhao and took this residence as his own. It was subsequently used as the residence of Guo Ai, the son-in-law of the emperor. Shortly after his ascension to the throne, Emperor Xuanzong had the Empress Dowager establish the Fongci Temple for the welfare of Princess Jincheng. She donated 200,000 coins and three carts of embroidered scrolls, and selected forty monks from ten temples in the eastern part of the city to reside there. Currently, there is a monk named Weizhe who transported a wooden model of the stupa containing the relics of Emperor Ashoka from Mingzhou. Two years after the completion of the temple, the young daughter of Yang Jingzhi, a junior official in charge of agriculture, at the age of thirteen, composed a six-line poem dedicated to the temple. She referred to herself as the twenty-seventh-generation descendant of Confucius from the West of the Passes and used the pen name De Lin. One of the striking lines reads: "The golden wheels of the sun and moon move, the green trees of Ya檀 in autumn. The tower is divided like the wings of geese, the bell hangs from the phoenix-shaped tower." Due to her composition, she was granted imperial clothing. Regarding the attire of Buddhist monks, the following terms are used in pairs: "elephant trunk" and "cow ear" (Ko Gu). "Five pockets" and "three robes" (Shan Ji). "Shame and gratitude," "shaking off dust" (Sheng Shangren). "Worn-out robes," "strictly maintaining one's body" (Yue Shangren). "Clothes worn for ten days" should be divided into three parts (Ru Shangren). "Mixed body" measuring four inches, covering two fingers (Ko Gu). "Ragged form," "knife-damaged" (Shan Ji). "Its shape like rice," "its color like lotus" (Sheng Shangren). "Red hemp, white beans, either green or black" (Ko Gu).

Guangzhi Lane Guangzhi Temple, originally the shadow hall of a monk in the official grape garden, where the master was named Huizhong. In the second year of Shangyuan during the reign of Emperor Suzong, he was summoned to the capital and initially stayed in this temple. The imperial edict read: "Coming with staff and alms bowl, the capital is not far. Having long fasted and purified his mind, to fill my humble heart." During the Jianzhong era, a monk built the Manjusri Hall, laying the foundation near the water, concerned about harming life. He then built a three-month meditation hall, praying for all creatures with one foot to many feet, and those without feet to go away. When digging to the water level, no insects or ants were found. Then, passing through the water again, insects fell into a well, called the 'Life-Protecting Well,' which has dried up to this day. Also, a bronze toad was cast for a smoke extinguishing lamp, famous throughout the world. Now the Manjusri Hall often recites sutras and is often given incense. The treasure terrace is very prominent; when climbing it, one can see the four directions clearly. Above, the windows have paintings by Yidui, below, paintings by Wu Daoxuan, none of which are their masterpieces. Prime Minister Wei Chuhou, from serving in the inner court to becoming prime minister, always came to this pagoda to burn incense and pay homage. The Puxian Hall, originally the dressing room of the Empress, when grapes were ripe, the Empress would visit this hall. Now the hall has paintings by Yidui, which are quite unique, with paintings on the four walls and depictions of skin and bones, the craftsmanship is extremely daring. Also, three demonesses are depicted, their bodies as if emerging from the wall. Also, the halo of the Buddha, with colors mixed and confusing the eyes. The eastern wall near the Buddha's seat has a brocade that looks like a broken ancient sign. Also, on the left and right, there are Indian monks and various foreign figures, but they are not as impressive as the west wall, which is very close.

A Farewell to the Zen Master's Memorial Hall: A Sequence of Couplets There is no emptiness under the name, and the stern appearance of Ao Cao. Thoroughly understanding the King of Emptiness, perfectly integrated into the realm of the Buddha (Shan Ji). A single word hits the vital point, suddenly awakening the drunkards. Unmoving as Mount Meru, (one says, "unmoving like Mount Meru.") In many ways, without limit in argument (Meng Fu). Frankly in the presence of the emperor, verse responses without evasion. You are like Vaisravana, the outer form as if taking off shoes (Ke Gu). Only with reason as measure, not speaking of strange and powerful things. Breaking down arrogance, compassion guiding greed and anger (Sheng Shangren). At that time, lacking Zhi and Xu, who understands the deep meaning? As appropriate, how could one speak of three, directly opening the non-duality (Ke Gu).

The Bao Shou Temple in Yi Shan Ward was originally the residence of Gao Lishi. In the ninth year of the Tianbao era (750 AD), it was donated as a temple. Initially, when the bell was cast, Gao Lishi held a celebration, and the entire court attended. The sound of the bell was so loud that it could be heard for miles, and people followed his wish by ringing it twenty times in succession. The scripture storage hall had an intricate and delicate design, and the two pagodas had large fire pearls that could hold more than ten dou. Li Zhuo, an official from Heyang who was interested in ancient artifacts, was friends with the monk Zengzeng. They once visited this temple together to view the old items in the treasury. Suddenly, they found an object wrapped like a quilt in a broken jar, which was torn and dirty, and dust rose when touched. Li Zhuo looked at it slowly and realized it was a painting. He acquired it with three county maps and thirty bolts of silk, had his family restore it, and it turned out to be a large painting of more than ten square feet. He consulted the Grand Attendant Liu Gongquan, who confirmed it was Zhang Xuan's painting "Stone Bridge Figure." Emperor Xuanzong gave it to Gao, who then left it in the temple. Later, the picture dealer Zong Mu reported it to the Left Army, and soon after, a small envoy with dozens of soldiers came to the house, announcing the imperial order to take it. It was taken to the palace on the same day. The late emperor, who had a fondness for antiquities, was very pleased to see it and ordered it to be hung in the Yunshao Academy.

The temple possesses a frame depicting the Bodhisattva of the先天, which originally came from the Miaojī Temple in Chengdu. In the early years of the Kaiyuan era, there was a nun named Weiba, who often recited the Great Compassion Mantra. Liu Yi, a resident of Shuangliu County, known as Yir, who was eleven years old, wished to serve the nun, but she sent him away and he did not leave. He often meditated in a secluded chamber and once told Nun Wei, “The Bodhisattva of the先天 has appeared in this place.” He then sifted ash in the courtyard, and one night a giant footprint several feet in size appeared, with complete wheel patterns. He then sought out a painter, who set colors at will, but none were satisfactory. There was a monk named Yang Facheng, who claimed he could paint. Yir often joined his palms and prayed upward, and then Yang would instruct him. It took nearly ten years before the work was finally completed. Later, the Bodhisattva of the先天 was sculpted in a total of 242 forms, each shaped like a tower, with arms divided into patterns resembling the wish-granting vine. The inscriptions on these images include one hundred forty-two references to the bird tree, one phoenix with four wings, and a water-bellied tree; the inscriptions are deeply strange and cannot be fully detailed. The painting designs totaled fifteen volumes. Liu Qi, a teacher and nephew of Cui Ning, divided three of these volumes and sent them upward to the capital. At that time, Wei Fenggu was the chief clerk, and he submitted them to the court. Later, on the eighth day of the fourth lunar month, they were bestowed upon Gao Lishi. The version currently in Chengdu is a secondary copy.

A Poem in Continuous Verses: Innate Praise The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, in her myriad forms, appears with an extremely strange appearance. Amidst the chaos and malevolent forces, the many demons worship her. (Contributed by Shan Ji) Pointing to the confusion and disorder, she is listed in the regions and boundaries. The matter is clearly spread; why is it not understandable? (Contributed by Ke Gu) Yama and the Tokugawa clan, the great sage is innate. The many images are interwoven, densely crowded. (Contributed by Meng Fu) Hundreds of millions of flowers bloom, and hundreds of thousands of lamps burn. Like a network of threads, vast and continuous. (Contributed by Shan Ji) The realm of Yama is filled with sorrow (or perhaps "filled with peace"), and the island of Loka is cleared of suffering. Right mindfulness returns to reliance, and the many faults are like comets. (Contributed by Ke Gu) The evil dregs can be discarded, and the delusional film can be shed. Bowing as if to the void, with a gentle and affectionate gaze. (Contributed by Shan Ji) A person who has lost all virtue, facing it directly. Not a single thought arises, not yet met with it. (Contributed by Ke Gu)

Testimonies: Gao Lishi called his elder brother (Ko Gu), called his father-in-law (Shan Ji) and called the general (Meng Fu), called the old man (Ko Gu), and the five wheels (Shan Ji). Initially, he wielded a spear (Meng Fu), often lay on a deer bed (Ko Gu), was six chi and five cun tall (Shan Ji), and was buried alongside Tai Ling (Meng Fu). He recited chrysanthemums (Ko Gu), his teeth formed an imprint (Shan Ji), his upper and lower countries (Meng Fu), dreamed of a whip (Ko Gu), and the Liu family grew beards (Shan Ji). The Jingyu Temple in Xuanyang Ward was originally the residence of Empress Tai Mu. The temple monk said, "Outside the Three-Step Courtyard, it was where Emperor Yao shot a peacock. Inside and outside the Zen Courtyard, according to the *Yumujì* (Record of Scenic Views), Wang Zhao Yin painted it. Inside the west side, there is the dragon king He Xiujì, who has spiritual power. Inside the west side, there are the fierce and mighty fire-eyed demons and the Northern Heavenly King. Inside the east side, there is the wise gate, and the wild demon tribe. A snake coils around the demon's head, and the sweat and smoke are terrifying. The eastern corridor has dangerous and strange trees and stones, even the senior monks find it strange. The western corridor has the Bodhisattva of Longevity. Inside the south wall of the courtyard, Huang Fu Zhen painted ghosts, deities, and carved figures, as if they were about to move out of the painting. Zhen and Wu Daoxuan were contemporaries, and Wu felt threatened by Zhen's talent and hired someone to kill him. Inside the Hall of the Ten Thousand Bodhisattvas, there is a pagoda decorated with hundreds of small golden copper pagodas.

During the Dali period, the son of Director of Public Works Liu was born with both hands joined together. At seven years old, he began reciting the Lotus Sutra. Upon his death, he was cremated, and numerous relics were found, which were then divided and stored in golden and bronze pagodas. It is said that this was Liu Ming (also recorded as Liu Luo). In the eastern corridor of the Buddha hall, there stands an ancient Buddhist hall, which originally came from Yong Village. All the statues in the hall are carved from stone. It is said that Emperor Gong of Sui passed away in this hall (Yong Village, also recorded as Wei Village). The paintings on the three main gates are also the work of Huangfu Zhen. The Vajra was said to have had a spirit in the past. During the early Tianbao period, the residence of Prince-Consort Dugu Ming was near the temple. Dugu had a maid named Huai Xiang, who was young and handsome, and often liked a scholar from the west neighbor. Because of a nighttime appointment at the temple gate, they were both bound and died by a giant snake. In the western seat of the Buddha hall, there stands a foreign deity, very ancient and simple. Before the Zhenyuan period, the Western Turks had two alliances, both of which recorded this deity standing on the altar and swearing. It is said that at that time, the deity had great power.

Poetic Composition by the Three-Stage Institute: Dense and grand, the Sui people sing of mulberry trees. With twin bows, the peacock is felled; one arrow wounds the greedy wolf. Standing a hundred paces to gaze at clouds, the moon is aimed with nine rules. The dragon is captured as waves are broken; the target is hit, forming a wall. It is as if gold and drums are pierced; the stone bridge is pierced with doubt. Adding force to control the river, to quell the wildness of the sky. Exceptional skill repels southern invaders; heroic fame reaches the distant lands. The beautiful tortoise is no match; the slain pig is not the greatest. The dragon arm surpasses the monkey's; starlight rises with the arrow's gleam. Boast of reaching the highest birds; sit idly and plan the grand hall. The Zhaofu Temple in Chongyi Ward, originally named Zhengjue, was destroyed at the beginning of the Tang dynasty. The land was given to build a residence for the princes, where Emperor Ruizong lived as a prince. In the second year of the Ganfeng era, the silk pavilion of Princess Changning was moved here, and the temple was rebuilt. Inside the temple, there was an old pond that was filled with earth from the eastern street of Yongle. Now, tree roots are often exposed in the ground. In the second year of the Chang'an era, the imperial court provided a set of life-sized golden bronze statues and nine types of music. The plaques for the north and south gates were personally delivered to the temple by Princes Qi and Xue, with a colorful procession of elephants and a large entourage. The fragrance lingered in the streets for several days. In the second year of the Jinglong era, another life-sized statue was granted, and an imperial decree ordered the construction of a separate hall for the statue, which was the statue of Emperor Xuanzong when he was the crown prince. In the second year of the Tianxian era, the imperial treasury provided two million coins, and a thousand skilled craftsmen were employed to rebuild the temple.

The Temple of the Sacred Image of Emperor Ruizong, outside the main gate, has walls adorned with depictions of ghosts and spirits, which were moved from inside. The paintings are highly distinctive. One ghost is holding a pheasant, and the pheasant's feathers seem to stand on end, suggesting a sense of unease. In the treasury, there is a painting of the goddess Guizimo, created by Li Zhen during the reign of Emperor Dezong. This painting often adheres to the established rules and techniques, and the figures holding mirrors are particularly well-executed. In the southwest corner of the temple, there is an image of the monk Sengga, which has always been considered spiritual. To this day, the people continue to offer banners and incense without interruption. A temple servant who comes early in the morning often lights lamps on the ground, doing so consistently for several decades. During the time when Li, a high-ranking official, was in office, a thief led the servant to the temple early in the morning, and officials attempted to apprehend him. Unable to bear the injustice, the servant climbed the bell tower and invoked Sengga, resulting in his body being shattered. In a daze, he saw a strange monk strike him with a ritual object and say, "No suffering, it will be treated." The servant regained consciousness, jumped down several feet, and emerged unharmed. Upon hearing this, the thief felt remorse and confessed, and the servant was ultimately exonerated.

Poetry. A gift to the monks in verse: translated from the Western scriptures, the incense of the Buddhist temple still lingers. Anxious for the common folk, the sun sets behind the temple wall (Ko Gu). The island is named for its fertile soil, and the mountain is remembered for its name. In debate, worldly wisdom is shattered; in meditation, the strength of demons is broken (Shan Ji). Xu Zhe's mind is deeply enlightened, Tang Xiu's poetic inspiration is long-lasting. Clearly reciting, the sound of the bell is interrupted; long speech, like a string of beads, is disrupted (Ko Gu). Following one's mood, writing on banana leaves; in leisure, entering the study. Randomly inscribed on ancient walls, strange paintings encircle the long corridor. Stories of Buddhist monks and their encounters with mysterious or difficult-to-understand words: competing for land, writing the word "zhēn" (Shan Ji); Yan Dou knows her uncles and aunts (Ko Gu); interpreting a dream of a sheep carrying a fish (Meng Fu); asking, "Who is the underling?" (Shan Ji); on the tower, the word "shī" is written (Ko Gu). Stories of Buddhist monks and their interactions with others: He Chong's ambition is vast, like the universe (Shan Ji); this child is weary from the ferry (Ko Gu); born after the elder, he ascends to heaven (Meng Fu); the two He's are devoted to the Buddha (Shan Ji); when asked his age, he answers, "Five years old, the Little Buddha" (Ko Gu); answering in four tones, "The Temple of the Heavenly Treasure" (Meng Fu); the Bodhisattva frowns, thus showing compassion for the six realms (Shan Ji); Zhou's wife, what is her flesh? (Ko Gu). The Temple of the Heavenly Saviour in Zhaoguo Lane, within the temple, there is a dragon robe woven by the Empress, and six embroidered garments. In the second courtyard from the south in the east corridor, there is a hall where the Preceptor of the Law made robes. The Manjushri Hall has several pines, very strange.

Poem on the Robe of the Venerable Xuanlü:** "Three robes cover us in the cold of midnight, Wearing them does not calm the mind of the nun. There is no way to cover the dragon palace, Dust flies from the fields, causing suffering." (After Xuanlü) **Response by Shan Ji:** "In the cold night of the South Mountain, when wearing the robe, One corner remains unmoved by the strong wind. Who would see this and feel ashamed? Even if intermittent, it should still protect the dragon." (Ko Gu) **Poem on the Strange Pine:** "Why are the cypress and laurel so sparse, While the elm and willow are far apart? No one dares to take advantage, Not even a single branch is to be trifled with." (Ko Gu) **Poem on the Courtyard:** "The courtyard is covered in deep moss, After the wind, the birds sing. When branches are broken, Even common grass avoids the shade." (Shan Ji) **Poem on the Secluded Path:** "The roots of the secluded path are exposed, The branches of the quiet room freely intrude. One tree in the wind is perfect, To aid the blue cloud in its song." (Meng Fu) **Poem on the Wind:** "The sound of sweeping the windows is constant, Heavy dew drips on the cold steps. The wind makes a branch sturdy, Observing the changing dynamics from afar." (Sheng Shangren) **Poem on the High Monk:** "The canopy obstructs the entrance to the building, The roots encroach on the narrow well. The high monk feels sad, Separated from the clear mist." (Ko Gu) **The Temple of Eternal Life in Yong'an Ward:** East of the Three Gates, there is a painting by Wu Daozi, which seems not to be to his liking. The Buddha Hall is named "Meeting the Immortals," originally the bathing hall inside. During the reign of Emperor Dezong, there was a Zen Master named Zhengzhi who often performed miracles. Sometimes he would work in the fields at Zhangdu's hermitage, and at night he would return to the temple, as if he were in the Golden Mountain boundary, which is seven hundred miles away.

Poetry of Leisure "Leisure is good, spending the day with pine trees as companions. This joy is unknown to others, yet the gentle breeze carries the sound of monks' voices." (Dream of Return) "Leisure is good, worldly affairs do not trouble the heart. Sitting by the window, watching the shadows of the tree move across three sides." (Ke Gu) "Leisure is good, the slow, muffled sound of a bell lingers. The topic begins on the scroll, and in the painting, a monk bears the surname Zhi."
